review_text: Definately a rushed game, get the the restoration patch from the workshop. Otherwise, the game is largely broken. That said, did you play KOTOR and wish you could have started as a jedi, instead of choosing some other class first? Did you want to stick your lightsaber in more people's businness? Did you want to fantasize about having more force powers and training people to become jedi? Did you want to train with someone, only to find out she'll only train without her armor on?  If you said yes to all of those but the last one, good news! KOTOR 2 exists, and it's great. If you said YES to the last one, you are a sad, creepy loner living in a basement.  My only complaint is that fullsize lightsabers are hard to come by. I ended up having four other jedi on my team, but not enough lightsabers for everyone.

I was hesitant at first to get into this game because of how simplisitc it looked. Not that that was a big turn off, just that I thought I could find something similar that would be more detailed. However, I regret not playing this game much sooner. The ability to micromanage your ship and crew like a real starship is incredible. You will often face situations where you must think quickly to adjust your power, repair damage, open bulkheads to put out fires, fight boarding parties, and much much more. It possibilities just goes on and on. It was a bit frustrating to die after all my hard work, but the game is so easy to get into and the deadly narrative of space exploration and combat makes failing not only more exciting, but more exceptable. I would actually like to see different modes of gameplay, but for the time being, I think I will be playing this game for a very long time. 9/10

Flat out one of the best games ever made.  Squad has managed to creat an experience that merges the goofy fun of being a 5 year budding engineer playing NASA with legos in the living room, and a literal crash course in physics and engineering. Be careful; between your first hilarious flailing attempts to reach orbit, that glorious lopsided assembly of parts that somehow manages to land mostly intact on Duna, and that meticulously planned gravitational slingshot maneuver sending a 500 ton astroid-turned-space-station on a mission to Jool, you might accidently learn something.  The huge library of mods only expands the fun. Looking for challenge and realism? Snag one of several mods adding life support requirements, or communications networks. Want more exploration? There are mods adding things like satallite mapping, orbital surveys, and additional ground-based experiments to carry out. Want to colonize space, build massive ships in orbital drydocks, or construct motorized contraptions that stomp their way across alien worlds? There are mods for that, too.  Squad isn't content to let the moders do all the work, either. The team is constantly working to improve and expand the game, and every update brings new parts, a more stable and refined experience, or otherwise enriches the kerbal's universe.  In short, this is a must-play for anyone who fondly remembers their legos, and those who never outgrew them.

So... Fallout 3.  I discovered the Fallout series through the release of Fallout 4, and I am glad I did. This game is seriously old, but I love it. The Story, characters, places... All of them are so rememberable! Definetly a golden game of it's day, and still is a good game. An amazing ride throughout the whole game.

Fantastical adventure with an enormous amount of detail and attention. I've been playing it throughout the years, since it first released on Xbox back in Spring of 2002.  You have a huge, carefully-constructed open world to discover with dozens upon dozens of unmarked caves and dungeons and ruins to explore. Hidden easter eggs and references scattered throughout :0  Extensive and thought out skill system. Customize your character into whatever you want. A rouge that favors spears and uses paralysis spells and wears medium armor. A mage that focuses on alteration to bend the environment to their will, while also specializing in sneak to steal anything not bolted to the floor. Splendid combinations!  Levitation as well. Don't feel like climbing or walking around a giant mountain? Drink or use a levitation spell and you'll soon be soaring through the skies... with cliff racers D:  Two other expansion packs, Bloodmoon and Tribunal, further expand upon the story and offer new locations, powerful items and other goodies. Definitely well worth the time to play through those. I quite like Tribunal as my favorite =]   And, of course, there are the hundreds of mods available, if you don't quite like the vanilla game in all it's low-resolution meshed-together glory. Make it look like an upgraded Oblivion if you so choose <3

So far really good game. It´s not the traditional Dead Island we know, with co-op, first person RPG. But its great. Art is really strange but interesting, adds some freshness into the game. Controls are also pretty decent, I am using controller for playing and I am happy. Cant give any proper words about keyboard+mouse controls, but I recommend controller. There is a prologue mission that cannot be skipped where you play as an unknown character and search through an unknown place. I must say its a really good start of a story, even I wasn't expecting. Rest of the game is set on the island of Narapela in Banoi archipelago. You play as Cliff Calo, the you son of a, I think its a reporter or someone similar. He and his friends want to uncover the mystery behind the mysterious events on islands...   *SPOILER AHEAD* Cliff stole his fathers boat to be able to sail to the islands. Cliff's friends thought they are going to visit Banoi, but when they reached the island Cliff says they are not going to Banoi. It was Narapela. The island seemed pretty peaceful, but they soon found a problem, if weren't zombies, but earthquake hit them and sunk their boat. Cliff was trying to save their stuff, but he was hit by an explosion and drowned. That's where the fun begins. Cuz i don't get it myself. *END OF SPOILER*  Also, fine launch, no bugs or crashes for me, runs great.

One thing I think a lot of reviews have failed to mention is the fact that your citizens actually do go back to a specific house everynight, the same job everyday and they do go out to shop or goto parks or what have you (Alot easier to track them with a small town).  So why do I bring this up you may ask even though its an amazing level of detail? Remember how Maxis and EA initially told us that the Glassbox engine had to be online to support this kind of detail? Well it didn't actually work in the first place in SimCity 2013 and still doesn't. Also this game is more in depth, has MUCH larger cities and not a 'moblie' game.  This game does SimCity more than SimCity did SimCity.

Ah, Murder Miners. This is a very weird game for me. This game came from the harrowing depths of the Xbox Indie Games marketplace *shudders*, which is basically the place on xbox live where all the 11 year olds who just discovered programming and learnt 6 days worth of stuff dump their ♥♥♥♥ that they put together in about 3 days, and normally using the stupid avatars that you make, all the while making a completely different art style than the avatars, resulting in an inconsistant, uninnovative terrible rip off of some big name game that trends over all of youtube, and gives these little 11 year old's a boner for 'creativity' and 'inspiration' for their newly found 'skill' in programming. This might sound like an exaggeration. It's not a joke. And thats the saddest part. Just open the most popular page. What do we find in the top 100? COD Rippoffs, Japanese Dating Sims by a boy who just discovered that girl's butts and boobs can arouse an e rrection, and now jacks off to Hentai porn, thinking that there will be lots more in the same situation, which there are apperantly due to the ammount of downloads this stuff gets ,Five night's at Freddy's ripoffs, Halo ripoffs, hunger games ripoffs, Slender ripoffs, and the HOLY GRAIL OF THE INDIE GAME MARKETPLACE, MINECRAFT RIPOFFS. Castle miner, Castle miner Z, Fortress Craft, Total Miner, Castle miner warfare, just to name like 0.3 percent of the minecraft ripoffs on this place. There's an f'n DayZ ripoff, for crying out loud, that is easily one of my top 5 worst games of all time. I occasionally look through here every year or so for about 15 minutes, which is all I need, to see if there is anything, just ANYTHING that can be even SLIGHTLY good, or fun, or have ANY redeeming factor, that cannot be used as a torture tool used by the CIA. And every year, I came back to my bed in defeat. Then one day, I found it. The very first indie game that was at all good. 2012. Murder Miners. The shining light of hope in a place of despair. The angel of hell itself. The Rocky of gaming.The Half Life of the cramped first person shooter genre. The one game that was different. The game that had promise. That had a future. Of all the little ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that stood on a mountain, that had been discovered and cramped for centuries, that had a mountain on the other side, this was the one that finally decided, 'You know what? I'm gonna jump to that other mountain. Screw these other chickens. I will be the one to make the jump, and discover new land, and save this mountain from its innevitable starvation. And it jumped. It finally jumped. And when it jumped, everything went in slow motion. Every couple of milliseconds of the short time that it was in the air, it made a few tweaks to its form and execution of this graceful jump. And everyone else on this mountain came up and watched. They all just stood there, and watched. They didn't do anything to help, not one even tried. They just watched as this beautiful object jumped. And then Murder Miners blinked for one second. One. Little did Murder Miners know, that this would be the last glimpse at life her would see. And she never opened his eyes again. She didn't make the jump. All because she closed her eyes for one brief second during her jump, to take in everything she had done during this jump. She just stopped making tweaks, and just took in everything she had done. It wasn't much, but she just thought it was enough, and continued with her jump, just taking it in for a bit. But then she bumped her head on the cliff, and fell into the spikes 7000 feet below her, with no chance of her ever coming back to life, brutally damaged beyond repair. Even if she could get back up, and revive herself, everybody stopped watching, and still, didn't even try to help, or even continue what she started, and this glimmer of hope was killed off more quickly than it had been born. Her parents tried desperately to make another child, that hopefully could successfully make this jump, and as we speak, this child is being made, but for now, there is no light in this terrible, miserable, absolutely horrible, helpless place of agony and dispair.    That is the story of the xbox live indie marketplace, Murder Miners' ascension and descent in quality, popularity, and general hope for the hopeless future of the marketplace. If you want an actual review, Murder Miners is a Halo inspired Online FPS, with the ability to destroy and place blocks, like in Minecraft. I know, at first glance, it looks like any old game on the marketplace that tries to combine shooting with minecraft, but its not. Really. I'd say its about 95% Halo and 5% Minecraft. In fact, if this was done with AAA production values, was refined, popular, and generally more playable, and improved upon, I'd say this has the potential to be better than Halo. Thats how much promise this game showed(notice how I used past tense.). And that is saying a lot, considering Halo 3 is my second favorite FPS ever. There are quite a few additions to this game that would feel right at home in a Halo game. Walljumping, check. Destructible Enviorments, check. Custom maps, double check. But in its current state, the game is just ultimately dissapointing. There are 2 main problems with this game, 1 of which is only on PC. The game simply does not have enough players. During late Friday nights and stuff, on PC and xbox, this game has on average MAYBE 4 or 5 games going on, and the ones I have experienced so far on PC all have turbo firing or some ♥♥♥♥ enabled that has time to kill that would make Modern Warfare 2 blush. If you are reading this right now, PLEASE buy this game. Please. This game desperately needs players, and 20 max players playing at once is just not acceptable. At least have the game, so that you can possibly get more people to play. Seriously. It's $4 for a four pack. Buy the four pack, and give some copies to friends. Come on. It is only $4. I bet you anything your CSGO inventory is probably worth more. Just if you can, try to get more people playing. The more people the better. And 2, this is a horrible PC port. JForce(the developers) have gone back and added a very few more options, but it is still not enough for a pc version. This game doesn't even have resolution options, which is just plain unacceptable for a 3D game. And you will probably have better performance, because I have a TERRIBLE computer. I mean, I'm talking a CSGO 5v5 on low settings at 800x600 only getting about 20-55 fps. But seriously, this game at times, especially when looking at open areas, just as huge frame drops, that are really rather sudden, and often times don't make sense, like looking at a big open space occasionally netting more fps than a wall. It still runs alright, but there is really only an option for ansitropic filtering and AA, that is combined into one setting, and windowed mode, and fog distance, but that is about it. I mean, Wolfenstein The New Order has more options, and I consider that a pretty bad options menu. This is just a whole 'nother level of terrible porting.   Overall, you have a soild game that certaintly isn't near perfect, not in the slightest bit, but still solid, and dirt cheap, that needs more players. Currently JForce is working on a new game called Murder Miners X, which is a sequel to this. Many people are upset that they are pretty much just ditching this game, and that does kinda suck, but it makes sense. This is exactly what JForce needs. A fresh start. And, the plan is to expand onto multi-platform, and this should heavily increase the initial popularity, and hopefully, long term popularity of the game, as it won't be buried under the indie games section. Buy this game now.   JUST DO IT.

Initially, I was very, very frustrated with the combat system in TW3. The complexity, specifically. I'd just finished ~800hrs of Dragon Age: Inquisition, so found the transition to the combat system in TW3 to be very difficult indeed. It's a very steep learning curve, so it pays to take your time and make extensive use of the combat tutorial at the start of the game. If you skip that, or you just click thru it to get to the game proper, you're going to have a hard time of it. TW3 is also *not* one of those games where you can get away with just button-mashing your way thru combat.  Some tips for those just starting out: 1. You *must* use the correct approach for the foe you're facing, as well as being adequately prepared before you enter combat (apply blade oils, imbibe potions etc). You simply won't survive against more difficult foes unless you do this. 2. Above all, be patient and pick your times to strike. You can't apply the same tactics to every foe you face. You need to learn how best to create an opening for yourself in which to strike, without leaving yourself open to counterattack. 3. Get good at dodging. This is *critical* to success in combat in TW3. 4. Get even better at dodging, and using counterattacks. This is particularly useful when facing a humanoid foe with a shield. 5. Use your Quen sign to give yourself some extra protection just before combat starts. 6. Time your strikes (don't just mash the attack button over and over). If you get this right, you can successfully chain several strikes together and not get countered immediately. 7. Loot *everything*, from *everywhere*, unless there's a guard who can see you. This also applies to picking herbs that you come across. This will often mean you have all the necessary ingredients for a new potion or blade oil, as soon as you find the schematic for it. It also means you can sell the junk to merchants to build up your coin reserves. Trust me, you'll need it once you start wanting to upgrade your gear :)  After becoming comfortable with the combat in TW3, I love it. CDPR have created an absolutely fantastic world and story to go along with it. I can see me easily spending many hundreds of hours here yet!

What a great game.   I've been playing for a couple years and I play it every day, at least once a day, to catch the Loot Fairy. A game can take between 5 minutes and 20ish minutes. You create a dungeon delving party of wizards, priests and warriors from the classic rpg races. Then you equip them. Your equipment dictates the composition of cards in your deck and your deck dictates how well, or poorly, your party performs in the dungeon modules. Rewards for winning the adventures are stronger weapons, armor and items and, hence, better cards in your deck. Of course your dudes level up and move on to more challenging modules.   I'm certain that if you played D&D, or any table-top fantasy RPG, in the 80s you will find this game very, very nostalgic and fun. You will also immediately recognize your AI opponents, Gary and Melvin, from your youth!  This is the only time I've written a review for a game on Steam and I play many games on Steam.  What a great game.  danntbilll

I played this game in beta and was disappointed. After it launched, the feedback I got from friends and community sites confirmed everything I wasn't liking in beta, so I didn't jump in. Recently, I started reading about the One Tamriel update and the expansions. It looked like they might have addressed many of my concerns, so I decided to give it another shot.  This game is now a solid Elder Scrolls game. When I play, I really get the feeling that I'm playing a Skyrim-like game (minus the ability to pause or reload). The graphics are great even on low (I'm playing on a machine purchased for battery life and office performance, not gaming). The mechanics offer enough complexity to require paying attention, but they aren't so complicated that they distract you from what is going on.  In particular, the Dark Brotherhood expansion is great. I really wondered how they would pull off that kind of gameplay in a multiplayer world, but what they did works quite well. The game is largely soloable at early levels, but occassionally you are thrown a quest that requires help. Sometimes you get an NPC to help, other times you have to grab one or more players to help out. The removal of level barriers makes it so that you can group with anyone you find.  Much of the world is open and seamless, which is fantastic. Sure, there are dungeons and such that require load times, but even most large cities can be entered without hitting a door or load screen. It seems like I spend less time loading in this game than I did in Skyrim, which is pretty impressive considering how seamless Skyrim was and that ESO is an MMO.  All things considered, I like the game so far on this go-around.

app_name: STAR WARS™ Jedi Knight: Mysteries of the Sith™
review_text: This is a VERY loose recommendation. It's honestly a 6/10 game (since Steam released it no longer as Dark Forces II DLC). Mysteries of the Sith, in my opinion, is more preferable to Dark Forces II in some ways, and a lot worse in others. It's better, because missions are more direct and have a lot more of a heavy atmosphere (especially the last three levels) combat is greatly improved, especially the Force and lightsaber combat. However, story-wise, it does have some good points, but utterly falls flat on its face. The story is 4 random moments between Kyle and Mara Jade, Master and Student. Parts 1 and 4 are what the story IS about. Parts 2 and 3 (Where Mara deals with Hutts and Pirates) literally has NOTHING to do with the plot, creating pointless filler. It could honestly have aided the plot, but ultimately it didn't. But Parts 1 and 4 between Mara and Kyle specifically are good, and definitely Part 4. It's created to be creepy, challenging, and mind-bending. Oh, and the boss battle is totally easy when you find out the secret. Took me 5 seconds to beat him.

Attila plays like a more fun version of rome 2, where you feel like you actually have to conquer new territory, or you will perish. This contrasts rome 2 in that you feel like you are constantly in danger of being destroyed, while in rome 2 you kind of just sit around wondering who to attack next. In Attila you are forced to be more aggressive, and the feeling tension it brings to the campaign map as well as the battle field is welcomed. However, for many of the positive things Attila brings to the table, there are plenty of negative decisions.   Pros:  -Siege battles are way more fun than in any total war to date. The maps are varied, there are more strategic options, and you have to be more creative than before, both as attacker and defender. Its not the same battle over and over again like in Shogun 2, not messy like in rome 2, and nothing like the broken ones in Napoleon.  -Diversity in clans and cultures are more pronounced, like in what units they deploy, their starting position, etc. Playing as the western roman empire, the saxons, the huns, or the sassanid empire offer completely different experiences from one another, and makes for great replay value.  -CA has captured the essence of the time period very nicely, making the immersion into the game excellent for us who care about the historical period in which the game takes place.  -Battle maps are pretty diverse throughout the game, which often gives you the oppurtunity to try out new strategies and keeps the battles from feeling too similar.  Cons: -The new Raze mechanic leaves a lot of the campaign map barren (The AI seems to love doing this), playing as the saxons my kingdom resembled more of a collection of patches accross Europe, instead of a strong kingdom consisting of combined regions. This is both annoying, and hurtful to game immersion.   -Micromanaging economy, public order, sanitation and so on is a ♥♥♥♥♥. It takes a lot of time, money and strategic planning, and it is inconvenient to do so from the menues. Its not like Shogun 2, where everything is neatly organized. It takes attention away from the fun things you should be doing, like battling romans, or conquering territory, or keeping the huns away (with violence). Instead you find yourself building wells and wondering how in the world you can cough up enough money to convert the roman buildings to your faction's type of the same building.  -Horde mechanic feels gimmicky and unpolished. It is even more awkward to manage your economy in this state.   -Naval battles are boring as usual. Nothing new here.  -A lot of unnecessairy micromanaging accosiated with dilemmas from the family tree. I really dont care of someone in my clan was drunk on duty, if the entire byzantine empire, and a horde of huns are atticking my kingdom. Some people may feel more immerged into the game by these diemmas, but I found them distracting, because it is way to many of them.   -As many have pointed out, the number of DLC is ridiculous,  All in all, I reccomend Attila to someone who is familiar with the total war series, and want a different experience than the other titles in the series. It is not the best total war game, but I think the positive aspects in my own attila experience outweighs the negative ones. I had loads of fun playing attila, which is utimately what matters the most to me in a game. For newcomers to total war: try Shogun 2 before attila.

app_name: Prince of Persia: The Forgotten Sands
review_text: A great game that follows the styles of combat, obstacles and most things from the original trilogy of Prince Of Persia (The Sands of Time, Warrior Within and The Two Thrones).  Been a fan of this series for a huge long time, I used to think the game didn't have that kind of charm and fun in adventure and platforming like the original trilogy, after the spin-off game Prince Of Persia was released back in 2008. (which in my opinion was a good game that has nice story with good characters, though it was a strange spin-off) but I'm really glad I was so wrong  Obstacles and platforming:  - One of the two special charms of the whole series, hard to dispute this game can pull off without these beautifully made mechanism of traps, and the design of the way to guide the player through them.  - Some of the obstacles are back from the Original Trilogy (the Spikes, the straight-up high walls, the swinging axes, the running saw wheel..) and some even from the 2008 spin-off game (sliding slope..) - Newer, bigger and much harder obstacles in the game (one of which is the multiple layers of water wall, which in my opinion is the hardest of all obstacles I have ever seen in the whole series, and also the swinging huge thorn tree log) - The platforming has been upgraded to a higher new level, this means whoever still got the mind back when playing the original trilogy, now can rest assured that their style of platforming will be upgraded to a higher new level. Since it requires 1 or 2 more steps to pass the ostacles. - The Saving Checkpoints were arranged nicely , typical of the whole series itself  Combat: - No longer a young prince, this guy now can make a lot of nicer attacks, combos and smoother acrobatic movements. However, The Forgotten Sands does not have the old mechanism of using 2 swords + combos (which were in the original Trilogy) , or stealth kill (The Two Thrones) - One thing has returned from The Sands of Time: You got a fight with a huge platoon of enemies by yourself! But that doesn't mean you can't take them all out. - In fact, the enemies in this game are pretty not that hard to face, which doesn't seem to be much of a concentration in this game, since the design, scenery, platforming element and story of the game are the main focus.  - The new system of using 4 natural elements in this game are pretty nice to help you with all the combat, though after a while, you'll recognize that they are only useful in facing some types of enemies. - A whole new system of upgrading (which was only from the spin-off game, not the original trilogy) will help you get so much better. And I agree with this decision of Ubisoft Montreal. No more running around trying to find the upgrades and water sink to recover the health (Warrior Within), you can get Health, Energy and XP from the enemies and vases (which is an old point of the original trilogy)  Design and graphic: - The design of the obstacles, the platforming in The Forgotten Sands are smooth - The color when using the elements are really nice, especially when you freeze the time flow of the water, each drop of the water wall is so clear to see and beautifully made. - However,... once again, it seems the enemies are not really the main focus of the game, so they are quite not that well designed, but it doesn't matter, what matters is what they can do to you though.  I believe most people who used to play the original trilogy should check this game out and get immersed in the World of the Prince of Persia once more. Even if you haven't played the Trilogy, this game still won't make you feel disappoint at all. In fact, it may even bring you back to play the original trilogy itself!  Rating: 9/10 by me. -0.5 because the Djin temple quite lacked the design and change in the 2 pictures (you'll see, they should have recorded the moments of the game) , -0.5 because the challenge mode lacks too much effort invested by Ubisoft.  Have fun!   P.S: Ezio is also spotted in the game, though in different shape. But who knows, maybe someday AC and POP can be brought together.  - Of course, since it was released back in 2010, try to buy the game when it's on sale.

This is a great game because it does one thing that no other game does: simulates a realistic depiction of the milky way galaxy while giving the player the ability to navigate the entirety of that setting. That alone to me is well worth $60. There is plenty of content for you, but it is extremely open-ended it its design.   Open-ended part i think generates a lot of the hate around this game. Many describe space as 'empty', which is understandable. But i also understand that space itself it pretty empty as well. More recently, many of the more devoted players complain of a new 'broken' ai that imediately kills them. In fact, combat ai has gotten considerably more cunning and powerful with the engineers update, with abilities comparable to another human. Every fight will make you crap your pants and you will have to run often.   What I'm getting at, is the devs of this game of true fans of Hard sci-fi, and that comes with a certain degree of realism. Space will feel like the void and combat will give you the shakes. Given realistic expectations (this is science people) i couldn't have been happier with this update cause i play for void and i play for the shakes.   Buy if you play for the shakes.

Despite my hours seen above, I've used this for around 3-4 years now (right as 2.5 beta was being worked on/released; great time to get in that was :P).  Superb software that has aged/grown well.  Modeling? Check, with ngons too! Rendering? Check, with optional path tracing too! Rigging & Animation? Check, with drivers! Sculpting? Check, with dynamic topology! UV unwrapping? Check. Vertex/Texture painting? Check. Post processing? Check, with nodes! Scripting? Check, in python! Video editing? Check, an actual free NLE for windows! Support for Game Engine export (FBX)? Check. Internal Game Engine? Check. Viewport extras? Check, with physics, AO, DOF, and actual real time rendering! Simulations? Check; smoke, fire, liquid, and generic! Dynamic painting? Check. Custom materials? Check, with nodes and Open Shading Language (OSL). Support for third party renderers? Check.  This software does it all. Pair it with GIMP and LMMS you have the bossiest FOSS studio that works on  all  major platforms.

Went in with fairly low expectations, but this turned out to be a pretty fun budget shooter with a surprising amount of depth. It's clear that the dev put a fair bit of effort into it and it's pretty challenging gameplaywise, with tons of items and upgrades to unlock. The colony management segments in-between missions to unlock further upgrades is also a pretty cool feature since it actually helps give the player an extra sense of accomplishment after having liberated a particular planet.  The only minor nitpicks I can think of at the moment are the presence of some minor typos that I've come across, and the UI could also use a bit more polish since it can b e a bit confusing to navigate our way around all the menus if you aren't using a mouse. Other than that though, I'd definitely recommend this title to shmup fans, and it actually has a plot that makes far more sense than last couple of shooters that I played.

I broke my keyboard.  Simply put, the best true platforming game to come out in YEARS. As you can immediately notice, the user scores are quite polarized, and I believe this is true to the nature of the game. If you find joy in surmounting difficult (But FAIR) platforming challenges, this game is the PERFECT game for you. Let me also state that the 10 I assigned this game was not some knee-jerk reaction to a merely clever indie game. This game is fantastic by ANY standard, it is perfectly executed. There is nothing in the game where I have ever said, man, I wish X existed. In a time where games like Fallout New Vegas and Civ 5 release with the swath of glitches and bugs in the game, it is refreshing to have a game ship out that is immensely bug free, it is absolutely precise. You may even grow to treat this precision as a foe, as there is a razor thin edge between success and death in each level, there is no 'give', if you touch a deadly obstacle with any part of your hit-box, you can count on dying. However, this consistency gives rise to the games greatest achievement, the overall fairness of the game. I am astounded that I read other user reviews that actually complained about the controls, they are absolutely astounding. To be fair, I do use a Xbox360 controller, I could see that without one it may take longer to 'pick up' but the controls are nevertheless, precise. The character becomes an extension of your will, you'll find sometimes that when an unexpected hazard appears, you react quickly and ably within fractions of a second, as if you were acting out of instinct. I am also disturbed to read some people claiming that this game's difficulty is just a mechanism to lengthen the game. I find it hard to believe we even played the same game. The levels are difficult to be sure, but they are absolutely fair, if you die, it is your fault, there are no invisible traps, your character will not move if you don't tell him to. And even if you die, the levels each can be completed in under a minute, so if you do die (and you will die, often) almost no progress is lost. You will not be spending 5 minutes just to get a chance to practice the spot you died at last time. The other reason I find this explanation difficult to believe is that there are over 300 levels. The game is already massive, it wouldn't need to be lengthened by unnecessary difficulty.  In short: A difficult platformer, but an absolute masterpiece if you are willing to take up the challenge.

This review has no spoilers.  I've played Planetarian way back and oh boy I lost count how many tissues I've used in that novel. This visual novel has no choices so you just get to enjoy the ride in one way. Finished reading this. I must say I immensely enjoyed it.   Story - Post-apocalyptic setting about a boy who wakes up with no memories. Yes, we know it's kind of tropey or not that unique but the way the story builds up definitely makes up for it. The pacing may seem a bit quick as there are little to no fillers for this novel. I frankly believe it's enough for anyone to digest what's happening in the story.  Sound - Normal ambient music that accompanies whatever is happening in the screen. And does it's job well. - BGMs. I think this is probably the strongest part of the novel as it accompanies the scenes well. If you're familiar with previous Key games, you know what to expect as I think Key's in-house music people are the same people responsible for the music in this novel. I'd buy the BGMs as DLC if it were even possible. - Character voices is fine. I'm not really into voices but I felt the voices done here were done well as it kinda ties with what's happening. No spoilers but you'd kinda get it if you trek the novel all the way through the end. - It's not a Key novel if it doesn't have any orgel/music box in it. I'm a sucker for anything like those.  Graphics - Well, graphics aren't really the main point of the VNs but I do like the character designs for it to fit in this novel. Itaru's doing the characters so there's that.  Characters - I think this is the part where it doesn't shine as well as the other aspects of the novel. It felt like the characters weren't that fleshed out very well but nevertheless, it's just enough for you to know and care about them. I've mentioned how the novel has little to no filler so that may be a reason for it.  Others - I don't think it's fair to compare this Planetarian as this one is more about feelings rather than Planetarian's tone of hopelessness (how I felt it). But they do share something common, I guess. - I remember Planetarian to be longer. Though that may be because I used to read slower back then. - No JP text. So if you happen to be an elitist or just take into consideration that JP text is a must, I'd advise you to skip this or at least wait if they will ever implement JP text in this novel. - Can't complain about translation as there is nothing to compare to. Except the voices. Some stiff and awkward translation of the spoken lines but it gets the point across. Most of the translated voiced lines are fine as it is. - Prepare some tissues. - Playing porn games? I suggest you use your fap tissues for your eyes instead. - For this price tag, I think it's fine for the story/experience you get. You'd probably avoid this if you're more into price per hour though.  tldr; It's great, go get this Or Go get this if: - You liked Planetarian - You want to cry - You want some easy reading - To know what is love (insert unfunny reply here) - Feel POWAPOWA inside

Just thought I would share my experiences with the beta since I bought it on Friday.  Initially I thought I threw £35 toilet. The game crashed constantly, the match engine stuttered and went through large periods where the match wasn ‘t even visible. After tweaking the settings and some “turn It off and on again” tactics I have had a great experience with the game so far.  I have seen some people bemoan the constant injuries, I have to say even when I had 8-10 injuries spread between the First, Reserve and Youth Teams I never felt like the game was conspiring against me and I used the opportunity to experiment with the formations and starting 11. It’s a squad game so it is important to make sure you have depth for when the injuries start coming in.  Having migrated from FM14 I am having a blast and this version should last me another 3 years easily.

(Don't let those 12.3 DOS Box hours Fool you, I have over 600 hours in UFOextender and OpenXCOM.)  X-Com: UFO Defense is a Retro classic that has still stood the test of time. I picked it up in 2013, with no prior experience of Strategy games WHAT SO EVER. While most people find the ancient, convoluted 90s interface... well, ancient and convoluted, I some how learned the game without even cracking open the instruction manual (Word of advice. Crack open the instruction manual. You will need it if you don't figure out the interface in 5 minutes like I somehow managed.) Despite all this, X-com is a game that I always find myself coming back to... here's why.  Pros:  -Procedurally generated maps means you will NEVER play the same battle twice. -The balance between the tactical and geoscape is one of my favorite parts of the game. Both would have been a solid game individually by themselves, but the combination of the two makes X-Com one of the greatest strategy games out there. - THE FREAKING INTRO. *Jumps out of skyranger blasting mutons with auto rifles to epic bit rock* (Note: DO NOT try to use Auto rifles against Mutons) - Random Generation for your Soldiers as well keeps the game fresh. - Challenging. This game will make you FEEL like you're defending the earth from vastly superior aliens, making victory that much sweeter. - The Art style: Campy 90s Sci-Fi at its best - The Music: John Broomhall has created one of my favorite game OST since Ace Combat 04   Cons: - Outdated Nineties interface: The Battlescape can be incredibly confusing to new comers. - Psionic Line of Fire: Download a mod for this, because Aliens spamming mind control when they can't even see you is BS. - Heavy Lasers / Laser Cannons kinda suck. Could have used more balancing in the weaponry department. (There are also mods for this. Download them) - ♥♥♥♥ING TURN ONE ALIEN GRENADES IN THE SKYRANGER YAGAHA KSA KHLSADHKASKDASD  (I think there's actually a mod that disables grenades until turn 3 for both sides. So you can actually get your people spread out.) - Ammo micromanagement is incredibly annoying  Rating?  10/10, Arguably one of the greatest PC games ever created. Just, for the love of god, use openXcom for modern systems.

Soulstorm is the final and in my opinion, best expansion for the critically acclaimed Dawn Of War I franchise that got unfortunately hounded by reviewers at launch, but was greatly enhanced by both official patches and mods that made this game one of the most famous and game-changing RTS's out there.  + Standalone expansion that adds the fanatical sisters of battle, with a a wide array of artillery and faith based attacks, and the sadistic, twisted dark eldar, who are nothing like the original Eldar faction and are the epitome of the term glass cannon with the highest damage in the game, but also the worst health I've ever seen.  + Balanced older factions making it a better experience for all  + Those that own Soulstorm do not need any other part of DOW I to play with others who own the franchise (assuming they too own atleast Soulstorm) but you are restricted from the factions added in other expansions giving you access to only 2 of the 9  + The meta-campaign returns giving you the ability to fight in the 9-way war for Kaurava.  + Airforce based units are added making fighter jets available to all (except the Necron, but they get a spell that hijacks vehicles permenantly now) adding a new level of diversity to the combat as they dont follow the same movement restrictions as ground troops.  +The game can be further enhanced with the legendary Ultimate Apocalypse mod adding new balance changes, better graphics, units, and redoes every faction from the ground up making them as the Codex Astartes (basically lore for the universe for the   heretics   those unfamiliar with the lore reading this ) states making it one of the greatest mods for any game ever. That is a fact, opinions do not matter in the 40th millenia.  - The ai is still kinda wonky on lower difficulties and will not always utilize their best features on higher ones. For example, the Eldar, who are meant to be a teleportation based army with weak hit-and-run style units, will instead opt for buildig multiple unupgraded guardian (rifleman) squads and rush you by the two minute mark. This works well if you didnt build a turret which mows them down violently, but it still doesn't fit with how that faction was meant to work. This can be fixed with mods however.  -Due to age, and a community divided over mods, finding people to play with isn't always easy. If you have friends into RTS games however, this game is a god(emperor)send.  -The new aerial units take a lot of damage from even basic infantry making them not very viable in many instances. So if your looking for some high end combined arms assault, all your gonna get is some quality infantry and armored vehicles fighting as dead pilots drop down.  -The controversial commander units are back, though not as dangerous as they are in the second game as they are still very much killable by even the weaker units. In my opinion, they aren't too much of an issue and as a concept add to the game, though balance is a tad bit skewed as some armies get their primary and best commanders at the beginning like the Space/Chaos marines and dark eldar when others have to wait for a base upgrade or other enhancement. I feel that these guys have a bit too much early game infantry harassment but by the end is no issue.  -A full-fleged story based campaign is still absent like in Dark Crusade but the new campaign format included in the two allows you to play as your favorite faction instead of either the Space marines in the base game, or 1-2 missions for each in the Winter Assault.   In conclusion, the game still holds up and is still fantastic to play in both standard and the Ultimate Apocalypse mod. Balance still isn't perfect, but the more you play you should be able to master and hold up as any faction making it one of the most biggest and greatest RTS games out there.

Upon the release of the new Travelling update, and another dozen or so hours of the game under my belt, I decided to revisit my review.  If you're a comic book lore-lord, there's probably plenty of fanservice and fun bits to sink your teeth into. Plenty of opportunity to play as heroes or villains that aren't often playable in games.  For someone that doesn't have any sort of emotional investment in Marvel though, it's....pretty average. The core gameplay loop is more entertaining than what Diablo 3 is doing these days, since there's a fair amount of variety in what you can do for end-game progression. Rather than provide you an endless progression ladder in the form of Greater Rifts as in Diablo 3, Marvel Heroes offers multiple acceptable paths for leveling, and several systems to interact with to gather gear, some of which is harder than others. The power curve is a little smoother - Diablo 3 has you feeling useless until you collect set items, at which point you power increases exponentially, whereas Marvel Heroes is content to let you feel strong right away, with some higher difficulty content gated off until you've reached certain gear thresholds that vary from character to character.   Since we've already gone into the diablo comparison - Blizzard put a lot more love into their spell effects and making sure everything has a nice, meaty feeling of impact. Spell effects and the way your abilities interact with the enemies can leave you feeling impotent at times, even if some of the spells or skills have large or flashy graphics associated with them. In general, the aesthetics are lacking a certain level of polish. Characters in Diablo can generally succeed without someone 'tanking' the hits, since if you're not powerful enough to manage without something distracting the enemy, you're playing on the wrong difficulty anyway. Marvel Heroes has a 'team-up' system that lets you call-in a sidekick of sorts, which serve different utility purposes - some may tank for you, using their abilities to quickly jump into the fight and stun or slow foes, giving you time to kill them or control them yourself before they've leapt into your face. Characters that don't need a tank or can summon one as one of their innate abilities are free to take a team-up that can debuff the enemies, or simply add to their DPS. I'd say it's a nice middle-ground between the mercenary system present in Diablo 2 and 3, as you have many viable options that can fit a variety of roles depending upon your needs.  The travel system has been dramatically improved. Before this most recent patch, you had to spend points in your travel power, and it started off with a base speed of 500, barely faster than your default running speed. Now you are automatically granted a travel power at level 10, and you can assign it a dedicated button independent of your hotbars. Better yet, it doesn't take away any of your skill points, and now everyone has a power granting them a movement speed of 650, which feels great. Heroes that can teleport still have a movement advantage, but with this travel update, the game still feels plenty playable and satisfactory, even if you can only run around on foot.  The game follows the korean MMO model of inventory management. That is to say, they give you a pretty small inventory and a pitiful stash size to work with, and then procede to shower you with more loot than you know what to do with. There are systems in place to help alleviate this, such as automatically trashing items below certain quality thresholds, and a pet system that requires you to feed them large volumes of drops in order to make them more effective at what they do for you, but this is a band-aid at best. Finding items doesn't feel cool, because there's so many stats, variables, and levers that each gearslot can be pulling, that comparing a new drop to what you're wearing feels tedious. Even after a few dozen hours of play, the best solution I've found is to ignore anything that's not at least 'cosmic', and generally only spend time evaluating new items if there's a large level gap in what i'm wearing vs what just dropped, or if it's unique and has stat bonuses or perks specific to the character i'm playing as.  Costumes are pretty expensive, too. They're asking roughly $10 for a single costume wearable by one hero, which is out of the impulse buy range for most people, myself included. You are also only allowed one free hero per account. To be fair to them, they do have a form of in-game currency that can be used to buy new heroes, and the drops feel slightly generous - between what you pick up in the course of regular play, and what you're awarded for doing side-quests as you work through the main story, you can expect that you'll be able to buy one new hero just about every time you level a character from 1-60, assuming you do so using the campaign. It's worth mentioning that the side quests are pretty grindy and depend upon random spawns and timed events to complete, which can be tedious to wait for and complete. I suspect that this is by design, giving you a way to measure whether it's worth your time to get this secondary source of splinter income, or just drop some cash on heroes.  For what it's worth, there's daily login rewards too, and on your 7th day of logging in, you get a large sum that's enough to buy 1 or 2 heroes. If there's anything in this game that encourages you to keep going, it's the drop system they've implemented so you can unlock more heroes. On every 10th and 30th day thereafter, you get a login bonus of 10 shards too, which really starts to add up.  As a paid title, I'd be wary of recommending it. But it's free to play, and has a pretty ethical (albeit expensive) business model that never sells you in-game power. If you like the Marvel universe or don't consider yourself above playing an average callback to games like Diablo, Torchlight, and Marvel: Ultimate Alliance, it couldn't hurt to install this and give it a shot.

La Mulana is.... an unfortunate game. It is very interesting and has a lot of potential, but it commits many gaming sins that can't be overlooked. This is a NES era kind of game that is designed to force you to use some kind of guide when playing it (think castlevania 2). If you respond to this, don't just say 'the game is supposed to be hard'. Dark Souls is my favorite game; I know the difference between a game that is difficult because it's designed well, and a game that is difficult because it is designed badly.   -Very little feedback is given for many of the puzzles. Many times if you solve a puzzle, the game simply doesn't show you what exactly you unlocked, or where. Sometimes solving a puzzle in one area will unlock a door or something in an entirely different area.   -Although clues are given, many aren't helpful or refer to something in a later area. Once you get to that area, there is no indication that you needed to use a clue from earlier.  -There are several points in the game where no clues are given for progression. Several times it is necessary to walk through illusionary walls, or break floors/walls to continue, with no indication you need to do so. You are left jumping around constantly attacking everything in the hope you will accidentally solve a 'puzzle'.   -The above point wouldn't be a problem if the game didn't frequently PUNISH you for trial and error. Yes, there are many rooms in the game where, if you attack random walls, lightning strikes your character and does a substantial amount of damage. The game teaches you quickly not to attack random things, but then at other times the only way to continue is to know where to do it. I have died many times, just attacking random walls to see if they would crumble, only to find out that I was indeed supposed to attack a specific part of a wall; but no indication was given to do so.   -Unfair instant death traps and rooms that you simply become 'stuck' in. There are many rooms that just kill you, and other ones that just trap you, forcing you to restart from your last save if you don't have specific items.   -Some puzzles don't reset. Make a mistake and you can never get the item. There are only a few of these, but there isn't any indication that the puzzle will be like this. Once again, the game punishes trial and error. Fortunately, I think they only do this with non-essential items.   -The game doesn't control well enough for some of the fast-paced action segments they throw at you. Boss battles typically aren't hard for any reason other than your character moves slowly and doesn't control in the air well.   If anyone ever tells you that they beat this game without ever consulting a guide or asking for help, they are lying. I really wanted to like this game; thematically it is amazing, and I love this style of metroidvania action-adventure. I find myself being forced to use a guide all too often, and it really ruins the gaming experience (seriously, some of these 'puzzles' are just impossible unless you know exactly what to do beforehand). If you really like games like castlevania 2, or don't mind having a guide open during your playthrough, then La Mulana might be for you. Otherwise, stay away.

I'm the kind of person who enjoys a mix of beautiful art/design/music, good gameplay and mostly a gripping story. And 'Dust: An Alysian Tail' has everything. To start, its art is just astonishing. Every scenario, character, monster and interactive itens (as well as your inventory with all your equipment) is gorgeous. For much time, playing many games from the present and the past, that was the first time where I would stop just to look at a floating rock or a blizzard storm. Everything fits so perfectly together with an awesome soundtrack and nice voice acting that I consider this game one of the greatest of this generation. Forget about all those 3D graphics and many polygons that we see in 'triple A' games. 2D characters and, if I'm not mistaken, hand-drawn scenarios made this game beat many others that we see in this industry.  About the gameplay, the game mixes some RPGs elements (levelling up, finding itens to make better equipment, solving sidequests) with a hack-and-slash action genre very fluid and it does not get boring sice the game keeps adding features to make the combat more challenging and interesting. However, it sticks with the basic. Nothing more sofisticated. Which, in my opinion, kept Dust faster-paced.  Last, I recommend playing the game at least on 'Tough' difficulty, because it is rather easy to kill the enemies since it doesn't require too much griding (keeping going back in order to get stronger). It has some environmental challenges that make some parts a little harder, but anything impossible. Everybody can play the game, from kids to adults, and everybody should. For me, the game doesn't has an replayable value, it is a game to play and enjoy the scenarios and the story together.  'Dust: An Elysian Tail' is a master piece, it will keep you thrilled from the first forest to lava and snow scenarios. Play, just play. The price is great, the game is awesome and it will last in your memory for a long time.

Path of Exile is a very deep ARPG with a rather unusual currency mechanic and a massive skill tree. It has a steep learning curve and can be somewhat punishing, but that's also what makes winning feel so rewarding.  The devs have kept their promise of making the game truly free. Most microtransactions confer cosmetic changes. The only microtransaction that actually affects gameplay is unlocking additional stash tabs (bank storage space). I found that when I only had one character, it was pretty easy to stay within the limits of the default 4 tabs. After a hundred hours of gameplay, I felt like a small payment to be able to hoard more items was more than justifiable (and a bit more to make my new character look super cool!).  The game is also kept interesting by regular challenge leagues where you start a new character in a separate game world with a fresh economy and different gameplay rules.   At a low low price of free, it's definitely worth a go!
A bit too repetitive for massive replay-ability for my tastes, but if this is the type of game you're looking for it's hard to see how you could be disappointed.

What a great game! Totaly addictive. The design is very minimal, but who cares? It's fun and it works just fine - what more do you need?  10/10 to this top game and its developers.  NOTE - The comments above were based on my first impressions after about 2 hours of play and progressing past the 'opening' stages. After finishing a complete game, and then continuing on in the sort of sandbox mode until eventual bankruptcy after several more hours, I'd revise the rating to 7 or 8/10.  The game works OK in general but a couple of things didn't seem to function correctly. After researching custom hardware I wasn't able to market it, for example.  The system for assigning people to development tasks is OK, but a bit counter-intuitive in practice. The biggest criticism I have is that the rating system, and the longevity of a game, particularly an MMO, is too 'black-and-white' and needs to take more things into consideration. The life cycle of all the games was much the same, the only difference being the profit earned on the basis of initial reviews - great games stayed on the market for the same amount of time as complete failures. There's also no option to reduce a price and sell a title as a bargain purchase or bundle several old titles for re-sale later.  MMOs all followed almost an identical life cycle, and there seems to be no way to keep an MMO afloat and profitable for more than 3 years, no matter how much DLC you make available.  It's still a fun game, particularly at the price, but some balance and tweaking to the algorithms would improve it, as would options to set the price for each game and choose when to withdraw each one, rather than all that just being automatic.

This game is very similar to the Kairosoft title 'Game Dev Story', however it is quite different. Whereas the original is quite easy and about racking up high scores, this requires a little more forethought and planning.  The pacing of the game is overall quite decent and the gameplay itself is addictive, that said there are a number of annoying omissions. The lack of feedback on how to make good titles means that the process is more than a little opaque, the review scores are generated randomly from a very strange algorithm.   That said, this is a fun game and well worth a buy if on sale.

This game is great. The vanilla version is very challenging in the first couple of attempts at building a city. The hard start mode is very unforgiving to an inexperienced player. Getting through the first couple of winters and growing a population is rewarding. That being said, the vanilla version can get a little boring once you have 'beaten' it and have a thriving city. In comes the mod community. I personally prefer to just use the colonial charter mod as it can easily be called an expansion to the game in a fantastic way. It adds a ton of resources and production chains. It completely refreshed the game for me and presented new sets of challenges with different ways to play. There are a ton of other mods available to keep this game interesting. It was developed by a single person which is staggering. I would say definitely worth the 20 dollars, but I got it when it was on sale for 5 or 6. Less than the cost of a movie these days and hours upon hours more of entertainment. Highly recommended!

This and three other games were all bought from my local gameshop for the usual reasons: someone recommended it and it was dirt cheap. I must say I was inordinately happy to find not just something to amuse for a weekend, but a 'proper' game. By 'proper' I mean that despite the long tutorials and even longer playtime of the levels (which still feel a bit tutorial-esque), I was gripped for a good 2 weeks or so. Just a couple of hours every night before getting online and getting my daily MMO injection, but it kept me coming back for more.  The slight predictability of the level structure after a while didn't deter me either, I wanted that next type of alien so I wanted to get to the next level just so I could get to play with them; see what they could do; work them into my now tried and tested Starbase designs.  I must admit however, that at first I didn't have much patience with it. After lengthy tutorials I was getting peeved at the smarmy John Cleese-esque narrator. I wanted to weld his cybermouth shut.  But, given time and a little bit of ingenuity with a Recycling Plant I was soon making efficient, profitable, teeming-with-life starbases that lit up the barren, war-torn cosmos ready for a new era of economic prosperity and peace.  Sometimes the control can be a little clumsy, and the camera controls take a while to get used to (much smoother now higher res is support). But the semi-intuitive control system and the nice clean HUD/interface more than made up for it. The graphics were good for its time of release and the sound wasn't annoying, on the 2 lower decks it was a background rather than a stimulus or a feature, but my favourite place to take a break from the credit earning worlds of the industrial/pleasure decks was up on the biodeck, with its bizarre yet soothing cosmic harmonies and whale-song-like chirps and blips.  As far as value goes you can't fault it; I first bought it in a Sold Out Software box and therefore paid very very little for it (especially as they were doing 3 for 2 and it was the cheapest of the three). This isn't a great game, but it is a good game - and that is a good thing. It couldn't have been much better and remain the same game, but it really only is a good game. But we all need a good game from time to time. Beautiful, epic masterpieces are all fine and dandy, but you'd be worn out if all graphics made your retinas melt and every story was an epic saga.  If you see it on sale and have a spare tenner then hell, buy it. It'll be worth the 2 weeks you get out of it.

An overall enjoyable game, as long as you don't expect to much realism in terms of political systems that differ from that of the UK. For federal systems many of the decisions wouldn't be made on federal level but by state or county governments. The same is true for the voting system: Playing Germany I would have expected to have to form a coalition with another party after being elected, as there is no 'first past the post' rule in the German system and to reach the 50% needed is near impossible for a single party.  Maybe implementing less countries to choose from, but putting more effort in research might have helped here. Apart from that it's an interesting game, with some nice choices for policies and their effects on a country and its interest groups.

Highly recommended if you like this style of gameplay, as really anyone that would get this, would get it for the story anyway.

Eldritch is a lightweight but fun action-roguelike with a Lovecraftian theme.  Eldritch is a roguelike, but it's a surprisingly easy one-- I finished the game for the first time after only about 7 or 8 total hours of play. Certain strategies make completion much easier, and the most difficult level out of the four is the second one. Since you can take the levels on in whatever order you like, you can optimize your playthrough to make beating the second level easier. If you're used to recent commerical roguelikes like The Binding of Isaac, The Dungeons of Dredmor, and others, Eldritch may seem very lightweight, very easy, and kind of simplistic. The bizarre difficulty curve-- with the second level being so much harder than even the secret fourth level-- may also be a bit of a disappointment.  Nevertheless, it's fun, well-made, and a good way to entertain yourself for 8 hours or less. The simple art is charming in its own way, and exploring to find your favorite strategies and item synergies is certainly entertaining.

So I actually had this preordered--then I cancelled it a few hours before release because there was a odd anxiety on the internet about it. Then I was going to ignore it for a while because everyone was downvoting it and calling it worse than Arkham Knight (which actually, despite my low/mid-tier rig, I had no problems running, so this may be another case of luck) so I played on a pirated copy to see if it was the hellscape people were describing only to find out that the game runs fine (even better if you disable GSync, VSync, Remove the FPS Cap, and use Bordlerless Fullscreen) and it turns out. I really like this game. So like an idiot, the next day I purchased it with my refunded money on steam, and now as you can see I've got 10 hours in so I feel like it's a good time to submit a review.  This feels like minecraft without the base-building (yet) in that you're spending a good % of your time scoping out resources and tearin them down.. but just like minecraft (even more, given the grander scale) the sense of exploration and discovery is great. But I do think that if you're an aggressive player (ie you skip dialogue, play mostly competitive/multiplayer games) you're going to be sorely disappointed in the product--there is danger, but it's minimal (I THINK HERE IS WHERE THE GAME WOULD BENEFIT FROM DIFFICULTY SETTING--AS IS, IT FEELS A BIT CASUAL) and the multiplayer doesn't appear to be much more than 'Oh hey, someone was here before me and named this planet after a dead gorilla' at least for now.   PS - I noticed a lot of negative reviewers mentioning things looking the same, planets all the same, etc. I personally haven't had this issue yet, for example my starting system had a archipelago-like planet with an almost tree root terrain with a sulfur-y earth and navy blue oceans. The 2nd planet I went to seemed to be all land, with many cave systems and a generally LSD-infused appearance, large purple avocado-like stones, giant dinosaur-like creatures and flying eels the size of sharks, and my starting planet was a massive, mountainous tropical jungle except the foliage was a shade of crimson red and white and the earth was a lush green. There's also a moon but I've not been yet, from a distance it looks.. well like a moon, rather rocky and craterous. And furthest away what looks to be an icy planet.. So yeah, if that's not diverse enough... guess it's really repetitive.  Performance: On my low-end midtier desktop (let me put it this ways, GeForce says that I don't meet the minimum requirements) I am running the game at 1920x1080, with all details on high except for shadow&reflections and getting at the worst ~25 fps in dense planet zones; in space I average ~50fps. I have had 0 crashes, 0 hang-ups, 0 bugs (occasionally I will takeoff a little TOO hard, all the way into space).  Would I pay $60 for this? No, not if I'm hesitant about the product or wouldn't have paid $60 for minecraft. THAT SAID, I am confident in the coming patches & additional (FREE) content that the endgame will be worth the money, and that I would like to play it as it evolves.  Off the top of my head wishlist of features for future updates:  1) a map of the world (with 'fog-of-war' covering unexplored areas of course) that i'm exploring so I can return to stations that I've been to OR AT THE VERY LEAST, A COMPASS/GPS & COORDINATES 2) the ability to either a) hail my ship or b) hail another ship for a pickup (at a cost/trade for a resource) when I realize I've wandered 23 minutes away from my ship. This could easily be an unlockable upgrade that has to take up a slot in your inventory/ship/both. 3) an explanation of why the worked Spiked/y is censored  4) more danger! either through scaling based off your combined inventory size (e.g. increasing the volatility of planets, the % of hostile/carnivorous creatures, barbarians/pirates on the planets) or altogether different game modes 5) a way to identify what spaceship belongs to what faction 6) less planet asteroids/more asteroid fields & planets with rings!

tl;dr is that it's an engaging game, admittedly with some flaws, and I'm glad I bought it.  The game is tense. Death comes quickly, sometimes without you even knowing from where, and the premise makes the game creepy. You get wrapped up in the game very easily.  It does have some flaws, as others have mentioned. There are some glitches, there are few maps, and not everything works quite as expected. Primary question, however, given the premise this game is being launched with, is will people keep playing?  As far as I'm concerned, I will. It's too limited to play for hours on end, but I'm more than satisfied to sit down here and there, play a half hour stretch, and then go cool off elsewhere. Hopefully the developers will fix some of the issues, so that the game's fully polished in time for us to enjoy the finale.  EDIT: The vitriol this game has attracted is saddening. Here's a developer trying to do something new - create a multiplayer game with a coherent story that concludes - and everyone's shooting them down for trying. I find it a really interesting idea, and wish more people would give it a chance.

Holy ♥♥♥♥! I remeber back when I played the Witcher 2 how I had hoped for an open world Witcher Game, that combines a lot of characters and plot from the previous game, while still innovating on the origional 'Witcher' design. And ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ I was not expecting this! The Witcher 3 is a masterfully crafted game, where most if not all choices affect the ending. One of my favorite aspects of the Wither 3 is the progression in how you craft items, brew potions, and increase your magic ability. In the beginning, you feel a little underpowered for such an infamous man. However, as you get farther into the game, you start to really develop your magical ability, weaponry, and armor, you start to feel your place in the world. You are not the most powerful person in the world, but you definately influence the area arround you. towards the beginning of the game you start to have to figure out what role you want to play, whether you want to focus more on magic, or get most of your power from potions, or if you want to do what I did and get everything. I reccommend taking your time with this game, as progression is slow, but very enjoyable. As for the story, the story was phenomenal. Each mission was unique, fun, and had some tie-in to how you would affect the end of the game. Now, I reccommend doing ALL of the side quests right when you can. At first I went to fast and skipped over and missed several side quests. For those who dont know, after you progress far enough into a crucial plot moment, most of your sidequests will be marked as a failure, so do them while you can. As for end game, there isnt much to do. All the sde quests are finished, and there is no way to go back and do them, unless you've been saving along the way. The only thing left to do are the monster hunts, and gwent. The monster hunts get kinda old after the first one or two, however if you are loking for that 'full game completion' they are necessary for that achievement. Gwent has just been given its own stand-alone game due to the love people had for it in The Witcher 3. I am not one for card games, although I do think it's cool that the devs actually came up with an all new game, with tons of unique cards to play with. Overall, I would rate my experience with The Wither 3 Wild Hunt a 10/10! I loved the story, the sidequests are tons of fun and are shrouded in mysterys sometimes. There is so much to do and explore, and I never had any hardware issues with the game. It has ALWAYS (from day one) run perfectly. So, if you havn'e already, pick up this game, GET IT. I know its not the $60.00 retail it used to be, and it is definately worth the investment.

I really enjoy Merchants of Kaidan. It fills a niche for a type of game I've been looking for, a non-linear Merchant game. After reading several negative reviews I started to get scared off but I was so curious I couldn't resist. I'm glad I didn't. The game has unlimited potential that it hasn't reached to this point but it is a good game as is. I'm hoping they really get to work and make it a great game. Some of the writing is strange but you get the point. Perhaps there are translation issues, dunno. The trading isn't completely random like others have stated. I used excel and have tracked every towns prices and the changes that take place as the seasons change. The prices aren't set in stone and that is a good thing as if they were you could just make the same runs over and over and that would be broken. The difficulty is pretty hard at the beginning as you struggle to find ways to make money due to the limited resources you are allowed to trade. But with a little patience and thought you figure it out. My guess is those that come into the game unwilling to map things out and make notes will not like this game without sticking with it. I for one hope the development team gets busy and adds more content to a wonderful base.

I enjoyed this sniper game, and I think you'll like it if you generally enjoy this type of game. It took me 8 hours or so to complete the single player mode on easy difficulty. Since I'm not a great player, it took some time and retries to clear the missions, Having checkpoints to avoid starting over each time I died was good, but sometimes I wished there were a few more checkpoints, because there were a few times where I had to replay the same tedious or annoying parts over and over because I kept dying before reaching the next checkpoint.  The story here is broken up into three acts, each made up of a few missions, and you're working as part of a team even in single player mode. I think the story developed well, and even though game play can be a little slow at times (e.g., sneaking into areas means crouch-walking for a long-ish time), I think that helps get you immersed in the story. I think your only weapons here are a sniper rifle, a pistol (I think), and a knife. While the knife is useful for doing silent kills, I don't think I ever really used the pistol. The game kind of seems designed to keep you using the sniper rifle, which makes sense. At a few points you have to set up on a sniping point from which you cannot move at all, which makes things a little more difficult if you get spotted and shot at, since you can't slide over at all to try to keep the enemies from guessing exactly where you are. There are multiple difficulty levels, though I haven't tried any but the easy one, and a multiplayer mode, which I also haven't tried. But the multiple modes and difficulties should make this game accessible to all sorts of players.  A few notes on this game (SGW2) compared to Sniper Elite V2 (SE2), which I played before this: easy mode in SGW2 doesn't mean line up the enemies directly in the center of the cross-hairs like it does in SE2; there is bullet drop and wind effects, but there's a kind of aim-assist mechanism where a small red dot appears showing you where the bullet will go -- you line that dot up with where you want to shoot. As noted above, although the game play in SGW2 is a little slower, I think I got into the story more. You don't have as many weapons/tools here as in SE2, but then again, you're in a team here and solo there. Also, it seems easier to stick with the sniper rifle here; a number of times in SE2, enemies would get close enough that I had to switch to another gun, but that didn't happen very much in SGW2. Both games are good, but operate a little differently (as expected).

What a wonderful game this turned out to be. Tadpole Treble is the debut game by Bitfinity, which among others includes Matthew and Michael Taranto. The former of those two is the author of Brawl in the Family, a really good webcomic I urge you to read. It unfortunately ended before Tadpole Treble came out, so you can treat it as the comic's successor.  Now, I mentioned Brawl in the Family because it had a handful of strips where Matthew got to show off his skill at composing music, and his great singing voice. Since Tadpole Treble is a rhythm game - sort of - these two are both used to full effect. You play as Baton, a newly-hatched tadpole who finds herself abducted from her home at Tadpole Pond by Coda the Pelican (you may be noticing a pattern here). She escapes, but unfortunately finds herself at the tip top of Opus Island. To get home she'll need to swim through all of its waterways, and evade everything that wants her for a snack - which is a lot.  Gameplay is a mix between a rhythm game and an auto-runner, with the twist that you need to avoid the notes making up the song instead of hit them. It has a pretty forgiving difficulty curve, and there's usually multiple safe paths, not to mention a super mode you can trigger that lets you plow right through them (but not other obstacles) after it's fully charged. The stages have a great deal of variety, and all of them feel like they have their own identity. In one you have to deal with a barracuda pursuing you through a dark cavern, in another swimming backwards as you head down a waterfall.   And the songs! Not all of them are voiced, sadly, but they're as unique as the levels, and fit them all perfectly. I saw a comment comparing them to the Kirby series in how they evoke different emotions, and that's pretty accurate. These guys definitely have talent.   Tadpole Treble only spans twelve levels and they're all only about three minutes, same with the songs that accompany them. That's not a problem for me, since it's priced accordingly, the levels are fun, and there's a decent chunk of replayability to them as well. There's the obvious S Ranks you can acquire for perfecting a level, and uniquely, F Ranks you can get by skillfully being as bad at it as possible. Each level also has Challenge Flies you can collect by performing a specific task, and bubbles you can exchange for extras like bestiary pages of all the animals you encounter in the game.  I don't think there's a game on Steam quite like Tadpole Treble, and while it's not exactly a rhythm game I'd recommend it to fans of the genre anyway, or just to anyone looking for something colorful, happy and humorous.

Here is 3 reasons why you should not buy the game !  1 : The matchmaking is broken.   You will end up waiting for 5-10 minutes to get in to a normal match, when the match is over the game will search for a new match ( assuming you do not rematch the other team ) and you will have to wait again. When the matchmaking works, you will be thrown into a game with people 50 levels higher than you. Ranked match is not working at all.. i tried to connect for days now and was unable.   2 : Lack of content.  To be an AAA game, there is very little content to justify the price. sure you get to 'customize' your weapons with 1 silencer, 3-4 sights and some paint that looks ugly unless you use real money to buy those who look moderatly good.   Customize your character? forget it. instead you get to choose from 20 different ones, 10 from each team but thats okey, all the 'operators' have different skills and they made it fun in a way but the problem is that it cost more and more cash to unlock new ones, meaning you have to grind to unlock all.  There is 5 or 6 different maps with 2 different gamemodes, hostage rescue and bomb disarm. To be fair the leveldesign is good and the sound is awesome, but after a while it gets repeated to death.  3: It's ubisoft   They deliver an unfinished AAA game that you pay full price for.. only to charge more money for the extra content that could have made the game better. I know Early Access games with more content then this.
