I can't remember the last time I was so enamored by a game that I felt I had to write a review. Undertale floored me in many ways. The music is diverse and sets the mood well, from atmospheric somber music to upbeat jazzy battle tunes that had me bobbing my head while dodging attacks.  The characters aren't incredibly deep or anything but they are all well written and funny, the game knows when not to take itself successfully and can play with your expectations really well. While I said the characters weren't very deep the story is amazingly layered and has tons of elements to find on different playthroughs. It gets amazingly meta towards the end and deals with player choice pretty well.  The only negative things I have to say are that the game can sort of strong-arm you into one particular ending and the game does run out of steam a little towards the end. Still, I was happy to play through again and meet these loveable characters for the second and third time.  The screenshots don't do the game justice, you can try the demo if you look online or look up a playthough but BE WARY OF SPOILERS and stay determined.

A survival horror-esque first person shooter in the style of Half-Life 2; scripted sequences, puzzle segments, and a well realized post-nuclear world with characters filling in backstory. The atmosphere is incredible, and you really buy the fact that these people living in the Metro have done so for a long time. The story is compelling, and the paranormal element really works. The gunplay is deliberatly clunky; flashlights need to be recharged, the compass and objectives have to be brought up in the form of a diary, and gas mask filters have to be replaced frequently. This all adds to the survival horror feel, as do the excellent creature design and sound effects; there is a very intense segment in a library that was both scary and memorable. The locations are amazing, too: The tunnels are dark and sinister, and the surface is beautiful in a twisted kind of way.   The only gripes I had with the game was that the combat did frustrate occasionally, stealth is EXTREMELY hard to pull off successfully, and some of the characters have cheesy dialogue that detracts from the mood. Otherwise, it's a finely made single player experience.

They took everything special about the Max Payne series and turned it into a standard, cover based shooter. I guess that makes sense because this game isn't devolved my Remedy, this time Rockstar did everything.  There's no more noir, no dream sequences, no graphic novel cinematics, no focus on Matrix-style shoot dodging, or fun for that matter.  Only in the first few missions is it optimal to Jon Woo dive at people, and it's admittedly very fun and over the top violent but that all changes 3-4 hours in. After this, the only enemies you'll be facing are fully armored PMC/UFE soldiers who are are nothing more than bullet sponges. Playing like this around them will get you killed and you're forced to use all your slow-mo sitting in cover and taking head shots. It's stupidly stale...  The only really good thing about the game are the cutscenes and acting. Max Payne himself may be a bitter, depressed husk of a human being who never has any growth, but dammit James McCaffery's voice and performance are rock solid, this guy is a great voice actor.   Everyone else still does a good job as well, if anything Rockstar is always on point when casting people, it's just a shame the actual 'game' part of the game became so bland.

It simply amazes me in this day an age, how people manage to screw up a simple reboot. You already have a fantastic, well respected and loved title, which that lone puts you 50% of the way to your goal, all you need to do now is to include what made that title good, and improve on what wasn't so good. A very simple concept.  However, Rise of the Triad misses this mark by a country mile, and for some reason the development team decided to follow the exact opposite of the formula I listed above. Allow me to explain...  Rise of the Triad was one of those game released on PC in the early 90s, when the home computer field discovered it wasn't going to suffer the same ♥♥♥♥♥♥ inspection as Consoles like Sega and Nintendo were getting over their subject material. It came along the same vein as Blood, Doom 2, and a host of clones such as Dark Forces, when FPS was the name of the game in PC titles. Having an adjustable violence setting was RotT's selling point, saying it was fit for play by children on one end, and chew your enemies into hamburger on the other. But what set it apart from the other titles of the time, was it's bizarre game modes, weapons, and power ups. Made my Apogee who was already worth it's weight in gold by that time, it got a dedicated following while still remaining a cult title for years. The game play was mostly solid, though it did lend itself to a lot of gimmicks like floating platforms and trampolines that shot you around the level which sometimes didn't work as well as it could. However, it made up for this with a menagerie of strange ways to dispatch your foes, including a 'God Mode' that didn't just make you invincible, but enabled you to smite people with a touch.  The reboot manages to remake this title, with absolutely none of the charm of the original, and compounds the broken gimmicks of the first, even adding a few new ones of it's own.   For starters, this games graphical requirements are outrageous. On par with Painkiller Hell and Damnation, this game uses an insane amount of PC power for a game that really doesn't need it. The backgrounds are rather dull, and the character models aren't anything to write home about, so I'm befuddled at the disregard for any kind of resource streamlining. This game also uses motion blur that does more to make you sea sick than enhance game play by any means, and until recently wasn't possible to toggle off. Secondly, the dialog, flavor text, descriptions, and spoken parts are horribly dated. Riddled with the worst in 90's humor, the game gives you the feeling they went back to 1996 and drafted the dialog writers of Duke Nukem 3D. 'All around bad ♥♥♥.' is not a clever description of a character in 2014.The enemies resemble WWII German solders and Occupied Berlin guards, once again reinforcing the idea that it's okay to kill in unspeakable ways, so long as the people resemble Germans.  Lastly, you will die.... a lot. Not because the game is actually difficult, but because the the aiming mechanic for the enemies is hopelessly broken. You will constantly be shot by mobs that you can not see, let alone figure out where their fire is coming from. And, exactly like the original the platforms and trampoline mechanics are wonky and unreliable.   So in all, save your money and go by the Throw Back Pack, that includes both original Triad games. At least those games troubles were due to the limitations of technology at the time, rather than just poor vision and terrible direction.

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: There's something to be said for a game that conveys so much about its characters and world and so many emotions with no understandable dialogue. This game is fantastic.  It's short enough that it can be played in one dedicated afternoon, which I highly recommend. I played it in chunks, and I think it definitely detracted from the experience. It took some getting used to the gameplay each time I started it back up, but I think it's a really unique way to play a game like this. The puzzles are easy enough to figure out, but still fun enough to keep from getting repetative. There are interactive items in most scenes that don't drive the plot, per se, but give that much more depth into the characters that you'd really be missing out if you ignored them. I loved the soundtrack, as well.   The graphics are beautiful, and this is the kind of world I would spend hours exploring. So many details that only left me wanting to know everything about this world. There are benches in most areas where you can simply sit and observe, and I would recommend doing just that, so you can fully appreciate the love and creativity put into this game.  I highly recommend this game. I think it's worth the full price, but I picked it up on sale.

I was introduced to this game via the Night Dive Bundle (Thanks Bundlestars!). I was originally planning to get it simply for Systemshock 2, but this wonderful game came along with it. after doing some research, i found out just how loved it was, so I tried it for myself.   Basic, spoiler free tldr Plotline: Create your party, grab 3 artifacts and stop evil dude from doing the same, whist exploding everyone and everything that stands in your way of saving the world.  The plot is pretty much put to the wayside in my own opinon, but it is interesting enough to be noteable.   At first, It can be a bit daunting for players used to newer RPGs, but everything is very well streamlined when it comes to the User Interface and inventories. That is, once you get used to it.     Combat: Basic lowdown: up to six PC's and two followers fighting together against enemies, using ranged, melee, or magic, with enemies in a 3d space. Works in a similar way to the older FFs, and is very fun at as well as aggrivating in earlier stages.  The game spices up this style of combat by applying the strategies of positioning and formations, which are vital for both staying alive and dealing damage. The enemies have a bad tendency to be either mediocore or brutal, crushing your party to a pulp at times. But, the combat remains rather balanced throughout, and exciting.  Characters and Character Progression: Much like Oblivion or Morrowind, each character has Basic Stats that can only be raised through leveling up, and Skill Stats that can be leveled both through gaining levels or by use in combat. Creating a character that fits his or her role into the party is absolutely esssential, and if not created properly, can permanently cripple the party. To survive basically, you need at least one Melee character in the front, a Ranged in the back, along with a caster to support them. Character classes basically divvy up the general roles into more specifc ones, with some classes unable to use certain weapons, you must choose carefully.  The combinations of both races and classes, and the distribution of stat points allows for immense variety in playstyles, from the practical to the ridiculous. Probably my favorite thing about the progression is seeing how far my party has come from the beginning. From my caster being barely able to cast basic magic to being a Lord of Death on the battlefield, erasing damage to party members and boosting their stats to ridiculous levels. Another thing that stands out is the wide variety of Voice packs that each character can posess, and can be changed at any time. From laid back to the edge of sanity, sultry or sneaky, your choice. My favorites are the Male and Female Chaotic voices. These add much needed soul to the game, as well as some laughs.  Graphics: The game looks 'Good Enough' for the modern age of games. Thankfully, with programs included such as Nglide, the game can run on just about anything. Laggy areas can be prevalent, even on modern, top of the line PC's, but usually, it will run at a glorious 60fps. What makes it even better, is that Steam Cloud saving for some odd reason works perfectly with this game, and the game's saves can flawlessly transfer from computer to computer.  Stuff I do not like about this game: This game is hands down the best of this specifc genre that I have ever played, but it still has its flaws.  -quests and objectives can be rather ambigous, and without the help of online guides, some of these can be very frustrating. -Crafting recipies are never revealed in game, which can be agrivating unless you have them online. -Travelling the world can be rather slow, as it is quite expansive, and portal spells can only be unlocked later in the game. -Saving is a bit awkward, having to go into the options menu every time can get annoying. -Starting off on the wrong foot party wise can lead to a near impossible playthrough.  Otherwise, I heartily recommeded to those wishing to sink a lot of time into a great, easily scaleble game.

Teslagrad is 2D puzzle-platformer released in December 2013. In an already saturated genre, Teslagrad aims to distinguish itself using the game's magnet mechanics. The protagonist can pull himself towards a faraway magnet and conversely force propulsion if he is standing on a magnet to leap large distances and heights in order to navigate and progress through the game world.  The biggest strength of Teslagrad is the balance between puzzle and platform aspects of the game. By using the tools the protagonist obtains as the player progresses in the game, both puzzle solving and quick platforming skills are required. The game does not have health and the protagonist dies instantly upon contact with the game's harmful obstacles. Thus the aspect of timing is crucial in many of the game's puzzles to progress to the next screen. The difficulty curve to progress is mild but it is raised for searching for the 36 collectables in the game, for both puzzle solving and platform skills. Speaking of difficulty there are handful of bosses in the game which the difficulty spikes for all of them. However those challenges are not impossible although for most players, it would take multiple attempts to take them down.  The controls are very crisp and responsive. Jumping feels good without being too floaty or limited. The default keybinding is slightly odd to get used to but it is fully rebindable with keyboard and gamepad.  The game's visuals are handdrawn in pencil and crayon. The art assets are relatively plain and unobtrusive to allow focus onto the gameplay. There are few instances of impressive art in the background. The game does not limit itself to certain colour palettes and various primary colours are used. This helps the player to distinguish what is in the foreground and interactable from the background art.  Music is mostly well-composed ambient with more intense orchestra coming in for the boss battles. Effects are well placed as it adds the steampunk nature of the tower atmosphere.  Overall, Teslagrad would be a worthy game for library of puzzle-platform fans.

Among the City line of sims we first had the SimCity. It was fun, it was manger-ish, it took planning, and we could have a giant robot stomp on the city. Then we had Cities, and Cities XL, which weren't half as much fun for a plethora of reasons. Then we had SimCityDumbedDown(WithSuperTowers).  With Cities: Skylines you sort of expect it to be Cities XL, but with lacking ingredients of fun and smooth gameplay. Instead, you kinda get the latest SimCity as it should have been. And frankly, Paradox could have done much, much worse.  Not many sandboxes can balance the seeming casual-ness of gameplay and grasp the intricate depth of play sufficient for one mistake to bite you in the rear later on. Not many of them have the visually entertaining look you'd expect, perhaps because developers haven't spent enough time with Lego kits and never had their parents bring in a chinese copy of a Lego kit by mistake. Skylines, however, manages to pull off both visually entertaining looks (and yes, the annoying grain filter is there for a very good reason) and gameplay that will at least cause you to return to this game at least a few times after your first, uh... no, you cannot really have a playthrough in a sandbox.  If you have achieved extreme planning prowess and want the game to dynamically challenge your perfectly balanced city-machine with an invasion of Godzillas, or, worse, by an EA take-over of your city's gaming industry, you may be a bit disappointed. But if you do not yet comprehend how much of a challenge a traffic congestion can be and how harmful it can be to your tax money, I urge you to have a go at this game.  For the price, it's very worthwhile.

UPDATE:  Love this game even more than the original now that I've gotten used to the mechanics. TONS of flexibility in regards to tactics. Good story and customization options. The timer that I (and most others) have complained about is really not that bad once you've played the game a bit. In fact, you'll find that those missions become much easier to complete once you get some experience with the core gameplay.  All in all, I'm happy with my purchase. There's plenty of stuff online about the game, so check it out if you're on the fence. 60 bucks is pretty steep for a point and click strategy game, but for gamers like myself who grew up with those types of games; it's totally worth the price.  NOTES FOR NEW AND POTENTIAL BUYERS OF XCOM 2:  Normal difficulty is pretty challenging; even when save scumming. If you're new, go with easy to get the new mechanics of the meta-game and combat down. Trust me, it will still be challenging for most.  I'm on my third run on normal difficulty. Auto save scumming my butt off. I've made it pretty far and think I've finally got the mechanics down enough to finish this run....but it's still tough.  I think after you beat it on normal mode, you can then try iron man. I'll probably do iron man on easy next. The thought of losing soldiers on normal difficulty seems like a losing battle and zero fun.         ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ Fist off, was worried I woudn't be able to run this on my below specs laptop, but the game is optimized enough to play on low settings. Graphics are still okay and gameplay is excellent! This game is deep. Tons of choices, customizations, and surprises. The only downside for me so far is the amount of timed missions there are. The ratio is far higher than their first iteration. I understand the design choice and how it fits with the story, but still dislike the frequency of them. I heard you can change it in the .ini but I'll deal with it for now and see if I get used to it.   This game is pretty challenging even on veteran (normal) for me. And I've played a ton of XCom and Enemy Unknown. Not ready to throw in the towel and try easy just yet though.  At any rate, if you liked XCOM, you will love this one. Watch some vids. What you see is what you get and I'm loving it. They took a lot of great ideas from their first game and improved upon them. Big Thumbs up from me so far.

I wholeheartedly was so excited for this game. Upon launch i got straight into it and was blown away, i recommended the game to many friends and was a firm follower of the No Man's Sky hype.. until i got about 8 hours in and realised that this game is literally an 8 hour game, just looped with new colours, over and over to create the feel of a vast, expansive world.  All of the interviews, all of the press-releases, the trailers, everything - the game is NOTHING like those.. this is clearly a money grab, and completely falls under misleading and false advertisement.  For those who say that its our fault for not doing our research prior to buying, etc.. thats what TRAILERS are for! It can not be the consumers fault for a company or organisation misleading and making false claims about their product. The game literally does not perform like any of the trailers or screenshots, and it is a game that gets boring after about 5 hours - albeit i gave it 8 before i got bored.   I wanted to give the game another shot, so i deleted my save and started again... same thing.. 5 hours in and i saw the same crap over and over.. its not 18 quintrillion planets, its about 18 at most, just recoloured and altered in tiny ways.  Seriously, don't purchase this game. Hello Games did the dirty and got away with it, and its about time the consumers put their foot down and stopped this nonsense.

As some of the reviews suggest; This is also available online as a flash game. And thanks to those reviews I remembered I've played it some years ago, and did have some fun with it.  The gameplay is simple, yet addicting like hell - Especially if you want to 100% this game. You move your knight from left to right, enganging enemies in a never-ending fight. Well nearly never-ending. You 'level up'/'gear up' your knight in the shop with money earned and picked up during gameplay, which helps make the game more compelling. However, it's pretty simplistic but in a way that suits the game decently. Since there's only one type of weapon and all enemy attacks (those who have it) deal the same amount of damage, there is no reason for a more 'complex gearing/leveling system'.  Which brings me to the pricetag. I only bought it because I was looking at some brief fun but most importantly: I had a 66% off coupon. I would NEVER consider a game like this (without trading cards) with a pricetag like this when you're done with it after ~5 hours. It may sound harsh, but the reality is I'm not sure if I'm ever going to play the game again after completing it. Atleast the developer put in a 'Start new game' feature which might help in replay-ability.  Which again brings me to my next point.. as others have already stated, you're not able to rebind the controls. You'll quickly learn them however, and I only found it frustrating once I broke 'zeh purhfect g4m3'. You're able to pause the game with Esc - However, if you want to resume, you have to press space. If you press esc it restarts, and if you press enter you'll enter the shop (or vice versa - Sue me, I can't remember haha).  Overall the game did its trick - I ultimately had fun with it. If you like casual games, with simplistic gameplay and 'level up system' this might rock your boat. The graphics and animations, in my opinion, are great. The sound/music is also awesome, however there's a slight audio glitch with the final potion making overlap noises if you stack them. The game doesn't crash or anything, it just sounds weird when the second potion is about to wear out).

Wishing for a toggle noclip cheat -simulator.  First you stroll around a town with houses you can't enter. Then you proceed into a snowstorm and are spooked of a red hole in the ground because hey red holes are scary and this is a horror game. After this you start going towards the tower with the red light. Sean Bean recites elementary school poetry as a giant cloud made of urine chases you into a collapsed church. You start seeing ghosts made of the aforementioned urine and try to run away from them for all two seconds before your character gets winded and has to rest and his vision gets all blurry. You stare at your useless map as you explore the incredibly detailed and interesting world made of rocks and trees and of course, snow. After seven seconds of exploring you notice that you have gotten lost again and decide to jump off a cliff to get back to the latest checkpoint. At this point in this amazing journey the game takes a surprising turn into something even more interesting. You start googling franticly for a noclip cheat and will continue to repeat this excellent gameplay feature until the end of the game. At the end you quit the game and uninstall it. What a journey it was, what a journey.  I recommend this game for everyone. Would uninstall again.

app_name: Boring Man - Online Tactical Stickman Combat
review_text: All of my enemies ran into lasers and died.  Then I fell and I died too after I won the round...  Next round, I exploded into tiny pieces.  10/10, would get exploded again.   On a serious note, this is a nice game to pass the time. I've had some fun with it and I've just started. I highly advise you to make your own server or join someone not too far from you. I'm from Portugal, and some Brazillians joined me, their ping was high obviously (above 400), but players in europe had below 100 ping (mainly 60~80). I am having fun, and I bet I will have even more if the number of servers increase. :)

I really liked the game right until the final boss. The gameplay is your standard RPG with the standard RPG about you being the chosen one and using your diverse party members to save the world. The gameplay was solid (rewards for exploring dungeons, combat, leveling up, etc.), the dialogue was good (with some humor and references to other games thrown in which was a nice touch) and overall a cute little game. I just wish you could explore the world because other than explore dungeons, they are no other side stuff to do.   I was going to rate this game a 8.5 out of 10, but then I got to the final boss fight and my rating dropped considerably.   This boss is either programmed very poorly or is just bugged. When someone dies, you can't revive them and the boss gains 10,000 hp (Or if she kills the healer, she gets 100,000 so if that happens, just quit); this goes with the theme of the boss, but still it's very annoying. If the boss hits anyone, but the warrior, they are dead (being hit for 999 with no chance of dodging no matter how high my dodge was) so you can't make a mistake. After skipping through the dialogue for the 5th time and losing the battle, I decided to give up because I'm not grinding; I didn't have to grind for any other boss so I'm not wasting my time here.   So, if you like RPGs, you'll like this game, but fair warning on the final boss because she is cheap as hell.

Evoland 2 is a wonderful adventure game that takes you through different decades in gaming, changing it's visual and gameplay style as you go along. The story focuses on travels through time and constantly references classics of the times.  Not only does Evoland 2 improve upon aspects from the first game, but it has taken the storytelling to a new level. Playing through the first game in approximately 2-3 hours, I didn't have too high expectations for the second in terms of story length, but I was pleasently surprised to see the story expand further and further, reaching a wonderful 20 hours of content on the main story alone. For those of you that haven't heard of it or aren't quite sure, this game is definitely worth picking up, since it'll have you hooked from the moment you start.

Playtime to 100%:  About 4 hours  Keywords:  visual novel, catgirls, slice-of-life, comedy, lewd  NEKOPARA Vol. 2's four-hour story follows the La Soleil gang as they manage the busy patisserie, where business is booming thanks to imouto Shigure knowing how to leverage the appeal of catgirls online. Protagonist (pervy) pastry chef Kashou has a chance to get closer to the other catgirls, particularly Coconut, the clumsy younger sister, and Azuki, the sarcastic older sister. There are fights, misunderstandings, and many, many tears, but ultimately love and affection will conquer all.  It's a  sweet, heartwarming story , but it's also extremely predictable and follows the same basic pattern as Vol. 1. Most people probably don't play NEKOPARA for the plot, but if you're looking for something more than silly slice-of-life, then this isn't for you. I would have preferred a little more variation as I feel that the characters lose some of their individuality when their issues all essentially boil down to being head over heels for Master, but their personalities do shine through in their speech and interactions.  The production quality is as high as ever with gorgeous,  vibrant artwork  from Sayori-san. Every CG, SD, character sprite, and background is an absolute joy to look at although I unfortunately didn't notice any new backgrounds that we hadn't seen in Vol. 1.   The  E-mote system  is used to great effect to bring the character sprites to life, and the movements and facial expressions are perfectly matched with the speech. Vol. 2 also retains the 'chest bounciness' slider from Vol. 1 and the 'petting' mode from Vol. 0, which are both fun additions.   Voice acting  is wonderful as always, sound effects are used well, and the music is lovely, although it is mostly the same as in Vol. 1. There are also two super upbeat  new opening and ending tracks !  While the Steam version is supposedly all-ages, it does get quite  lewd  in places, and I was surprised by just how risque some of the content was. There is no nudity, but some of the conversations and sound effects go beyond being simply suggestive.  In conclusion, while this newest installment doesn't bring anything new to the table, it's a ton of fun to be back in the bright & bouncy world of NEKOPARA, making Vol. 2 a must-play for fans of the series. It's also a visually beautiful, high-quality visual novel with a great translation, so if you're new to the NEKOPARA phenomenon I highly recommend checking out Vol. 1 to see what you think.   Click here to read the full review  [merumeruchan.wordpress.com]   Follow the Visual Novel Fans curator list

Developer: The Creative Assembly Publisher: Activision Release Date: 22.09.200  Played On: (Various but most recently) Nvidia GTX 760 AMD FX 6300 3.5Ghz 8GB RAM  (Upto several hundred hours to complete depending on faction, difficulty level and campaign length chosen).  Where to start with Rome Total War? First released back in September 2004 the game recently reached it's tenth anniversary. I've played it on and off since release and the recent anniversary prompted me to go back and play through it again. It's a game that mostly stands up to the test of time for me and well worth playing today. I didn't play the Barbarian Invasion expansion included in the Steam edition as it's quite a distinct entity that should be reviewed in it's own right.  Like all games in the Total War series there are two elements; the campaign map where you manage your empire, build cities, recruit units, move armies etc and the battle map, where you fight battles in real time. This gives the game some great depth and whilst you can auto-resolve battles this can be a bit hit and miss and you'll want to fight them yourself to get the best results. The Imperial Campaign is the main game mode where you can choose from a number of different factions and play through either a short or long campaign with prescribed victory conditions and different difficulty settings. There is a decent tutorial to ease you into things and plenty of advice is available throughout both with the optionn to turn it off for veterans.  The Imperial Campaign is backed up by being able to fight custom battles where you choose the map, faction etc and Historical Battles which recreate a number of famous battles of the era such as Hannibal's invasion of Italy and the Battle of the Teutoberg Forest. You can also play online against other players but this wasn't something I did.  The main campaign has a good deal of depth and each of the factions has different styles, units, cities and starting points which offer a wide variety of challenge. Playing as the Romans is the initial starting point and they are divided into three factions which compete against other and have some additional mechanics to account for including the Senate of Rome, the Marius Reforms and some other suprises which I won't spoil. Rome also generally has the strongest units and buildings which can make things somewhat straightforward for an experienced player. However a lot of the late game units of other factions more than match them and playing the campaign as Eygpt, Carthage, the Britons or others is very enjoyable.  The mechanics of the campaign map are very broad and offer a wide variety of tactical options to pursue; bribery, assassination and spreading plagues amongst them. I played through on the very hard settings which I'd reccomend any veteran Total War player to do and the campaign offered a good challenge with interesting scenarios and incidents along the way. Whilst my progress was steady I wasn't able to steamroll the AI into the ground within a few turns. I did play as Eygpt who are one of the easier non Roman factions to play but still a challenge later in the game.  The battle map is probably the slightly weaker side of the game and whilst some battles, particularly sieges, can be a bit difficult or frustrating, most are straightforward affairs if you have assembled a balanced army and have a relatively comprehensive grasp of the tactics to use. However the various factions armies all have different strengths or weaknesses and there isn't the rock, paper, scissors situation you see in some games. The Roman armies have the strongest armies as a general rule of thumb but other factions e.g. Eygpt, Germania have some very good units too and what works with one may not work at all with another.  I've not mentioned performance or graphics at all as the game is ten years old and there generally shouldn't be any issues nor will it look fantastic.  I'd also take a moment to mention the additional content available online from Europa Barborum and a couple of other sites. These add a huge amount to the game and are well worth a look if you want to take things further after playing the main game.  Overall I'd definetely reccomend playing this if your a fan of the Total War series and I also feel its a good starting point for the series as whole. Those interested in the era itself will certainly find it enjoyable.

Not going to dethrone the GTA franchise by any means, but a fantastic game if you know what to expect. Beautiful graphics and atmosphere with a lot of details, and a lengthy (Not a bad thing) narrative in an age of 4 hour campaigns. It's a story that could only play out via several hours and really demonstrates what the medium is capable of in a semi-complex storyline (A large part of the ending you see coming halfway through admittedly, but it wasn't for the reason you would expect). The open-world elements are semi forced but fairly fun with no real incentive to explore (This game was almost cancelled halfway through so a lot of polish in free roaming does not exist outside of the narrative). When it's on sale for under 10 bucks next year and you're in the mood to be immersed you can do much worse.

I am Mason. Mason's brother tells me to knock stuff down to collect scrap. I only have a sledgehammer. I swing at a concrete wall. The wall shatters and crumbles before my Superman strength.   I go over to a silo-like building. I start smashing it. Smash! Smash! Smash! I smash all of it at the base except for a door. And I thought, 'I wonder if this is like Minecraft where everything will just float there...' I smash the door. The entire silo collapses in on me. WHOOPS! I survived. I ran over to another place. I smashed some more. I was told to blow up two buildings. I threw so many explosives on them. I detonate them. Dust every where. No more buildings!    Later on...    There's a high-value structure I want to take out. I go looking around the fence but the guards are everywhere. 'Screw it,' I think, 'I'll just run right into it with my stolen Mars humvee-truck-thing!' I crank up to full speed. I ran over a badguy. And, I don't quite remember, but either by accident or by unsuspecting fantastic fysix, my car spun to the right and flew over a rock outcropping and flew into the air. It crashes into my target building. I get out. Car go boom. I throw explosives everywhere. Building go boom.   I had a good time.   Mission accomplished!   Later on... SPOILER ALERT!   It's the last mission for this first part of the map I'm in. I took out 11/12 of my targets and only have one more. The armored car I was given for my mission was roasted a long, long time ago. I'm running to my last target now. Bullets from enemy firearms and truck-mounted turrets fly all around me. I'm running down a hill like my life depends on it (probably because it did...). Two trucks try to run me over. I dodge and keep going while I hear all the enemy trucks smashing into each other as they try with all their effort to turn me into tire tracks or a heap of lead. I jump over a rocky outcropping. The first of 3 guards didn't see me because he thought I was going to go around. Silly guard, don't you know the fastest way from point A to point B is a straight line? You should retake geometry. I feel like Chuck Norris, Rambo, Han Solo, and Indiana Jones all combined as I run up to my last target, swing my might hammer Mjolnir, and bring down said target like a house of cards. I make a dash for some more outcropped rocks. My screen is turning grey as my health is leached from me.   The game pauses of its own accord...  'MISSION ACCOMPLISHED'  Did all of this my first hour-and-a-half in the game.    8/10 would be a martian Chuck Norris again.

PAY TO WIN? Not really. Much like TF2, the strongest equipments are free. You get 2,000 Blutes (currency to buy weap customization) when you level up which help with exploring other playstyles. Anything useful can be bought with in-game currency, but most aesthetics are quite overpriced for real money. Unity of Command is a simplified strategic turn-based look into the eastern fronit during WW2 - similar in many ways to the classic Panzer General, while adding some fresh ideas of its own (such as the concept of supply, which enables increasingly realistic maneuverig in the spirit of the historical blitzkrieg. Having said that, the game also suffers somewhat from oversimplification, repetitiveness and rigidity. When considering buying the game, consider:  Pros: -Easy to get into, yet challenging enough to try and master  -Some tactical planning and thinking required, luck and chance is involved so there is some variety in gameplay.  -Quite immersive, the total seems more than the sum of its parts.   Cons: -The AI seems quite simple: you make a certain set of moves and it always reacts similarly.  -Unrealistic winning objectives - sacrifice your army simply to gain possession of a few winning hexes for one turn and you suddenly have 'won'?  -No air units available  -Only scenarios where offensive is to be taken (AI lacks the ability to launch a coherent offensive?) - some defensive scenarios would be welcome  -'Brilliant victories' often depend on chance (e.g., chancing in-game weather), yet proceeding in the campaign in some cases requires such victories - the game therefore makes you grind several scenarios over and over again.  -The scenarios always start the same - some alterate starting positions would be welcome for variety.  -After you win the campaign, there's a smiple 'Campaign completed' message and nothing else - really?  -Music can get repetitive and the graphics are not the selling point of the game.  Overall, I tend to recommend the game for people interested in the topic and looking for some easy-to-get-into strategy gaming - not at the level of Metacritic (average 84/100), but rather at the level of the user reviews (7.2/10).  In other words, it's quite ok.

Should be called Stealing Things Simulator 2018, really really-stic, wonderful graphics, great grameplay.  THERE IS A TL;DR PART IF YA SCROLL DOWN, K? K.  The menu/start-a-game thing is really stupid, you have to sit and wait for the contract you want to play. If you don't want to sit and wait for the right contract, you can buy that contract using your off-shore shekels. That part pisses me off since you sometimes have to sit and wait for like 5 mins.  Another bad thing is the invisible walls, you feel like you could go anywhere you want to but then poof, you get stopped by an invisible wall.   There's one more thing that I might be alone in thinking, and that is the Perk system. So there are these perks right? And these perks play a HUGE role in this game, and the way you get these perks are by leveling up, nothing new there right? But if you're at a low level, you can't get all the things. For example there's a Saw perk, which lets you unlock the saw tool. This saw is needed if you don't want missions like the Bank Heist: Deposit to get reaaaally boring. For you who haven't played the game, in this mission you have to enter the bank vault and unlock these deposit boxes, not so hard right? WELL, without the saw you have to pick the locks by hand which takes like 20-30 seconds each, and there's five boxes per letter in the alphabet minus Y and Z, which makes a total of 120 boxes (i might be wrong on this, but search for the bank heist 'bank heist deposit' so that you get a clue on what i'm talking about). I did the whole mission stealthy-stealthy, and it took us 10 minutes to get all the boxes open. Imagine 10 minutes of just sitting there and looking into the wall. Really fun. (I do realize that the boxes are surposed to be a bit of a gamble but still, if you want to ace it, it takes far too long)  Otherwise it's a great game, missions are pretty much always different in some way, the cameras, the coke, the guards, the everything, switch places everytime you play the mission so you can't just speed run the whole thing and know the exact location of stuffings, you have to search the place for the dank bank monys. The game is really strategical, which makes it so different from other games in my opinion. You can actually stealth a bank, meaning that you can enter a bank silently and get out without having to deal with the coppers. This keeps you playing as you want to complete a heist as smoothly as possible.   I missed a lot of positives, but there are a lot of positives, and they outweigh the negatives by far.  TL;DR awesome game, has a few flaws but the positives outweigh the negatives. If you ever thought to yourself 'I wonder how it would feel like robbing a bank', then buy this game.  8/10

Every 2-3 months, for some inexplicable reason, I get the sudden itch to mod New Vegas to an unbelievable point. I install the game, download around 100 mods, sometimes even up to 200. Then I try to get all of them to behave. I then begin to play until I am able to make sure every mod has done what it is supposed to. After that, I uninstall everything.  Then the cycle begins anew.  Don't know why, maybe I like to subject myself to a weird sort of stress. It's like a challenge that no matter how much you are failing, the worst that could happen is that you stop your game from starting.  So basically, New Vegas is one of the few games out there that have the dankest of mods, and modding itself is such a great feeling for some reason.  So not only do you get a neat game, but if you enjoy modding just for modding, this is for you.    It's a great platform to start modding and a great one to get some practice in. If you like to mod, this game is a must have. If you like to create stuff for games, this also is a must have.
It's all a matter of taste, but if you can dig open world games then it's a must-buy. Bugs and quirks aside the experience is too huge and enjoyable to miss.

Quick Summary: If you loved Strike Suit Zero so much that you wanted more of the same, but without a campaign story, then Strike Suit Infinity is for you. Infinity is more like an arcade game than its predecessor, and your goal is to get as high a score as possible in each level, usually by killing everything as fast as possible.  You play a pilot in the United Nations of Earth (just like in Strike Suit Zero) but instead saving the galaxy, you’re just going through missions in a simulator, one after another. For each level (called “rounds” in the game) you need to kill all the fighters, bombers, and corvettes that they throw at you and survive the capital ships (frigates, cruisers, carriers) long enough for them to jump out. If you manage to do that, you’ve “beaten” the level and the next one unlocks. For each level you beat, you get some credits that you can spend on reinforcements for the next. For example beating level 1 might give you 5000 credits that you can spend buying fighter support for level 2. Note that once level 2 is over, any fighters you bought disappear, so you don’t want to buy reinforcements unless you think you’ll need them. If you don’t buy them and get overwhelmed though… game over. You can choose to start over at the higher level but you don’t get all the extra credits from beating the earlier levels. For example if you choose level 10 to start, you might start the game with 20,000 credits, but if you had worked your way up from level 1 and carefully saved your money you might have 50,000 credits by level 10. Also, I mentioned that you only need to survive long enough for the capital ships to escape in order to beat a level, but if you want more credits/higher score you’ve gotta blast them all.  As for the actual combat, the game plays pretty much identically to Strike Suit Zero. You pilot one of 3 strike suits (basic, marauder, and raptor) and can build up “flux” by blasting enemies. The flux allows you to transform into strike mode, where your space fighter turns into a kick-♥♥♥ robot with cool guns and rapid lock-on missiles that can splash an entire screen full of enemy fighters if you’re good at aiming. The raptor strike suit is kind of light a faster but weaker version of the basic suit, but the marauder is a heavy bomber. Instead of the fast lock missiles you have slow anti-capital ship torpedoes, like the bomber in Strike Suit Zero. Instead of the flak canon gun, you have a long range anti-capital ship canon. Basically, the marauder is for dealing with the big enemies and isn’t nearly as good against strike craft. Unfortunately, you cannot change which strike suit you play during missions without starting over, so you’ll probably be using the basic one most of the time (it’s the best all-purpose ship).  Pros: +If you liked Strike Suit Zero combat and wasn’t a big fan of the mediocre story, you’ll probably enjoy Strike Suit Infinity.  +As a space fighter sim it has a lot elements that I enjoy: lock on missiles for enemy strike craft, the ability to blast weak points and turrets off of giant ships, and a “special move” transforming into strike mode for real ♥♥♥ kicking. +Your level scores are compared with people in your steam friends list so you can see who’s the best.  Cons: -I really miss having a story for single player gaming -If you want a high score in the later levels you really need to work your way up from level 1 to have enough credits. Some of the enemies can totally wipe out your reinforcements (kamikaze drones and mine layers).  -Just like Strike Suit Zero, you have no control over your allies. For example you can’t order them to attack specific targets. It would be nice to be able to order your bombers to focus on capital ships.  Overall, the game is a fun extension of the original Strike Suit Zero. Don’t just jump directly into Strike Suit Infinity though or you will have a hard time. The slow progression of the original game makes the increased difficulty more reasonable.

Well, on the surface there didn't appear to be too much to this game - 4 player coop with 5 characters to choose from; rats instead of zombies; a few different mission types; but generally find your way from one end of the zone to the other.  Turns out that the formula doesn't need to be a whole lot more complicated than that!  The creature design is effective, and when you hear a rat ogre coming there is a genuine sense of dread. More than one wipe has resulted from the Ai director throwing one at us just as we approach the extraction point.  The aforementioned AI director (a la Last of us) means that every run through is different, and keeps a sense of interest in the levels, especially as with only 2 human players we have had limited success on difficulty levels higher than easy - it really is that hard, but the challenge/reward ratio is good enough that you still try.  One interesting mechanic is the loot drops - no trading between players, and limited control over what class the mission reward is for; play a witch hnter, every chance you get a dward axe for mission completion - and only for completion! Stumble at the last hurdle and you get pretty much nothing!  The only way to play this game is with friends - i can't see a whole lot of interest in a solo run, it truly is a well tailored co-op experience, so go and get a few copies, give them to your friends and have a crack!

app_name: Infinity Wars - Animated Trading Card Game
review_text: Updated review after many months away.  Same story as in the early access. Lots and lots of potential here. It just oozes it everywhere. However there are 3 big things that made me uninstall this for the last time.  1. Most importantly is bugs. Bugs everywhere. I have lost so many matches that I actually won because the game froze and I was forced to quit out. Not to mention endless animation loops that force you to quit. also there are constant disconnects mid match. This is crippling in rift running where you only get 3 shots and your done. When you have a killer deck and end up losing it because bugs force restarts then it really kills your joy.  2. Booster packs take foreeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeever to earn once your boosted xp from low levels goes away. you get 170-350 currency for losing/winning. a booster costs 5250. as you can see you need a lot of wins or even more combinations of losses and wins to get 15 cards. There is trading but no auction house which this game desperately needs. 15+ wins for one booster might be tolerable assuming you win all matches except for problem 3.  3. If you play at the peak hours its tolerable, but god forbid you want to play on non peak times. There just isn't a large enough player base for a pvp focused game. Blood Rayne : Betrayal is the latest installment in the BloodRayne Franchise.   Mechanically, it is a competant side scroller, with fairly responsive controls and a complex and satifisying moves list.  There are some hickups with the Dashes and the Backflips, which can be difficult to pulloff, but they are mostly manageable. Whilst it is true that the game demands a high skill cap, that is only if one wishes to get the perfect score on each level, and it ultimately comes down to simple pattern recognition. Other wise its not actually all that difficult, with the exception of some of the later levels.   The art direction and music is fairly well done; the soundtrack is fast paced with gothic and orchestral undertones, and the animation quality and level design is for the most part good.  Time wise the game should take about 7-9 hours to complete, depending on how often you die. There is a considerable amount of replayability though, as there is a level rating system and collectibles.  As a Bloodrayne game there are noticible differances from the previous entries; for starters the Rayne in Betrayal has a considerably different design; she looks younger, possesses much smaller armblades and does not wear her iconic black and red leather outfit, instead wearing a predominately black gothic-inspired outfit.  In addition, she is missing most of her powers and equipment - She no longer has her rage move, her harpoon, her carpathian dragons, nor her signature feeding method. However, despite this Rayne still feels like Rayne; though minimal, she does have her sense of wit, she is still a tough character, and she still fights with acrobat ferocity, just like in the previous games.   Story wise Betrayal has no relation to the previous games; Kagan is still alive, the Shroud never happened, there is no mention of the GGG. So if you were hoping to a resolution to BloodRayne 2's cliff hanger, you are not getting it. It does introduce a new character, however he's only really there to give Rayne a new gimmicky power.  For the most part Betrayal does feel like a Bloodrayne game; there's that familiar over the top level of violence and humor and the story is gloriously ludicrous.  Overall it is a well crafted 2d platformer and an adequate, albeit unorthodox entry into the BloodRayne series.  However, I still cannot draw myself to recommend this, as Rayne's original outfit, which was featured on the very cover art of the game, is not in the game itself. I found that to the extremely misleading, and a clear attempt to pander to the franchise's fanbase.

Grey Goo   is a real time strategy game developed by   Petroglyph  , a break-off of   EA   that contains many prominent members of   Westwood  . Namely for me, Frank Klepacki.    Sound 5/5   The music in this game is truly amazing. Klepacki took a risk trying the orchestral music type, and it paid off. He has made other music like this in previous games, like   In the Line of Fire   from   Tiberium Dawn  , but that was mere dabbling. The entirety of   Grey Goo's   is in this orchestral style, and it is perfect. The individual tracks really make the game come alive, and they all sound unique.   Grey Goo   reintroduces a mechanic from other games that I love, the jukebox. Letting me listen to the best tracks in whatever order. The soundtrack is amazing, and I listen to it on its own.    Design 5/5   The hotkey system in   Grey Goo   is one for the ages. I almost can't play other RTSs anymore because I've become dependent on it. Its fast and sleek. The alien race, the Beta, are so real. They have a distinct culture, and even a language made for them. The cutscenes look so real, its unbelievable. There is much to say about the design in this game, but mostly its just flawless.    Gameplay 3/5   There have been a lot of complaints about the gameplay, and I can see why. There are very few unit types, and there is no distinction between infantry and heavy units. Its rather linear to say the least, in a genre that should be more wide. Everything takes so long to make. The game struggles very hard to just stay alive when the unit cap is reached. However, the armour and damage mechanics are quite unique, and the Goo, despite being labeled as an overpowered Zerg ripoff, are very balanced and fun to use. Epic units are plain, unadulterated fun to use. The simplicity of the game, despite being quite the flaw, is kind of refreshing.    Story 2/5   Bloody hell, its not a ripoff of   Starcraft  , alright? I'm not going to describe the entire story because its quite intricate, and this is a review, not a synopsis. At first glance, it seems rather analagous to   Starcraft  , (the Beta are the Terrans, Humans are Protos, etc.) and it does borrow elements from   Starcraft  , but it still remains its own. Unfortunately, the story is tattered, and fifteen campaign missions is simply not enough to craft a believable world from. It could be so much more, but its too short.    TL; DR    Grey Goo is a unique, and refreshing take on the dying RTS genre. It falls a touch short in some areas, but compensates, making a well-rounded game, definitely worth your time.    Conclusion    Price Worth: 100% Rating: 8/10

I'm really suprised that this isn't more well known as a quality point and click adventure. It has a very good plot, mostly good voice acting, sensible puzzles and well done sound and visuals.  The best point of this game is the plot quality. It has a really interesting story featuring characters with depth. Your character, a Catholic monk, arrives at a remote monastery with his young acolyte and is asked to help solve a potential murder. Things get complicated quickly but the story never loses credibility. Good scripting (with a few minor mistranslations in the English version) and very solid voice acting make this a great story to experience.  Other good points: Understated but well composed music. Very nicely drawn background art. Good puzzle quality   Things to make you think twice before buying: It is a bit too expensive given its length (10 hours gameplay at a guess). I don't feel ripped off but try to get it in a sale. The style of character artwork is a bad choice. The game's material has moments that are quite dark but the character faces are Disney cartoon.  Character walking speed can be slow although double clicking to quick-travel between screens makes this much less of a problem.  In short this is a good story well told with challenging puzzles. Maybe a little too expensive at £15.

This is a really hard one to make up my mind about. To start with I really liked it, and then as the differences between this and other TMs became apparent I felt increasingly less pleased with it.  Those points have been discussed elsewhere by others, so I'll not add to that. Except to say that I did start to make a list of 'pros' and 'cons' comparing this with TM2, and quickly found 8 plus points and 21 negatives. (Although quite a few of the latter turn out to be things which have been community-provided for TM2; not in the original implementation. Although the points still stand, as I can't see the community getting behind Turbo in the same way.)  But in the end I've decided it's a caveated 'Yes' for me.  The thing is; I think it's actually a pretty good game in its own right. And if you completely ignore the orignal TMs for whatever reason, there really isn't anything else quite like it out there afaik. It certainly has that 'just one more go; I've almost got it now. And that last bad time was all entirely my own fault' feel to it.   If you are new to TM, and/or want an accessible twitch-racer that's fresher and more 'this gen' than the (now a bit tired-looking) other TM games, then this may well be for you. It really is TM meets the console generation. And depending what you are looking for, that might not entirely be a bad thing.

This is a decent game, but it is by no means a great game. Whilst it offers a campaign, a snapmap mode, and multiplayer, multiplayer is completely dead in Australia, and snapmap isn't very exciting.   For those interested in DOOM for its campaign, you'll love it if you like to shoot things over, and over, and over. DOOM is essentially the same variety of enemy types, thrown at you again and again as you enter room after room. The story is near nonexistent; close the hell portal on Mars. Though, I'm sure most people won't play DOOM because of its story.   The graphics and performance are pretty solid - the game is very fast paced. I didn't mind DOOM, but I have to say this is one of the most repetitive games ever. I loved ID software's Quake and have played all of them, and Unreal Tournament as well. That said, DOOM pales in comparison to these.   You get a variety of weapons and acquire all of them at the halfway point of the game, as well as the runes. Whilst they do spice up the combat a bit, the pacing of the game is quite poor. It starts off very slow, but as it develops, battles become much more intense. The developers basically throw more enemies at you in waves. In rooms.   I really can't stress the repetitive nature of this game enough. You clear wave after wave of enemies in spacious rooms every level, and it became really stale for me towards the game's end. Again, there's also no player base anymore. Give this game a rent if you want to try the campaign. Is it worth a buy though? I'd say, when the game was initially released, for sure. Now that there are better FPS's out there, save your money.

review_text: The story is strong, especially when played on mature mode. This game... I played the prologue because it was free - I mean, how can you go wrong? Ok... I've played a few free VN here and they can go VERY wrong. This one went pretty darn right. I was intrigued by the story, girl ends up with magic power? Yeeeah, I'm in.  The free bit isn't obviously the full game, it's more like a teaser - you get the back story, and you get juuuuust a teeny bit of each of the guys to get you interested and hooked ;) - it works. I am not a romancey person, I usually want to roll my eyes so hard they'll pop out of my skull at any mushy stuff (cold hearted jaded B here? Yes, yes I am LOL). But aaaaaanyway, I played it and the story was interesting enough I though 'why not' and I bought Shou and Tatsuya's routes (and Shinji's in a hot second as soon as it was released - as I will do for Takumi and Hikaru). I LOVE this game. I've been recording my play through for my YouTube channel and I am loving every second - I play to play through all the routes at least once (and maybe try for all the endings after I'm finished with my LP).  Highly reccomend this game - the characters are great, the story is interesting, and each guy and path is unique so playing each doesn't feel like you're repeating anything. Even the 'villans' are intesting (while also being total creepers that you can't help but love for the fact that they ARE creepers). The artwork is great also, if you can't tell from the previews. Plus, you lose nothing by playing the prolouge for free so... you know... worth it.

The only thing I'd change about it is, some puzzles require literally split second timing. That aside, I'd say that Limbo was definitely worth the pocket change I used to buy it on Steam.

Terraria, like Minecraft, is about finding resources and crafting stuff. But it adds adventuring elements that MC doesn't have. Each trip into a new cave in Terraria is just plain fun. I was buzzing on this game for my first few hours.  But soon, I was loaded with inventory that I may or may not need. And then I realized that most of this game is exactly like the parts of Minecraft that I hate: building and maintaining a house, and crafting junk so you can get more junk and craft more junk. The adventuring part fizzles and you feel like a five-and-dime store.   Meanwhile, you're spending more time in the Terraria Wiki and Youtube trying to figure out what to make or do than you're spending in the actual game. Without finding a beginner’s guide, Terraria is brutally difficult: fight monsters, fall down a pit, die in the dark, repeat.   I'd prefer a similar game that gently nudges me into making more new stuff as I go along. After 5 hours of Terraria, I'm all Minecrafted out. Enough.

The Witness is an open world puzzle game. Weird combination, huh? That concept actually works rather well. You work on a puzzle for a while, can't figure it out, and instead just move on to another part of the island. This solves the everlasting puzzle game problem of not even wanting to start it up if you're stuck. The (many!) puzzles are singular screens which have you drawing a line across a grid. A very basic concept. And with that very basic concept The Witness does absolutely everything it possibly could. It will introduce lots of new rules, need you to take the surroundings of the puzzle into account and even have you taking notes in real life. 'Then why are people complaining that the puzzles are too similar?' you ask. Well, that kind of line puzzle is all there is. The Witness really needs to be praised for how much it does with the limitations it set itself but it never answers the question why it limited itself so much in the first place. The island is huge and even 5 or 6 hours into the game you will still find yourself running into new places you haven't seen before. And not because you just unlocked them, it just actually has that much content. Content made out of puzzles that is, this game really is for the absolute puzzle game buff who wants nothing else out of it. It also likes having some big fps drops and on some graphics cards it gets occasional random crashes but it's biggest downside remains its price! I don't feel like paying 37€ for a collection of puzzles and a pretty island (did i mention this is one of the prettiest games i've seen in a long time?) to walk around on is worth it. I do however recommend buying it if you're really into puzzle games AND you can get it while it's on sale.

But why I'm still playing this game? Oh, it's too late... No, just a minute more... 8/10.  Resume. Good hack-n-slash RPG, pretty simple in graphics, but nice night-tale. 7/10.

Disclaimer: I primarily played this game with a non steam copy.  Jesus, this one brings back some memories. I remember actually being on the forums as this game progressed. I remember some of the EA forum members as well and all the drama that happened both from the forum members and the EA staff, like EAApoc being 'kidnapped' by Nod to keep the third faction a secret, thus mysteriously EALAPredator made an appearance to take his place and he totally isn't the same guy behind the username.   Regardless, I was too poor at the time to buy the game on release. I got it a bit later when it was $30 to buy the special edition thingy that came both with this and Kane's Wrath.   With that said, I hold Command & Conquer series very dear to my heart. Red Alert 2 was probably one of my favorite games of all time for a long time... Hell, it probably still is. However, Command & Conquer Generals was a huge slap in the face to fans of the series. Command & Conquer Generals is a great strategy game, but it's an insult to the C&C fanbase to call Generals a Command & Conquer game, an even bigger insult to claim the whole series connects to Generals somehow (I bet EA doesn't make those claims anymore).   EA has a history of not doing Command & Conquer series justice, especially today. I will say that this game, as well Kanes Wrath, is probably the LAST true Command & Conquer game with quality gameplay. The game is great.   For any strategy gamer that is unfamiliar with C&C base building, it's simple. You have different factory types, just like any other strategy game, but you do not need any form of constructor unit to build structures. You have a construction yard, you build around the construction yard, you can no longer build structures if you lose said construction yard, etc. All the newer C&C games don't implement this any longer, which is a shame because that is what made this series so special. There is only a small handful that implements RTS in such a way, so it's strange for them to basically change the formula from being unique to being generic.   With that ramble nonsense said, this game has proper C&C base building and it works great. It's fluent, it's easy to understand, it's great. What makes this different from the old school C&C games is it implements upgrades in a way similar to C&C Generals, which is great because it's much more flexible and clean unlike the older C&C titles that implements upgrades.   The resources gathered in this game comes from a source that is typically never ending, making it necessary to take over strategic points on the battlefield. The maps design that comes with the game is generally well crafted, making it possible to attack enemies by multiple points. The great thing is how many maps you can download from the community using the 'World Builder' tool, which is a map editor/creator. The only bad thing is how this game doesn't use workshop like it easily could.   The single player campaign is great. It's not too cheesy, it's just good quality B-Movie material. The game has great live action scenes, which the C&C series is famous for, that tell a great story in a creative way. They're not directly telling a story, you are told a story by being given orders and playing the missions. Rarely, there's an exception to this chemistry, but it's a good system that worked well for all the other C&C games and is oddly pretty unique to C&C how it's implemented.   Online is dead. However, when it was alive, it was good. You can still play LAN, which I'm sure you're aware can be used through programs like Tunggle or Hamachi. The gameplay is great with other players.  The AI in the game is greatly challenging. It's fun to play against both humans and computers alike.   Overall, the game is fantastic. Kane's Wrath is a great addon to consider if you enjoyed this one.   Pros- Great single player Simplistic and easy to learn gameplay, but rewarding to master fast paced, fluent, and the battles are overwhelmingly satisfying great variety in maps, especially with community based maps  Cons- Not a whole lot of variety for playable faction choices such as sub factions (this is addressed in Kane's Wrath expansion) Online is dead, you'll have to use LAN $15 is pretty high for a game of this nature and age Replayability for singleplayer is not overwhelmingly high  8/10

I'd like to preface this by saying this game does have potential. It just needs a bit of fleshing out.  There isn't much substance to the game at current; it lacks any big RPG elements that keep you hooked to other games of this genre. It's just running around killing zombies and ♥♥♥♥, as if that isn't commonplace enough. So this game tries to shine in other areas.  The humor, at times, can be quite witty. The origin of the zombies is especially funny and the pre-level dialogue definitely gave me a few hearty chuckles.  However, the main character (aptly named 'Dude') is often set up in situations reminiscient of third grade lunchtable comedy. You know, when you have to basically set up the scene with your words and then pantomime everything. (The sandwich level cutscene definitely shows this)  It's like saying, 'Imagine I'm in a bulldozer,' then acting like you crash through the wall of the teacher's lounge to create comedy. It doesn't work. It's a juvenile sense of humor that I hate.  This is especially disappointing because the maker of this game obviously has the capacity to make some funny ♥♥♥♥, the delivery is just flat-out bad. The voice acting is, to the trailer's acknowledgement, also bad, which totally killed some of the funnier parts of the cutscenes. There are a few redeeming moments, but those are sparse throughout the 100 minutes it took me to beat the story mode. If you want a really funny, cheap game, you're better off playing Dungeons of Dredmor.  The one redeeming factor of this game is its soundtrack, one of the best I've ever heard. This game's music is definitely on par with something like Hotline Miami. If, despite my reccomendation, you still buy the game, get the soundtrack with it.  If you're really willing to shell out $5 to fulfill some fetish of yours, just buy the soundtrack available on Bandcamp. It's a better use of your money than playing through this game.

TL:DR; A solid 10/10 from me. Well worth the asking price for a couple of hours of good, clean, psychological horror fun.  I really enjoyed this game. Good voice acting from everyone involved (though Jim Sterling's voice doesn't quite fit the look of the character he plays, he does a good job regardless), nice artstyle, puzzles that don't require copious amounts of psychotropic drugs to understand, and intuitive, no-nonsense point-and-click controls. Updated Review 9/25/15  After my 3rd time playing the game, wanted to update some items.   Pros - * Graphics Now that they've released like 38 patches, the game looks pretty good and runs great. Running the game in 3840x2400 and it is beautiful. (will post system specs at the bottom) * I like the upgrade system so that you can gradually upgrade your assassin, both with skills, and the his gear / outfits / weapons.   Cons -  Everything else. The biggest culprit is the combat system of the game. They've removed everything that made the previous iterations great. I'm a huge AC fan and have been since the series was released, but this game is just awful. I understand that they wanted to make the game more difficult, but the way they went about it was about the worst way they could have done it. Removed counter kills, human shields, disarms, the ability to pick up weapons. They've given every enemy on the battlefield a firearm and to reiterate they removed human shields. Human shields were put in to AC3 and AC4 for the specific purpose of giving you a viable and sensible way to engage enemies with firearms at close range and survive. Absolutely ludicrous by the developers. The combat system alone is so awful that I absolutely cannot recommend the game based on that factor alone. It is THAT bad.   Furthermore, the camera system during combat makes it almost impossible to see the enemies as they are engaging you, especially in tight quarters. You will most often just end up dropping smoke bombs left and right so you don't end up dead. When you attempt to parry, sometimes it works, and sometimes it doesn't, even if your timing is perfect. If they wanted to make the game more difficult, they can simply design various enemy classes where certain types of enemies have to be handled differently (break defense, tripping the enemy from behind, etc.) - see the Batman Arkham series' virtually perfect combat system for my meaning. But don't take away what's made the whole franchise great - good mission design, great combat system and great story.  Clunky controls - both climbing, and the aforementioned combat. When I press a button it takes a second or two for Arno to respond and do what he should. All previous AC games were not like this - you pushed a button, and your character responded immediately. I'm not sure if this is an engine issue with the game, or if they purposely designed it this way, but the controls need to be much smoother and much more responsive.   Bad / Nonexistent present day story. I'll re-iterate from some of my previous AC game reviews - killing Desmond off was about the dumbest thing they could've done. The present day story is now boring, and as a result gives you as the player no anchor point to understand what the heck is going on or what the purpose of going back in time. When Desmond was there you were able to have more in-depth conversations with your cohorts (Shawn, Lucy, Rebecca, etc.). The earlier games did a great job in this area and developed a solid, detailed, coherent story.  Poor character development. This is an area Ubisoft has been failing in since AC3. By rushing through the time periods they are not leaving themselves much time to properly develop each main character. Connor, Edward, Shay, Arno, they rush through their lives so quickly that they really don't have time to develop them. By the time you're just starting to get to know the character, they've moved on and are ending the game only to move on to another character - this is why Ezio was loved so much - not only was AC2 just a magical game and a solid 10/10 in every measurable category, but they developed his character - everything he did, every upgrade he got for his equipment, every move he learned, everything was explained and made sense. They should try to stay in a given time period for 2-3 games to properly develop a character and close out his life as well as to show proper chronological story-telling from one assassin to the next, especially since they share the same genetic line.   Unexplained story happenings - I don't want to spoil anything, so I'll just say at the end of the game you come into contact with certain artifacts, the origins of which are never explained - Ubisoft has been doing a terribly poor job of explaining things in the story and developing it properly like they did through the first 3 or 4 games.   I absolutely cannot recommend this game to anyone. AC series aside, I'm not sure if I've ever played a game I disliked more than this one. I'm not sure I'd recommend this game even if it was just 5 dollars. It's that bad. Save yourself some cash and steer clear. The developers succeeded in making combat more difficult. They also succeeded in making the game not the least bit enjoyable to play. A new development team is needed to take over the franchise. Whoever worked on AC2, ACB, ACR, and AC4 need to be working on Unity's successor. Whoever the game director was for AC Unity should be doing QA only going forward.
