(I have mostly played this game in offline mode, that's why the review only says I've played the game for a minute or something.)  The awesome: -Storyline, lore, and amount of content is well-thought out and I can definitely tell that's where the bulk of the effort was. -Non-fixed class system - build your character however you like. Of course it means that it's easier to make an inviable character, but with great power comes great responsibility. -Your squad has personalities and you can talk to them. If you liked the recruitable teams in Dragon Age or Mass Effect, you will also like your Shadowrun squad.  The good: -Nonlinear - some missions have several paths through them, and there is a central hub where you can take your choice of missions. Your choices have consequences on the storyline, for people who like continuity and immersion. -Techno/cyberpunk soundtrack reminiscent of Deus Ex. -Wide variety of items, some with unique powers and skills.  The bad: -The store page lied about the amount of disk space required - it requires over 6 GB, much more than the listed 2 GB -Shadowrun attempts to be overly dark/gritty/mature when it doesn't need to be. Are the devs going for an ESRB M rating or something? -Shadowrun attempts to be somewhere between a near pen-and-paper RPG and a near tactical/action RPG - in practice ending up at a bad spot in between those and succeeding at neither. Skill and attribute checks are binary - for example 'if you have this conversation skill or at least this number in a skill, go ahead, otherwise find another way around' instead of a d20 system where you have Difficulty Class checks - an unskilled character might luck out on rolling a 20. On the other hand, the turn-based combat doesn't fit tactical action very well - cover is usually unreliable and there aren't enough indicators of where you would be covered from in a given location. Also if you misclick, there's no 'revert move' button - the most you can do is wait 20 seconds to reload your save (see below). -When you get codes for locks, terminals, and doors, the game doesn't automatically display them while you are interacting with the lock/terminal/door. This would be a very helpful feature to have (Deus Ex: Human Revolution has it. The original Deus Ex doesn't, but that's OK because it has a UI that actually responds when you click on it). -Opaque leveling system. There's no visible experience meter, recruitable character level-ups are unpredictable, and skill points are earned from mission objectives instead of from enemies. Deus Ex: Human Revolution had a much better experience point system balancing rewards for defeating enemies (particularly in skillful ways) with rewarding alternative solutions (such as crawling in air vents or stealth)  The intolerable: -The entire game lags badly. My computer isn't a power-gaming machine, but it handles big budget/big publisher premium games like Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim, Mass Effect 2, and Deus Ex: Human Revolution just fine. On the other hand, Shadowrun: Dragonfall lags. Really badly. On par with a bad Ubisoft console port to PC. As in taking 12 seconds just to exit the game normally, 20-24 seconds to load a game (from a solid-state disk). The game sometimes just slows down to 8 fps randomly and frequently. Combat gets boring having to watch characters run to various places and there's no 'skip animations'. -The UI is crap. There's no documentation or list of keyboard shortcuts. Bringing up a menu forces a 2 second wait for the menu to pop out instead of actually just giving me the darn menu when I ask for it. (and yes, the UI lags as badly as the rest of the game) Sometimes in combat, things that you may need to click on randomly become unclickable. I get the impression that Shadowrun: Dragonfall was originally built for tablets and then given a low-quality, no-optimization port to PC. -The game sometimes crashes (and screws up my resolution). There was also one instance where the AI got stuck in an infinite loop while taking its turn and I had to reload my game. -Skills, abilities, and mechanics are generally poorly documented in-game. Special attacks have no indicator of how many action points they consume. When buying abilities and items in a shop, the descriptions often run out of space and there's no way to closely examine an item before buying it. Some items give extra abilities and skills, yet there's no way to tell what you're getting short of actually bringing them into a fight.  Overall: Shadowrun: Dragonfall is a diamond that's so rough that you need to handle it with gloves to avoid getting cut. There's a lot of good in it, but it's held back and ruined by numerous game-breaking problems. It's a shame to have to give a game that could have been excellent a thumbs-down, but there are just too many design problems, technical faults, and other flaws to recommend this. It's also a shame that Steam's review system forces you to give a thumbs-up or thumbs-down with nothing in the middle, because this game deserves a 'Mixed' review more than almost any other game I know of.   Should you get this game? -If you're a big fan of the Shadowrun world, enjoy immersive RPGs where your choices matter, or RNG-heavy RPGs (and also have a high tolerance for technical problems and overly slow games), then I would suggest that you buy it at a deep discount (at least 2/3 off the regular price). Otherwise, it isn't worth your time or your money. -If you're just looking for a cyberpunk-themed game (not specifically Shadowrun), then just get Deus Ex... because Shadowrun is like buying an orange soda and getting seltzer water with a hint of lemon and lime peels instead.

app_name: Nameless ~The one thing you must recall~
review_text: This game is a romance sim with a wonderful story that keeps you wanting to know what happens next. I have played and watched other people play romance games like this before, but most of them are different in that they have a mechanic where you raise a stat (Like intelligence or beauty) in order to progress with your love interest. Even though I liked these games too, that mechanic felt repetitive and tedious at times, because I just wanted to find out what would happen next in the story. Nameless, however, does not use this mechanic. The game progresses based entirely on your dialogue choices. How you choose to speak and act towards the other characters will affect how the story unfolds.   I have only one real negative critique of the game, which is that it could use a patch to fix the spelling and grammatical errors in the translation. If you are someone who absolutely cannot stand misspellings and typos, then you may not want to pick up the game until those are fixed. However, if you can look past the errors (which probably occurred during translation), then you will find a really great story, which is the main reason why I recommend this game.   The story and characters are very strong. The voice acting is done well, and it gives you a sense of each character's personality. The characters feel three-dimensional, and they are very likeable across the board. I was especially happy that the main character has a real personality and sense of humor, unlike some romance sim main characters, who feel rather flat and colorless. The romantic interests also have very interesting personalities, motivations, and stories of their own, which has kept me wanting to play through until I have seen every piece of the story. I have not played through every path yet. However, the stories and characters in Nameless are so intriguing that I plan to try to get every ending, good and bad. The creators of Nameless have made a game that has a story which is sweet, funny, sad, and even sometimes terrifying, and I heartily recommend it.

I have played this game series for well..... Years, I probably have my old Shogun: Total War cd-rom disk lying around somewhere so I can say without doubt this is why I have begun to hate the modern versions of the Total War series. The problem was though I couldn't put my finger on exactly what was wrong and after poking and prodding both the A.I and the stats based mechanics this is the conclusion I have come to.  The A.I in this game as well as the brilliant rules that have been set out by Creative Assembly to make realistic combat have been utterly broken.  I'll start by explaining the mechanics, you have morale in this game, by that I mean, they've assigned a number that reacts to whether you are flanked, hit from behind and so on or better yet if you're troops are bombarded with artillery and your forces will run away if they're scared enough. However, rather than making morale the main part of the game as they did with my favourite game of the series Rome: Total War where even the mightiest veteran units could fall with a well placed cavalry charge or ambush. You are now forced to spam recruit units all the time and they always have to be 'the most powerful' because in the newest games statistics matter and not strategy.  When comparing the older games with the new ones you will see exactly what I mean, no longer can you hold off the evil A.I that tries to attack you with large numbers with carefully organised peasant ashigaru, no, you're forced to spam samurai and make sure they have enough experience points to combat the blatantly cheating A.I within the game ( which I will get to next ) even if you managed to completely outwit the A.I, having them chase you all over the battlefield , picking them off bit by bit, unless you do it absolutely perfect, your troops will run away. So they bothered to put ♥♥♥♥ing morale in as long as it benefits the A.I number spam tactics by which I mean if there's a huge force regardless of what you do your troops will still run the ♥♥♥♥ away after barely having fought which gives you an automatic defeat. Oh and for those idiots who will defend this, this was never so bad in Rome: Total War or Shogun: Total War, you can hold off entire hordes of ghauls if you organise right in the older games but in the recent games it's all about unit spamming.  Now I could go into detail about the other stats but since creative assembly haven't bothered to balance them at all I'll only give a brief summary, the Ashigaru units aside from maybe bows are pretty ♥♥♥♥ing useless ( good luck using them in multiplayer that ♥♥♥♥ is even more imbalanced and broken than the campaign, I don't know how they managed to make it all worse but they did ) the units seem to be separated in to tiers. The peasant Ashigaru units are obviously Tier 1, then there are sort of base samurai units at Tier 2, then there are upgraded versions of these samurai that require rare resources at Tier 3 and then there are these ridiculously powerful units that can only be acquired getting these unique resources that are almost only at the western part of the map. I'm not even talking about them being slightly more powerful there are youtube videos you can look up of these stupid units taking on several units without even doing anything, it's utter ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t.   https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mUJNKDGW2so  - Even provided a link just in case rabid fanbois of this series try to claim I'm ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥tting, because they're also part of the problem of why this game series is sucking right now  Now you're thinking aren't you? So what? Big deal, there's a clan there called Shimazu that I can pick and I'll just hog all the resources to myself, there's nothing wrong with that. Well there's something very wrong with that, why should you have to cut yourself off from game content just because of a developer decision? Game content which you actually paid good money for? Because it's not like anything that creative assembly is asking for is cheap with the main games, this is a blatant fault of the games developers and is pisses me off when people try to blame the gamers for broken mechanics.  The most annoying problem of all however within the Shogun 2 series, is not the non-existent balancing of units. Oh no, it gets much worse in the campaign when you actually look at how you're fellow A.I daimyo act. Creative Assembly have managed to make diplomacy completely irrelevant and the fanbois of this series will tell you it's because it's 'Total War' and you're not supposed to have allies I haven't even bothered looking at the official forums for years but I bet you can see them still parroting this old line. See here's the problem, the total war series was never about 'just' war, it's about strategy and tactics, it's a strategy game, not only that it's had many historical settings where the various factions you play had steadfast allies for years. The problem in Shogun 2 is that the A.I you're faced with so vicious and backstabbing you have absolutely no choice but to wipe them out completely, no longer can you forge a good relationship with an ally long term who doesn't have any resources you want, they will either backstab you or you have to take over their provinces yourself.   Even vassals which you get in this game are utterly useless, for those who don't know, you sometimes get an option when conquering a province to create a vassal clan. The idea is that you create an A.I partner for yourself to manage the province and be a real ally for you in the war. However they are just as vicious and backstabbing as the main A.I in the game and will gladly betray you, the only difference is they're completely weak and just not as aggressive towards their counterparts compared to the way they act to you. So even if you give them all the money in the world and have an entire army guarding their stronghold they barely do anything to attack the enemy. A theory I have is that the A.I is capable of reading the amount of troops you have recruited in your clan so the vassal is constantly reading that the enemy outnumbers them so it just hides in their only stronghold and never uses them. The reason I know this is because the moment you beat the crap out of a large enemy force in real time the A.I reads that their army has been destroyed and immediately hides any small armies it has in their stronghold as well.  While there are mods you can download to lessen the affects of this, in the end all the clans will declare war on you regardless there's this pretty annoying mechanic that exists across all the Shogun 2 games called realm divide. While it's not necessarily called that in other games it all acts the same way. As you progress and expand the A.I will become more and more aggressive now, this wouldn't be so bad if it were a case of them warring amongst themselves as well. However in all the games I have played they have pretty much all decided to pick a fight with the player specifically! What is the point of this? There's no strategy involved, it doesn't actually make the game more challenging, all it does it become incredibly annoying because you're constantly having to micro-manage several fronts and since you can't enlist the help of A.I in this which would be incredibly useful you have to either put up with it or summon the patience of a buddhist monk and build up your forces as much as you can while beating back lots and lots of enemies. For people who haven't played the total war series.  I'm actually running out of characters now so I'll be brief with this one, the A.I cheats, badly, I don't understand how they could ♥♥♥♥ up game difficulty like this but the A.I manages to magically spawn stacks of high tier samurai even if they only have one province. The main culprits if you want to test this are the Ikko Ikki, Takeda and at times Hattori or Shimazu.  Fanbois who actually praise this game series as it is now should be ashamed of themselves.

This world, is amazing. It is hard to talk about this game without spoiling too much, but it definitely went viral for a reason.  The music, characters, and bullet hell turn based combat system are amazing and I would easily play this game for those reasons alone. But what really makes this game shine is its story, and how the game changes according to how you play it.  There are two major paths, Pacifist or Genocide. They feel COMPLETELEY different, changing events and the world drastically. The game truly makes you feel the path you are taking. Doing a Genocide run after the Pacifist path is a trip, and may not be for the faint of heart.  Note that the game knows what you did, even if you load a previous save.  I loved playing this game, and I would highly recommend it. Definitely go for the extreme endings to get a true feel for it. There are a few spoiler free guides that you can use to get a gist of each path if you need.

GooCubelets 2 manages to somehow be worse than its predecessor. It suffers from all the same graphical and design flaws, but also adds in hefty amounts of artificial difficulty.  In GooCubelets 2, you have to move goo blocks to color-coded destinations while avoiding pits and other obstacles. Like the first game, there are levels where you have to move quickly over short gaps and ones where you control multiple blocks at once. However, GC 2 adds in a new 'black ink' block that instantly fails you if you move any block to a space adjacent to it. The game also has 'control reversal' spaces and colored buttons that destroy random platforms.  These new mechanics could've been implemented well, but their execution is absolutely horrendus. For starters, the kill spaces that the black blocks have are barely labeled if at all. I found myself accidently moving into one without noticing numerous times. In addition, the camera angle makes depth perception difficult, so I always had to move slowly behind these blocks.  The control reversal spaces are placed haphazardly throughout every level after they're introduced. There's little rhyme or reason as to their locations - they merely exist to throw you off. These killed me more than any other thing in the game.  The colored buttons are okay at first - just don't move onto any. That strategy works up to a certain point, after which the game requires you to press specific buttons to destroy specific platforms. It doesn't indicate which button should be pressed - it leaves the player to 'discover' which one through repeated trial and error.  Graphically, GC 2 is somehow worse than the original. There's still a white floor overlapping a white background, and no shadows or any sense of depth, but now non-rectangular grid patterns that make determining distance nearly impossible. Again, this design choice could've been done well, but the developers failed miserably.  Like the original, the final puzzle    is a giant mess: it's gigantic, and one tiny mistake means you must redo the whole thing. However, unlike the original, you must control two blocks across platforms while dodging all the terrible new mechanics. I finished the whole game up to here, then gave up after several tries. I even tried a walkthrough, but still failed, not even passing the first big chunk of the level.     Overall, GooCubelets 2 is an absolutely terrible game, and the price doesn't make it any better. This should be a free flash game (but even that wouldn't save it). The only saving point is that there are trading cards that sell decently. Unless you can get this game for less than 10 cents, avoid it like the plague.

Long review ahead, but I promise it's worth the read!  This was one of the times I pre-ordered a game solely based on its trailer (and it will probably be the last time).  The Final Station's trailer really impressed me with its music and atmosphere, the retro-style graphics and the gameplay it presented. Looked quite simplistic and a bit melancholic. that is exactly what I was looking for.  I hoped to be tought how to play the game, advance through better weapons and better zombies as time moves on and experience the story of this game in the manner it was presented in the trailer. I expected the gameplay to be a bit hectic and varied, how it commonly is with retro shooters. The train segment looked fun as well, a bit of micromanaging in between - sounded good to me.  The big problem is: I was left sorely disappointed. Let me tell you why.  This game is not at all what I hoped it was going to be. First of all, you do not get tought any controls from the get go. There is a little tutorial, but if you don't look up the controls yourself at some point in the game, you won't get to know em. This is a very minor thing to be honest, but it confused me a bit. Why drop me in a tutorial where you could go through every single control option perfectly... and just not do that? Doesn't make sense to me. This theme carries on to your first actual train ride, where you are gives a bunch of things to keep track of without being told how to. Yeah, you can figure it out yourself quite easily, but again it's extremely weird that the opportunity to at least *label* the options was not taken. Also the status of the passengers isn't labeled either, so you have to figure out yourself that this white drop means 'bleeding' and that the guy will take continuous damage over time until he dies.   But anyways, these are smaller things that I just found odd. My main two points of critique are a bit heavier. First point: the way of storytelling. You are just dropped in there with no context, no idea who you are, where you are, what you are doing and so on. The story just kinda unfolds and you have to roll with it because you have no details at all to cling to. Everything is just 'given'. Like you are playing a video game adaption to a comic book, where it is expected that you already know about half the story. The way it is conveyed didn't appeal to me either. Just a bunch of rambling from the train guests that you *miss anyways* if you have to micromanage something in the front part of the train. the speech bubbles just disappear. Really odd design choice again. Also there are a lot of missed opportunities along the way. Why can I only talk to *this* person and not the others? I wanna know more! Why does my character never reply at all? I mean - he does reply, but for some reason it is not shown on screen so I have no clue what he said. Well, sometimes he makes a little '...' speech bubble, other times he doesn't for some reason, the result stays the same. What put me off even more were the two situations I came across, where you were actually able to make a response choice. That only happens during little in-between text chat segments when you are actually able to see your own answers. The first one was a choice between '...' and 'Thank you!' which I am sure had no effect on the story prograssion at all and the second one was a choice between exactly one option. I have no clue why they even made that a 'choice' you had literally no alternatives and usually the character talks by himself anyways. All of this felt weird and extremely inconsistent.  Second point: The actual gameplay. It's just a bit... boring. Fighting zombies gets repetetive *really* quickly and is just weird sometimes. Melee hits don't connect, bullets seem to not deal damage - maybe I just suck, but there were situations where I really felt like something was off. Anyways, the fights get repetetive really soon and even adding new zombies or a new gun later doesn't get that stale taste out of my mouth. The whole 'limited ammo' aspect is also not pushed proberly either as you do run out of ammo - yes - but melee combat is just so chunky and a bad alternative usually. It doesn't really depend on skill, it just depends on which zombies are left after you ran out. For example you are rarely given any means of defense against sprinting zombies. Combat doesn't feel good at all.   Apart from that I really dislike that you cannot get any information on cities further down the road or get info on your next objective / place to drop people off (so you could calculate if its worth keeping that bleeding dude alive or not). Again a really odd choice.  So, to conclude, that is my opinion. I'm certain a bit of the sour aftertaste came from my high expectations and the good trailer, but surely not all of it. I am just really disappointed. I *wanted* to like this game so much, but I just can't. The fights feel stale, the ambience doesn't really get me at all, Communication an interaction is really unappealing and inconsistent.  Overall this game had a ton of good ideas, even in a genre that is as oversaturated as zombie shooters, but it kinda failed to deliver. As I said, I really wanted to like it, and I don't want to trash-talk it in any way, but I really can't see much to praise except for the absolutely stellar soundtrack.  You have to decide for yourself if you want to buy it. I'm sure some people could really enjoy it. But i can't and maybe I can make you reconsider your choice.

quick review: rest in pieces, very tepid recommendation for american made SF game  What made SF3 so great was all completely removed for SF4, and in lieu of parry, parry, punish we then had jab jab focus backdash. I know I'm oversimplifying things here, but I always felt SF3 (and eventually my all time love, 3S) moved passed the clunky controls of SF2 and Hyper fighting and all that towards something that was more active, more offensive, more about feeling that zing you get from being aggressive and being rewarded for it. Parry made everything safe, yet not. A mechanic that gave even the ♥♥♥♥♥iest characters a fighting chance but not one that could be over abused (the basic rock x paper x scissors mindset was still the real meta) — grabbing now leading to more offensive ideas instead of outright wrecking your opponent (SF2). SF4 did nothing to tell the player how to establish advantages with safe-jumps, how mechanics like option-selects work, or anything -- the gameplay was dumbed down but somehow the mechanics were re-mystifyed to like an Alpha 2 level (where the OG in the arcade would woop you with X-ism combos) and everything else was like one of those big grids with conditional statements caught between arrows, all of them in this game pointing toward BACKDASH, or if you had meter, reversal, focus cancel THEN back dash. Ugh. I guess that's all right but now that we're into Ultra territory (with all most 50 characters in a game who at Capcops is even THINKING 'balance' anymore?) and G4WL now replaced with laggy steamworks, I'll play something else. What I'm wishing for Ultra (I've yet to play it) is that they buff things quite a bit and then ease them back down with small patches. SFxT (a *far* superior game to any SF4 incarnation) did this beautifully but was sadly canned by the FGC because of Capcops decision to release a tamer product and then re-release the game with what should've been the standard all along --- guess right twice (or thrice), have perfect execution, and end a round. Roshambo'ing on the playground never needed a patch to its rules. Seems Capcom lost sight of their own directive long ago. We'll see.

I have no idea why this game's getting so much positive reviews. I'm really thinking that dev has bought some reviews for his title.  The game is really simple: a top-down shooter, where you control a space ship that's constantly shooting stuff in front of itself. You control the aim with mouse and the ship with WASD. Ship can sustain up to 5 hits before it dies. There are power-ups (changing your fire-mode for some few seconds and health pick-ups).  I've got all the achievements in my first session, and it took me literally 10 minutes. What else is there to do in this game? There is no progression whatsoever, there's no co-op, there's no missions/challenges/anything.  There are high scores. there are 2 game options (regular and 'no light' one), there are 3 ships. That's about it, the whole game.  Granted, it's cheap as hell, but why would you buy a game that you won't play more than 10 minutes?

To all the Left 4 Dead 1 fans, get this. This has Left 4 Dead 1 in it ironically.  This game is the sequel to Left 4 Dead 1. I remember the first time Left 4 Dead came out. Played more than 1000 hours on xbox and now a bit more than 214ish hours on PC for Left 4 Dead 2. I still sort of play Left 4 Dead.  Another time to remember is when Left 4 Dead 2 first came out. A large group of gamers complained that the game is not 'scary'. That 'the original one is better and had a better atmosphere'.   Of course there is the racism but let us skip that part, yes?  Anyways, this game added more content to the L4D world. Gamers were satisfied with the chainsaws, new infected, more diversity of weapons to choose from, and melee. That's right. Melee. One of the most satisfying things to do in a zombie apocalypse.   Introducing 4 new characters: Brave Rochelle, Hungry-Hungry Coach, Goofy Ellis, and Spoiled Nick, the game happens to be great no matter how many haters hated it. Also introducing the new weather changes which impacted the gamer's survival in this shooter game. Gone was the night. Then comes the day and hellish storms.   This game is worth it. Especially when it is on sale. Under $10 for Left 4 Dead 2 AND Left 4 Dead 1? I'm in!  Another addition to having this game on PC are the modded servers. Not everyone likes it due to its confusing mechanics and overpowered infected. But it is an excellent challenge for the gamers who find that killing noobs or pros in a different way is satisfying.  In conclusion, this game has done wonders for the community and the loyal Left 4 Dead Fans. Enjoy it or Watch the game plays. Thank you. :)

'Don't Starve' not only brings the challenge of surviving in the wilderness (with the realisticness of losing sanity), but there are plenty of supernatural beasts and items for you to fight, discover, and create! Not only that, but the game brings in quite a detailed backstory if you take the time to figure it out.   In 'Don't Starve', there's quite the range of playable characters too! Starting off with a scientist named Wilson who was brought into the dimension when his works overall failed. I haven't had any issues with game performance so far at all, and I have a system far from the best. Running FX 8350 CPU (OC only to 4.3), R9 270x gpu, 16 gb ram. That being said most of my settings are just on High, some a little more. I am also just running with most settings at high, and not pushing it too much yet.  Definitely has the Crysis feel to it graphically. Some of the texures could use improvement, but overall are well done.  The guns seem a bit clunky as others have mentioned. They are not bad, just not great. It does seem that you can steady all guns, even pistols which makes a big difference. I do love the ability to mod the hell out of the guns, this should hold some promise. This gives you the ability to change your strategy on the fly which can be a very good thing.  Voice acting I feel is passable. I would say maybe 10%-20% above par, but certainly didn't draw me in quite the way it could have. They make their point, but they don't give me an adrenalin rush of fight or flight.   My biggest annoyance so far, and there have been few, is that when you shoot the enemies, they just seem to absorb it. I personally would like to see them falter a bit, show something that I just nailed him. I want to know that they felt it, I want to know that they are hurting...maybe I ask too much.   Overall I think that I will enjoy it. I am curious as to the amount of play looting and crafting has on the game, but with an open world, I think I will have gotten my money worth. If I find glaring issues, I will ammend.

Not only did I want to like this game, I TRIED, I REALLY REALLY tried to like it. I love Stardock and Sins of a Solar Empire is one of my favorite 4X games so I tried to give it a good shot. GL3 is one of the only games where I've tried to slog through the tedium, poor mechanics and bland unoriginality in the hopes that I was just missing something and this was actually a great game. Alas, after trying the campaign, tutorial and a straight forward, no strings attached game... I resigned myself to the fact that this game is just a really boring rip off of Civilization IV with worse AI.  Okay, the ship and race creation aspect is pretty cool, but it's basically aesthetic and adds little to the game overall. And in fairness they've also copied a few aspects from Civ V as well, like trade routes and city states. The only really original aspects of the game are space stations, which the computer pumps out constantly and will completely cover the map before you've advanced enough to stop them. Even if you're tearing them down with impunity, the computer can churn them out just as quickly as you can blow them up. The reason for this is that the AI is so diabolically bad that the devs have bolstered it with a series of cheats to stop AI races destroying themselves before the game even starts - like attaching robotic legs to a horse that died in the paddock so it can still run in the race. But they're not fooling anyone.  Apart from that, I was honestly having flash backs to a game released 10 years ago: diplomacy is identical to Civ IV, right down to the other races constantly nagging you for hand outs. Combat and troop types are similar, despite the ability to watch a little battle cinematic, and entirely pointless once you get to Corvettes anyway. The biggest difference in combat is that you have to send a troop transport to capture a planet, but then Birth of the Federation was doing that 17 years ago, so again it's hardly a game changer. The split tech tree struck me as a simplified version of Beyond Earth's.   The worst part is, I could actually forgive them for ripping off all their game mechanics from other franchises if it weren't for the fact that this game is just slow and boring. One of the great things about the Civ series is that, even when you're not doing a lot, you're always working towards something - you have a plan, or a series of goals or you're keeping your eye on a possible opportunity or building up to something. GC3 is mostly just a load of empty nothingness - habitable planets and resources are far between, conquest is too slow to be effective, economics yo-yo wildly with little discernible reason... It's just sloppy.  My overall impression is of a game made half-heartedly with the intention of copying a couple of successful 4X titles in the hopes of making some easy money. Stardock - I'm disappointed.

Short version:   I had a lot of fun playing Transistor. It's one of those few games that manages to be respectable from both a technical and artistic perspective. While the presentation is stunning, one of Transistor's greatest gameplay strengths is that it greatly encourages taking your time and setting up fights. Customization of, and interaction between, abilities and attacks is a key feature here. The variety of ways to start and end a fight is pretty amazing and there's always a new method to try. While not particularly new to the genre on their own, a lot of obvious features combine to make Transistor feel like a unique experince. My only real complaint is a few jarring difficulty spikes and drops. Overall, a great game that I loved start to finish. Not without a couple flaws of course, but I would say it accomplishes everything it sets out to do and sets a very strong example for the genre of strategy ARPGs.    Less-short version:   Transistor is a story-driven isometric ARPG from the makers of Bastion, which it has a fair share in common with. In my opinion, It's definetly a solid title. First of all, It throws you right into the action when you start it up. There's no real opening cutscene or exposition to speak of, which I loved. Instead, you learn about the weird, artsy world of Transistor as you play. An important thing to note is that this game requires the players' attention to be really good. Everything in Transistor wants you to think. If you try to blow through it or brush it off, you probably won't end up enjoying it.  The combat system is the real selling point to me. It's fun and rewarding to fight, which is what an ARPG should be all about. It achieves this by including a freeze-time, turn based mechanic where you can take a breath and queue up attacks in the middle of a fight. Between that and the sheer variety of different attacks and movement options, there's a lot of creative ways to kill something. The catch is that you can only equip four different functions (combat/movement abilities) at a time outside of a couple passive slots unlocked later. Because 'dying' disables one of the functions equiped, you have to prioritize certain functions over others. On top of all that, functions can be apllied to other functions to alter the way the original attack works. Add a dash move to your projectile to make it pierce enemies, add an explosive to your melee to make it AOE, etc. This rather complex system is not only great fun to mess around with, but works wonderfully in practice and makes Transistor a seemless ARPG/strategy game hybrid.  To underline the functions and insentivise you to use them more, much of the game's lore is only acessible by completing levels with certain attacks or passives. You need to use every function in every slot to reveal the full story. This idea didn't always work for me. More often than not, the little flavor text given isn't worth scrambling your carefully crafted setup. Still, it was a nice break from the 'read a couple pages from a computer entry' style of storytelling that most games use. Transistor doesn't have much voice acting, but what's there is certainly passable, if a little bit annoying toward the end of the game.   The big problem I had with Transistor was the difficulty. While most of the levels were challenging but fair, the bosses were all over the place. Some were a cake walk; others were too frustrating. One in particular actually had me checking the Steam guides for optimal loadouts and stats. The out of place spikes in difficulty were jarring and removed me from the experience at some points. With all that said, I still give this issue a pass simply because I may have just not experimented enough or was using bad loadouts.  One thing I really loved were the challenges/minigames you can play in the hub...beach? They're all fun and engaging. Sometimes I felt like I played them more than the main game! Although that wouldn't really suprise me, considering Transistor is unfortunately on the short side. The length isn't too bad, but definetly dissapointing. Another thing I didn't like was one of the plot twists near the end of the second act of the story. I won't spoil it, but it was kind of a let down for me. You'll know it when you see it. The ending itself was fine, though just a little cliche.  The story may a bit hard to follow, and the difficulty might be a little wonky, but you can't review Transistor without mentioning the art and music. This game is nothing short of gorgeous. So much effort was put into making everything, from the backgrounds to the enemies and even the UI, look good. The music is my favorite part though. Once again, you can feel how much heart and soul was put into the music. Ashley Barrett nailed the atmosphere with her occasional vocals. If you want to see for yourself, the soundtrack is on Spotify. I highly reccomend giving it a listen if you're bored.  All in all, I would recommend the game to anyone interested.

app_name: Heretic: Shadow of the Serpent Riders
review_text: Heretic was the first licensed Doom engine game. And... it shows it's limitations at times. Both from a stylistic point of view and the technical side of things.  Doom was a lot of things, but above all else the game was very polished and it felt inspired. From it's soundtrack to it's enemy design, everything felt like it needed to be there. And it genuinely felt like the developers all had a central vision they shared for what the game eventually became.  Heretic on the other hand feels like it's stuck in Doom's shadow. The game mechanics feel far too similar to Doom and the aesthetic of the game, while impressive for the time did not age well. All of the weapons in Heretic feel more or less like reskins of Doom weapons. Apart from the graphics it feels like the same basic game but with a much less interesting aesthetic and soundtrack.  The things I appreciate Heretic more are for it's historical context like how it was one of the first FPS games to come bundled with online multiplayer support, how it had a very large custom map community and how it serves as a prequel to the HeXen series. But I really only recommend Heretic on it's own if you're a Doom fanatic such as myself, since I unfortunately see the game as very forgettable.  http://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=487092473

Imagine a delicious cake, prepared and baked by one of the best chefs in the world. Now imagine that cake covered in cow dung.  Daikatana is pretty much the same. There's a very good game hidden inside it, but some of the elements are so ridiculously bad that it ruins it. Still, if you're willing to put in some work to get past the bugs and design flaws (I recommend installing the 3rd party patches), you can enjoy it.  For example, the 'sidekicks' should have been completely scrapped from the game, or at least polished a lot more so they don't get stuck every 30 seconds. The first level is terrible, swarms of frogs and dragonflies are NOT good enemies in a FPS.  On the other hand, the plot is original, there are lots of different weapons that you can enjoy, the levels have some interesting exploration, most RPG elements are good.  I think this game deserves a remake. With better graphics, a new engine and some modifications it could be a lot better.

This was supposed to be a revival of the original 1998 game 'Half life'. It was supposed to be a recreation of it in the newer source engine. Unfortunatly though, instead of fully utilising the power of the newer engine valve barely made use of any of the new features and the features they did incorporate eg ragdoll physics were very poorly done. It was also heavily plagued by large amounts of bugs throughout the game. This was supposed to be something amazing but unfortuantly it turned into a massive failure and the backlash from that caused multiple other valve games to not recieve remakes or the remakes were cancelled halfway through, these included team fortress classic and counter strike. Although counter strike luckily did get that proper remake, that we now call Counter Strike: Source. Luckily though for the fans of the original Half Life a proper remake was created but not by valve. Instead it was made by some very very dedicated modders, the remake was originally a free mod by the name of 'Black Mesa' but has now been made into a standalone game. Unfortunatly though the zen levels have not been remade yet but are being worked on by the team o f modders.   I would not reccomend playing this, if you want to play half life then either you can play the original or if you want something newer then try black mesa.   Additional Notes: I have played this all the way thorugh but my time doesnt reflect it even remotely becuase i lost my time thanks to a glitch/bug

A game created from the developers of Amnesia series, Soma is a continuation of their line deeply engrossing atmospheric horror games. Without going too much into the story, essentially you are within an underwater base wrought with machines that range from thinking they're people to being horrific manifestations of something unknown.   The mechanical abominations that hound you are differing in size and ability, and each require a unique approach to being outsmarted or avoided, a nice addition over the previous games that featured a less than diverse cast of monstrosities. Perhaps my favorite part of this game however is it philosophical elements that make you questions things you might have not before, and for the sake of not potentially spoiling or ruining the story I will leave it at that.   Overall I would recommend this game to anyone who enjoys deeply engrossing experience and especially recommend to anyone who enjoys the thought of being mentally engaged and questioned throughout a game.

I began playing this genre, Survival-Horror, since back in Penumbra Overture, when I looked into the company Frictional Games, I awaited the 2010 release of Amnesia: The Dark Descent, which was a beauty. As I eagerly awaited the release of Amnesia: A Machine for Pigs, I preordered and eventually played it. I found it to be quite lacking in basic elements, but SOMA made it all up to me.    It really stressed the big question, to what extent can something be Human? It also asks questions about reality and existence, and what they really are. This level of Philosophy found in a mere Indie Horror game set an impossible standard for other developers and in my opinion, if Amnesia didn't already, crowned them king!  Definitely Recommend this game.

Rome wars: Attack of the Clones  It's a great looking game, no denying that the devolpers have engineered a stunning looking title. what they saved only making 3 character models they spent making nice landscapes.  The fighting is standard, tweo button followed by QTE finisher. It works and it works well, that doesn't save it from getting extremely tedious though as you'll spend 100% of the time walking from one place to fight a horde of clones to the next until eventually the game ends and your free to carry on doing what ever your were doing before you bought this. It's got some good gore though if that's your thing, bits of bodies go flying around the place.  The game's story is well crafted and quite engaging, playing a roman commander who is tough as nails and saves Rome from a pasting by the Barbarians. I won't spoil any of the story for you here because if you plan well you can finish this in one sitting. Just take a decent dump before hand and ply up any game supplys you need to survive 6 hours or less.  I keep talking about the character models, it justt can't be overlooked. The game suffers from having so few models you'll fight the same guy over and over in waves each time he's wearing a slightly different coloured loin cloth, which just speeds up the tedium this game already teeters on with it's combat. Though if your imaginitive enough you can pretend you're a Roman who has been taken from his time to fight in the future against engineered cyborgs modeled on fighters from the past, except they only had one or two pictures of what they looked like and he had moobs.  If this is on sale at around 90% off, go for it. Otherwise save your money and buy a real Roman outfit and fight a bunch of shop dummys.

This remake of the original 1996 version doesn’t restrain itself from entering territory to cause audiences to experience revulsion and consternation while still being enlivened by its irresistible story and characters. The retelling of this horrifically flawed masterpiece is engaging, immersive, and truly delivered what the fans of the Resident Evil franchise beloved and hated since it began.   Though not a perfect game by featuring constant backtracking, fixed camera techniques, and limited control scheme, there’s enough bonus material including exploration among the decisions players are responsible for at their own risk to keep the replay value sustained for hours.  If you have the willpower to accept a challenge - enter this world of survival horror!

A fun turned-based JRPG. You are the players of a Dungeons & Dragons-type tabletop roleplaying game, run by a dungeon master. Your characters have a race, a class, and some inherent stats from themselves that help you whether you choose classes like thief, warrior, paladin, mage, etc. There are some unlockable classes as you go along and two expansions (only one--Here There Be Dragons or whatever--is available on Steam. The other can be bought in-game). If you've played the first Knights of Pen and Paper, not a ton has changed. They've slightly changed the way stats are generated, how saves against conditions like confusion or rage are done, and the graphics are better (now more of a 16-bit feel instead of an 8-bit feel). There are also 'rare items' available as drops that are super powerful, providing resistance to conditions, or making your attacks better, or making your character more resilent. If you enjoyed the first one, you'll enjoy this one. You definitely don't need to have played the first one in order to enjoy this one. This is an entirely new campaign. There's tons of pop-culture goodness in there (both in the background art and dialogue), so it's fun. As for skills, there aren't a ton of them, but like with any RPG you can level up your skills how you choose to fit your play style. Likewise, there are a bunch of different locations with interesting art, but after you unlock all of them you'll be going back to the same places over and over, so it can get a bit repetitive. Likewise, I did a LOT more grinding in this version than the original to be able to beat some of the bosses, so that was frustrating, but there is a 'Bestiary' function that unlocks more information about enemies (including bonus damage if you collect enough kills of a certain beast), which made the grinding at least have some use.  Altogether, I played about 30 hours (Steam said 37, but a lot of that was idling and some grinding I did voluntarily), but that included the Here Be Dragons expansion pack, which added three new areas, some new items and a ton of killer dragons.  Overall 8/10. I prefer the original, but this one has a lot more depth and a much longer playtime. Still, I wouldn't mind starting from the beginning with the new classes I've unlocked. A lot of them I unlocked so late they would die in every combat so they never got a chance to get awesome enough to use in combat to their full potential.

review_text: With the yearly releases of these games I feel offended with just the thought of playing them. Come November you spend $60 on a game thats very similar to the last with nothing really new. Then a year later they stop support for said game and you get another installment; thats the SAME AS THE LAST in which you spend ANOTHER $60 and then the cycle continues for a good decade if not more. Thats not adding to the fact that for the last few installments they have had season passes.  By the time Infinite Warfare comes out this game will lose its player base, especially with every new entry, and in turn the amount you spend on the game isn't even worth it and some users spend close to $120 for this crazy business practice.  There is effort in this game for sure, the multiplayer is engaging enough and there is a great team behind it. However with how often they gotta release these games I feel bad for the talented people that are stuck working on these COD projects.   As for their 'crazy business practice' it might be awful, but the fact that people buy these games yearly and buy all of these passes and then complain why they do it Is beyond me. You're the problem too.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
review_text: This is a review for local co-op SPLITSCREEN....  Black Ops III is quite simply the best local co-op FPS for PC....  The options for local splitscreen play are many....  Want to play splitscreen co-op campain online or off...? You got it....  Want to play splitscreen zombies online...? Here ya go....  Want to play splitscreen online multiplayer like Team Deathmatch...? Merry Christmas....  I would recommend this game to PC players looking for a great local co-op FPS game experience....  With the cherry on this sundae being dual monitor, fullscreen, splitscreen support for the PC version!   https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dEAwBrRhwaA   There are barely any PC FPS games that offer these kind of local co-op splitscreen options, especially built into the games actual options menu....  Treyarch should be commended for taking the time to make sure these modes and options even made into the PC version of the game....  Where as almost every other developer would have tossed these features aside for the PC port, Treyarch stuck to its guns and not only kept them in, but improved upon them as well....  The only problem I have is that the only mode that can be played offline is campain....  Treyarch should really add a feature to play zombies and multiplayer offline with bots as well....  And maybe a feature to use a seperate Steam ACCOUNT FOR PLAYER 2 in splitscreen....  But other than that, I feel what you get with Black Ops III is by far the best local co-op SPLITSCREEN FPS game on PC, bar none....  Thank you, Treyarch....

Let's begin with the fact that I haven't played horror games before this, and didn't watch any videos of the game. I orginally  thought that you spent 5 nights at Freddy's home. After I starting watching horror movies, I found that none scared me. Not Candyman, not Halloween 1 and 2, not the crappy Halloween remake. I realized that I wasn't easy to scare, so I started watching videos and became intrigued with the game and decided to purchase it. At the time, this was the only 'horror' thing that had scared me. This game is accuesed of being JUMPSCAREs: The Game, and first off, the atmosphere is unnerving and really gets you worked up, and 2nd, there is nothing about the 6 jumpscares that are in the game and they do a good job of scaring you. People say the game is to simple, and to an extent it is simple, all you use is the mouse, but you have to watch foxy, save power, close the doors after seeing them with you lights, and keep an eye on Freddy, making it, in reality, quite complex. The game is very hard to complete and I took an hour to beat Night 5, but it is extremly rewarding and satisfying when you hear the 6am chime! The back story is extremly intresting if you take the time to look at it. Five Nights at Freddy's is a very fun and addicting game and I'd reccomend this game to ANYONE who is a fan of horror, point and click, or strategy games. I'd give it a 9.5/10. I am heavly anticipating the 3rd installment!

While the first impression is that this is a child game, this game is actually as fun as Plant vs Zombies.  It isn't tower defense style, it is more of a timing management game (ie. store management, flight controller, etc), you have to keep an eye on your customer queue and your 'factory' (tree) and make sure your products (fruit) will be available on time, or at least so that you don't lose too much money if you fail.  The game is easy at the beginning and if you rush starts getting hard after world 3, you are allowed to grind for upgrades on the previous levels, although with diminishing returns. You are also given a lot of bonus items to make your life easier on the later levels, or whenever you want extra help. The UI is excellent because it doesn't force you to give each command on time, you can queue comands and just watch the level unfold (and this has its own mechanic as you can't cancel them, so you may screw yourself up if some future event doesn't unfold as expected).

I'm gonna let y'all know now that I got a copy from the devs. Thanks guys!   Uncanny Valley is a survival horror story following Tom.   Tom took a job at the facility as a change of pace and to get away from his past, I cant go much more into that unless I spoil it for all of you so I'll just get on with the review.  Gameplay The game plays really well. only came around a few bugs, none of which were game breaking, some animation errors if you get hurt (example; stand still you look hurt, start to move and you look well again) but over all it's an easy game to learn to play. It doesn't tell you if an item can be used on anything so you have to expariment on some things, others use common sense. The horror aspect is there from the get go making sure you have no idea whats going on or what wants you dead and its just awesome with how it does it.  Music Very creepy. Does it's job very well with making a simple street creep you out like something is going to kill you.  visuals  It's a pixel game but don't let that fool you! this games visuals may not be as gory as a full 3D game but it is still pretty scarry even with pixel graphics!  Story The story is going to take more then 1 play through as there are a number of endings. The story is good, keeps the mystery there untill just the right time where you are hit with the truth and it wants you dead..or not...Like I said there are many endings and it all varys on how you play, how you did or didn't do your job or if you decided to sleep early or not! it all changes what happens ever so slightly...Or not so slightly depending on what choice it was.  Pros!  Many endings!  Choose your path! Scarry!  Around 1 - 2 hours for 1 play through.  Cons :-(  Can't skip the intro :-( No clear Direction of what to do later in the game :-( Animation errors :-(  Overall this game is an awesome game that you will get quite a bit of play time out of, that in my opinion is worth the price. 7/10

Sorry Bethesda, but I cannot recommend Fallout 4 in good conscience.  It's a step back from both Fallout: New Vegas and Skyrim, Bethesda's last game (2011).  Variety in the world is woefully inadequate. As you roam, it's impossible not to notice recycled art and more of the same everywhere you go. The same few buildings, the same few layouts.  The main quest line is uninspired and shoddily put together, and 'real' quests that aren't just repeating, randomly-generated fetch or kidnap quests are few compared to Bethesda's previous work. Fallout 4's landmass may be geographically bigger, but somehow it manages to have less than half of the number of quests Skyrim had.  Fallout 4 trades proper communities for 'settlements' - user-created regions where the player is the designer, the architect, and the construction worker. No matter your status in the grand Wasteland, it is you who will be placing crops in the ground if you don't want a nameless community (all inhabitants are conveniently named 'Settler') to starve. Real communities with people that have names are few enough to count on one hand. Bethesda has essentially shunted the heavy-lifting of level design to the player and called it a feature. I'm surprised they got away with it.  In short, Fallout 4 is a flawed open-world game. Fallout: New Vegas and Skyrim had me all hyped up for Bethesda's latest and greatest, but unfortunately this time, Bethesda couldn't deliver.

Doom's CAMPAIGN is a great run and gun shooter which revives many great things from the first two original games. If you like arena shooter type of controls, fast action and dodging projectiles, then this is a shooter for you. There is a storyline that may get sometimes in the way, but not too much to totally distract from the experience. As a very positive note: the game is extremely well optimized for different PC-configurations.  Only gripe I have with DOOM is the pacing. Sometimes you just have to wait too long to get new enemies to shoot, but it's very tolerable after you get the satisfaction of the very great core gameplay once some more monsters come in.  Multiplayer is only an average Halo-like shooter which is clearly aimed for pretty laidback players. It doesn't showcase anything new to the genre at this time when we have many innovative multiplayer shooters available. Fortunately after the campaign you can create your own maps or play other user created maps with the SNAPMAP editor, which comes with the game. You can even create COOPERATIVE maps in snapmap from 2 to 4 players.

If you have the right expectations, this is one of the best games you will ever play.  Expect: - epic scale combat - well designed weapons - well designed enemies that require various tactics to beat - weapons and enemies that have mass and you can feel the recoil and force of explosions - great sound - great prev-gen graphics - great soundtrack - many graphics settings you can tweak - great performance on modern hardware, even mid-range - about 15 hours of gameplay from singleplayer Do NOT expect: - a well written story (choppers get shot down 100% of the time) - various environments (all desert all game long) - deep emotional connection between characters - challenging puzzles - targeting to matter (enemies are one big hit zone) Other Cons: - enemies get overused and annoying at some points - really massive battles are 4 or 5 - giant spiders, if you suffer from arachophobia, do Not play! - questionable v-sync - game touches several cool concepts, even bangs a few of them, but doesn't take them to dinner after that.  Overall highly recommended if you can get it on sale, probably not worth 20 EUR on today's market though.

The game features quick matchups against the AI or in coaching mode (where only plays are called); games can be customized according to weather conditions and quarter length. A simple franchise mode consists of twelve games against randomized opponents (that can repeat with no rhyme or reason). Mod support is strong, with easily editable rosters, uniforms, and logos. An improved user interface makes calling plays look and perform better. Updated graphics and improved (though still repetitive) commentary are also new this year. There are only eight plays for each formation (a slight improvement over last year’s six), but hot routes make for more flexible options. Mouse-based passing remains engaging, where a portion of the field is aimed at instead of a specific player. Kicking controls are improved and appropriately difficult. Axis Football 2016 lacks a play clock (time runs down a specific amount between plays) and does not have player fatigue, eliminating substitutions during a game. The simulation is much better this year, resulting in more plausible results. In short, Axis Football 2016 is a vast improvement over last year’s iteration.   https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AjtwZrBmRkY

Spec Ops: The Line is an excellent example of why I no longer trust video game reviewers. Somewhere along The Line between entertainment and art, curators and plebs alike decided that a game with a point was somehow exempt from basic rules of gaming. It’s like if Mona Lisa spat on everyone who looked at it we’d still consider it genre-breaking. Except, Spec Ops isn’t Mona Lisa, it’s a trite piece of undercooked antiwar masturbation that appears to fear rupturing an aneurism if it avoids a cliché. Admittedly, I am comparing it to my larger recollections of war stories, not the current state of the subject within games. Compare Spec Ops to Stanley Kubrick’s Paths of Glory and the chasm between entertainment and pacifism Spec Ops apparently thinks unavoidable appears more of an artificial border. That is to say, Paths of Glory is every bit as cynical as Spec Ops but with half the tedium. I am not the first to point out that Spec Ops is a horrible game. Its benefactors admit this with the caveat that it somehow lends credence to its message. The Cynical Brit said it “isn’t a good game per se, it’s deliberately mundane 3rd person shooter which makes you think about the horrors of war.” It’s the “deliberately mundane” that irritates me. A deliberately mundane game is often indistinguishable from an incompetently mundane one. Cover-based gunfights that can be best described as soggy could be a statement about the stupidity of war, or a testament to how hard it is to make crisp-feeling third-person firefights. Considering that Gears of War is the only successful such attempt in recent memory, it’s all too convenient to say the gameplay was supposed to be bland. The blurb from its Steam store page describes this allegedly meaningful mediocrity as “tactical squad-based Delta Force gameplay throughout a horizontally and vertically oriented world”. Perhaps I expect too much, but anyone who pens prose such as this should not be trusted to make any adaptation of the Heart of Darkness. The memorable part of the gameplay is being constantly overwhelmed by hugely boring enemies who appear to be completely fungible with the exception of a single heavy type, who by describing as ‘heavy’ I have already imparted more information about their humanity than the game ever bothers to. Which is another issue with the deliberately mundane hypothesis. The storyline meshes with the gameplay precisely not at all. As I stand knee-deep in American soliders I shot to death because they stood between me and the next cutscene I’m expected to find my accidental shelling of a few dozen civilians the moral low point and story high point of the entire affair. Why? Ten minutes ago I intentionally killed men whose principal crime was proximity, and now I’m expected to feel remorse over the ones I offed on accident? Perhaps the convenience of intentionality ran dry by this point in the plot, because apparently designing mundane gameplay does matter yet designing to murder does not. Who’s ever heard of manslaughter anyway? While I’m worrying about intentions, this game does an impressively bad job of concerning itself with mine. Sometimes, I was killed by the nameless faceless ‘baddies’ because I was too bored to be bothered to think about any kind of tactics. The rest of the time, I was killed because the game figures “run headlong into the enemy” and “cower beneath chest-high wall” are basically synonymous. Neither was any quick recovery possible, because unlike in Gears of War, the far superior gameplay progenitor of Spec Ops, I could not reverse my inertia with the tap of the ‘don’t-get-killed’ button. Instead, I stood to my full height, rearing my all-American chest to their all-American steel, slowly reoriented myself to our homeland and attempted to correct the game’s mistake. But allow me to judge this “Story Rich” and “Political” game by its story and politics. Besides the name Conrad, the only feature Spec Ops retains from the Heart of Darkness is inexplicable motivations. And I don’t particularly appreciate that in the original text. Why does the protagonist continue on? That his unwilling companions constantly question him only compounds the issue by making it apparent. The complete lack of confusion on his part lulls the player into a blind acceptance of the plot, as we are presumably supposed to feel about the soldier’s acceptance of their mission drift. If you’re wondering where the plot holes are, just list the plot points. Why does he continue on into the city despite his orders about the retaining wall? To save the soldiers he watched die? What about the rebels who kill those soldiers; why do they vanish? Why does he accept the CIA so readily, while accepting that his fellow soldiers must die? And at no point do his Delta force underlings question him. These are men who are better educated and generally older than the average soldier: Men who are trained to question things normal soldiers aren’t. Even at the end, the last underling can’t bring himself to actually defy the protagonist. He merely won’t go with him any longer. Apparently, Conrad’s existence is enough to throw the protagonist into a fit of vengeance but his murdering of fellow soldiers, disregard of common sense, morality, and orders isn’t enough to inspire anything but words from his compatriots. I suppose they made the mistake of not being player-controlled characters. The Heart of Darkness doesn’t work without xenophobia. Chinua Achebe was right to point out that the text portrays Africans as inhuman, but it’s a necessary literary move for the text to work as a whole. It may be wrong, but you can’t just take the xenophobia out and expect the story to survive. Unless you’re Spec Ops. There are no foils for “good” Europeans to be compared to within the game. No “savages” on the shore to provide a visceral reminder of what the main characters are pretending not to be. All that’s left is people acting strangely. The ideology of imperialism cannot even be mocked if there are no apparent victims to it. And the handful of quivering refugees are as easily regarded the victims of the sandstorms as they are of the American military industrial complex. My principal emotions from playing the entirety of this quagmire of a game were frustration, confusion, and apathy. Frustration with the gameplay which clearly hated itself, confusion as to why any of the characters were doing almost anything they did, and apathy at the results of the decisions I didn’t understand. If this game is political, what political views should I take away from it? That we should be careful before dropping mortars on thermal signatures? That Delta Force commanders should not have total jurisdiction over places at which they just arrived? There is no political message here that is any less tedious or obvious than the game itself. Unless you need an eight-hour reminder that killing is bad (m’kay), stick to Bioshock for literature-inspired gaming.

This game was quite unknown to me and probably is to most people. It's a game released in 2009 by Blue Omega Entertainment and published by a bit more known company, Codemasters. Damnation is an Gears of War style game where you proceed in a linear environment and shoot people from behind cover. You regenerate health to recover and you have your sidekicks whom you also must keep alive. You can carry 3 weapons and different kinda cartridge based weapons is given for you right from the start already.  The start is completely weird, you have this guy in some cave who starts telling a story and soon you're being showcased some war at bridge without really knowing what time it's set in, why they attacked and most importantly.. when your team arrives.. why they arrive, who they are and where the heck they came from? This game doesn't explain alot of the reasons it also doesn't seem that interesting either. Forexample on this bridge scene there ends up being some guy who slaughters armed men with a sword because for some reason after these guys see how their fellow soldiers were just killed in closecombat, instead of them just shooting the sword guy from distance, they decide it's a good idea to confront this guy and go and die :D Alotta stuff in this game really makes no sense.  The game offers platforming, faster running and in general more agile character when compared to GoW. This is also where things start to go wrong. Reason Gears of War gameplay succeeds, is because it focuses on only one aspect, covershooting and all the effort was put on that aspect only. Damnation tries to do alot of things without really excelling on anything. Forexample the platforming feels very clunky and just bad because the controls aren't really suited for that kinda gameplay because they're tanky and yet at the sametime somehow also still overly sensitive. In Tomb Raider when you turn, it doens't instantly turn, in Damnation your character does instantly a 90degree turn and this makes very confusing to control your character on some very narrow space. Gunplay feels also bad because the character's handling just totally doesn't fit for a shooter kinda game because it also was balanced for the platforming style but apparently the movement doesn't really work that well with either gameplay style. So you are left with a goofy game that feels cheaply made.  The game is a very much handhold game. It heavily holds one's hand because if you don't do some act your sidekicks tell you to do (ie. shoot this object), they just do it themselves then. Aka game plays itself. Map design is also very non-challenging because everything is just so obvious and completely linear. Enemy AI isn't also good because sometimes they just stay still looking at you without taking any actions against you, so you'll just have to end their life because you can't stand looking at that brainless enemy.  Now some of the good things.. This runs on Unreal Engine 3 and due to that, it's also very configurable thanks to the .ini files and because it's an UE3 game it also runs pretty well. As a aesthetic style choice, this game had potential kinda because it tried some weird steampunk cowboy thing and from what I know.. that kinda stuff hasn't really been done.   Main menu works pretty well and loading screens are fast. But then you have this audio side of it.. when characters speak, it sounds as if it was said up in the space. Aka there is absolutely no spatialization. The voices totally do not react to environment in anyways (no echo, no reverbation..) they just come and disappear, sounds really odd. I tried to see if that thing would be fixed if you use hardware accelerated audio and so I switched from the .ini file the game to use OpenAL API but that didnt' fix anything. The game still suffered from the horrible environmental audio where the panning/location of sound also was still completely off. Forexample if you stood right next to your sidekick, on leftside of her, the sound would come from the backright instead of from just right. Like wtf?   Visuals aren't also that impressive because as I soon noticed, the overly bloom and postprocess filled world was put for a reason. So that you cannot see the dated look of it. Once you disable those effects from the config, the game ends up looking really barebones. You see all the low res textures that the background environment has and also the actual area where you play doesnt' look that nice either.  Not recommended even from a sale because nothing in this game feels good, nothing.

This game just brings back a ton of nostalgia and amazing unique 3D puzzle action fighting style. Seriously this game is unlike any other I've played as it was one of the original Xbox RELEASE games along with Dead or Alive 3 and ofc Halo Combat Evolved, the one and only.  With a first time playthrough you will definitely find this game challenging but refreshing as its constant change of pace keeps you on edge. One minute you will be farming spooce and the next you are leading an army of mudarchers, guns blazing or possessing some big Slig shooting everything in sight until your way is clear.  Admittedly, to start with (BEFORE BETA UPDATE RELEASED 2016) this game was very buggy and has sound, graphics and interface issues. However, with this new revamp, FIFTEEN YEARS after its release in 2001 welcomes and revives an incredible experience which takes a good amount of hours to fully complete the game, with a variety of enjoyable moments along the way. New sound, slightly HD oriented graphics, reduced amount of bugs and a lot smoother gameplay, the PC port for this game alone has made this hugely worthy of being played.  Replayability is not as much of a problem for this game since there are often more than 1 way of completing the 23 levels to sink your teeth into. Personally I found at least 4 ways to complete one of the levels, so its completely personal and often changes unknowingly with each playthrough. Not to mention there are TWO endings, which I didnt even know about until yesterday apparently lol  Whether you want a great single player experience to hook some time into, or want something new, if you havent played this game before you should definitely consider it. As an all time favourite of mine since childhood, I can only really say positive praise for this game AND the fact they didnt let it die, and updated it making it so much better for PC seriously...  TL;DR ♥♥♥♥ing amazing old game revived to be great on PC even 15 years after release for Xbox.  P.S. MAKE SURE YOU GO TO PROPERTIES -> BETAS -> SELECT BETA and accept, otherwise you are stuck with the buggy version unfortunately. Also make sure you have a controller for this Xbox game obv, gl with mouse and keyboard.

review_text: So, I've written a No- review before where I bashed this game for being unfair (not hard, unfair).  While I will still hold that against the game, I can't keep that No-review. I've been mercilessly smiten by this unforgiving game.  It is excruciating but very fun and it really grows on you.  As I stated before the enviroments are beautiful and while I'm not really a fan of the 'hidden story', it is at least very original.   All in all I recommend this game, because what you can hate, you can also learn to love and you will hate dark souls at times, but keep playing, keep learning and you will eventually start to enjoy it.

This is the gold standard by which stealth games are measured, and my personal favorite game of all time. There is an interesting and mysterious world that you will learn about as you play your way through, sneaking through the shadows. The graphics are a little dated, but there are mods that can bump them up a bit, and the dark environments and wonderful sound design preserve the atmosphere.  In my opinion, this is the best game in the series, though some disagree and prefer the first one, and you may wish to play that one first for the story. The game still has an active community, with fan made missions released regularly.  A new game is coming to the franchise in the beginning of 2014. I'm looking forward to playing it on release to see how it holds up.  NOTE: I own non-steam versions of this game, so my hours played are inaccurate.  UPDATE: I am sad that the new game does not hold up to the originals.

The Wolf Among Us is dark. It's gruesome. It's a world full of choice and, most importantly, it feels real. I found myself absorbed into the events that pertained around 'Fable Town' and while the massive cast of fairy tale creatures ranged from everything to talking frogs and demonic beasts I never felt like any one of them was out of place. Every character felt well rounded and like it belonged into this universe. I felt compassion for some, hated others. I sensed their desperation and was empathetic with their situations. The amount of choice you'll get from playing the Wolf Among Us is absolutely staggering, straight down to the series multiple endings. Being able to play as 'Bigby Wolf' and deciding what kind of person (or monster) you'll become added an extra layer of depth to an already impressive plot - I truly felt like I was making a difference in FableTown and that every decision would effect others. TellTale did an amazing job at defining each and every character and gave you the power to define who you were. Did you want a character to die because you wanted justice? Or do you show compassion and forgive and forget? The weight of these decisions, both small and large, carry themselves all the way to Episode 5, the final installment of the series. It's hard to get into detail without spoiling the plot, but know that you'll be making choice after choice - even more so than The Walking Dead - and it'll be up to you to live with those consequences. I highly reccommend this game for those looking for a deep, mystery centered story.  + Well Crafted Plot + Power of choice + Musical Score compliments tone + Voice Acting is well done + Scenematics and quick time events will keep you on the edge of your seat  - A couple characters fall flat near the end in comparison to the rest
