This game feels inaccurate in its portrayal of depression. None of the friends save for Amanda seem to be helpful individuals. I would hope that if I had the behavior of our protagonist, someone would try and talk to me or do something. Depression is a legitimate struggle to get out of, but this game makes it feel like a game. By trying to simulate someone’s very personal experience, you make someone with a legitimate medical issue seem whiney. You can’t really relate to the protagonist if you haven’t had depression, and from people who have had depression, it would seem inaccurate. My brother has Bipolar disorder, and has intense periods of depression. He acts completely different from the protagonist in these periods. No one supports the protagonist unlike the girlfriends and family of the real world. While some people don’t get the support they need, I feel someone would at least notice early on. If you wanted to make an impact, make the game from the point of view of someone only slightly depressed, and have game options that can make it better or worse. Starting from the bottom makes it hard for the “typical” person to get into the mindset of the depressed individual.   Aside from the faults in its portrayal of depression, the game feels boring. The red crossed out text is just frustrating in the early game where they are not even a conceivable option. They would be better off when they are something a player could access. The static-text is aesthetically unpleasing as it stands out from the rest of the games aesthetic. The photos on the top often feel like its going for an artsy feel, but overall they feel pointless. This game feels like it’s trying to be artsy, being important. It wants to be profound and interesting, but it comes across is trying too hard to be sad and not capturing what it set out to do. The game only got the attention it had due to scandal, and it shows. The gameplay is boring and the commentary is weak. I would not recommend the game to anyone who wants to understand depression. You are better off with an old textbook or internet entries. The only saving grace is that this game was free, so at least I didn’t lose anything other than a few minutes of time.

At first glance this game may seem a bit redundant. And this is why I waited till it was on sale to purchase the game.   However, my thoughts now are that it is definitely worth the full price of the game and i am happy to have bought it.   There is a lot of 'Depth' to the game in itself. You may think ok wow you get to play as a shark or a diver and do the same things over and over. Although that would not be true. The ranking system along with the perks system add a lot of dynamics to the gameplay making each round completely unique and different.   Along with the different diver's and shark's each have their own unique abilities and I think that really adds to the game.   The shark is my favourite to play. It is very tactical and can be equally as scarey even playing as a shark when you are going up against some seasoned players or vice versa.   I only have 9 hours logged in so far but i will probably update this review once i have played it a bit more. For now i give this game an 8/10 because of Originality, Gameplay, And overall playability. What it lacks in the graphics department it more than makes up for it in other areas.   So to anyone who is about to buy this game i will say this.  Good luck and have Fin!

Ladies and gentlemen, I present one of the strangest RPGs I've ever played: Hyperdimension Neptunia Re;Birth 1.  This game takes place in a personified version of the seventh generation console war. If you don't understand what I just said, the three major seventh generation consoles (being PS3, Xbox 360, and Wii) along with the Sega Neptune are litterally at war with each other and are personified as goddesses that rule different lands, which are not-so-subtley named after their represented consoles. All of the characters are actually done quite well too. This game is basically about you (Neptune, who has lost her memories, for the most part) trying to unite the four nations to overtake evil stuff.  This game will bombard you with a bunch of references to many, many, many games; a lot of cheeky fourth wall breaking; and... well... jiggle physics.  As for the actual gameplay part of the game, 'Meh?' is kind of all I can say. The dungeons (which are the only part where you control an actual character) are very generic and could have been a lot better, but this game is a PSVita port, so I understand it (not that it is justified due to that). The battle system at its core is extremely repetitive and simple, though some parts of it are so extensive that it's not even worth tampering with. I just found myself using power and special attacks through the whole game. One thing however, is that the enemies are generally references to things.  Speaking of enemies, this game is very strict about its leveling. The boss fights are a joke if you're over-leveled but they are near impossible if you are even slightly behind in levels. It's actually surprising how they were able to accomplish that.  In the end, you're not really playing the game to play the game.    tl;dr  If you want a whole bunch of game references, cheeky fourth wall breaks, and/or jiggle physics, then this is a good pick for you. While it's an RPG, you don't really play this game for the gameplay. You play it just to progress and get more funny conversations.

I've been playing this game long before the Steam release (start of open beta), and I must say, this is definitely a game worth checking out. It's a great mixture of collectathon, RPG, sandbox, and adventure games in a Voxel MMO setting. Right now the servers are down for maitenance, so I might as well write a review for this game.  To keep things simple, I suppose I can list some pros/cons.  + Fully buildable/destructable worlds + Thousands of mounts, ships, fish, badges, etc. to collect + Great community + Almost all items in this game are created by the community (Even I have some items that I created in the game, look for Ghastly Guillotine and Sakura Bough in the styles if you're interested   <-- look at this sellout over here  ) + Far from P2W, all classes and items are obtainable without paying a cent + Updates every week + Very fun if you get friends to join in + Raptor mounts  +- Current 'community' economy works, but could be better (Glim/Flux) +- Griefing is very hard to do, but possible  - Currently, the optimization isn't too great - Not much endgame content currently - Slightly buggy in some places - Needs more raptors  Don't be scared by the fact that Trion made this, considering their poor business decisions in the past. The F2P model for this game is one of the best I've seen yet, and this game is really well executed for what it is.  Definitely recommended.

So... You can complain about the very small amount of playtime i have in this game, but i can explain. I downloaded this game about a year ago, even with my absolutely crap computer. The game lagged a lot, but i gave it a try. I really enjoyed the game even after just minutes of gameplay, but i noticed the lag was just too much for this kind of game. So i sadly uninstalled it. But now... i saw this game again on Youtube and wanted to give it another try, just in case the optimisazion had been improved. And it was. I ran back into here, with my newly found knowledge thanks to pepole like Axemun and Schthe Plays on Youtube, and, boy, has the game changed! Tons of new things have been added since my last time: new classes like the extremely tanky Revenant, the glass cannon Lunar Lancer, the healer/summoner Chloromancer (it also has pretty much the highest DPS in the game if you use it right), new mechanichs, like the Dragons, the new PVP arena... It's just a great game. It's a lot of fun, but it does get repetitive. One HUGE complain: since... what? A month ago? Classes can't be purchased with Cubits (one the standard currencies of the game, togheter with Flux, Glim, Fragments of Wonders... [yeah, it's pretty complicated]), but only with Credits (premium currency), other then with Class Coins, used to get a single class per coin, and you pretty much only get 4 for free, i think (not sure), and there are more then 15 classes in the game. Why would they do that? Because of this, i wouldn't suggest the game right now, unless you just want to try it. But i have a feeling they're coming back some time soon. At least i hope.

All this buildup, and you think you're about to begin Act II, or Act III if the scope is smaller than HR, then you get the 'New Game +' and realize the game is over right when it was about to begin.  90% of this game is waiting for the story to start, the last 10% is disappointment that it's over when it was about to begin.   Human Revolution had a story that was clear. Characters that had depth. Plots, character archs, conclusions. This game is great as far as the tools from Human Revolution being fleshed out. The scope has been drastically shrunken down. DRASTICALLY smaller feeling than Deus Ex, and Deus Ex HR.   The game is still better than any non-Deus Ex game, but basing the game on its own merits it falls short story wise. That is a main, actually, it's THE main part of Deus Ex that sets the series apart from the rest of the plebian games around.   If you don't play good games you'll love this title. If you've played and really liked and held Deus Ex and Deus Ex HR high, then you'll be left with an empty feeling at the end.   TLDR. 1 hub, unclear stories, unanswered quetions, Microtransaction blindside, and the game ending when it barely felt like it had started. Buy it if you want, but don't expect to feel whole at the end of it.

I'm kind of torn on this game  Pros:   -The game is art. Units look fantastic, environments are gorgeous, the UI is beautiful. The soundtracks to both the base game and Fall of the Samurai are flawless. There's so much polish and shine here that didn't come back until Warhammer.  -The game makes usability strides over previous TW games. The UI is relatively clean and easy-to-use, and it's pretty easy to jump in and start playing without having to go through a prep walkthrough just to start.  -Battles are very responsive. The game finds a balance with engagements being long enough to enjoy watching, but short enough to acknowledge that the player made the correct tactical decision.  -Campaign map terrain is very well laid-out. You'll see none of Attila's ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t of armies running around you over constant open plains. If you are in the way of your enemy, the enemy will have to fight you.  Cons:  -Naval battles are still kind of crap. While they look their best in Shogun 2, they're still slow and plodding with a lack of transparency as to how they work. Fall of the Samurai's are outright broken in a few spots. In that, a defending AI opponent will just sit on the edge of the map, not doing anything.  -Ludicrously long loading times. Don't think you'll get out of it with an SSD or running a massive modern gaming rig. Even then, you'll still have to sit through pre- and post-battle loading screen for up to over a minute at a time.  -Battles get repetitive. Frontline units boil down to archer, sword, cavalry, or spear, and each faction gets basically the same exact units. Some factions get specialized versions of these units, which is one of the only ways to make it feel distinct.  -The battles don't look very great depending on who you are. I know many people like S2's close-ups, but I'm not a fan. The only way units will engage in combat is through large, extravagant 1-on-1 duels. So when two groups clash, they immediately break formation and start dancing, and it looks like a mess.  -I don't like how easily cavalry falls to spears. While I appreciate how it's meant to work, it's really annoying how quickly cav dies to spears, and that if one your horsemen gets caught escaping from spears, your whole cav unit will turn around to engage and be willingly slaughtered.

I love BattleLore since back when it was Days of Wonder's property. Naturally I picked up FFG's version when they redesigned it to fit in with the Runebound universe. While the switch from DoW to FFG did have it's ups and downs I do to this day love what they did with it.   Now it's on computer, and I do in fact recommend it. If you've played the board game you'll be instantly familiar with the mechanics....except for them altering the Lore phase in ways that I don't really see as needed. I'd like the option to actually take more Lore instead of a card. Seriously.   If you're unfamiliar with the board game, fear not. The learning curve is not too steep. Many units are pretty strait forward in their use and after a little bit of practice manuevering becomes pretty second nature.  Of course, you will be praying to the mighty and fickle gods of RNG (Random Number Generator) since all attacks are dealt with dice rolling. If you're not a fan of having well laid plans ruined by bad rolls, you *will* find this game frustrating.   So yeah, overall I'm satisfied with the purchase. I can turn it on, play a few matches and forget about it for a little while. I'd like to see more DLC, considering the board game has more units for both factions and an entire Undead faction as well. There's plenty to add, but what you get for $10 is pretty good, IMO.  Of course, there's nothing wrong with waiting for a sale.

I really enjoy this game, and has been playing it non-stop since I got it. And it's very ♥♥♥♥ing good. You could say 'It's like Euro Truck Simulator in space', like some have said, but it's more than that, with combat, exploring and factions. It's very appealing with the beautiful scenery and the big wide world to explore. But even though it's all these things, there is also many flaws in the game. It's not enjoyable for everyone, and some find many parts of it boring. I understand that. But I just find it absolutely astonishing to travel many lightyears to visit foreign space stations, and dock in marvelous space-ship halls. I never grow tired of that.  The solo play, which I have been mostly in, has some stupid AI, but it's a good introduction to the new players, specially because the guides doesn't provide enough information on how to play the game. I suggest looking up some youtube and webguides to get started, as it has a harsh learning curve. And it's up to you if you are willing to learn this game. It can be maleficent, but also very dull with the same sceneries.  The combat is really fulfilling, and it's a great feeling to destroy a wanted ship and collect your bounty. Though, you'd really want a Flightstick, as the mouse and keyboard experience is not that great. Some of the missions you'll get is way to difficult, and the game shows no mercy. If you go up against the big guys, you'll be fleeing with your tail between your legs.  Either way, I hope that, if you pick the game up, you'll enjoy the hell out of killing stupid space pirates and exploring far away lands.  I will update my review as the game is being updated.

app_name: Minecraft: Story Mode - A Telltale Games Series
review_text: Minecraft is an extraordinary building simulator game that allows you to mine blocks and then change them into complex structures. It’s so easy a five year old can do it, but it’s fun for any age because of the imagination you can put into your creations. Not only that, but it includes a survival system that adds a whole new level of enjoyment to it. This makes it so while you are building things you have to try to protect yourself and your buildings from various monsters.   The only thing the game was missing from being perfect is more NPCs and various towns people you could talk to (Note the game has villagers, but they are for trading items to and nothing more.) So believe me when I say I was genuinely interested in a Minecraft “Story Mode”. The idea of it is cool and it sounds like it would be a great addition to the game. Hell, maybe it would've been if done right.   But it was so poorly executed. Instead of a story mode integrated with the game itself, what we got was another telltale excuse for an adventure game. Don’t get me wrong, I like the telltale games. They are kind of a guilty pleasure of mine, but the company really needs to work on putting more adventure into the game and less pointless dialog that is so unoriginal to the point where it starts ripping off of it’s own stories to make a new game.  So to start off, we take control of the character known as Jesse. Jesse can be customized to be male or female, with the three diffrent skin options for each gender. It’s not an RPG so I can understand why we didn’t get a full character customization option. It is nice how you could choose your gender and would respectively get a male or female voice depending on what you picked. I know something like that is starting to become the norm in gaming, but it still does impress me when the developers put in the extra time and money to get a male and female voice for the protagonist so we don’t have to feel pigeonholed into what gender our playable character can be.  After we make our character we are then introduced to the story. I won’t give too much away as it’s really the only thing that makes this game worthwhile, but our hero (Jesse) finds themselves in a dire situation with only their friends and a couple of fabled heroes to guide them. Along the way you will make choices as in any other telltale game that ultimately affect the outcome of the chapter and among other things, change the story. There is a big fuss that goes around about these so called choices, but I don’t want to get into the whole telltale story formula discussion. You need only have to play two or more telltale games to see what I am referring to.   Here is the massive issue I have with this game. The action sequences or QTEs known to most people is the thing that drags this game down. QTEs (Quick time events) are a lazy way of putting action into a game without having to develop any kind of combat, platforming, or action system of any kind. For instance in a game like Super Mario you have to time your jumps and use the controls to get across obstacles. However, with QTEs instead of having to actually move your character and manually control yourself, you instead just simply press or mash down a key such as Q, W, E, A, S, D, etc. Once in awhile QTEs are okay. There doesn’t always need to be tons of action to make a game fun to play, but telltale has a way of just littering the game with mindless QTEs after more mindless QTEs.   Going back to what I said earlier about Minecraft, it’s not the type of game where you just mash down a button that lights up on the screen to do something. It’s a game where you can take your time to build great structures and craft interesting objects in order to further progress in your world. To be fair, telltale gives an attempt to implement the crafting system into the minecraft story mode, but it’s so seldomly used that it’s almost insulting to anyone who has actually played minecraft.   There is a part early on in the game where your characters actually get to build something made out of blocks. Instead of allowing the player to go around and start stacking away we are treated to mashing down Q so that the game can automatically build what we are trying to make. It would be like if you went out and bought the newest Call of Duty or Battlefield game only to find that you no longer go around aiming your gun to shoot, you instead have to press down the buttons that appear on screen and the game will automatically aim for your opponents head to kill them. Do you know how much that would suck? Well that’s what it’s like playing this game.  Overall, I do not recommend buying this game. I consider myself to be a hardcore minecraft and adventure game fan and even I cannot stand what Telltale did to this game. Telltale really needs to go back to the drawing board and start learning about what made the point and click games in the 90s fun. It was the exploring, the weird quirky ways you had to solve problems, the complex puzzles that stood in your way and so on. Monkey Island would of been a lot less fun if all you had to do is walk down a hallway and just talk to people along the way.

Hearts of Iron 4... I was spewing venom at it. Being a big fan of HoI3, I was not happy about the seemingly dumb'ed down mechanics of the game. Specifically, the supply system. I loved the fuel simulation of HoI3, and really that's the only area that was changed that many had a problem with. Everything else was an improvement, and even some haters of HoI4 can't argue that the planning system is a vast improvement over the manual movement of before.  So when I got my hands on HoI4, despite my concerns, I was pleasantly surprised. I still miss the fuel simulation, but now its done in a way that I can understand. And lets face it... No on, no one understood how the supply mechanics in HoI3 really worked, even the devs admitted before that they had no clue cause a lot of it was abstracted simulation that had a lot of problems with it. On the surface, it worked. For the most part. Under the hood, it was a mess.  HoI4 cleans up a lot of areas that 3 didn't do well. Sure, some players got a kick out of manually managing and directing each and every single unit. Not everyone is obsessive compulsive like that, not everyone can pump 300 hours into a game. HoI4 gives you access to both worlds. Want to be an obsessive compulsive armchair general? Go right ahead, it takes nothing away from the experience. Want to sit back and let the semi-competent AI do its thing for you? Go right ahead. And when the AI screws up, you can jump in and fix it.  So whats the problem? I've read some negative reviews that complain about the whole planning system and how they 'miss' the old way of doing it in HoI3. Ehem... Sorry to inform you, but you can still tediously manage your units as before. So negative nancies, get over yourself!  This game isn't perfect. The AI is still pants-on-head ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ in some areas. But AI development is an ongoing process and hopefully in time, things will improve. HoI4 has improved though in every other way and takes the tedious process of waging war out of the equation. I actually feel like a general playing this game. My one major gripe with 3 was that I literally had too much control, and waging war became a process that I didn't look forward to. Now I can.  One thing to add: Paradox's hideously overpriced DLC TFV that just came out should be avoided until it can be gained at a hefty discount. I'm usually supportive of Paradox and their DLC method, but there is no way in hell, for the amount of content you get, should TFV be seriously considered as a purchase at the price they are charging. app_name: Warhammer 40,000: Dawn of War - Game of the Year Edition
review_text: I know shouldn't let the Campaign overly influence my opinion on this game, but man I found this boring. It hasn't aged very well at all. I can forgive the poor draw distance, and the chunky graphics, as technology moves on long after games have been made.  Yet... the story was pretty boring and by the book, which is a shame when you consider the huge amount of lore you can draw from the Warhammer Universe. Like other RTS before and after it, Dawn of War suffers from the ease in which you can build up a super army and just blitz your way through the map.   There are some nice animations and kill sequences within the game, which is a nice little touch.  I'll give this game another go somewhere down the line and play Skirmmish/Multiplayer against other people, which I think will be a fairer reflection on what it has to offer. I do like the idea of the Army Painter tool to create unique paint designs.

Tight controls, a wealth of abilities and an interesting narrative. Ghost is a great game that clearly inherits a lot of sensibility from Unepic-- but it has evolved in nearly every way.  Ghost's difficulty settings include traditional easy, medium and hard settings, but also include 2 modes of play- a traditional Metroidvania approuch (ala Symphant of the Night) and a roguelike setting that strips most of your powerups from you after you die, with a chance to recover some of them from your grave.  I've been wholly impressed with the balancing of the Hard difficulty: it challenges me without trying to prove some sort of 'I Wanna be the Guy' point. The Roguelike setting has proven interesting- creating a system of esclating risk and reward that is woven into the gameplay seemlessly.  All and all, I'm happy to have picked it up. I think you should too!

If your looking to find your first strategy game, you can do no better than Civ V. I grew up playing the third game in the franchise, and despite the game having many flaws and being   incredibly   hard, I enjoyed the game immensely.   Civ V changed and perfected things beyond what fans of the series thought possible. From getting rid of the unit stacking, minimizing city micro-management, to changing the movement from squares to hexagons, so much could have gone wrong and instead created a modern masterpiece. Any and all bugs or imbalance issues were fixed in the updates or DLC's.   I've played 200+ hours and still going. Wait for a steam sale and nab a genuine copy of the bestselling build your your own empire simulator!  (Oh, and it has multi-player and community mods.)  5/5 stars.

One of the best indie titles around! This game takes a few small mechanics of Minecraft, all the good of ol' SNES action RPGs, adds in a lot of mystery and co-op fun, and all ends up as one of the best experience $10 can get you.

If you played Minecraft and noticed the lack of adventure or excitement, then you will be pleased with Terraria. The game is full of unique loot and items that do interesting things, mysterious biomes to explore, big and scary bosses to be defeated.  Where tiers of gear in Minecraft only goes Iron > Steel > Diamond this game takes it MUCH further. In the beginning you will start as a scrub with wooden tools being scared by slimes, and after a few weeks of progressing further and deeper in to the world you will eventually feel like a colorful god. And as you get more godly, the world around you gets tougher as well - which means it'll contain even more epic loot.  Now, four years since the release the developers are still adding content to the game. In just a few hours a patch that DOUBLES the amount of content will be live. Do you realize how big of a deal that is? This game already has so much content and replayability that even after 130 hours I'm still far from having had enough with it.

To be perfectly clear, I love this game, I think it is a fantastic game and it is one of my new favorite games, but you can't deny the fact that this game is a ♥♥♥♥ poor PC port.   This game represents everything wrong with PC development. Let me make a list of everything wrong with this game.  -Locked 30 FPS -Lack of graphical options -Horrible performance and frame drops -Graphic glitches (texture bugs/artifacts) -Constant crashes -Small, inexperienced team ported the game (12 people developed this PC port)  So the game is locked at 30 FPS, and can easily be unlocked in a config file, but is cleary locked considering at anything above 30 fps you get constant FPS drops and it becomes an unplayable stuttery mess. I have a mid-range system too, nothing too high-end, but I should be able to mange 60 FPS at the lowest settings at 720p (Core i5 4690k, R9 270, 8gb Ram) but nope, at 720p low settings, I do sometimes get 60 FPS, but if I even move I drop to 40-30 FPS, and it becomes hard to do, anything really. It is VERY noticable while driving too, and it just becomes a headache. There are also only 3 standard graphic options (leaving out gameworks ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t), texture quality, which only has 2 settings, low and normal, shadow quality, which is low-high, and Level of Detail, which is also low to high. This is unacceptable and needs to be fixed. This game shows EVERYTHING that is wrong with modern PC ports and this needs to change.   As stated before, this is one of my new favorite games, but when it comes down to it, this is just an unacceptable port.  *Edit: Will update after a patch is released for a more 'proper' review of the game.

As a small preface, I have played the game for over 100 hours (some offline), and have played through it three and a half times, plus most of the DLC.  Alien: Isolation, unlike it's brand predecessor Aliens: Colonial Marines, stays true to the title by retaining the core horror elements that made Alien so great, and less focus on the sci-fi action that made Aliens so great. As Amanda Ripley you come to Sevastopol for the Nostromo flight recorder to learn what happened to your mother, Ellen Ripley. All hell has broken lose on Sevastopol and now you face danger and terror at every turn, and at the center of it all, the Xenomorph.  Alien Isolation looks long and hard at Alien, and decides what built up suspense for that film was not the terrifying action moments, it was the slow-burning tension, and this game perfectly replicates that. The game paces itself phenomenally, electing to not even show the Alien until potentially an hour and half into the game, letting you get on edge, excited, anxious even.  Then when it comes, you wish you could go back to before in a heartbeat. The Alien is a cunning creature, learning your behavioral patterns and adapting to your strategies. Just because you could distract it with a noisemaker one time, doesn't gurantee that it will fall for the trick again. The terror is very real, and you truly feel as though you are being relentlessly hunted, with the chase only given up at chance times. You carefully think out your every move, you wait for it to leave and ponder if it will quickly run back, you watch in horror as it stalks your room, creeping towards the closet you reside in. Any noise you make can be heard, and likewise with everyone else. Just because you're being careful doesn't mean someone else in the area is, and the Alien will gladly come for them, sweeping you up as a bonus. The AI that Creative Assembly has constructed in this game is one of the most intelligent and adaptive programs to date.  As you wander Sevastopol, you sometimes get glances out of windows, giving you unparalleled splendor to be seen in the wide, expansive views of space, brought together with the hand down, phenomenal lighting effects. While graphics are very servicable, the game truly shines visually with it's dynamic lighting. Swinging lights on the ceiling bounce back and forth, illuminating different locations, while shadows are crisp and fluid. The models themselves have great facial construction, and the Alien is the best we've seen in years.  The game has so many little details added for atmosphere, it truly feels like you're on the set for Alien. Little Drinking Birds sit on desks, common utilites are exactly like in the films, and there is an obvious 1979 Future feel to the world. Computers take time to boot up, save stations require you to wait for the activation lights, and small maintenance minigames look and feel exactly like the film.   The game, while mostly linear, has a sort of Metroidvania feel to it despite you getting everything in order. Most of the time, items you recieve start out weak, but you find upgrades to them, allowing you to access new places and find new secrets. Going off the beaten path can reward you with crafting parts, blueprints, audiologs, and logs from the Nostromo itself.  The game allows you total stealth freedom, giving you numerous craftable devices, different paths to an objective, and dynamic hostiles, which interact with eachother in different ways. You can craft different devices like noisemakers, EMPs, Molotovs, and Pipe bombs, and employ them how you see fit. Missions feel dynamic, and while most have a certain reach a point, activate a thing, return, layout to them, the game keeps it fresh with scripted events, new paths unveiling, and different enemies.  The Alien however, remains the star of the show, but finds himself put to the side for some time to allow for greater world building and pacing. The game isn't afraid of missions dedicated to immersion, with one putting you on LV-426, the planet from Alien and Aliens. It's a complete love letter to the fans, with audio logs recorded by all the original actors (Minus Ian Holm, but you wouldn't think listening to the impersonator). The game spends a period of time without the Alien, most have complained, but what it really does is desenstise you to danger, so when the Alien returns, you get to relive all that introductary fear again. So much attention to world detail really pays off, and objects/locations/style of construction are all near identical to what you see in the film.  All this tension is complemented by the fantastic and dynamic score, rising in excitement when you are spotted, flaring at moments of action, and incurring deep feelings of dread.  Everything in the game just works so well with eachother, the game truly feels like an authentic experience. This is an unbelieveably high reccomendation for any Alien fans, and horror fans will find themselves completely enthralled with the experience. People not into horror games as well will most certainly find plenty to love about the horror hit of 2014.  As a final note, major credit is due to Creative Assembly, who until this point made turn based warfare games, for absolutely nailing everything about the horror genre, first person genre, and Alien universe on their first try.

I love this game more than any other team-based zombie apocalypse survival game I own through Steam. Its very gritty and unforgiving towards blunders made by new players, and yet still has time to put relaxation times in its unbeleivably long levels. The game is built for multiplayer, so playing solo can be alot harder than with others. A few issues: 1. Running zombies are overpowered as all hell. I put 5 55.6 carbine rounds in a normal runner's head and it still slapped the ♥♥♥♥ out of me. I'll blame that on RNG. Plus their damage rolls are pretty ♥♥♥♥ing high. 2. Zombies do a buttload of damage, regardless of difficulty, although on easy you get the respawn function. 3. Melee combat can be very buggy. Zombies are supposed to stumble when hit with a 'power attack' or when beat upside the head. RNG turns it into a 40% chance that it will do so, and if it doesn't, there's a good chance all or most of your health and ammo can be kissed goodbye. 4. The range on the zombies' slaps can be downright ridiculous and enraging. I really don't think a child-turned-flesh eater can stretch its arms over a meter and a half to punch a full grown man half to death.  Other than these issues (which can make me not want to play this anymore, but I keep coming back for the awesome gameplay and music), the game itself is amazing. I love playing while getting lost in the music and atmosphere of the game. I reccomend this for any Left 4 Dead fans or zombie fans in general. ~Doge

I am only here to give you a good and non-biased review to update you on War-- Infestation: Survival Stories changes.   Introduction  While I see many people hating this game and calling it a disaster or burn it with fire. I am safely say that WarZ or what is now called Infestation: Survival Stories, is now launching itself from a once very buggy and untrustful game. I rember when you could die from getting in water, now you can swim in it, I remember when promises of vehicles were never fulfilled, now we have vehicles that are bugged yet are in development for something so much more. I can see the anger of each other these reviewers but I have to say, it's changed alot, now I can say that I recommend this game, but NOT in the state that it is at. I believe all these things will be perfected but the game was just put into steam at alpha stages. I honestly regret buying the game at this time but I still believe if I leave it alone for a while and come back maybe a few months to a year later it will most likely be a great game. But this only relies on the developers themselves to focus on the game. I don't want to see bug fixes every other week, I want to see bug fixes every week, because if you don't you're going to get left behind. You know that if you don't get it to what oyu wanted it to be, Dying Light and DayZ are going to completly obliterate you. And trust me I've played more amount that a mere 6 hours, I played it before on one account and that account was messed up by steam. This is one of those games where you expect one thing and get something completely different, and this is really something good different. Yes it does have some similarities with most games of its style, gather resources, build upgrades, research tech, fight wars etc. I’ll run through the positives then negatives…  The Good; Fairly good AI, with a working diplomacy, this game is not tweaked by cheating but by actually trying to beat you by playing the game with the same rules as you. Excellent factions, each with a unique feel and style, making it worth playing a few times with each new faction/race. Some brilliant mini game mechanics such as winter effects and side quests. Nice battle strategy mechanics with some pleasant character animations, tons of units, and lots of different strategies to try. Army and character customization is excellent and based on resource acquisition.   The Bad; The games can be long not a big completely negative considering the style of game but can mean you take a huge break and when you do finish you are left with lasting end story or cut scene but a simple score scene. This is sad considering how detailed each faction and race is with such a detailed backstory.  Overall excellent game with very little wrong, thumbs up!

Hello everyone! Just finished playing Attila over the free weekend here, and as an experienced Total War player (yay original Medieval on Steam!) that as of yet has not bought any titles from the Rome II series thought I would add my review to the many here.  The Good: 1)- Horde mechanic. Ever since Barbarian Invasion was released a decade ago I have loved the idea of this feature and Attila's development of this to include buildings is promising. I could see future TW releases that take advantage of this. 2)- Factions everywhere! Love to see all the major cultures represented even if the historical accuracy is, um, well, in line with inflammable pigs.  The Bad: 3)- Lack of unique units. Somehow we have stepped back into the realm of Empire's original release, where the board is covered mostly with generic units spiked by a few faction specific units. AoE II did that style 15 years ago. 4)- Diplomacy. Although it is far, far better than the original Rome, the Imperium feature largely makes maintaining vassals and peaceful relations with factions impossible. Just burn it all! :P  The Ugly: 5)- Tactical simulation engine. Ironically the strength of the original Rome, Rome II's battles are still a mess and not a challenge to anyone who has played a TW game. The AI left its generals unguarded in most battles and seemed to be better at randomly swerving back and forth more than anything else. I did not even play or read any tutorials and beat an enemy army that outnumbered me 2 to 1 in my first battle (only on normal difficulty though). Call me crazy, but the battle animations still look beter on my low powered laptop in Medieval II than they do in Attila. 6)- Historical accuracy. I am not sure what is worse: Scythians with Roman onagers, or the fact that every unit can sprout a boat out of nowhere and float around the world. Must we have Huns in boats?  Summary: As a historian I am always cheering for the success of all titles such as this, but I cannot give my approval based on what CA has released in the past. This title featues a combination of some of the difficulties faced by the original Rome game engine (mainly bad diplomacy) and Empire's engine (generic units, bad tactical game engine, numerous CTD bugs) to create thoroughly underwhelming title that overall seems like a step back from Shogun II. Better luck next time CA.

app_name: Game of Thrones - A Telltale Games Series
review_text: Just finished playing the whole game and let me tell you, it's bloody brilliant. The story is fantastic, it takes place during the show's (4th?) season and onward. It always keeps you on your toes. Every decision always has a major consequence in the entire game, there is no right answer.   It's horribly good.

This is a visual novel with large number of endings and also an expansive and well written story. The basics are very standard VN staples (i.e. your character gets dropped into a strange situation with a number of ‘interested’ parties to interact with) but it has been approached with a distinctly Russian (unsurprisingly) twist. The plot is carried through well but gets a little introspective and overly convoluted, the tongue in cheek poke at the rose tinted memory of the soviet era and its institutions is of note. The characters are all memorable and fleshed out well in their respective paths. Its good to have something different i.e. isn’t Japanese or the rubbish vn’s from the US. + Good story with multi-layered plot A different take on the VN standards Good characters Long - The art is of variable quality – a strange chimera of Russian orthodoxy, Manga inspired and Western comic book(some of its good but other bits just look creepy) Sound is basic (understandable on the budget) Occasionally gets over convoluted  Definitely worth adding to your collection and far better than a large number of VN’s you have to pay for

The original Total War: Shogun 2 is probably still my current favorite Turn Based Strategy game, and one of my all time favorites. I've got a lot more hours on this game than indicated in my profile, as I have played it with Steam offline way more often than online. I'm not as much a fan of the Rise of the Samurai Campaign or the Fall of the Samurai stand alone expansion.   Pros:    Excellent graphics for the time, still decent by todays standards.    Individual games are not too long drawn out   Lots of great info on the Sengoku Jidai (waring states period) of Japan   Units on the campaign map as well as in battles respond/talk in Japanese. This is a plus for me as I can understand it all, but it may be a con for people that don't know a lick of Japanese.   Relatively well balanced at this point in time of the game.    Cons:   Some historical accuracies in order to keep things less complicated. Ie: Tokugawa Ieyasu didn't change his name to that until 1567 (game campaign starts in 1545). His father was Matsudaira Hirotada, Daimyou of the Matsudaira Clan based out of Okazaki. The Hattori weren't a clan but a secretive ninja family based in Iga, which wasn't a province but an actual independant republic until Oda Nobunaga conquered it in 1581.    Naval units take way too long to circumnavigate the coast of Japan.   Need a really powerful system to run the game at high resolutions and max details even today.   Certain key figures of the time are missing. Ie: Toyotomi Hideyoshi   Overall, even with the shortcomings, this is a really good game that many fans of Turn-based Strategy games will really enjoy, as well as people that are interested in the fuedal era of Japanese history.   Solid 8.5/10

I want to address the reviews. There's a very clear pattern here. 0hrs - 50hrs are negative, 50-200 are positive, and 200+ are negative. I want to say I completely agree with these reviews, I hated the game until I hit 30 hours, and I see how there isn't enough content for 200+ hours. (I'll try to keep this as short as possible.)  For starters, this game appeals to a VERY small amount of people. You have to be OBSESSED with sci-fi, you have to be okay with grinding, and you have to be okay with sitting around doing nothing for a few mins while youre traveling.   Now that we've got that out of the way, the learning curve for this game is very very steep. You will be dying a LOT when you begin the game, and you won't really find much to do when you first start off. This is why the 0-50hr reviews are mostly negative. However, watching videos and getting help from experienced people certainly helps a bunch.   After you start to understand how the game works and the credits start rolling in, that's where the game truly shines. This golden 10 hour period when things finally click is the best time in the game. It's where you have so many choices and so many things to do that it becomes overwhelming. The 50-150/200hr is where you'll have the most fun.  So to sum it all up, if you LOVE space and sci-fi, if you're okay with a bit of a grind, and if you're okay with nothing happening for a while as you travel, you'll love this game. It's essentially a walking simulator in space with lots of combat, trading, exploration, and politics.   If you're not looking for anything that'll last you too too long (maybe 100-200 hours) definitley pick this game up ASAP. Updates are still rolling in. See you in space, pilots!   EDIT: 250+ hours and I'm finally tired of this game. Take from that what you will.

Mediocre game which has aged terribly.  Here's what I like:  The Game  - It's a digimon game. There's a serious lack of them. - It's an MMO that doesn't run like ♥♥♥. Yay cellshaded graphics.  The Gameplay  - Ancient and simple design. Easy to pick up and understand.  Here's what I don't like:  The Game  - The UI is absolutely terrible and a strain on the eyes. There is a serious lack of graphical options and changes to the placement of UI elements do not persist between sessions. - The sound is terribly loud and the music is unbearable to listen to. A lot of sound choices for certain actions in the game will give you a headache over long sessions. Not to mention a lot of it gets redundant and unamusing pretty quick. - The game looks borderline unfinished There's a lot of unoptimized quests, localization errors, glitched, and unexplained server disconnections.  The Gameplay... Ugh  - This game makes FF14 look like Sonic meets Mario. It is slow, sluggish, and literally a 'press 1 to kill' kind of game. - Leveling at first is pretty rapid, before becoming incredibly slow. Having to level up multiple digimon separately on top of a tamer which levels around 4x slower than they do means lots of grind, lots of back tracking, and a lot of beating your head against the wall (Read Further). - Kill stealing is a huge problem in this game. All rewards and XP as well as quest progression is given to the player who attacks first. This makes doing quests which require killing specific digimon and absolute nightmare. It literally took me 30 minutes of frustration to complete a 'Kill 10 of X' quest. Sound fun? - The Non-English speaking audiance is more than abundant in this game. Coordinating parties or finding English-speaking guilds proved to be impossible. - Egg hatching is a joke. Do not spend any serious effort, time, or money attempting to hatch an egg. 3/5 eggs are borderline worthless. 4/5 is a minimum viable egg and I've already busted 50+ eggs trying to get 1. Of them, I only managed to get 5 to 3/5. The success rate is lower than both the break rate and the failure rate. If you're one of those people who like playing slots, maybe you'll get some amusement out of this - I certainly didn't.  What's sad is, they have had multiple years working on this. A lot of these problems are totally fixable and would improve gameplay a lot. There's a number of glaring issues with the game I didn't feel like touching on because they're simply problems with the game's initial design and instead of fixing them you may as well make a whole new game all together.  Tl;dr - 2/5. Played worse, definitelty not impressed. This game would have some potential if it got some serious developmental support, however as it is, it's impossible to recommend.

Toy Soldiers, is a single-player tower defense game released in 2012. It was developed by Signal Studios and published by Microsoft. The game is set in World War I, which I believe is historically factual, and allows you, the player, to play as the British and Germans in the two main campaigns, and the French in the smaller third campaign. The idea of the game is you defend your Toy Box from enemies of waves. This is an very simple and neat independent game fit for the casual fan. This review will cover the campaign and level design, the gameplay, performance, the issues, and other minor details.  The campaign has four campaigns. The first is the main original campaign featuring 12 missions. You play as the British that defend your position against the attack Germans and their units. The second campaign is 12 missions and encompasses the same maps as the first campaign. The only difference is after the completion of the first campaign as the British, you play as the Germans that attempt to retake those positions in a tower defense manner. The third campaign is 3 missions and you play as the French defending against the Germans. The fourth is an additional 3 mission campaign that you defend against the Germans, which have alien-like units. The campaign's basically recount battles in World War I with backstory, meaning there is no original story. This is nothing bad as it is refreshing to have a game set in WW1. I particularly enjoyed the first and third campaign. The first campaign was very good primarily because it was original and enjoyable. For instance, the campaign had a variety of level designs that fit the historical aspect. The third campaign was fun as you were allowed to play as the British with different level designs. As you can tell, I highly liked this game as two campaigns out of three had various levels. Furthermore, the second campaign was sound, however it became very boring as it progressed that I did not finish it because it featured the same maps as the first campaign. While it was refreshing to have night battles and have it be more challenging, I found it highly repetitive. Also, it continued based off the first campaign. For instance, the first campaign required the British to defend against the Germans. The second campaign required the Germans to retake those British-held positions from the first campaign and then defend against a counterattack. I found that to be thoroughly well done. Lastly, I do commend the layout of all the maps, as it added to the game.  Toy Soldiers featured beautiful artwork alongside light-hearted animations of characters, which captured the overall bubbly feel of the game despite being set in WW1. Furthermore, the game allows you to control the units you build. For instance, I took screenshots in the battles and I had an artillery unit that I controlled and when it shot, one of the little troopers fell off the platform and had to climb back up, which was funny. The game features playable aircraft and tanks. This is a brilliant and immensely fun feature because one wave will have aircraft, so instead of having Anti-Aircraft units defend against the aircraft, you can launch your airplane and dogfight. It is limited to an extent as these aircrafts are not scripted to attack you, rather always attack your toy box and units. The controlling of units fits with the destruction graphics, as buildings, walls, trees, so forth can be destroyed, adding a layer of realism into the game. There are features in the game that I felt could have been expanded on or features that could have been added in the game. For instance, the first wave of every map has an army of your soldiers running onto the battlefield and attacking the enemy units. While this makes for a very cool visual, I felt this could have been expanded upon, just to add some variety into the gameplay. Furthermore, I assume the playability of tanks and aircraft was to counter the limited number of units in the game, as there were only five units in the game with three upgrades. I do somewhat like this approach as playable aircraft and tanks mix up the gameplay of the game, but maybe one-two more units would have benefitted the game. Lastly, the game would have highly benefitted from a feature that is normal in the tower defense genre, the ability to choose what soldiers our units should attack. For example, I should have the ability to 'tell' my mortar team to attack the last units in its range. I felt this slightly hurt the game.  The game has a very nice soundtrack that expresses the nature of WW1 and the music within the years of WW1. Within the game, the soundtrack does frequently glitch when controlling units and through the animations. This is nothing too damaging, though it can get annoying.  I had very little performance issues with the game. For the most part, I had a solid 60 FPS. Only one occurrence I dropped to 55+.  There are some minor issues with the game other than the ones already documented. Things that made the game much more harder was the bug where I could not build a unit on a very important placement. Moreover, there was no saves while in battle, which is very annoying, as things like crashes and little mistakes in-game would have required you to start from the beginning.  Toy Soldiers is a simple and nice game. It features fun gameplay elements and enjoyable campaigns. It correctly executes the little things that spice up the game like the animations of soldiers, the playability of aircrafts, and the soundtrack of the game. This game is good, but it has potential to be even better by fixing the issues, expanding on the core gameplay, and differentiating the second campaign. I played most of this game in 10 hours, though I would say with finishing all campaigns and achieving the extras like achievements, a person can probably get up to 20 hours with the game. At $9.99, I would recommend this game, however, I do not because there is a sequel titled 'Toy Soldiers: Complete'. The Complete edition has Toy Soldiers and the sequel Toy Soldiers: Cold War, and more and is priced at $15. I totally recommend skipping this and buying the Complete edition. This game is a gem. Do not confuse this with AirBuccaneers... this is the vastly superior game. Crews of 4 man two ships per team in this steampunk air combat multiplayer-only game. The graphics are decent enough, and it performs as you would expect. The developers are very active with this title; they recently (as of writing this) updated the game adding a ton of new content. The downsides include the fact that the multiplayer progression system is based on achievements, which means you won't be rewarded with breadcrumbs as much as, say, Call of Duty, and promotions will be few and far between. Also, a lot of power is placed in the hands of the pilot - this often decides games before they even begin. Overall, I had a lot of fun with this title and it impressed me greatly.

review_text: One of the best RPG games I've ever played. 9/10

I was sceptical at first, but after trying out the demo I was hooked. Don't be fooled by the minimal interface or anime-style characters if either bother you - this game has a story to tell and does it in a gripping way, breaking elements into bite-size chunks that make you beg for more. After playing the demo for its full length, I bought the game at full-price and continued to complete it in one sitting. For an entirely narrative-based game, a good 3 hours of gripping gameplay is quite a feat, and with only half the content unlocked and plenty more endings to see, there's a lot of space for replayability! Concept art prizes for completion percentage, too! All I can say is 'give the demo a try'. You'll probably either love it or hate it, but I certainly loved it. This game hit all the right spots at the right times, emotionally connected me with a character, strained that trust, then ultimately rewarded both her and I with catharsis. Worth every penny I paid.

Alien Isolation is an excellent game!  It's a tense, white knuckled (If you're using a controller), heart pounding game! Alien Isolation has it's moments of being truly terrifying. If you want PTSD this is your game! I spent my time crawling through hallways and jumping my gaze from motion tracker to environment. The AI was truly unpredictable, for the alien at least. The others well…. Isolation is at it's best when you're exploring and dodging the alien. There are times when you encounter humans, but their AI is god awful and ALL OF THEM are hostile, not a few like we were told. You also encounter Working Joe's, which are creepy, but no where near as fun as encountering the alien.   In terms of presentation Isolation is one of the biggest mixed bags out there. The environments are drop dead gorgeous. The lighting, fog, sound effects (OH MY GOD THE SOUND DESIGN IS AMAZING), and movie accurate models are excellent. But the characters and their animations are horrible; they all look like sausage links. At one point when Amanda is held at gunpoint her face wasn't animated and she looked bored. Speaking of Amanda she seems like a ghost, she doesn't cast any shadows or reflect from water, visors, or mirrors. This is baffling to me since it's clear that CA spent a lot of time with detailing the environments. Textures are also mixed; on some objects they're jaw dropping; the alien is beautifully detailed and so is the motion tracker. But a good majority of other objects lack detail. The cutscenes were the most jarring, suffering from random black screens (I guess the editor never checked his edits), along with stuttering and lifeless animations.  The story is easily what made me so mad about the game; it sucks! Lacking competent storytelling, Amanda's story screams wasted potential. The voice actors are horrible, except the alien (wink wink). The dialogue is horrible and I didn't give two craps about anyone. None of the characters go through any arcs! I was at least expecting that out of Amanda. Literally no one has a personality to them other than this one guy you meet for about ten minutes at the games start. The story of finding Ellen is overshadowed by such gripping sub plots such as: get a med kit for that one girl, doing other survivor's work, and stopping a person from doing explosions. There was one moment where a character flew into a window and I burst out laughing at how poorly animated it was and at Amanda's 'No!' scream.   Over all though Isolation is an excellent game, it may lack in story and graphics but it gets the most important thing right: the gameplay.

Overall 2.0 Graphics are great and all but do not counteract the other things that hurt this game to the very core.  3/7/2016 Tried the game again after about a year and there are still some day one issues along with a new bug of the alien coming out of player vents, not the ones in the ceiling and giving no notice IE gamesounds as to it moving through said vents, dying with no autosave kills the immersive feeling having to backtrack 30+min and replay the same content due to the new bug.  I was able to get farther than last time but game still remains broken after a year + so I can only NOT RECOMMEND this to people due to the devs flat out fail years later.  Gamescore remains 2.0 / 10

Oh I really enjoyed Transistor. I finished the regular and NG+ playthroughs of the game across a span of about 3-4 days. One could probably rush through the story in as little as 3 hours, or take time unlocking all the lore, doing the side challenges for extra xp and game knowledge, listening to the catchy music, and stopping to smell all the roses along the way and end up taking easily triple or quadruple that.  The game and combat mechanics are pretty fun, with a number of subroutines that you unlock or add to your Transistor weapon as you level up. You form actual skills with those subroutines, using a combination of active slots, support slots (that you slot subroutines into to improve active slots), and four passive slots (buffs to improve your character as a whole). Each subroutine does a different, but thematically similar, thing depending on whether it's currently in an active, passive, or support slot.  There are thus many different possible and valid skill combinations and loadouts, and I found myself constantly swapping back and forth between them as I went through the game, especially once I started doing the side challenges where constraints force you to experiment and find new and efficient (or slow and steady) ways of killing things. In addition, each subroutine represents a character within the game's lore and to unlock the full lore for a character, if so desired, requires you to use their subroutine in each of the three slot types for at least one battle, each slot type unlocking about 1/3 of their character bio.  Combat itself is a combination of real time and planning mode, and is split up largely into a number of self-contained encounters. Planning mode pauses the map and allows you to preview, queue, and execute in quick succession a number of attacks at once in an interface similar to Xcom or Shadowrun Returns, except that you can preview and rewind options before finally committing. There is no miss chance, or random skill damage, damage is constant but modified by variables depending on if the enemy is stunned or backstabbed, for example.  Carrying out actions in the planning mode enables them all to be done in less than a game time second, so it become a power play of sorts whenever planning mode is up, but each ability used in planning mode has a cost which is subtracted from the remaining amount of action points you have in that round of planning. Once you leave planning mode, however, you cannot use most skills until the cooldown for the next planning round is up, leaving you with a few seconds of vulnerability between planning rounds. However, skills used during real time do not trigger a cooldown, and so in some cases it is very beneficial to fight things in real time (or a combination of the two) instead of always in planning mode.  An interesting mechanic that I did not figure out until I was into NG+, was that while you have a certain number of action points to spend per round, not actually spending them all allows your downtime before you next skill use to be proportionally smaller, which is critical versus a certain enemy in the game. On the flip side, spending 'over' what you normally have in planning mode locks your skills for that much longer before you can use them again!  The story in the game was pretty neat, if a little on the short side, with a narration style similar to Bastion, but this time the narrator is also the Transistor and with you from the start. Like Bastion, you are dumped right into the plot to start off, and the story is revealed in pieces as you go along. The narration is touching in places, boring in others, but overall quite well done. That ending though. It came unexpected to me the first time, as I didn't think that was the end of the game yet, but hit harder the second time through for me when I knew what was coming. I didn't want to end the game. I did love the ambience though! A combination of the music and the dystopian steampunk background scenery. I am a sucker for night-time big city skylines, and the art in this game evoked so many wistful memories.  A couple cons of the game: It's very easy to miss certain lore terminals and the game often doesn't let you back up if you went a little too far in one direction. It is often not obvious which way is 'onward' and which way is a dead end with a lore piece. Your only options then are to load back from the last save (and you can only save at certain locations, though there is pretty much one before or after nearly every fight) or wait until NG+ to see what you missed. Or YouTube. The narrator is also overly talkative at times, though I guess that's a personality trait. There are also several plot holes in the story to me still, like *why* the Transistor's ownership got passed over to Red, and several other things I feel are little plot holes here and there that are not adequately explained.

I figured Rogue Legacy would be a solid homage to the Metroidvania genre with a nice, difficult twist, but it goes so much further than that. With its compelling progression and customization systems and an excellent use of randomization, Rogue Legacy is a clear and definitive step forward for the genre.  Any action-platformer game hinges on the player developing a set of reproducible skills -- learning how to read a room, confront enemies and chart a path through treacherous territory. Later Metroidvania games like Aria of Sorrow and Other M shied away from this and instead emphasized character empowerment and customization to the point where base skills like dodging and timing became almost unnecessary. Rogue Legacy deftly solves this problem by randomizing every room on each playthrough while also forcing the player to accept a unique set of strengths and weaknesses on each play session thanks to the game's hereditary system. After a couple hours, the player starts to understand the logic of the game: how each region works, where the greatest challenges are, how to chart a path through the castle and how to compensate for unexpected adversity. It's a brilliant example of a game that teaches the player a difficult and complex system at a steady pace over dozens of hours of play.  If you're a fan of Metroidvania games or a fan of challenging action-platformer games, you're more than likely going to get your money's worth out of Rogue Legacy. But if you appreciate dissecting and understanding great game design, Rogue Legacy is absolutely essential.

So what is wrong with this game? - Each time when launching the game you are auto-redirected out of the game to a news page. - The newspage is totally blank... - The settingsmenu is hidden - The pre-configured inputkeys for keyboard and pre-configured controller setup is mindbraking.  * keyboard left and right arrowkeys are inverted  * controller wheel steering is configured to go back in the UI menu. Meaning you can not discover that a settingsmenu exists. I had to unplug the wheel from the pc to be able to go to the settingsmenu, plug it back in to adjust all keys, X and Y axis, etc.  - There is no decent force feedback support (a core part for a racegame) - There is no option to modify deadzones of controller-input (a core part for a racegame) - There is no hud cam (a core part for a racegame) - The driving experience is arcade to the bone. After having driven a kilometer ingame I'd seen enough of the entire game. There is no challenge to be found in this game.  On a scale to 10 I give it a 0 for a pc game. The game is made for the console and should have stayed there.

Though, the difficulty is always fair (except Level 8's midboss) - you won't be seeing any instant-death spikes or bottomless pits here.. with the exception of one level, where the bottomless pits are entirely justified, given the environment. ('Open the air locks!') ~ Given that, there still are the 'Easy' and 'Casual' difficulties, which reduces the damage you take from enemy attacks and gives you regenerative health, if you find the game too hard.  HIGHLY recommend!

This review is written over a year after I completed it.  Here's why.  I used to play a few JRPGs every now and then. I'm not a super fan of the genre, but I love RPGs overall and I don't hate the semi-turn based skill selection combats.  But for a good long time I disliked every and each JRPG I played. Bull**** stories, less and less player control each game, tons of text containing only small talk (I love reading long text as long as it's meaningful), and crap controls slowly made me hate the genre.  Many recent games that people love, because they love JRPG are IMHO bad games. Bad in every possible meaning except cute graphics.  Now, however I've finished Child of Light in a few days. I couldn't stop playing it until it was over. I didn't see why back then, but now I do.  This game is smooth. It doesn't take away player control every minute. Combat is really good too.  It has a decent story. It's a very classic fairy tale, but it doesn't bother you with bull**** small talk for hours. Both the PCs and NPCs are well designed and make you actually care about them.  The graphics are more beautiful than most JRPG.  Sidescrolling RPG is a great idea.  Music is great, too.  All in all even if you aren't into JRPGs, I can't recomment this game enough.

Why is this marketed as Action, Adventure and Platformer??  Anyhow, it's fun but lacks depth. I have never played this 'Super Crate Box' that reviewers speak of, so the game this reminds me of is Super Smash Bros., except much simplified and not as spectacular. The main goal is collecting muffins while killing and avoiding an endless stream of enemies at the same time. This all takes place on a very small stage (like Smash Bros), but the weird twist is your character is randomly changed to a different character every time you collect a muffin. Each character has only one attack. Probably the worst thing for me, personally, is there is no way to 'beat' a stage, unless you count getting five stars as beating it. But in Smash Bros., for example, you beat the enemy and that's how you pass the stage. In this game, there's an endless stream of enemies and your goal is to stay alive long enough (and collect enough muffins) to upgrade your characters and unlock new stages. All you are doing is trying to beat your own highscore, pretty much. So this feels like one big mini-game to me. Seems like it will get boring soon, but I haven't tried multiplayer yet.

This game took me by surprise.  I was expecting something scary with nice visuals, I got some much more than that. As usual with Tale of Tales, you're not going to run around and be rewarded with an achievement everytime you poke something (call it a walking simulator if you must). And like Sunset and The Graveyard (other ToT games I've played), The Path may not have strong graphics but its aesthetic is incredibly strong. Each girl has a story and a personality you can get glimpses of as she comments on what she sees; and as you lead her through the forest, she meets her 'wolf' - which is where you can go wild with theories. After that, she ends up in a bizarre, Lynch-like version of grandmother's house...and disappears. Let's be clear: if you expect the game to clearly state what happens and why (and I like that type of games too!), you're not going to be happy. The Path expects you to find something in the imagery and girls' fate that resonates with you, and work with it. I'm happy to do that and I felt a strong connexion with these girls who are so subtly defined, but that's not a given.

The first few hours were great. It was exciting, I had a goal of surviving and getting off the planet. But a few hours in, things started get frustrating. Where it used to be exciting getting to a new planet and seeing what it was like, soon the planets just feel the same. There are limited types of planets. Animals on different planets look the same, buildings are identical on all planets, the flora are identical apart from the colours, all alien species act the same, ships are simply cosmetic differences with inventory slots. Variety is limited. Mining becomes too tedious. And even then, I was willing to push on, grind it out for hours to see whether there was more down the line. I grinded and finally bought a new ship and realised it was just the same but with a larger inventory - an inventory that ultimately didn't matter, because there isn't anything fulfilling to use it for.  The design flaws I'd been happy to put up with became too intrusive; the inventory system is really bad. So many of the features promised are simply not in the game, and while I was willing to ignore that and simply enjoy it for what it was, it couldn't hold my interest for long enough. I can't recommend this game at the price they are asking. It is a 15-20 dollar game at best.  And all of this pains me to say, because it has so much potential. The possibilities were endless. But at the end of the day, Hello Games has over-promised (I'd even go so far as to say they lied, or promised stuff they later realised they couldn't implement. It isn't simply over-hype, there are features blatantly missing) and they under-delivered.   There are some good aspects I should mention for the sake of fairness. The colour palette is beautiful. The first 4 or 5 hours were really exciting and gave me a great gaming experience I won't forget easily. So, if I had to give it a score, I'd say 5 out of 10.

The Fall is a 2D sidescroller with light elements of puzzle and combat. You play as Arid, a combat suit tasked to protect the individual inside as they both fell from the sky into an abandoned facility. One of the most fascinating things about the game is how the game explores the relationship the suit or it’s AI has with it’s wearer (or pilot in this case). The game also explores what it means to be a machine and how much of a ‘machine’ they really are when they are gifted a degree of self-awareness only a living breathing individual might possess. Both Arid and the other AIs are constantly tasked to withhold their protocols while at the same time bending it if it means they are to achieve their goal, which for Arid — protecting her pilot at all cost. This forces Arid to intentionally put her pilot in danger only to open up certain protocols to better serve her pilot. I found this wrestle between being programmed for and self-awareness to change the programming parameters to meet her goals really fascinating.  The game is very atmospheric. The sound and visual art style are played to their strengths. The game does not use much color, however it uses the little color palette it is given to highlight the world to a maximum effect. Same goes with sound with only important audio cues related to gameplay are highlighted to give spatial awareness to the player.  The game has light puzzle elements which majority of the gameplay is comprised of. They aren’t very complicated, however often they are not very clear either and might induce a bit of trial and error when trying to solve them. There is a very basic layer of combat thrown in there and is one of the weakest parts of the gameplay. However there aren’t many instances of the combat to become cumbersome.  The biggest frustration came from the controls. The way you keep holding right analog to target and right shoulder button to enter inventory and finally press A to accept — all this just for using an item is incredibly frustrating. There are simply too many unnecessary steps for the player to do just about everything. Luckily the developers have acknowledged this issue and are working to resolve it for their next game.  So this sums up The Fall. A great sci-fi story wrapped around a game which took me by a surprise. While it is a shame the controls are very annoying, but it did not matter in the grander scheme of things. I am already looking forward to the sequel.  8/10 - Very Good

This may be the most incredible single player RPG I have ever played.  CD Projekt Red has always produced quality content, and this game only reinforces that claim. I had high expectations, and CD Project Red did not just meet those expectations, they shattered them. Everything about this game is beyond great. From the visuals to the story to the combat to the dialogue, this game is a masterpeice.  Pros:     Amazing visuals. One of the most beautiful games I have ever played.   The music and audio is beyond fantastic. The combat music never fails to get my blood flowing, and the general ambient audio adds an immersion level that I never thought was possible to achieve   The combat is challenging and exhilarating. Do not expect to have your hand held duing combat. There aren't even any iframes!    The story is fantastic and well written. Ever got lost in a great adventure novel? Unable to put the book down and go to sleep because you need to know what happens next? The same will happen when playing Witcher 3.   Massive open world with (almost) no loading screens. Its true the world is seperated into zones, but those zones are MASSIVE. So massive in fact, that you will probably forget about loading screens for hours at a time.   Replayability. You beat the storyline for the first time? Great! Now do it 3 or 4 more times with different outcomes! The choices you make even at the beginning affect you at the end.   Content. The open world of this game is PACKED with stuff to do, from diving for treasure, uncovering hidden caches, slaying beasts much more powerful than you, to clearing wildlife from areas to allow the residents to move back in, and much, much more.   Questing is interesting and fun. Sure, some of the side quests are standard fetch quests, but they dont  feel  standard in the slightest. Honestly one of the best questing experiences ive ever taken part in.   DLC. CD Projekt Red will be releasing DLC for this game for free which is a really cool thing to do.   Cons:   Buggy. In an open world game like this, there's always going to be bugs, and this game is no exception. The worst I have experienced personally is nVidia driver crashes.   Proprietary nVidia technology. With Hairworks enabled, AMD users can expect a significant drop in FPS (there are fixes out there though). This game also relies on some gameworks libraries which blocks AMD from being able to optimize.   Hardware demanding. With Maximum visual settings enabled, i was pulling about 46fps on average with an i5-4670k @ 4.0GHz and 2 GTX 970s in SLI.   I can 100% recommend this game to anyone looking for a magnificent and immersive singleplayer experience, or a story driven RPG. You will not regret buying this game.
