Exploration is improved by quite a lot although I can't really say much about the combat in the game. Some features (Like the crafting throwables) felt tacked on, not really fun to play with and littered the playing field with rubish. Overall, some plus and minus which leads to a slight improvement imo.   Biggest let down is the story. First one is fantastic where Lara is shown, sorta a damsel in distress at the start. As the story progresses, Lara was shown gradual transformation by surviving life threatening events and some very rude awakenings. One of the best part of the first reboot is the almost at the end where Lara has become such a badass her mere presence sent her enemies scampering and she was shouting, intimidating and taunting them.  On the sequel? Not so much. It tries to create drama on her relationship with her dad yet it rarely show ANYTHING about her direct interaction with him. Very few dad/daughter scene and we were asked to just take it in faith that she's driven by her dad's past. Right. And the ending? ugh........ I was expecting more from the team.  Another minus is that I beat this game in 12 hours in hard mode :(  I still enjoyed the game though, so take this recommendation with a grain of salt.

Basically you play as Gordon Ramsey and a guy comes into your resturant (Hells Kitchen) and he's an absolute big job so Gordo kicks off 1 at him but it turns out the man is a TIME WIZARD and casts Gordy far into the future to the planet Zog, fortunatley the ALIUMS there have the technology to send him back but that crap is expensive yo and those ALIUMS gots to get PAID. So Gordo is tasked with appeasing each council member so they'll send him back to his little restaurant where he shouts at people and SPEAKS LOUDLY AND SLOWLY.  Each council member has a different twist that reflects and parodies different common tropes and clichés of the modern gaming industry. For example one stage sees you fighting zombies in an urban/industrial environment, whilst another would see you in a modern military shooter scenario complete with the wubwub soundtrack.  Whatever your videogame tastes are, Gordon's Kitchen Massacre is a real crowd pleaser and cleverly parodies tired videogame clichés which is often genuinley funny, the authentic voice acting really brings the Gord to life and makes you feel as though you really are the chef from hell('s kitchen)  11/10 would buy again, game of the year, every year.  nah just kidding it's rubbish

I thoroughly enjoyed this game, and it has actually been one of the best games I've played for getting me to actually care about the characters within it. The controls are a little less responsive than some other games we are used to but I enjoyed the fact that they were challenging as it meant you as a player developed an attunement to the controls as the dragon got older and thus there was a parallel between the growth of player and character.  Also my goodness I have never cared so much about a tiny green sprite before, The Cheif (Lizardman with the red cloak) as I will call him was my favourite and I was totally in the mindset to defend him from anything and that was really interesting for me. Normally I'm faily distant with NPC's as games will have story points or dialogue basically explaining 'How you like this character' which really doesn't work with me, but the Chief doesn't say much, it is never stated that you character likes him or that you have some in game reason to care for him. He's just there, doing his own thing, being self sufficient and just being there to help you along. It's really quite stright forward but it worked amazingly well for me caring for him.   Now I'm not sure if the Dev's read/can read this but if they can, I reaaaaaaly like the fact that the NPC's are self sufficient, or at least that they aren't relying on you as a player to survive. Their lives and deaths appear like they will go on without the players interaction and that is really powerful because it makes them feel real. There isn't this fake sense of 'The world revoles around the player' it's just things happen and characters deal with it and it's up to you as the player to decide if you want to help or not.  I think that it is just really darn good because in real life people don't just instantly rely on someone who turns up out of the blue and neither do the NPC's in this game.

Writing this review, I'm 7 hours into the game. So, what can I say about it ? Is it possible to review GRID2 without mentionning GRID ?  I don't think so. So, let's kick it off with the new things. The things they added. - The LiveTrack. 100% unrealistic, painful if you like to know the tracks you are racing on, the LiveTrack is used for classic races or Endurance... Speaking of which: - Endurance mode. Basically, it's a fancy name to try and make you believe it's also brand new, when it's really just a race that ends at a specific time rather than a specific line. - Story. Let's face it, the first GRID was minimalist, story speaking. But it also didn't need it, and the lack of it - or the small presence of it - didn't bother me. This time, however, there is a scenario. This time, you are building the WSR, the World Series Racing. Some cinematics, some (fake) coverage by ESPN... It definitely feels more alive.  Now, for the things they modified or deleted. - Cameras. No more interior camera - which is a shame, we'll admit it - and, more importantly, totally trashed up TV cameras - taking away all the value of the replays. No really, replaying your best drifts on GRID was like watching 2 Fast 2 Furious, in GRID2 it feels like the cameraman browses /r/trees on a daily basis. - Teammate. It was fun, knowing your fellow crew member was out there, helping you out on the track, pushing opponents out of the track, stealing the first place and apologizing deeply when he finished last. Now, all you have is the guy talking to you since day 1 (but you still don't know who it is) and complaining he'll have to mend your car when you wreck it. - The Drifting. It's hard to explain. But the drifting in GRID felt a hundred times better. Firstly, you were  alone  on the track, without stupid people pushing you out of the way because they forgot which race category they were in. Secondly, the vibe was astonishing - the japanse speakerman going crazy over your moves, the crowd... And lastly, it just felt more responsive. The combo system was nice and clear, the way the car drifted was purely arcade but so enjoyable... - No more rep/money system. GRID2 brings in the 'fan system', already seen in Blur, for example.  - Aaaaaand the way the sponsors work changed too. And this time, it clearly is below what GRID offered.  That's not all, these are just the things that pop out first when I think about it. Now, the graphics are really nice, there are plenty of cars to choose from, and the tracks are still interesting. So ?  1) Stay away 2) Only recommended when on sale  3) Recommended  4) Highly recommended 5) This is a must play  It's probably still better to grab it while it's on sale - hint: it's really cheap - but if you like arcade racing, and/or the first GRID, well, go for it.

Best expository-note-reading sim EVER!   Seriously though, I really did enjoy this game despite its flaws. Anyone who has played Alan Wake knows that Remedy can craft an excellent story and bring it to life in a cinematic way. And much of Quantum Break does this... sort of... Unfortunately, there's a lot of the story (and I mean  a lot ) that you have to find out by reading notes, emails, signs and so on. I seriously think that as much as 40%-50% of my play time was spent reading - to be fair, this time is a little inflated due to my dyslexia, but, even if you can read twice as fast as I do, that's still  way  too much time spent reading. A video game is an interactive, audio-visual medium so when you have to get large chunks of exposition by reading office memos and emails it really pulls you out of the game. On numerous occasions the pacing and storyline momentum came to a screeching halt when I'd open a door only to find a room full of bulletin boards tacked with notes, memos on desks and open computers, all flashing, all telling me, 'Hey! Know that action-packed story you were really getting into? Well, buckle up 'cause here's 30 minutes of reading all about it!' And far too often I found that I'd have to be reading these items  while  I was supposed to be following another character. 'Oh, yeah, I know we're kinda in hurry to get out of this building before that corporate-mercenary death-squad breaks down the door and kills us both, but first I just gotta check a few multi-page memos and read through a half dozen emails full of exposition.'  What's upsetting is that the story  is  really good; it just feels like they weren't given enough time to create the game that they wanted and were forced to shoe-horn in half the story in text format into the game they had so far. The plot is engaging, the acting is excellent and I even liked the idea of watching 4 20-minute live-action 'episodes' between the acts that can go one of two ways depending on the choice you make before the episode. The shooting portion of the gameplay, while I found enjoyable, was nothing special - the game's strength is in its story. I just really wish they had taken out 80% of notes, emails, etc. and put that information into the dialogue, gameplay and cut-scenes.  On the technical side, the game runs alright. Apparently, when the game was first released as a 'Universal Windows App' - a phrase I hope to  never  hear again in the same sentence as a AAA game title (sorry, Gears of War Ultimate Edition; sadly, your ill fate was established before you were even released) - it used DirectX 12 and ran like absolute garbage. Now, a few patches later and a switch to DirectX 11, the game has been released from its purgatory on the Windows Store and can be sold on Steam, and it runs far better than it did. That's not to say that it runs really well - I'm running it on a system with an R9 390 GPU, an Intel 4790K 4.0 GHz CPU and 32 GB of RAM at a resolution of 1920x1080 and I had to turn down several settings from their max just to get it running at a stable 50 fps or more. By default, the game is set to render at a lower resolution and upscale to the resolution you choose, and even has a tip next to the setting that tries to dissuade you from turning it off. The scaling doesn't look terrible but it's not as crisp as I'd like it. I don't know exactly what resolution it renders at when scaling up to 1920x1080 but I found using it can add anywhere from 10-25fps to its performance. It's, no doubt, a graphically demanding game (just look at the trailers), and, while it performs far better now than it did on release (at least from what I've read, and if you doubt me I suggest you look it up for yourself), it's not as well optimized as I'd hoped, but if you have the hardware it’s definitely playable and will look better than it does on console.  I will recommend this game, but, unless you've been dying to play it, I'd wait until the price drops to ~$32 or less. It's a great game but its flaws are far too glaring to ignore.

I think the developers need to look up what some of the objects actually look like and not place more than one of the same type of item in each puzzle. It was difficult completing some of the hidden object puzzles without using the hint button because the object I was looking for was not the same as the object listed. Several puzzles have this issue. I'm not even close to finishing the game and I've had to use hints to find the last item of each puzzle.  Items I was supposed to be looking for vs what it actually was:  Watch - hint pointed to a clock (can't remember which puzzle).  Boat Shack: Net (needed to be more specific since there were mulitple fishing nets everywhere) - hint pointed to a butterfly net. Barrel and had multiple barrels - hint pointed to a bucket.  Trunk Outside Boarding School Front Door:  Jar - hint pointed to a soda can. Paper bag - There is a paper bag in the back of the trunk in front of the postcard. That wasn't correct. It was apparently a torn corner of an envelope with a stamp on it. Postage stamp - stamp off the postcard and the torn envelope corner were not correct. It was an item that looked like the back of a blue Bicycle playing card. Beaker - item was a corked vial.  In the Fountain: Trumpet - hint pointed to a curved lead pipe. Frame - was some bent metal wire next to the item that looked like an empty picture frame. Twig - was some kind of grass leaf.  Basement: Racket - was a ping pong paddle.  Desk in the Boarding House: Candlestick - the one on the desk is not the right one, it's the one behind the chair.  Upstairs Bathroom: Brush - was a feather duster. Flasks - were the test tubes / vials.  Upstairs Room at end of hall up the ladder: Bundle (bundle of what exactly??) - was the rubber band ball.  Also, you have to be really exact when clicking on an item, otherwise it doesn't register. Other times i'm clicking exactly on something and it doesn't take until about 4-5 clicks later.  I have yet to finish this game and I would not recommend it.

I have to admit, at first I was skeptical about Bioshock Infinite. I am a massive fan of its predecessors but the small amount of information that I had heard about the third game in the series had me unsure of whether it would carry the legacy or be a brutal flop. Finally, I gave this game a try and I was blown away by it.  Instead of making a return Rapture, Bioshock Infinite brings you to the flying city of Columbia. Your character, Booker DeWitt has made his way to this city to find a girl who you have been hired to collect. From very early on the atmosphere is built in such an amazing way that I can think of few other games that can compete with it. The story is full of incredibly well-written plot twists, one that even made me stare at the screen in shock which is quite the accomplishment for a game. Obviously, I want to avoid spoilers but I will whole-heartedly slam down my stamp of approval on the story from start to finish.  The gun play and vigors (the new plasmids) are very reminiscent of the earlier Bioshocks and not a whole lot has changed there. It's still an enjoyable first-person shooter but these mechanics are nowhere near as mind-blowing as the story. I did, however, find a good deal of enjoyment from a few of the new guns and vigors that were tossed into the game for us to have fun with.  In conclusion, there isn't a whole lot more that I can say about this great game without dropping any spoilers. I had a great time with it and played it through fairly quickly; I had beaten it in around twelve hours and that includes the time I spent searching around the area for additional content to find. Though short, it is most definitely worth picking up, especially on a sale, and I highly recommend it to all who enjoyed the first two Bioshock games. You will not be disappointed.

May contain spoilers.  This is a prime example of reasons I dislike modern shooters. First of all: When I bought this, it wasn't even quarter-way finished. There was nothing that acted as, or could substitute a tutorial. This game is incredibly complex. Second of all: It didn't even have a full campaign. Only one pathetic episode where the most exciting thing you did was run away from paratroopers. Woohoo. I do not like that to do exciting things, like drive tanks and fly stuff, you have to complete all the way to the third episode. I'll get to that in a minute, but the only vehicles you got to use in the first and second episodes were a truck and a passenger car.  The developers forgot that fun is the most important thing in games. When in the second episode you were supposed to drive a bunch of people at night and get stopped three times for a total of 15 minutes, I was hoping something exciting would happen at the last stop which was significantly longer than the other two, like people would start shooting and I would get to escape in the car, but no. I literally wasted about 15 minutes of my life. It was the worst 15 minutes of my life.  Now I'll get to why I can't even get to the exciting part. There's a certain mission with ambushing a convoy, but the game doesn't teach you what exactly you're supposed to do. You're given so much freedom on this one, that the game doesn't even teach you how to do it. You don't even know what you can or can't do. There are other missions like that which I managed to pass by luck.  There really is a huge map for you to use. Shame that you never actually get to explore the 'open' world which is restricted by missions. I mean, why would you create a giant map, and then not let the player use it how they want to? I would give Loadout 4 out of 5 stars.   I absolutely enjoy seeing a free game, and love it even more when it is a fun one. Loadout not only lets you customize your character's appearance but also your weapon. With the starting base of a rifle, you can build anything from a machinegun to a shotgun to a sniper rifle. There are also rocket launchers, beam rifles, and plasma rifles; all of which have there own customizable pieces.   Game types are pretty standard of most shooter games, and are laid out on some fun levels. You have your typical slayer and slayer objective games, as well as Capture the Hammer (CTF). All of the levels are large enough to give you breathing room when you need it, but aren't large enough for you to get lost in them.   I enjoyed seeing the level split for games. Players levels 1 - 9 are stuck playing normal games, but once you hit level 10 you can move on up to ranked matches. You get the choice to start your own group or to jump right in with random players. You can also choose to play a game against bots if you want to learn the control before playing against others.  Another thing I enjoy about this game is the 'daily prizes.' You have the chance of winning not only in-game currency to go toward upgrading your weapon, but you can also win character appearance items that you would normally have to pay for.   I think the biggest problem I have with this game is that you cannot use the standard in-game currency to buy character appearance items, but instead have to purchase the currency required. I think it is best for a game to give you the option of saving up the standard currency instead of giving you only one option. I think it should be the same for the weapon modifications as well. There should be an option to pay for those upgrades with the purchased currency if the player so chooses to do so.   Overall, I really enjoy this game. It's a fast paced, fairly simple shooter game with lots of customization options for the players. Also, it's free! I recommend this to anyone looking for a fun shooter game.

Arkham Origins is another valued contribution to the Arkham series. It is set as a prequel to the prior two games.   However, it does suffer majorly from bugs. This will show up with flying and some of the functionality of the downloadable content. For instance, sometimes when I glide, Batman will shake and will glide up. If using an alternate Batsuit, you cannot interrogate suspects. There are a few other minor instances, but those are big ones.  Past that, I felt it was a great game. It had elements of Batman: Year One and Knightfall in it. The gameplay was fun. The addition of the Batplane to transport me to checkpoints in the city really made things nicer. Not having to fly everywhere like Arkham City was bliss. The story was mostly straightforward, but it's hard to do twists for this game when we know what's coming from the prior games.   The combat system was modified slightly, adding characters that can counter your attacks. A great contribution in my opinion. Were we to presume that no one with any martial arts experience would be hired to fight Batman?  The Riddler is there in his usual likeable and annoying self. Somehow he's managed to leave things everywhere for us to find. Again. A word of advice to those people who do get the game. Don't run around in Detective Vision all the time. You'll miss out on all the work they put into the game.   The boss fights take figuring out. Don't be afraid to make backups and restart.   Have fun and knock yourself out. Though I really recommend knocking villains out, it's up to you.

FTL is a great game, especially if you like games you always lose at. FTL is a great roguelike, with a strange almost strategy aspect to it. Its nice to be able to command your crew around your ship, strengthening or repairing different parts. However, its extremely difficult to manage a crew while fighting another ship and being boarded by enemy crewmen. Thank god for autofire, right? On one hand, I really love this game. On the other hand, I'm disappointed. While it is really fun, it could've been made on a low budget, but instead the developers (a two man team) asked for $10,000 on Kickstarter. They got much more than they asked, and ended up with over $200,000. That's great, right? I mean surely this means they can hire more people, or spend more time working on features and things. WRONG. They continued to work on it as they had, and ultimately made a game that while great, could be a lot better. Luckily, that's my only real complaint about FTL. It's still amazing, and hopefully they will release add on content in the future. The game is all random, so you really have no idea what will happen next. As you plot your jumps running from the rebel fleet, you will be constantly attacked by pirates, rebels, and confused 'allies'. You will find burning space stations, and have the options of trying to put out the fire, dock to rescue survivors, or leave them to their unfortunate fate. But you don't know what will happen when you choose one. If you dock for survivors one time, maybe you save them and bring them home, and gain another crew mate. You do the same random event later, and fail to rescue the people, and end up losing a crew member to the fires. You never know what will happen next, and that is a truly great feeling. Overall, the graphics aren't much (but they aren't bad either), the gameplay is incredible, the story is generic (but the species and relations are interesting), and the game is very easy to run. 8 / 10

Resident Evil 5 is designed to be played cooperatively from start to finish. For good or ill, you're either ideally playing with a human partner or you have an AI companion in tow throughout the entire game. This affects every aspect of the followup to the masterpiece, RE4: from the inventory system, the level design, the difficulty, the puzzles... it all revolves around two characters in play. It's a very different game from previous entries, veering more towards monster arena shooter than any type of horror or traditional tense atmosphere.     It's a very “brown” game, released in an era when that was generally used as an insult toward generic shooters with limited color pallets. Here it's fitting, with the majority of your time spent out in the sun bleached African slums and savanna. Playing as Chris Redfield with your partner Sheva, you're almost immediately assaulted by hordes of a new strain of infected locals. The enemies are numerous and nimble, testing your aim and keeping you on your toes as you try to avoid getting trapped in a corner. You still do not have the ability to aim and shoot simultaneously, so combat is a test of pinpoint accuracy and situational awareness. The first two thirds or so of the game feels very monotone in both look and action; you're fighting similar enemies in brown arenas, broken up only by the occasional on-rails shooting setpiece or boss fight.     The action isn't bad, just on the repetitive side and it doesn't hold up particularly well compared to newer titles that have improved and iterated on the formula. Most encounters boil down to popping some shots off while standing still, running away, doing a quick turn, repeat. Thankfully, the last two chapters add some variety and make the entire game better by proxy. Exploring a brightly lit, futuristic underground lab and a well realized cargo ship are nice visual changes of pace. Your tactics also get a major shakeup as armed infected now start to regularly shoot back at you with automatic weapons and come in armored forms, causing you to really think about where you aim before wasting ammunition. Mechanics like taking cover behind walls are also introduced, and the stop and pop pace is threatened by cattle prod carrying psychos slowly encroaching. The urgency and intensity explodes in the final act, moving the game's needle from fairly generic third person shooter to something definitely worth playing.     Resident Evil 5 would be unplayable if your AI companion sucked, but for the most part Sheva does what you expect her to do. While she does have the habit of getting in the way of your aiming laser from time to time or thinks it's a good idea to be up your ♥♥♥ when fighting a boss with an obvious weak spot on it's back, she's more or less competently autonomous. You have full control over her inventory, so equipping her with a sniper rifle and loading her up with ammo is a good idea as she'll provide decent cover support. The concessions to cooperative play actually streamlines the inventory system in a good way; the game does not want you to pause at any time, so hitting the inventory button brings up an easily accessible grid. Once you understand the options, using, dropping, combining, and swapping items between characters is ultra snappy and intuitive. If it went back to a Resident Evil Zero style system this game would have been a ♥♥♥♥ing disaster.     Like most games in the series, Resident Evil 5 is built to be replayed multiple times. Along with a New Game Plus mode that let's you play as Sheva in single player, experiencing her point of view during the limited times she splits up from Chris, there's also the straight arena, score based Mercenaries Mode. You earn points for playing the game in most modes which can then be spent to unlock characters for Mercenaries, costumes for the main game, collectible figures, visual filters, etc. There's quite a bit of game here. There's even a versus multiplayer mode, but that was garbage even when the game was in it's prime.     Included in the “Gold Edition” is two single player (well, coop...) DLC missions; one has Jill and Chris returning to the Spencer mansion from the first Resident Evil. If that sounds great, hold your horses; for some reason they took the time to model almost the entire mansion in the RE5 engine, then did almost nothing with it. You fight a total of four enemies... the same type of enemies... then have a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ Wesker boss fight and the whole thing is over in an hour. That's disappointing. The other DLC mission, Desperate Escape, is better though not really essential. It's a side story with Jill and some helicopter pilot that was in the main game for two minutes, and you're bombarded with enemies the entire time. It culminates in a Horde Mode-style defense where you're tasked with holding out against increasingly difficult enemies while waiting for a timer to tick down. It's not bad.

I've played many the first person shooters in my time. This game does a good job of making a sort of tactical shooter such as arma more fast paced like call of duty. No panic knife to instantly kill someone shooting you because you got startled and clenched your controller or similiar. The players are varied, as you can expect but luckily most of the ones ive played with acted like adult gamers should. This game has a rather steep learning curve. That is to say, you are not rambo, and the enemy will put you down quicker than hardcore mode on CoD. Which is good, the challenge keeps 10 year olds away for the most part.   Its an indie game so you have to give it a break when you see computer code every so often. It has a few hiccups but none of them are game breaking. This game rewards teamwork but punishes 10 people in a crowded stairway against an RPG. The AI varies in intellect, accuracy, and tactics. It makes it feel a bit more real as not every AI enemy behaves exactly same way. Though I admit to finding it curious that the muslim enemies yell 'grenade' or 'reloading' in english. The class restrictions are well placed and every gun seems to have its uses. I was and still am very impressed with this game, not only as an indie game but as a first person shooter as well.   It's worth the time to check out. Not gonna set you back $60 and I didnt find any mircotransactions. If youre looking for a fresh experience and an alternative to the usuals of the genre, try out this game.

After a lot of hours poured into trying to understand and enjoy this game, I just can't. As much as I love sims and management games, there are too many half-baked ideas and over-simplified systems.  - Zoning is a nice idea, but the brush to set zones is massive and impossible to use with any real precision. The handful of pixels that make up stations, is easily smothered by the massive zoning tool. And precision can be the difference between a perfectly priced line and a total failure.  - Ticket pricing is extremely finicky. Doing absolutely nothing but letting the simulation run can see costs fluctuate from $1 a ticket, up to $30, and back down to $1. All over the course of the week, and with no discernable pattern(rush-hours, night time, mid-day, weekend, doesn't matter). There is no real control, or even logic, to the numbers that are your life-blood.  -Side missions should be a good source of additional income to speed up gameplay, but instead lead the player astray and are not helpful at all. The paths/objectives requested are not lucrative at all and the small rewards do not make up for the costs of the projects requested. Holding onto 'population increase' and 'vehicle purchase' missions can be helpful, but are passive in nature and can be very time consuming. The point of the game is to build and manage, and to do so well. Why do side missions work to hinder the core mechanic of the game?  - While the game environment will make way for, and adjust to, whatever roads/rail systems you put in place, the control of routing lines, vehicles, and population can be incredibly difficult. Often it is easier to rebuild the existing road system instead of using what exists. But even then, the resulting design or plan the player has can be difficult to implement with the cumbersome and demanding pathing/planning system.  The game has a lot of good ideas and systems that just aren't fully fleshed out, realized, or functional. And what you do get is not very well explained, defined, or even remotely consistent. I did better taking out a massive loan, building two huge subway lines, then letting the game run for hours on end in the background. Trying to build interconnected systems similar to real world cities only lead to spectacular failures and wasted time.

Got to use a Magic Eye poster for the first time in 18 years. 10/10  The use of gaming as a medium to focus on atmosphere and story instead of action was really refreshing. I think it was great to go into the house with a set of assumptions, then watch them be shaped and molded by the evidence found around me. I kept catching myself formulating different hypotheses about the family's whereabouts, enjoying the affirmation of supporting evidence and the chiding bits to the contrary. Not many games can offer that kind of immersive narrative.  I can admit that if I had paid full price for Gone Home, I would probably feel a bit cheated, as the replay value is quite low and playthrough time is quite short. That being said, if you can buy it on-sale for say, the cost of going to a movie, then this is a great way to spend an evening.  Also, downvote on all of the negative reviews not offering spoiler warnings.

This game... I don't really know what to say about it. It's not bad as a game, it's not good either... *sigh* Okay, lets go with this.  Acceleration of Suguri X-Edition (Basically the name of this releace) otherwise known as AOS... as I said it isn't good, it isn't bad either. To be frank this game should have sold well on Steam, it presented a unique idea which is bacicaly... um imagine Super Smash Bros with characters from Suguri in it and bullet hell mechanics to top this mix off. There I explained most of the game... Can I go now? No? Okay, I'll keep going then. Gameplay is a bit odd at first, as some effective stratigies from the origional Suguri game, or even Sora can not be performed here. Orange_Juice had to do a thing called... uh... 'balencing' a word not exactly in the dictanory of Suguri or most bullet hell's in general. The cancel for instance (In the origional game you could dash and leave an attack animation early... in Sora there was a penality for doing this, but this is before Sora) has been removed entirely... or it's really hard to pull off... most likely the former. Another gameplay error would undoubtadly be the timer and health bars, if a charactor goes up under one of the health bars they disappear entirely as the bars don't turn transperrent, so it's possible to lose YOUR charactor. Now... I have a few words for the art style here... I feel as if there were about thirty people designing Suguri's menu sprite, it looks over designed... but simple at the same time. I can't put my finger on why I dislike the art here, it just dosen't look right. Then you get into a story cut scene and the artstyle changes completely to more like 100% OJ('s) art style which looks good, great infact and it kind of rectifies the menu and after battle sprites, in battle looks good too, though the timers really kicks you in the ♥♥♥ if you lose yourself in there.  And last but not least... Rockin' Android... WHY??????????????????????????????????? You invest what could only be described as ALOT of money into localization and then make the servers for a game which you'll get the most bang for your buck from about as relibile as trying to launch a mountain into space with one engine from a Fiat 500 and two ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ on planing. *Sigh* rant over now okay... deap breath...  Look I'd love to recomend this game, as a game it isn't bad, it isn't great either but Rockin' Android put too many eggs in the basket that was inside a cannon marked 'Too be fired in twenty minutes' The only saving grace for the game in the end (Because gameplay is kinda samey) is the fact that you can get Suguri a little bit cheaper through the bundle they're both in. And if you're invested in the charactor (EG me) you can find little bits of lore surounding the charactor in text sequences that lasted longer than the arcade mode on easy. However I couldn't be bothered to unlock all of these as they (Undoubtadly) would require online play... which with the kind of dead couminity is quite hard... not to mention the servers.

If even the very thinking about games like Baldur's Gate puts a smile on your face - buy it, there is a chance you might like it. If you aren't into retromodernhipsteroldschool - drop it.  I loved the old Baldurs, but for me, the Pillars were just... lacking. Something got lost along the way. I couldn't truly get into the setting, I couldn't form a bond with the new characters and the very game mechanics, where your keen-eyed ranger misses a paralyzed Cyclops at point blank range, started to annoy me much sooner than I've expected. All I saw in this game, was just a shallow and aged clone of Baldur's Gate, that should be left on a shiny pedestal in the museum.  I suspect, that the source of my problem with this game is the Witcher 3. I played the Pillars shortly after finishing the masterpiece from CDP and couldn't stop comparing those two. I'm aware that comparing them is like comparing the banana to a hand grenade, but still - when you go out of new Lambo and drive back home in a Chevy, you will notice some differences. For me, Lambo wins.  EDIT: I know, it is unfair to compare Witcher 3 and Pillars, but I have my reasons. Firstly, the games are sharing the same price range, basic Witcher is even cheaper than Pillars and you'll get a bunch of DLC for free. Two expansion packs that cost you like, 20€ together, are swelling up the game by almost 50% and are like proper cuban cigar: big, long and surprising you with multitude of different sensations as you enjoy them. Secondly, the main factor for me in every game is fun. I don't care about graphics, heck, one of my most favourite games of all time is the brilliant Dwarf Fortress. Anyway, Witcher served me loads of fun in climatic, immersive setting, while PoE served me a bit of fun in the beggining, just to hit me with a load of painful grind, lackluster characters, unintresting items and boring combat system. I would have more fun replaying the dusty Baldur's Gate with some nice mods!

So, I don't normally write reviews, but for this 'game', I'm going to make an exception because it seems to encourage the discussion of how one interprets it.  To begin, I have to say I was kicking myself after it ended.  I felt like I'd jumped to so many conclusions about who   Coda   was, only to get backhanded by The Tower. The one thing that hit me hardest though was the message   'Stop putting lamp posts in my games.'   I just never expected the narrator to lie to me. It made me question what was genuinely   Coda's   work and what had been altered.  The whole thing was a real emotional ride.   As someone who loves to perform on stage, The Stage was the hardest level. I immediately looked at it from a performer perspective, rather than a gamer's perspective and I just wanted to cry.    Being told to back away from the stage and the giant steel bars coming down over and over broke my heart.    The levels regarding the   machine   resonated with me. I've always viewed my creative 'source' as a lone flame. Sometimes it's just a little spark and other times it's a great roaring fire that will keep me up all night as I write stories. So I felt a mixture of sympathy and fear at these levels that   tried to find the source, interrogated it, hated it, demanded it to apologise until eventually tried to kill it completely, as if removing the source of creativity would remove the frustration when the ideas wouldn't come.   I suppose it scared me a little.   At the time I couldn't imagine someone so frustrated they would willfully simulate destroying their own creativity.    Of course, then I reached the end of the Tower and felt like a real ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ for   jumping to conclusions about this person who in reality was just going through a low point and knew full well that the engine can't run at full power all the time.    Overall there's just so much to talk about.  But despite that, I guarantee that once you finish playing, you'll feel just a little bit empty inside. For me at least, I went into this expecting a thought-provoking experience that I'd have to work hard to understand, but would  eventually  understand.   Instead, I feel as if I simultaneously understand it completely, and yet will never be able to understand it at all. Because, I know someone like the Narrator; someone who   needs outside validation to feel fulfilled. That, without external praise and acknowledgement, they can't function.   I can't relate to that, I struggle to understand that frame of mind. Which is funny, because the Narrator    can't imagine not needing external validation.    So it dawned in me that I relate more to   Coda   and that's where the empty feeling starts. Because what if people interpret my work, my performances, my creativity the same way I initially viewed his?  I don't need external validation and I definitely don't need people assuming things about me based on what I've created.  And... just like   Coda, I've cut people off for being overbearing and clingy. I've always been aware that it's not pleasant for the person I cut off, but I've always justified it because they're a negative presence in my life and my happiness comes first.   I never expected that doing that could have such a catastrophic effect on someone's state of mind. Or, I dunno, perhaps I did.   Look, tl;dr this game is not really a game. It's an emotional experience that'll leave you reeling.  It's a work of art in truth and if you're into that kinda thing, this game is for you.

What really gets me about this game isn't the whole thing about dating birds, but that this game is incredibly dark once you get underneath the cute otome surface. It's those hints of darkness in the setting's recent past that keep me playing even through the less appealing parts (some cheesiness, predictable angst, and Okosan... ugh, I do not like San.)  One thing I appreciate is that Hatoful Boyfriend doesn't try to convince you that characters that are unlikable and/or creepy ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥s are appealing love interests. You pursue them for the sake of game completion, knowing full well that your character's getting into something unappealing and possibly even dangerous. In some cases you might be surprised at what you find out. So you keep doing it anyway.  I've played otome where you're wooing some misogynistic jerk or antisocial personality, and you're supposed to believe you'd actually willingly fall for that for the off chance he might turn out having some redeemable quality or sympathetic sob story. I can't stand that.  I like Hatoful Boyfriend--but keep in mind, the only other otome I've played that I liked was Idea Factory's Hakuoki, a game based on Japan's Meiji era where the members of the Shinsengumi basically become vampires to avoid their historical deaths. And yes, you romance them. It's glorious!

HyperRogue is an innovative turnbased Roguelike that takes place on an infinite, hyperbolic plane (hence the name) The goal is to collect as many treasure as you can, kill monsters as well as completing some quests. What makes this game interesting and innovative, is besides the geometry it uses, the fact that it plays like a mixture between rogue and chess, but with a twist. It features various lands, every single one with its own treasures, enemys, orbs, geometric patterns and rules. This game is perfect for 'just one round' as well as for 'just one MORE round', for its customizability in nearly every aspect and for the creator, who actively feeds the game with updates and bugfixes. And one last tip: don't get lost ;)

Strictly speaking, this isn't actually a game.   It's a shiny way to commit chronocide.   It's something to do while you're waiting for what you really want to play to finish downloading or updating, or you just don't have enough time to put into actually playing anything.   Of the various clicking games I've come across (Cookie Clicker, et al), this is one of the better ones. It starts off painfully slow, but if you stick with it, it eventually ramps up. And thankfully, it doesn't require constant clicking to advance. There are skills and power ups, and leveling, and there's some RMT stuff in there so that you can spend money on it and get the trading cards if that's your thing.  There's no skill required to advance, just time. And to get all of the achievements will take months. However, it's not a power hungry game, and your heroes are earning gold while you're not actively playing.   Pros: Will work on any system that can run Steam. There are achievements. Good for when you don't have a lot of time, or you're waiting for something else. Something to do while watching TV. It's free.  Cons: This isn't a game. Clicking gets old fast. It'll probably take more than a year to get all the achievements. It's the exact same thing you can play on the website.  Verdict: If you've got a lot of time to kill and can spare the pittance of space it takes on your hard drive, why not?

This key was obtained from the developer for the purposes of review.      Summary:  A game that just needs more. While its base combat and mechanics work, it wears out its welcome very quickly, as the variety of enemies, environments, and tactics don't last for more then a couple of hours. Compared to other games of equal price or depth, it doesn't compare as unfortunately it's aesthetic doesn't bring anything interesting to the table, and the mechanics have been seen before. While it's definitely easier to play then it's free to play counterpart on the mobile platform, the f2p aspects there don't feel that necessary (they feel out of the way in fact). In fact, the best way to understand if you'd generally like the gameplay of the game is to play the google play version of the game, and realize that you've got a keyboard to work with after that. But in the end, Cardinal Quest 2 just doesn't do enough to stand out from its competition.     Gameplay Footage and Video Review:  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lV729q5lQmU      Lists:    +:     Simple combat works on its core mechanics and simplistic skills and item system to a certain extent. While void of true complexity, there's balance of elements here and a reasonable challenge.     Like some small touches like the auto-selling of equipment that's obviously worse in every way to your current set.    Heroes, while at first seem unbalanced, do have a reasonable balance in the end.    The alchemist does feel like a unique character that stands out from the others.    Glad to see the f2p elements from the mobile game gone.    -:     Game lacks variety for the long haul. After 2-3 hours, you'll see what the game has offered in pretty much every way. It misses the KO punch to really draw attention to it. Lack of enemy variety, equipment, and environments as a whole.    Certain control problems with missing key strokes show up because of the mobile port. Can be annoying at times when you have to use the mouse to click on something     Compared to other games in the genre, it doesn't have the content to warrant the 15 dollar price tag. Games like The Enchanted Cave 2 and Tallowmere bring more content in the end.     Certain extra elements hinder gameplay despite being a new ability due to unintended side effects that it can have. For example, auto-charge sounds great at first, until you realize you attack allies and the shopkeeper who will hammer you down, even if you don't want to.    Very uneven. Rough in several places, while smooth in others, making an uneasy experience.    At times, doesn't do a good job of explaining extra stats or elements that a weapon/armor can have, and what the exact effect is.    Lack of Cardinals. But in all seriousness, saying there's story campaign in this game is misleading, cause there's really no story.

I'll admit, first looks turned me away from the game. I wasn't exactly sure if I would like it. Then a few dear friends suggested I get it. Some passive, other a bit more... enduring in their goal to get me to play.  After getting in some time both registered and unregistered, I gotta say: this game is a work of art.  But what makes it so good? The simple art style of it gives it a vibe of the games of yester-year (I'm thinking NES era), and the music ranges from serene to just getting you pumped for whatever comes your way. But the biggest selling point for it was story. Personally, I love a good heart felt story, and this is one of the best that I've encountered in my life. The character arcs, the depth of the various people you encounter in the game, the threads that tie it all together... that's what makes it so memorable. I'm certain even the most stone-hearted of individuals would feel something stir after playing through the whole thing. It's an experience you just don't forget at all.  My only lament is the multiple paths. After doing a certain heart warming run through, it's almost too painful to turn that entire world upside down after what you've been through. some of my friends have actually admitted they could not go for the other ending. After everything, it is really hard to even make that choice. You just wanna keep going the main route over and over to just enjoy those heart felt moments, savour them down to the last bit of warmth they can muster.  But my lament is only short lived. Even with its potential for a darker path, the entire game shines with the knd of polish main stream game titles could even HOPE and DREAM of. And for that, we thank you.

This game is really funny, but even more sad, especially on the True Pacifist route that I just finished. At the time of writing this review, I haven't completed the genocide route, and I'm planning on taking a break from this game before I return to it because yes, I am emotionally attached to the characters. But for me, that's just the kind of game this game is; the game itself is not as fun as the characters are, and all the dialogue you have with them, and how you get to know them and all their problems as you tried to solve them. Overthinking it, I guess this game is about solving everybody's problems.  But despite that, it's really fun helping them solve their problems, and it's one of the things that make this game stand out from all others. It's never ever too difficult, it's very fair; the most difficult aspect is memorizing bosses' attack patterns, and you have infinite retries, so you should manage to get all the way through the game. Like I keep stressing, this is more of an exposition game than it is about features. The emotional attachment you-or at least I-had to the characters at the end were so touching it inspired me to write this review. The game isn't perfect in all ways, but in terms of setting up a universe like this one where the characters actually have relationships with you, and you can manipulate them however you want; really this game just gives you   the feels

Welcome to the Borderlands! In this game, you can pick one of 4 characters to play as, who all have their own individual skills. Pick one, and play with friends as you progress through the story.    Pros:

Incredibly fun twin stick shooter. Lots of lights, colors, and MUSIC. I played it at a friends house and decided to buy it myself and use the music that I like rather than his. I loved playing this game and getting the achievement. Being a game based off of music you really need to enjoy listening to music to play it. The music is pulled form your computer (itunes usage is available through a cheap dlc) and there is some pre loaded music that the game comes with. So if you have no music on your computer you'll either have to use the internet radios it gives you, download some music, or just use the couple of songs the game provides. The Shadow Operations Unit dlc does add extra ships to the game each with advantages and disadvantages of their own, but it is not neccessary to fully enjoy the game. The Beat Hazard Ultra is not absolutely needed to play the game and enjoy it but I highly recomend buying it if you can, even better buy the bundle and get all the dlc for the game and get the full experience. The only downside to the game is that by default settings the SFX sound is turned up rather high and the sounds of the game tend to become to much for me to enjoy the music and lightshow it provides, this is simply fixed however by turning it down to a lower level and the SFX are hardly noticable anymore. Very good Twin Stick SHMUP

I feel like I've waited all my life for this game to see an official English release.  The translation here is quite sketchy (the 1994 DOS vaporware version's translation was much better) and there are certainly other issues (see other reviews mentioning the letter at the end not having subtitles) but anyone who has ever wanted the Princess Maker series in English to pick this baby up. It's just as fun as ever and opens up the possibility of PM3 ~ 5 being translated. I could die happy with these games translated, even as roughly as done here.  No censorship, but there wasn't much to censor from the original Japan-only release of PM2 Refine in 2004. The art was toned down in the original Refine release. No Mad Eddy. Steam overlay doesn't work. Can't screenshot in Steam. There's a bug where Faith reduces to 0 when you sell things out of your inventory when your inventory is maxed.  Release isn't perfect but it works and it's better than nothing. Community has figured out how to edit the game's images day one, I'm sure a method to change the game's text is soon to follow.  [Update Sept 30 2016: CFK are reaching out in a thread in the forum to sniff out bugs and text issues based on player feedback. This is a lot more than I expected from them. Hopefully they fix this release's problems. I'll update this review when/if they do.]

What started off with the wrong foot for me finally became almost pure love. At first this game was trash to my eyes: it made my sight hurt because of the art style, it bored me in the beginning, it made me want to bite my thumbs off and spit them straight to the screen. But after giving it a second chance it really grew on me. I started playing more and more until I finally loved it.  All in all, it's still not a 10/10; I see MANY defects in the game. But the story is so... so... I don't know which words to use to describe it. It's so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ good, and more. It is, after all, Bioshock.   The whole setting of Columbia, bright and open, is a high contrast and big change from the dark depths of Rapture. There are no more mutant monsters; this time, you're the enemy.   The gameplay is fair with a controller, though I really hated playing it with the keyboard and mouse; it was really obvious they didn't have those in mind when they stated the mechanics of the game. Specially in Hard difficulty; I died a lot with mouse and keyboard, but died a lot less with controller. It's the first time I see a shooter where you can aim better with a controller. Anyway, after making the switch to my trusty XBOX 360 controller, all got better.  AND THE STORY FEATURES A MULTIVERSE AND TIME-TRAVEL, INCLUDING PARADOXES! I LOVE THOSE!  If you've played all the previous Bioshock games (or at least the first) and loved it then this is a must have for you. You'll love the story. The gameplay is not as good as the first one, but still after a few tweaks it's still enjoyable.  And don't forget to grab the DLC as well if you can afford it, you will see a lot of old friends.

I haven't hacked a bank yet, but I can't wait to do that. This is one of the only decent hacking games on Steam, and is the best one by far. I remember playing this at a friend's house a while back and going rogue on the Uplink agency. They almost caught me, but what they didn't know is that I had a motion sensor on my gateway computer that, when triggered, would cause the explosives on the gateway to explode, therefore keeping them from finding me. Overall? I give it a...oh shoot, they got a trace on me, gotta hack fast!

First of all, this game is gorgeous, absolutely gorgeous. You'll notice it from the beginning, and still you'll probably be amazed by it deep into the campaign. After that, there is no too much to admire.  It plays like a puzzle game about creating elaborate deaths that look like accidents. Once the puzzle game breaks down, it becomes a 3rd-Person-Shooter. Trouble is, I found myself forgetting about the puzzle rather quickly, eventually just shooting my way in and out. I frustratingly got to this point because of the reminders of my score everytime I made a mistake trying to make the perfect kill. The constant score updates ought to be something left for a second playthrough. On top of this, the assassination puzzles are claustrophobic rather than an open toybox of options. I guess if you want a narrow 'cinematic' experience with the occasional break of puzzle/shooter gameplay, this game is just for you. It ain't for me. I'd rather have the sandbox loaded with option, with the only story being, 'Kill this guy. Make it look like a accident.'

A classic shooter with a lot of horror elements thrown in. The two expansion packs are included, bringing some welcome variety to the gunfights and the scare sequences.  Pros * Great shooting mechanics, you feel deadly as the decor and enemies are shattered by your bullets * Graphics that were gorgeous for their time, lighting is still handled pretty well * Interesting storyline even though its tropes are well-known by now  Cons * Repetitive level design, repetitive enemies, repetitive storyline ; how much time can Delta Force operatives be slaughtered and helicopters can crash without becoming boring? * The pacing is predictable, you will not be afraid during gunfights because you know there will be no jumpscares, and you won't feel threatened during the creepy parts  As a word of advice, the AI is very interesting so try to vary your actions during the battle segments and don't save scum, otherwise you may feel bored for a period of time.

Alien: Isolation is not just a game for Alien fans. It's for anyone who enjoys immersion, smart AI, and a good scare. I had not seen the original film Alien (1979) until the night before playing Alien: Isolation, and I'm glad I did. The level of detail and love given to recreating the atmosphere of the original film by 20th Century Fox and Creative Assembly is admirable if not groundbreaking. There's no tacked on multiplayer. There's no frills. You get to spend about 20 hours trying to survive in what plays like a compilation of everything great in the survival horror genre, but it has its own unique spin where there's just enough action to keep things alive... and scary as hell. I generally consider myself to be desensitized to virtual violence, but I legitimately flinched and yelped when I was suddenly pulled up an airduct and devoured. The Alien's AI is absolutely remarkable, and I was constantly outsmarted by it. That is the icing on the cake to me.   Do yourself a favor and pick up Alien Isolation. It's worth every penny.

Postal 2 is essentially a VERY watered-down GTA-esque game with the same juvenile humor and open-world style where you're allowed to either cause chaos in killing sprees or pursue a number of missions. Postal 2's missions are simply the protagonist ('Postal Dude') completing a number of menial and trivial tasks (picking up groceries, going to the Bank, etc.) while occasionally being pitted against various enemies (like protesters who believe video-games cause violence - only to whip out automatic rifles and begin indiscriminately murdering people, a group of Jihad leading an assault on a church, etc.) In regards to gameplay, unlike in GTA, you're placed in a relatively rural environment, and there's an odd complete absence of vehicles, except for those in parking - not even NPCs drive any vehicles. Weapons are aquired by finding them or stealing them; there aren't any avenues through which to purchase weapons or ammunition, though thankfully most of the weapons are easy to find.  In summary, if you happen to find this game for a good price (like I did, at $2), it's well worth the purchase for several hours of mindless indulgence.

This is an atrociously lazy game. TL;DR: it's not worth full price; not even close. It might be worth about $15 at most. The gameplay isn't significantly different from One Finger Death Punch, which I got on GoG for a dollar. Graphics are significantly better, but in service of a meaningless and uninteresting story.  The Good: + Decent basic gameplay mechanics; fighting is fun and fast + Interesting Uruk Captain and Warchief mechanic + AAA graphics + Stalking and fighting captians and warchiefs can be fun  The Bad: - Terrible, awful, contemptable final 2 boss 'fights'  - Seriously...  - The second to last fight has you just stealth attacking an enemy who just wanders about aimlessly 3 times.  - The last fight... the boss of the damn game... is a 3 button quick time event.  - No prior fight, no use of the game's core mechanics whatsoever, just a QTE.  - That is ♥♥♥♥ing disgraceful. - Fights become pathetically easy after mid-game (even warchief fights) - Ranged enemies are not well thought out (few to no counters and little to no indication that they're attacking you) - The story... it might as well not exist. - Gollum is shoehorned in. Seriously, he serves no purpose to the plot. - Characters are not developed at all, and quickly shuffled away. - Several hunt quests with Torvin the Dwarf are marked as main quests and yet contribute nothing to the plot. - Game canon says your main character can not die. Why not just throw himself at the horde until they're all dead? - 'Banished from death', give me a ♥♥♥♥in' break. - Nonsensical and completely irrelevant 'twist' at the end. - The combat is a 3 trick pony. It gets old about 10 hours before the game is over (1/2 way).  At first, I liked the gameplay enough to excuse the lack of plot and characters. After about 10 hours (1/2 way through) I got bored of the mechanics, as nothing new or interesting was introduced, and the plot and characters were still garbage. The ending... the final 2 bosses ruined all the goodwill I had. ♥♥♥♥ this game. It only feels like a complete waste of time.  WB: How in the hell are you going to have the entire end of the game completely neglect all of the mechanics you spent the entire game building up?! What the hell is wrong with you?  Grade: D

Bottom Line: In my opinion, Project Cars has this game beat in every aspect. Glad I got my refund!  Reasons include:  Extremely limited content, very few tracks, even fewer cars, no music, lackluster graphics.   Additional evidence of a poorly made game: garbage UI, no weather effects, no track map overlay, no useful telemetry... basically all the items Project Cars has is missing in AC. And where the F is Laguna Seca? What racing game leaves out such a major international track?   The only advantage I see to AC is the sandbox modding you can do. Why pay for an unfinished product that you have to mod to even function the way you want it to? Think RPGMaker for a racing game sounds cool? I did too until I saw the lack of polish even for top-tier mods- for example the 'community made' Laguna Seca track was horribly glitchy on top of being incorrect for the iconic corkscrew turn. Where are the fan made R34 GT-R's and C7 Z06's? Not worth waiting around for someone's pet project to turn up while you pay for this barebones game that the devs left to the community to support.

10/10 One of the best RTS games I've ever played, up there with battles in Rome Total War. Although this game requires a learning curve, once you learn it, it can be a hell of a lot of fun. The amount of units give you a wide variety of ways to deal with the enemy, this goes for both the campaign and the multiplayer. However this game does have its bugs and glitches and does require pretty good internet and decent PC/Laptop to run, the awesome clutch moments and hilarious moments you can have completely outwiegh all of that. The mods are alos really good, making realism even better although a little bit harder, I suggest you rack up a few hours of the vanilla game before taking mods on because they really change gameplay. If you love the World War 2 time period and love RTS games, this should be your dream to play, however if you play War Thunder, a few things are different so keep that in mind. Little side note before I stop, the game has a LOT OF EXPLOSIONS, LIKE A SH*T TON. THERE ARE EXPLOSIONS THAT MAKE VEHICLES DO 360 SPINS 100 FEET IN THE AIR, IT IS F*CKING AWESOME! BUY THIS GAME!

I've been looking forward to play this since the Ludum Dare entry, and I was prepared for it... because, you know, I guess this game is'nt for everyone. It's slow, hard and at times terribly frustrating. There's no way to save your progress within a stage, which in some occasion may require a hour or more of gameplay; everything is done by picking a voice on a context menu, in a turn-based enviroment; and you die a lot! Nevertheless GWBW is a gem, a little piece of sci-fi narrative providing a unique, haunting ambience and an authentic sense of distress unlike any other game I've played. There are torture scenes so crude that you may want to avert your eyes from the screen. In another occasion I realized that I was playing not for fun, but because I was experiencing that a certain task was so overwhelmingly important that it had to be completed, no matter the price to pay. What this game lacks in action is compensated by the fact that it constantly pushes you in a grey zone where moral values are seriously questioned, and where you're asked to CHOOSE for the best.  While not being an essay in ethics Gods Will Be Watching gives a glimpse of what it's like to walk on the razor's edge of moral judgement.  A great soundtrack and a lovely (although not top-notch) pixel graphic complete the picture. 9/10

I, uh... wow. Been a while since something I've bought on Steam has utterly blown my expectations out of the water the way this amazing little number did.  Let's start with the easy tangibles. fault is a kinetic, multi-part visual novel (VN for short) translated and localised by Sekai Project. For those unfamiliar with the terms, a VN is kind of like a computerized comic book or graphic novel. You have backgrounds for different locations in the story, varied character art to express different emotions, and a couple other differences (such as the ability to save), but by and large they're simply a way to tell a mostly non-interactive story via a computer. Some VNs allow the player to make choices that affect the game's ending, but a kinetic VN such as fault only has one ending that the player can't affect. This isn't a good or bad thing in and of itself, but some people will prefer being able to make choices, while others will prefer to sit back and let the story guide itself.  I'd like to talk about the artwork next. As several other glowing reviews testify - not to mention the actual screenshots from the store page itself - the art is pretty amazing, both the background and character art. I'll admit I've got a bit of a skewed perspective here; most of my VN experience comes from older VNs, and obviously artwork from newer entries in the genre will be a step up. Still, I think it's safe to say with how many other people have consistently lauded the artwork, it's safe to say that, even compared to its contemporaries, fault's artwork is in a class of its own.  Now, the soundtrack. I think it served its purpose quite well - no tracks grated on my ears; on the contrary, it was quite nice. There wasn't any instantly memorable track that jumps out at you like Fate/Stay Night's EMIYA or Clannad's The Place Where Wishes Come True, but that's easily forgiven. I did notice a problem that I also noted in my review of WORLD END ECONOMiCA: Some songs have a somewhat painfully-noticeable loop point. It wasn't as particularly egregrious as with ECONOMiCA's abrupt stops and starts - the songs here have smooth fades - but I feel a couple emotional moments were hurt because of a song that was too short to naturally cover the scene. While I'm on the topic of sound, I should note that this VN contains no voice acting. That's a showstopper for some people, but at the same time, voice acting in VNs is a relative luxury; you're missing out if you're only reading voiced VNs.  And speaking of emotional moments, the writing. Oh, the writing.  Don't be put off by the screenshots or by the admittedly somewhat slow start. There's a fair amount of topics that the game touches pretty eloquently upon, such as cultural divides and how those standing across such boundaries are perceived, the inner machinations of (vague spoilers)   a sociopath   and how they come across and are dealt with by those around them, and even (vague, but major thematic spoilers for late in the game)   a Ghost in the Shell-esque discussion on what defines humanity, and what defines a particular person as that person  , which caught me completely off guard. You might also want to keep the Kleenex handy; this game has instances where it rivals the tearjerker moments of Jun Maeda's best works. And if you read my review on planetarian, you'd know exactly how much that statement means.  I will say, as the previous paragraph mentioned, that the VN has a slow start, and takes an hour or so's worth of reading to really hit its stride. And, well, it's short. 5 hours - how long it took me to read through the entire work - is pretty short, especially for a VN that has only one ending. That said, this is a multi-part work, and as I write this, the 2nd installment is already available to purchase on Steam. Yes, you do have to buy them separately.  All things considered, fault: milestone one is one of the best VNs currently available on Steam. I highly recommend checking it out, even if you've never read one before.

Magnetic By Nature isn't a terrible game; on the contrary, it's a somewhat fresh and original platformer! Unfortunately, the pacing and level design, coupled with some bugs and horrible interface made the game boring to me. It was simply not fun, and that's the only reason I'm not recommending.  Again, it's not bad, and you may very well get some enjoyment out of it, if you enjoy platformers! It's rather easy, with most levels being completed in less than 3 restarts, a couple of more difficult ones not passing 15. And I'm not that good at platformers.  I'll just briefly explain each aspect I did (or not) enjoy.   The gameplay revolves around magnets, as the name suggests. You can be attracted or repeled (and attract and repel some objects). It's unique, as far as I know, and it did provide some interesting sections. The level design, however, lacks a lot of pacing and progression. It seems like just a bunch of random level thrown together. The first two worlds introduced mechanics; that last 3 didn't. If you look at Super Meat Boy, there's clearly a lot of thought into teaching the player how to overcome certain obstacles that are recurring in later level, in a harder form.  In this game, levels are very, very gimmicky. It's about a quirky feature of the physics system that helps you complete the level, but not really a challenge. And all of the levels are like that, for the most part. There isn't an obstacle that you have to learn, really. It's just... a bunch of levels. And for me, that wasn't fun at all, it just felt like a drag.  The controls are a bit floaty, which I didn't like very much. Then, there's how levels work: there's a collectible in every level. Some are really easy to get and go well with the level. Others are just completely out of the way. Meh, it can provide a bit more challenge, but I don't feel like it added a whole lot.  The bugs include getting stuck (in mid air, for some reason), leaderboards not seeming to work, levels completed not tracking properly, sometimes, etc. The getting stuck one was particularly annoying, as it happened several times, for no reason.  The game encourages speedruns, looking at the achievements. That would be really cool! ... If the leaderboards worked. I've never been able to see how other players did in their levels. Which kind of defeats the point. The game wasn't very interesting for me to speedrun it either.  What I mean by horrible interface is how cluttered it is when explaining very simple controls, and how unresponsive and inflexible the menus are.  For example, there's no level selection. What you can do is go to the Hub (always resetting your position to your starting point) lose some seconds (or minute) traversing to the world you want, and then select a level. But, if you're in a level already, you NEED to go abck to the Hub to select another one. And it's a waste of time! When you finish a level, you only have two options: restart, or go to the next level. Nothing else.  There are other problems I could mention, but it's not worth it.  Again, even though my review was very negative, it's not a terrible game. Read other reviews: people seem to like, and that's cool! I'm just writing about my experience with it. Some of the levels were fun, but most were completely forgettable and not challenging.   The other individual aspects are great! The artstyle (although I have some reservations) was pretty good, the soundtrack was very good as well, and used properly, keeping you in the flow of the game. I just didn't have any fun, by end.

When I got this game, I was hoping it was a randomized places to go, it's not. Everytime you start over, you are greeted with the same place, in the exact same order. I will recommend this game only during big sale, but I will not recommend it on full price.  Aside from repetitiveness and the lack of randomness, The combat system is pretty good.  Choosing the route to try to get extra treasures and other stuff is pretty good too.

review_text: A fantastic lead up to The Phantom Pain. I recommend this game for anyone that is on the fence about getting The Phantom Pain, wants a cheep high quality (crazy high quality) game, or even if you are getting or have The Pantom Pain. Ground Zeros has a more casual play that you can finish in a hour or five depending on how you play. Ground Zeros takes place before The Phantom Pain and playing it will add content when you play The Phantom Pain based on your achievements in the game. After playing through the first time there will be more missions and modes unlocked. For me this is the point I am at and I will be playing several more times before I start The Phantom Pain even though I already have it installed. I guess I want to get everything I can out of Ground Zeros I can before I start The Phantom Pain.

For a long time, I have had an excellent review of this game. However, until the game is changed in some way, I will be sticking with this. First off, a bit of explaination. I play decks that have a higher level of complexity. Synergistic interractions and well-thought gameplans are why I play this game.  So, with the Shadows over Innistrad/Oath of the Gatewatch patch, many of you may have noticed a change in how the game operates mechanically. The game has always skipped over main phases that you had nothing to do. If there was literally NOTHING you could do during your main phase, the game would skip to your pre-combat step without waiting for your click on the 'continue' button. This is perfectly fine.  With the new patch, the game will now skip your main phases if there is nothing you can do at SORCERY SPEED. This means, if you have no creatures, land, or sorcery spells, your main phase will be skipped. Not an issue for many decks. However, I often find myself wanting to do something during my main phase in order to facilitate a sorcery speed interraction. For example, Casting [[Inspiration]] to draw into a creature or land. At the current iteration, my only option is to wait until the game skips my first main phase, cast [[Inspiration]] during combat and allow the game to so generously give me priority on my own turn so that I can play my cards.  I understand this is an attempt to speed the game up, as it is often the case that an instant speed spell or ability is desired to be utilized outside of main phases. However, this is not 100% of cases and three games of mine have now been lost because the game so brazenly skipped my main phases. Please put an option into the settings menu to wait on main phases regardless of having some action to do at sorcery speed.  At 166 hours now and not likely to add many more until this is fixed.

This is a Warhammer 40K game in a 40K universe more than any I've seen in other games, the single player story feels like a constant battle. It feels Immersive and as if your part of the space battles. From what I can tell as well certain choices in the single player campaign have effects later on in the game which different parts of the story told differently.  Each faction plays differently and has its different strengths and weaknesses which may for interesting games. It has some nice customizable options aesthetics and appearance with some RPG elements such as abilities, skills, upgrades and crew. I would have to say it’s been a while since I’ve enjoyed a single player experience while not feeling a complete grind, not to long and not to short (just right). A physics-based platform game where you control an egg and you have to reach a glowing beam of light without hitting spikes or falling into pits, the game has got peculiar controls: hit the LEFT or RIGHT keys to make the egg jump in that direction, do it while holding SHIFT to make a higher jump, most times you won't jump far enough for getting over gaps so you'llhave to roll a little bit to get momentum and jump further. Learning how to move can be hard but once you've mastered the controls the game isn't particularly hard, it sure isn't easy but it's nothing too excessive or anything.  That if you want to bead the game normally, there also are harder versions of the standard levels that are simpy full of spikes everywhere that will really test your skills and patience, other that making you lose tons of time. Due to the huge amount of skill needed to move around and a slightly random factor in the character's movement the game can get a little bit frustating, thankfully the lightly colored and simple art style mixed with the music, classical orchestral compositions, will easily make most hard lvels easier to digest. Buy it only if you like hard platformers or rage games like the Unfair Plaformer or Cat Mario.
