I enjoy TCG/CCG's. I play about 10 regularly right now, some of which are in my library here at Steam. I enjoy CCG's because of the strategies and thought that is required to be put into them. It's fun to create decks and try them out either in PVE or PVP. I enjoy making the decisions in battle, determing where to focus my attack, how to defend, what type of utility to employ, etc.  THIS game...is not like that at all. There are ZERO...and I mean that literally...ZERO decisions that the player has to make in a fight. The computer does everything for you.  What you do is merely collect cards, add select them to go into battle each round, and let things fall as they may. It's the same sort of cheesy game found in mobile markets. If you've played some of those games...you've played THIS one. They all operate on the same premise: select cards to be used before a battle...then watch the ai take over the entire match.  There's nothing to this game, at all.   I suppose it could be used as an elementary stepping stone to an actual strategy CCG, but then, why? Almost all strategy CCG's have tutorials or ways to introduce new players into the game.  It claims to be 'next generation' yet it's anything but. Next generation is what Hex, Infinity Wars, Spellweaver, etc. are doing. Hell, even Hearthstone has more strategy and decision making than this.  I spend a lot of money on CCG's. I enjoy collecting cards and opening packs...but not just for the sake of collecting and opening...but for the opportunity to be creative with a deck and it's possible strategies. And that just doesn't exist here.  It's a good thing that this is a free game, it would have been refunded. I can't imagine spending money on packs just to let the ai do all the work for the player...but I acknowledge that some players just don't want to think about strategy and just want to let the ai do all the work for them (or have a system in which strategy is not allowed, like this game).  If you are a fan of TCG's and CCG's because you enjoy creating decks and/or the thrill of making decisions during a match, this game is NOT for you. It's free though, so check it out for yourself. But for those wanting something of a MTG, Hearthstone, Infinity Wars, Hex, etc. experience...you'll not find it here.

app_name: Warhammer 40,000: Dawn of War - Game of the Year Edition
review_text: Great game. As both a W40K fan, and a big fan of RTS games, this was a game made in heavan for fans of both or just RTS games in general. Excellent sound design, options, graphics, and the gameplay is fantastically well designed. It's a tad traditional, but it does enough to make itself stand out, such as focusing on reinforcing squads, and the unlimited resources adds an extra source of tension since both you and your enemy could end up slugging it out until the last man falls if you dont equip them properly or suck as a general.  Overall, great experience, but the expansion packs, specifically Dark Crusade, are better in terms of content and unit variety, but still worth a playthrough for completionists sake as the story is followed up on in DoWII  8/10

Pros: * Gorgeous handpainted graphics * Really nice, atmospheric music, a bit western style, that really helps set the tone for the game. * Voice narrated throughout the whole game, Logan Cunningham does a masterful work with this. He is really well chosen, and he is such a skilled voice actor, giving so much life and charisma to the narrator character and subsequently to the whole game. * Very customisable difficulty level, 2 difficulties in game (casual and normal), but then also a variance of 1-10 extra difficulty that are optional an unlockable through gameplay, and more modes unlocked after first playthrough. * You can choose playstyle, and you have very many weapons and abilities to choose from and pick your preferred playstyle. It doesn't feel cookie cutter. Some people felt the game play was repetetive, but I feel it is totally up to you. If you get tired doing the same thing, then just swap weapons, and you have a new experience! * Really nice idea with areas where you can practice the different weapons, and get rewarded for your effort. I upped my skill in playing these types of games generally, because of this :) * Finishing the game unlocks 2 more game modes, and a bit of more content that adds to the story - good replayablity value. My first playthrough was pretty fast (Took me about 13 hours), so there's more where that came from if you want. * Each section is pretty short, which makes it easy to pick the game up, play for half an hour, feel like you've accomplished and got to finish something, and then you can go do whatever boring chores needs doing irl atm, without feeling that you had to interrupt something. * The story is imo really good. I played through the ending again because of it. I laughed, I got sad, I got angry, I got happy - so many feels during the story of the game. I felt it had an ambigious ending that I also really liked. I found myself thinking about the ending for several days after.  Cons: * I don't know. The reason why it doesn't get 10/10 is because I've decided to only give that to maybe the top 5 games I've ever played, so I'm a bit restrictive with that. But no, no cons, not really!  Comment: I played it on normal difficulty, but I didn't activate any additional difficulty because I wanted a pretty casual experience, just enjoying the story and beautiful game. Some people thought it too short, but imo it's perfect length. It's one of those games that makes you feel a bit regretful when it's closing in on the end, but in a good way. I bought this on sale, but I would have payed full price if it was a rainy day, and I had no other game to play, and wanted a really nice little game to play. I immediately went and bought Transistor (next game from Supergiant Games) after I finished it, just because I liked Bastion so much.  Grade: 9/10

Hey there,  If u´re a fan of any Alien Movie, this one is a MUST.  This game is a Love-Letter to the movie series, and tells the story of Amanda Ripley, (daughter of Ellen Ripley) who is seeking for any answers about her mother´s disappearing, 15 years ago.  No, you won´t be able to kill the Alien. Its a perfect organism, and it is hunting YOU n everything alive. You can´t run away from it, but you can hide,.. eventually.^^  The Alien isn´t the only danger you´ll meet. There is trigger-finger happy survivors,  super tuff synthetic´s (robots),  and environmental hazards.  To save your progress,  you have to use Emergency-devices, which are placed across the Space-Station. (no autosave)  You have to solve different puzzles/objectives along the story/path. Open doors, hack Computers, seek for Keycodes, and collect items to craft handy devices. In an enviroment that is desolate and becomes more n more dangerous, the further u continue.  Pro´s: Fit´s seemless to the story of the first movie. Level design is brilliant. (look/feel n path´s) Sound n Atmosphere. (like the movies, deep n intense) Gameplay is very nice/stable. (if your sys meets the specs) The difficulty-level feels just right.  Con´s: A few graphical n physics misbehavings. Melee weapons sometimes disappear. (only optical) Facial-expressions / Cut-scenes are not 100% perfect in sync or look. Your not able to jump.  For me, its the Alien-Game i always wanted. Its an realy intense thing, with alot twists n turns along the story. Maybe not 100% logical in some details.  (n´ Yes, i´m a fan. Especialy of the very first movie, from 1979)  A masterpiece for the Alien Universe, (with a few lil issues // but which game has none?) Don´t take some of those bad reviews serious, they are not correct or unfair n not detailed enough.  personal rating > 9.9 of 10 regular rating > 8.5 of 10

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: I was going to write a list of good and bad points but honestly there are very few good points I can think of.  Bad Points: - Ships are just plain broken, transport ships appear more powerful than dedicated warships. - No seasons. Shogun 2 had seasons, why take it out? So you can sell it later in DLC, that's why. - Full stack battles usually last no longer than 5-6 minutes. - Optimisation is terrible. It was supposed to be more optimised than Shogun 2. It is too confusing and some aspects just don't work. - The political system is not functioning, it is all too obvious that it was rushed out. - AI, both battle and campaign, is atrocious. I'm done with Grim Dawn, it was fun, I played about 100hrs in which I've created 2 characters, I've reached Level 44 with one and 74 with another, was able to glance through the first missions on the last difficulty (Ultimate) and acquired a couple Legendary items that was useful.  The game has a good interesting story, although it's very digressive because of multiple factions, I often was confused with the multiple 'main' plots. Don't get me wrong side-quests are a good thing, it's just that I felt there wasn't a compelling main story. I've finished the game I thought 'That was it?'. Aside from that the stories and quests are very well written and presented, specially the ones you collect as lore items, it was intriguing to always look for another piece to know what happened next. A big plus also for quest branches, allowing to actually change the game and story.  The gameplay is good, there's good amount of skills for each class with different builds and play-styles, not to mention the amount of masteries with the double-class sytem. The combat system often got me excited, often got me mad, it's great to put your skill to some use and not only the random click-fest that usually is ARPGs. I mean, there's interesting combat mechanics, like healers, and support mobs that have to be killed first, it's movement intense and you have to coordinate the skill usage.  The graphics are good, the scenery is dark, bloody and gothic, the animations though could be better, for example, the character animation when it's running often feels like it's on a roller blade, character posing stand-still is ugly, and often I got stuck in the environment after the animation of one of the movement skills I had.  Itemization is a problem, I got several items that I had no use for me while lacking items to progress the game, I understand this is a RNG game, although some sort of mechanism needs to be in place to fulfill this gaps, like the loot system implement in Diablo, or a quick and easy way to trade. Anyway going alone or 'self founded' as the community says is quite a task, and would demand a lot of time. That's the main reason I've got bored: looking after loot, not finding and knowing that would take a lot of time to get it.   TL;DR;  It's a good ARPG 'Diablo like' game with an well written story, good graphics and regular to poor animations. I definitely recommend if you like ARPGs.

I have really conflicted feelings about Oblivion, and most of my complaints lie where they do with other Elder Scrolls games. Let's be completely honest, the combat in these games aren't the highlight of the show, and Oblivion is far from an exception. What would be excusable for a combat system becomes laughably tedious as the game progresses since all enemies scale to your level. Levelling, as it stands, is incredibly tedious. Instead of an XP based level up system like traditional RPG's, Bethesda opted for a 'skill-based' one. To level up, you must progress your seven major skills (which you can pick when you make your class). The only way to level these stats is to GRIND. and GRIND HARD. If you're not inclined to just cheat your stats with the console, you'll be stuck spamming custom made spells that use as little magicka as required to get any sort of real boost.   The major problem of Oblivion is it's scarcity in good content. The sidequests are phenomenal, and some of the most interesting and creative you'll find in any video game. They range all the way from dinner party mysteries to vast journeys into unmarked areas. The main quest is, quite simply, boring as all hell.   It starts reasonably decent, and the first Oblivion gate was a cool expirience. But once you have to go around Cyrodil, close one or two gates for EVERY CITY, then be the errand boy for the Emperor it gets tedious quickly. The 'boss' encounters are underwhelming, characters like Mankar Cameron are just souped up wizards and Mehrunes Dagon is just a big demon guy walking in the background while you run through the streets.    Many skills just seem completely useless as well. Acrobatics for example. Sure it's kind of fun bunny hopping around, but the dodge isn't that great and the mastery skill of jumping on water is practically useless. Mysticism, Alteration, and Illusion are practically worthless with the exception of a couple spells, like Paralysis in the Illusion family. The reason they are 'empty skills' is because enchanting your armor will get you far better bonuses than the spells and allow you to alot your magick into vital things like Restoration. Athletics can be useful when it's mastered maybe, but running faster and regenerating fatigue quicker? Horrible. You don't really win at all with Oblivion in context to the combat. It's either a spongey mess of mouse clicking, an absolute cheesing with high level destruction or 100% chameleon/shield enchanted armor, or just a bore of sneaking around and getting an 8x bow attack.  The absolutely fantastic and beautiful thing about Oblivion however is the immersion. Books laying around referencing ancient battles, Ayleid ruins hiding powerful Liches, or mini events like the Bloated Float Inn being pirated when you sleep onboard. The little tidbits of good content are paced out well to counter the absolute chore-ish nature of the rest of the game. The relief of finishing a long questline of fetch quests like the Knights of the Nine expansion is both satisfying and underwhelming at the same time, because once you realize you just went through the same copy-pasted caves and ruins just with some flavortext to keep you interested, the expirience sours. As with any Bethesda game, mods are almost essential to the expirience. It doesn't have to be 100 different loli-maid mods either, just the basic fixes or fanpatches and graphical improvements are fine enough to keep the expirience fresh.   Overall, to somebody that has never played Oblivion or many other RPG's before, it's a fantastic game. You'll enjoy it for many hours over many characters. However for someone that has a lot of expirience with other RPG's with more polish, you'll find yourself disappointed by a lot of it's shortcomings.

Kentucky Route Zero is a lot like being home. The environments have a pseudo-surrealist/realist feel to them much like the of Twin Peaks if you are familiar to the popular cult show. It has a unique style that scratches an itch that few things can satisify. So, just on how well this game conveys tone and world building alone makes it recomendable.  It's a simple adventure game that follows the likes of Telltale's the walking dead, it isn't so much about puzzle solving but about the relationship of the characters and how much involvement you decide as a player to invest in the story. But instead, you don't just control the actions of one character you often switch to many different character's perspective and you get a sense of being not just a passive reader, a recipient, of a story but as a writer or an actor too. It's a nice feeling, because everyone is likable and there is a lot of heart and love in the backstories of the characters however simple they may seem. They're just good folk. You can tell the developers of this game have a passion for play writting style because a lot of the scenes come off as stage directions which give the exchanges a unique feel.  I feel we are on the brink of redefining what the term video games mean. Of course video games really well describes what the first iteration of games look like, things like pacman or space invaders where there are clearly expressed winstates and scoreboards. But, as the medium has matured we have gotten narrative focused games like Bioshock or even something like Metal of Honor/Call of Duty, where it is implied but not really that winining the game is by finishing all the narative content. And, now we are even in more obscure territory with things like Kentucky Route Zero or Dear Esther and Proteus, where the process of calling them 'games' seems almost silly. This could be the start of something, maybe a new medium, or a change of definition. In any case KR0 is a good example of the expanding definition of this genre of 'non-games' that has suddenly gained popularity.

DISCLAIMER: I recommend any version of this beautiful game other than the pc port.  Deadly Premonition might be the greatest game of all time. While this review shall be focusing on the abominable pc version, I'll say this about the game itself: the graphics might be terrible, the sound effects were probably pulled from a free sound website, and it doesn't make much sense storywise at several points. But if you believe that games are about being immersed in a truly unique world, if gaming is not about getting an arbitrary number of points but is about telling a story in an amazing way that makes you care for the characters you see, I'd highly recommend you get this game for PS3 or Xbox 360.  Now onto the pc port, it's garbage. The problems are so numerous I have to seperate them into multiple catagories.  Crashes: Crashed 4 times in about 3.5 hours...maybe I could get a fanmade fix, but it's NEVER the fans' job to make sure the game works.  Controls: Space bar is draw weapon and aim (You can only fire and reload if you are aiming) I got used to this, but my problem is, while you can rebind many of the controls, you can't rebind aim to something resonable (Such as right mouse button) Also, certain buttons on the controller won't work if you bind them, for instance, I wanted to put the lock on command to left trigger. After I bound it, it did not work. Same as when I tried to put shoot onto right trigger.  Framerate: First half hour runs at about 10-15 fps, it ran fine for about an hour, now runs at a steady 5 fps. Uninstalling and reinstalling did not work.  Driving controls: They work fine (If a bit wonky) with a keyboard. When you use a controller, the physics speeds up so the cars, which only go about 60 mps in the game, can easily hit 150 on the open road and it makes the steering hyper sensitive. I thought at first the car's physics might have been locked to the framerate on consoles, explaining why on pc the cars go faster, since the pc version has a higher framerate. But why does it only effect controller?  In conclusion, the pc port sucks it's certainly not worth the bucks buy the game on PS3 or xbox, It will truly, knock off your socks.  So says Mr. Stewart.  Thanks for reading my review! My friend and I also have a youtube channel called, 'CultGamerAlliance'. Check it out if you get a chance!

app_name: Valiant Hearts: The Great War™ / Soldats Inconnus : Mémoires de la Grande Guerre™
review_text: Valiant hearts isn't a war game like Call of Duty or Battlefield. It's a game about war. It lets you see the not-so-important-anymore WWI from different angles. Death, blood, sadness everywhere, losing friends and family, losing sanity and hope, only wishing for this t be over. An ending that tears your manliness apart. Wonderful game that doesn't judge; provides you information and doesn't tell you what is right or wrong. You're feeling it all the way. Great game.

Most of the Telltale games gets you really invested in the story and it's safe to say, that the story plays with your emotions. The Walking Dead: Season 2 is not an exception.   When I played the first episode, I thought to myself, that Telltale Games will not pull off something like the original game and I was right at first. The first episode and maybe, half of the second didn't make me attached to the characters. As the game progresses, you start caring about several characters, about the events going on without even noticing. If you are left with a choice where you have to pick a side or do something, you will wish you had that extra 10 seconds to think about it, while at the start of the game it didn't feel as important. You will feel even more attached and invested in the story after each episode, so don't jump in conclusions right when you start playing it. Trust me, I didn't want to play like a little girl at first either.   I recommend TWD:S2 not because it is a good game, but because it is one of the few games that actually makes you feel something inside, makes you think about morals. Pretty much shows the human side of you. While on games like Postal, Manhunt, Killing Floor etc. you'll feel anger and no remorse. Some games make you absolutely neutral. It's safe to say that both The Walking Dead games are worth your time and the full price.
If you dident get emotional at all for you.   Then you dont have any feels in your body.  This game is one of the best games I ever played and the Feels will hit me every time!   10/10 -IGN

review_text: This game is a real piece of ♥♥♥♥. It has bugs even inside of other bugs, the controls are weird and not helpful. Enemies can kill you instantly at some parts of the game, leaving you to wonder what the hell just happened, and the terrain is really faulty and hard to understand. The game itself is very good, even if Sonic moves slower than in Colors or Generations (P.S: hold RT and he runs a little faster, if you haven't already done so), the levels have plenty of optional routes to be taken and there are a significant variety of challenges and achievements to unlock.  The one and most significant problem is the porting itself of the game. I know my PC isn't *that* much in today's game standards, but an i5 5000 series, 8GB RAM and 2GB VRAM should be more than enough even for the recommended settings. Alas, playing this in 720p and no other effects enabled becomes a mess after a couple of minutes (literally). As far as I'm concerned, Lost World runs in the same engine as Generations, which in turn runs fine on my rig... So, where's the catch?  The only clue I have is the constant 60FPS lock the game has and cannot be turned off, where everything (you name it: particles, enemies, shadows, and even the timer) must match that requirement or else the game says 'get a better machine to play'. I read somewhere that setting the game to lauch on --f via Steam solves the issue as it forces the game to accept a lower frame rate. But... why should I do that? Why do I have to go through the hassle of doing this instead of releasing a patch of the game so it does that already? I've paid for the game, so at least some extra useful options would have to be included. The game is over an year old on Wii U!  That must be why it's 75% off mere 4 months after its original Steam release...

I've always been wary of games that try to tackle historical events, because they rarely seem to get their subject matter right. There's something disrespectful about historical games like the early Call of Duty titles, which placed players into real wars that real people fought and died in, exploiting those experiences by transforming them into mindless entertainment for the masses. Tackling this kind of heavy subject matter requires a degree of nuance that bleak loadscreen quotations or pressing F to pay respects don't even begin to provide.  1979 Revolution: Black Friday doesn't share those problems. Created by Rockstar Games veteran and Iranian national Navid Khonsari, whose family escaped the turmoil the title aims to portray, the game tells the story of the Iranian Revolution of 1979, which lead to the deposition of the Shah of Iran and the end of the Pahlavi dynasty. The influence of this period in Iranian history cannot be understated, yet knowledge of this point in history remains woefully elusive to the American public consciousness.  It's fitting, then, that the game starts out with so much exposition. This isn't a Call of Duty title looking to capitalize on history as a mere backdrop to action-heavy gameplay. The Telltale-esque title places players into a series of unfolding events as an Iranian photojournalist recently returned home from Germany. Early in the story, players will take a walk through a protest-crowded street, snapping pictures and listening to their companion expound on the issues facing Iran leading up to the 1979 revolution. Westernization, income inequality, government censorship - the developers are clearly aware of their audience's unfamiliarity with the setting and do a fantastic job of the setting the stage for the players.  It all comes naturally; your character, after all, has been out of the country for awhile, so it stands to reason that he might need to be caught up on current events. After snapping a photo, players are presented with a quick blurb of information and given the option to learn more, leading them to small snippets of Iran's history in the game's journal.  Fortunately, unlike many titles tackling heavy subject matter, 1979 Revolution is at least somewhat able to stand on its own as an entertaining game. It's informative without being preachy, and engages players with choice and consequence and quick-time event systems Telltale Games or Life is Strange veterans will find immediately familiar.  1979 Revolution is best described as a political thriller, and it isn't long until your jaunt down exposition lane is interrupted by the proverbial ♥♥♥♥ hitting the fan. It's a character driven story, showing Iranian nationals with a variety of different political and religious views reacting as their country is turned upside down. The voice acting is crucially well executed, thanks to a cadre of veteran film actors. Almost everyone involved with the project has a prior work that I've seen or am at least familiar with; I recognized the voice of Mozhan Marnò from The Blacklist, House of Cards, and A Girl Walks Home Alone at Night immediately.  The game is an indie title though, and you can tell that the funds for its actors may have been diverted from other areas of its limited budget. The art assets in particular leave a bit to be desired; it will quickly become apparent that character models and animations are being reused for the game's extras. In a game like this though, pursuing flawless writing and voice acting for its characters ultimately should trump almost everything else.  1979 Revolution does end a bit abruptly. It's not as bad as say, The Banner Saga, but I was expecting the narrative to go on for an additional chapter, or maybe for it to show a bit more of the revolution's aftermath. It's not a bad ending per se, but it does sort of build and build and then just end in the middle of its own climax.  Overall, the game succeeds in bringing a historically accurate, better understanding of the titular revolution to those who play it. It's not a deep look and it doesn't get too bogged down in historical details, instead focusing broadly on the people of Iran as they lived through this formative moment in their nation's history. 1979 Revolution is worth checking out on that basis alone if you're interested in experiencing a sample of pre-revolution Iran and how it evolved to become the nation we have today.  If you're not interested in that, I'd find the the title harder to recommend. This is a game of ideas, and it follows that the title is inextricably linked to the message it wants its players to receive. Standing on its own, its narrative isn't as satisfying as a Telltale Game and its gameplay doesn't touch Life is Strange, but if you're generally interested in experiencing a bit of history, that can make the title shine.  1979 Revolution: Black Friday is a short game at just over two hours of total playtime, and is available on Steam for a full price of $12. I'll leave the value judgment up to you.

Undertale is an RPG with just the right amount of charm to make it stand out amongst other RPGs, it feels like it has a lot of inspiration from the Earthbound series, which isn't a bad thing.  I originally bought undertale thinking It might end up being just another RPG that would get put on my backlog because I couldn't be bothered to finish it, but no as soon as I started my twelve hour play through, Determined to go full pacifist, simply because it was different from the usual mash attack for every battle and heal when low seemed oddly charming. Almost immediately I found myself getting pulled in with its quirky humor and lovable characters.  I found myself getting lost into the story and wanting to know more about all of the characters in this world, it was an amazing trip from start to end and I can't recommend this game enough to anyone looking for a RPG to play, but is sick of the typical grind.  This game bursts from the seams with character and the music is absolutely fantastic all the way through the game is sending a really powerful message and as much as I want to play ever last detail, after getting the true ending...I literally cannot bring myself to go back through the game and do a genocide play through...It would hurt to kill all the characters that grew on me.  So if you find yourself looking at undertale and wondering if its worth the buy, I would say hands down yes, this game is something else, its been years since a single player game has drawn me in and made me play it from start to end in one full sitting.   * Edit * I have now gone through a Genocide route and I gotta say, both routes are throughly enjoyable, each reveals different sides of the story. A nice way to get replay value out of your game.

While more simpler and straightforward than other entries in the  Civilization  series,  Civilization V , despite possessing an overwhelming sense of something missing, is a fun and enjoyable game nonetheless. A game that seems more tailored to newcomers to the franchise,  Civilization V  pays great respect to presentation, with a more alive and animated gameplay that   Civilization IV   had left mostly to the imagination, bringing new life into the world that players have been running around in for the past two decades.  While experienced  Civilization  players may find emptiness in the many removed and replaced features and aspects compared to  Civilization IV  and previous entries in the franchise, others can find elation and waste countless hours with a game that, on it's own, proves to be one hell of an experience. What contributes mostly to the game's attractive atmosphere are the opponents you play against. In  Civilization V , all leaders that you compete alongside have been given such rich personalities through vivid animation and luscious voice acting, giving new strategy to gameplay that isn't necesarily a physical aspect of the game, but rather an emotional one. For example, Caesar's passive-agressive attitude and Wu Zetian's consistently defiant personality gives weight to the player's actions against these particular people, deepening the fog of war and giving a new dimension to the gameplay.  While  Civilization V  is undoubtedly better with the   Gods and Kings   and   Brave New World   expansion packs which have been released in the years following the game's original release, the original 'vanilla' release is still a great game on it's own. Having played it bare for five years, I still think  Civilization V  is one of the best PC games on the market. Bold and beautiful, it's definitely a game you must play at least once before you die. Or twice. Or a hundred times.

.....this is a difficult one. This game has some serious issues, it has flawed mechanics, unplayable classes, unbalanced weapons as well as a very strange story. But there is something about it that I love.  It has deep music, a very interesting plot. Scary elements as well as mind-screw abound. It also has the darkest techno-future atmosphere that I have ever seen. But it's a pig. Really, it's hard to love and easy to hate.   It is a game where you can go invisible and strike enemies with samurai swords. A game where you will have great difficulty completing on your first run and will be left wondering at the end what to make of it all.   I won't lie it isn't a game for most people, it's very offbeat, very unclassifiable. But underneath all the poor gameplay and bad story-telling, there is something about it I like.   It has atmosphere. It's sombre, dark and sometimes scary. Darkened corridors where beasts could come at you from nowhere if you don't pay attention. Red alert lights flash while you thud your cyberboots around, looking for an access panel to hack to stop the damn defense turrets from targetting you. All while on an alien and completely inhoispitable planet somewhere in a distant future which is calous, cruel or downright ignorant to your plight.   Or closer to home where cyber-punks fight the megacorp soldiers. Chinese writing sprawled in holographic advertisements accross walls. Tenement blocks under siege from monstrous creatures. The spawn of an alien force known as the metastreum. They are relentless, and they never stop coming. But you have a mission to complete, you can let the soldiers and beasts fight amongst themselves. It's the future and it's dark, just like it should be.   That is the atmosphere of the game. But ultimately, it suffers from too many set-backs to make it a good game, or even an average game. Really, it's worth no more than £3. And even then, only if you are a certain kind of person. Check it out on youtube to see what I mean.

Fun little game. Not very long, and you really need to play the original Puzzle Agent, since they drop you off after the events of the first game without any refresher on the characters and events you should have already seen. It's a nice little adventure game, but instead of puzzle solving being done with inventory, they're the typical brain teasers that you might have done as kids (Ex: making paths of lights by arranging mirrors to reflect them, moving around objects into a certain arrangement, some number sequences, logic puzzles, etc.) But the challenge is decent but not agonizing, and even if you take your time and plan things out you might still make mistakes because occasionally the instructions are a bit vague. There are also plenty of hints you can collect and use if you're not very good with logic puzzles. Don't pay over $5 for it or you'll feel disappointed, but I got it for $2 and it's well worth it.

I picked up the game a while back because I heard good things about it, and it was incredibly cheap. Unfortunately, the only thing the game has going for it is the dialogue - and there isn't much of it. There are no towns or NPCs after about the halfway point, so what started out as a witty parody of the RPG genre ended with a bunch of incredibly lengthy and boring dungeons that really highlight many of the design flaws the game has. Combat is unbalanced, each character only has one or two attacks worth using, dungeons are basically time wasting walks since there are no puzzles or objects to interact with, etc.   It was incredibly promising for the first hour or so, but it felt like the developer got lazy and/or ran out of ideas, which is pretty ridiculous considering it took me less than 4 hours to beat the game. It's a cheap pickup. but I just can't recommend it due to how much of a chore it became. Really hoping Cthulu Saves The World feels more like a complete game.

The writing is a little dodgy here and there (if you explain the main act of the magician, suddenly all the small but nevertheless impossible tricks become so trivial that no explanation for them is needed...) but I liked the final revelation. Unlike many other Sherlock Holmes games (and that is also an unwelcome trend in crime novels in general), there was no detail revealed during the big monologue at the end that turned the whole story upside down. It all made sense with the information you already had from during the game.  The gameplay is enjoyable. With the interaction highlight and a very indepth built-in hint system, anyone can finish the story.  Thumbs up!

After sinking alot of hours and money into this game I think I can review it. When I first started playing around 3 years ago it was great and I instantly fell in love with it, I spent money on G1C and made cars, clothing and bought guns and had fun designing different personas and because the customization is amazing you could make Miley Cyrus, make a tour van, get guns to suit her, make your theme when you kill someone her song and make your tour van blast out an entire song of hers. Or you could just make ♥♥♥♥♥ and hentai like 80% of people do there. The game in that sense is amazing but when it comes to combat it is still fun for the most part but has horrible matchmaking, hitboxes, ranking system. Lets say you are a Trainiee (The lowest rank and level 9) and you press K to ready up in a mission you will most likely get put against a Gold level 205 who has no fall damage, kevlar, fast car and a rocket launcher and that completely ruins the experience and they've even added a district with NO matchmaking so whoever presses K to enter a mission gets put with anyone and to me that seems like a cheap way to add stuff to the game without trying to fix anything and lets say you were to pull up next to your friend and run over his toe at 2mph he will die... INSTANTLY and that just makes the whole combat feel slow, broken and horrible.  There are three sets of money in this game, dollars, Joker Tickets and G1C, dollars are earned by selling on the ingame markey place, so cars and symbols, songs and themes and are earned by doing missions, killing people on bounty and such, its grindy to earn as a cop and ok to earn as a criminal as you can simply steal from stores on a low populated district and earn around 100k in a couple of hours but it gets broken by the fact that premium members who pay for membership get more money per mission and such and so the person whos talented at designing songs or symbols or who has the money to buy premium gets richer faster. Joker tickets are earned by completeling a set of objectives in Fight Club districts where its basically team deathmatch and you will get notifications like 'Kill 5 people with granades' and you will get another after that and then you can earn Joker Tickets. These can be spent on getting temporary perks like car surfer to ride on cars or normally expensive weapons and cars, the next currency in the game is G1C this is real money in the Gamers First market place and its where the pay to win comes into play. You can either be like me and spend £350+ in the game and get 3 legendary weapons, alot and alot of cars, premium and symbols and stuff, or you can get lucky and buy a box for £4 and then get a legendary weapon worth millions in game but this marketplace is horrid, its all pay to win weapons and really makes the game not very fun to play when against someone with weapons from the store.   Customer Service from Gamers First is also horrible, I got banned about 2 years ago, maybe a year and a half ago for no reason, they said that I used an aimbot and such but i've never even downloaded one and after spending about £200 in the game at this point I was annoyed, I contacted them and explained my situation and even offered them to scan my PC using teamviewer or screenshots of my APB folders and game but they all said every time 'Theres nothing we can do, you are banned forever' and I gave up on the game and forgot about it for a month and tried to login and I was unbanned, so its very strange and I still dont have an explination for the ban to this day. Customer service hardly respond quickly and they always respond with an automated response, no real human so thats a massive dissapointment.  The missions consist of 'Capture this, deliver this, break this, steal this, deliver this, do this, go here' and you can do ALL of that, literally do ALL of that and then if your opposition has the better shot or gun you will lose the mission because at the end of EVERY mission it all comes down to who has the better car or gun. As a criminal you will most of the time be breaking into winwos and setting doors on fire to steal hard drives and deliver them, you can do ALL 5 set of missions before the end deathmatch and still lose the mission but if you are an enforcer and you stop the cirminal delivering the first hard drive congrats. You win. The missions you can for the most part live with but after a while they start repeating and get quickly boring so you'll find yourself in the Fight Club districts or the Asylum in team deathmatch just to spice things up or you'll find yourself in the very fun new dynamic missions that are present in the regular districts where they resemble the most recent Christmas event where you find a granade launcher and you need to kill 15 other players who try to kill you before delivering it to a contact, the granade launcher gives you buffed health and a buffed launcher.  Overall this game is very fun at parts but very very annoying at times so if you can put up with the Pay to win, the small amount of hackers, the bad combat, the boring missions and the horrible customer support to play a game that resembles a bad GTA with AMAZING customization then this game is great. I can put up with it so I love it, its my favorite game of all time so far and it still is even though it has its flaws and faults I love it and theres rumors going around that G1C are making a new game engine with updated graphics, physics and such so maybe, just maybe if its true and released before this game dies it will change the entire game for the better.  TL;DR: A very buggy game with awful combat, pay to win players, customer support, matchmaking, updates but its worth it if you like customization and want a game where you can make a million personas with cars, songs, symbols and clothes and if you can put up with the bad things you will fall in love with this game.

Heresy!  What do you get when you take a different, albeit risky, spin on a popular franchise? You get Dawn of War II, a squad/tactics-based strategy game set in the Warhammer 40,000 universe which is also the first proper sequel in the Dawn of War series.  What is striking in Dawn of War II is how Relic decided to throw away all of the base-building and army management mechanics - staples of the strategy genre - and focus on squad/tactics-based mechanics instead where you can control of up to four (out of six) unique squads and capture points throughout various missions in the campaign. In other words, it is something like taking exclusive control of heroes in Warcraft 3. On top of that, light RPG mechanics were seamlessly implemented to give a bit more depth to the gameplay by allowing the player to level up their squads, assigning attribute points, unlocking new abilities, collecting dropped loot and customizing war-gears to their will; essentially a character progression system. These RPG mechanics, while basic, do provide some diversity in tactics when preparing for a mission.   With no prior knowledge of Warhammer 40,000’s lore, aside from these Space Marines worshipping this Emperor of theirs and barbarically killing Tyranids (and other races), as if they are BJ Blazkowicz on steroids, Dawn of War II is actually capable of providing an interesting and somewhat immersive story, albeit nothing new to this genre though, with a fairly strong narrative even to someone who knows absolutely nothing about the Warhammer universe, like myself. About an hour or so in the campaign, you will quickly familiarise yourself with the lore in no time. However, this time around you are allowed to play whichever mission your heart desires in the campaign, with each pick having a certain consequence depending on its objectives. If you want to take a break from the main story-missions, distress calls act as secondary missions for you to complete within a limited time, despite most of them getting generic after a time. At least this creates a layer of non-linearity in the campaign, even if it is all just an illusion. And once again, unquestionable high production values all around coming from someone like Relic, especially in its visuals and voice acting. Where do they find these voice actors?  Relic has been known for making good campaigns in their games, even memorable ones as seen in Company of Heroes, but ultimately everyone plays these games for the multiplayer. And there is no denying that Relic did well here too, again. With co-op added, it is possible to play the campaign with a friend and/or battle against each other in either the Victory Point Control or Annihilation modes. But the real meat of the multiplayer is in the Last Stand mode where you have to survive waves of AI controlled units. It is simply addicting and so much fun to play in this mode, especially with a group of friends. No wonder most current servers just run the Last Stand mode, because it is that popular.  Sadly where Dawn of War II falls short is in its small and quite uninspiring map designs, repetitiveness in some of its mission objectives and levels, underwhelming boss-fights, squads’ poor path-finding and the lack of other playable races in the campaign (see the expansions packs); having different perspectives from different factions is always a bonus in a story. Also, once you clear out the campaign, there is little reason for coming back to it straightaway since the story, while pretty good, is not worth another playthrough for quite some time; unless you want to play on a higher difficulty, collect all of the achievements or maybe delve into co-op with a friend.  And frankly, while the gameplay is probably not as deep (as you may wanted it to be), it can still deliver plenty of hours of fun regardless. Come to think of it, the reason why it is fun is because it is simplified, stripped down from its original roots. No more headaches from micromanaging all your troops across big maps. In actual fact, it is almost like playing a MOBA game, minus some aspects and without that overwhelming depth in gameplay. Moreover, due to the small size of most maps, a mission can take can up to around 15 minutes to complete, with multiplayer matches lasting potentially longer depending on skills. This eliminates the possibility of long-winded plays and getting tired by them, although this is fairly subjective.  In conclusion, Dawn of War II is a good example where a developer took the risk to try something dynamically new on a popular and acclaimed franchise to at least satisfactory results. Whether this might have caused them to lose some hardcore fans whilst also gaining new ones, I personally do not care. It is a solid squad/tactics-based strategy game nonetheless, backed up by a story faithful to the Warhammer universe, a well-written narrative, high production values (as we are accustomed from Relic) and a strong multiplayer component thanks to its co-op and Last Stand mode, but definitely not without glaring flaws. Overall nothing mind-blowing, but it is tons of fun. If you are looking for a straightforward traditional RTS, either pick the original Dawn of War or try any other RTS out there instead, if you are desperately missing the good-old base-building and army management mechanics. Otherwise, this is a solid recommendation regardless if you know anything about Warhammer in general.  And who needs to wear a helmet in combat when you have oversized body armours which weight a tonne and have jump packs? Crazy, I know. And very impractical.

Not addictive enough. You start with 4 base guns (rifle, rocket launcher, beam and one more) which are typical defaults in any first person shooter.  PROS Capture the flag mode is quite fun. Here, it is capture the Hammer (which is a one hit kill mega weapon) making it more entertaining.  Domination mode has been modified to be slightly more fun. Here, it is Raise the Undies (where you try to raise a pair of boxers on a flag pole). There are multiple domination points which change whenever one point is captured. At any one time, only one point is capturable. Compared to typical Domination modes, where camping at the captured point is compulsory for defense, moving the capture points makes everyone rush to a single point to fight around it, increasing interaction.   CONS Grinding. Having to grind and level up to get weapons/skills that already exist for FREE in Team Fortress 2 is a severe waste of time e.g. invisibility, sentry. If you can get the same abilities in TF2 for free without grinding, it makes no sense.  Extremely similar items e.g. grenade that comes in 1. explosive 2. pyro 3. healing 4. buff. Still a grenade just different effects. Does not make it amazing.

Triple Town is one of the best puzzle games on iOS, and the Steam version brings a few differences. The time attack mode is replaced with a capital city where you can build farms, mines, and military structures for bonuses and cash. You also have a persistent inventory now, instead of using tiles as soon as you buy them. Overall, it feels like a slightly more PC-oriented game. My verdict: buy it, but wait for a sale. $10 is a bit too much for an (admittedly robust) match-three game.

Insurgency is the number one tactical FPS that is avaiable to both those looking to play serious games (like ARMA) and to those looking to just run around and have fun (say, Battlefield-ish).   Obviously the game strikes a rather big middle ground. The game takes itself serious - and that is a good thing - and has gritty realism that is not suited for everyone. If you get shot once, you will die in most cases. At the same time the game does not make you feel like you _lost_ even when you die. It's satisfying to watch your teammates play in most of the game modes (I suggest Ambush! I love the gamemode, even when I'm dead. I can enjoy myself, laughing at my teammates and the VIP with other dead teammates.)  Dying feels more like 'oh ♥♥♥♥, I made a mistake' rather than '♥♥♥♥ING HAX! I AM GONNA REPORT YOU!' which is rare in most competitive FPS games.   The game runs well, even on my laptop, and I'm sure it's better for those with desktops. The large amount of mods available make the game fun to play, and the guns can be turned into pretty much anything.   Also there's anime girls available for those that want it. It changes the graffiti around the maps to anime girls.  On to the bad sides:   The game still frequently deals with hackers. It's not every match, it's not even once every week, but there's definitely a lot of hacks and they don't seem to get banned. Either that or the detection system is ♥♥♥♥. Mostly it'll rely on the enemy team realizing, and kicking, but more often than not you have to complain for multiple games in a row before they do that.   The game can be frustrating at times. The inventory system is a bit unclear at times but easy enough to understand, but besides that frustration, I also frequently struggle with boredom after 1-2 rounds if I am not playing a high action, fast paced game. For some of the longer modes, the maps feel too big.   The map variety is also decent, but should be bigger. You can play some of the maps once before you know all the spots, and some of the maps you can play 10x and not know all the spots. Sadly for some maps it's impossible to know all spots because the game, like Battlefield, forces you to stay in a certain area. I would understand if it was just around the enemy spawn, but it often is like half the map.  However I definitely encourage you to buy this game. It's great!

There can never be enough recommendations for Fallout: New Vegas. So I'm here to add another one. This is a game I will never stop playing. Even though I've gotten all the steam achievements, I still haven't explored all that this game has to offer. I've seen and memorized much of the Mojave Wasteland, but with each new playthrough, there is something new to learn and something new to do or discover. This is one of the best games I have ever played and I can never thank enough the friend who first got me to play a Fallout game. I'm not sure what to say about this game that hasn't already been said-nor am I sure how to properly praise it in some new and informative way. I will try to do so, however. For starters, there are at least 4 main paths and endings to arrive at depending on whether you help yourself to everything, or pick a side with the NCR, or Caesar's sexist Legion, or help out Mr. House, the mysterious ancient protector of New Vegas. I believe I played through the main story 4 or 5 times to unlock every achievement. I wanted to do everything there is to do in New Vegas. I STILL want to do everything. I tried out a few playstyles for my first few runs through the game. At first I was a thieving monster helping out the sexist Legion as a melee focused woman. MY second playthrough, I tried to be as good as possible and helped out the NCR the whole way and mostly used pistols and other small guns. On the 3rd run, I helped out Mr. House as a big gun toting canniballistic psychopath. My last couple runs I focused on melee and boosting my damage resistences and then boosted my little pistols as much as I could to shoot down people who ran away from me. I found that was my favorite playstyle. I really love the feeling of freedom that this game brings. There's an ample amount of customization to make your own unique Courier-especially with the addition of the gun mods and the new faction system that lets you know your standing with each of the groups in the game. I could go on and on for hours about this game, but I'm realizing I won't say anything someone else hasn't already said. I mostly just felt this game deserved the recommendation and am trying to honor it by giving it one. Like I said at the start-this game can never have enough recommendations.

It is short though, like 2.5 hours. There are maybe 10 beautiful scenes with a puzzle or two each, mostly figuring out how machines work so you can progress. Many of the puzzles use a unique device (the goggles she looks through) to see the scene two different ways in order to solve puzzles. My biggest criticism is that I see no real reason to play it again.

Made it all the way to the last *stage* so to speak, and I'm never playing this game again.  Why? Completely unbalanced random BS utterly destroys any momentum you can build on your own. Too much the game is bogged down with random curse cards that leave the outcome entirely up to chance. Too often I have a good run going, then randomly I get attacked with three curses that destroy my health or give me a stupid handicap like making it so everytime I block I use up food (WHAT!?).  And the combat, ugh the combat. First of all, it's basic Batman combat, but it's done rather poorly. Many times (EVERY BATTLE IN THE END GAME) I'll be evading one attack only to have another enemy attack me mid-evade, which I cannot avoid. Screwing me over. And the arenas are so small that in many cases divide and conquer is impossible.   Oh, I'm pretty sure this game would be impossible to play if you're red/green colorblind. So keep that in mind.   A great idea destroyed by lazy design. Avoid.

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: GROUND ZEROES
review_text: Fantastic port, sets a great precedent for future Metal Gear titles coming to PC. Visually the game is incredible, even in 4k the textures are sharp and clear (aside fom the character models in places). The game itself is one really well put together stealth level, really love how it plays out.  The game is, of course, short, but everyone already knew that going in. For the price though, I would say it's worth it (I say this during a sale).  A word of caution: Even though the game runs incredibly well, the options in the options menu have negligible performance benefit. Either it runs or it doesn't, changing settings won't take it from 20 to 60 no matter what you do. Maybe that will be fixed with future patches, but if you want to see some numbers, look at the tweaking guide Nvidia put out for the game.

I don't know what to say about this game, so here is a recipe for pancakes,   Ingredients:  4tbsp plain flour (about 100g) 1 large free range egg ½ pint semi-skimmed milk + water (about 300ml - ¾ milk ¼ water) Pinch of salt Butter for frying How to make pancakes: Sieve the flour and salt into a large bowl and make a well in the mixture.  Break in the egg and stir to make a breadcrumby mix.  In a jug add 1/3 of a pint of milk and top up with water to make 1/2 a pint (200ml milk, 100ml water) - this makes a lovely light batter.  Add the milk and the water mix bit by bit, beating constantly to avoid lumps.  Tip:*If you have a food processor just throw all the ingredients in and zap until smooth!  Pop a large frying pan over a medium heat.  Add a large knob of butter (roughly 50g) and melt.  Pour the excess butter into a small bowl and reserve it for later.  Using a ladle pour a spoonful of the pancake batter into the hot pan and swirl to get a full covering - cook for about 1 min each side (it's ready when it's easy to flip over).  Serve immediately with sugar and lemon or other exciting toppings. We love cheese and Marmite for an extra British twist! Delicious!!

Hey Guys! First of all - DEFINITELY take a look at this game for yourself if you haven't already. Trust me, it will be worth. The gameplay is super fun, great squad interaction, quick arcade style combat.  Store is.. like stores are in every F2P game. You gonna have a pretty rough time racking up currency for new characters, called Mercs here, once you spent the 25.000 you get pretty early in the game (50.000 per Merc, few exceptions that cost 30.000). Still, no real advantage for paying players except that they obviously get the shiny things faster.  Balance between the Mercs seems alright, most got pretty obvious tradeoffs. BUT(!!!) the problem is not with Mercs or guns being overpowered, but the Matchmaking seeming completely random. Most of the time its either you roflstomping the enemy or the other way around. Being matched with a team of level 5-10 against 6 lvl20+ is extremely frustrating.  What i really like about the game are the secondary Objectives included in the maps. Those have different effects, that all result in a change of pathways, forcing attackers into choke points as defender, or forcing defenders to have a longer way from their respawn points to the primary objective. This makes for some really interesting interactions with your squad splitting up, like somebody sneaking on a primary objective while your team distracts the enemy at a secondary one.  Overall, this game is FAR from flawless, but if you like jumpy fast-paced shooters and/or thought games like BRINK had a lot of potential, definitely give it a try.  TL;DR + Fast Paced Action + Cool Objective Mode + Most Guns just feel great and powerful + Not P2W  - Matchmaking is a joke, creating obviously unfair teams. - Kinda grindy to get to the 'end game' equipment, no real progression system  Download it anyways. NOW.

So one gripe with the whining and moaning from a lot of reviewers i'm hearing about 'bullet sponge is breaking my immersion' - you are aware this game is an MMORPG, it's an RPG first and foremost, set in a modern day world.  Saying 'I'm really sad because my bullets aren't killing the boss in 5 bullets' is like saying 'I'm really sad because it's taking me more than 1 fire ball to kill goblins boss Gearbreaker in deadmines... he's just a goblin, 1 fireball should kill him.'  I feel like a lot of Call of duty, CS:GO, and Battlefield players were drawn to this game - and likely weren't RPG fans - and are sorely disappointed with their experience because they are expecting a tactical shooter. Instead , we have a game that is like Mass Effect (cover based shooting RPG) but in an MMO format.  If you're playing this expecting to 'BOOM HEADSHOT! 1 SHOT 1 KILL, TIME TO TEA BAG' , you're the wrong audience and I'm sorry you didn't do your research prior to playing the game. It was NEVER touted to be a hyper realistic game - it was always advertised as a RPG first and foremost, set in a modern day setting.  The only other game to do this that I can think of was APB... and well, we all know how that turned out... At least the setting of New York and the design by Ubisoft to create a living breathing world draws you in. I literally watched the life fade out of a AI civilian as he keeled over and slowly died in the street. I ran up to him seeing if I could offer food, water, soda - nothing. The breathing slowed until the chest finally stopped moving.   This game is incredible when you realize what it is, and go in with that expectation.

Original Sin boots old-school RPGs into the modern era by providing accessible yet complex mechanics without the spoon-feeding and a tidy co-op mode. A certain charm is attributed to a childish tongue-in-cheek presentation of some rather mature writing. The story is driven mainly by exploration and actually having to read every so often. It’s a shame that inventory management is ham-fisted. Combat is turn-based and challenging; but you can get creative with the environment and open-ended character builds to set-up strong synergies between party members. Original Sin makes you feel like a kid with its tone and an adult upon mastery of its systems; making for a fun lengthy romp.

Purchased this game after reading reviews. Not what I expected and in all honesty it took me several attempts - starting a play-through, abondoning it in frustration, then weeks, nay, months later having another crack it, rinse and repeat. Needless to say it took four attempts to finally get over my pre-conceptions and expectations and come to appreciate this game for itself.  Games can be educating, take the Total War series for example, and once I'd got over the shock of a fantasy game not being even remotely connected to my usual Anglo-centric Middle Earth leanings I avidly enjoyed the Slavic mythology. It's less noble and haughty, more down to earth, more folksy and rustic. It ties one to the land like the peasant one truly is. I've had a break but I'm ready for another playthrough and to my delight I find several DLC expansions available since my last foray, and free to boot! I highly recommend this game. It's up there with all the other brilliant ones, you know 'em. Go on, treat yourself.

If you've ever programmed anything, this is a game you will love. I had never programmed javascript before, or used a noSQL database, but at the end of my first day playing h a c k m u d I knew everything that needed to know. The next day I made a casino dice game to try to make some money. That hasn't caught on (yet!), but in the meantime I studied some of the npc patterns and wrote some scripts that allow me to scrape their delicious member and project accounts. Today I'm going to work on making a spambot to advertise my casino across chat channels, and maybe expand my casino with another game. I have a scratchpad containing all the things that I still want to learn and do in this game, and it's going to take me a while to get around to all of it. I've barely scratched the surface of npc interactions, and I haven't even considered pvp and how to get started with that!!  If you are new to programming: when you are a newer player, there is a character count placed on your scripts. This is important for progression in the game, but unfortunately is a bad programming practice, generally. While you can design and implement just about anything you want (if you have upgraded to a high enough character count), keep in mind that at the begninning if you are new to programming it will be more difficult because all of your variables will have unhelpful names like r, t, and s, instead of the better practice of longer-length variable names.  10/10, would spend time coloring ascii dice art again.

I don't usually review games unless they really standout and this game is special.  It's as simple or complex as you want it to be. Do you just want to build simple straightforward structures and fight easy and meduim-easy foes? No problem. Stay on the surface, Have fun. Want to get insanely technical and build automated structures? Want to be challenged by enemies as you struggle to survive? No problem. This game has it all.  What I love most is that you can open Terraria at nearly anytime (even on low-end PC's) and play the kind of game you want it to be without jumping through hoops or affecting future endevours.

I, personally, enjoy this game a whole bunch. It has problems, sure, but it's still so much fun.  The game is a 2D moba style game. Now, when you think Moba, you think 60 heroes and too many items and complexity up the bum. I want you to throw those ideas out of the window, because this game is NOT about auto attacking or using abilities, it's about twitch skill, platforming and a whole bunch of other stuff, and it's SO fun to play.  However, it has some problems I feel must be mentioned. Firstly, some heroes in the game (called 'Nauts from now on) are absolutely silly and stupid to play against, and when compared to other 'Nauts, are at a big disadvantage. Some 'Nauts are also straight up overpowered, mostly at first, but hopefully the game will be balanced out later.  Bring your friends, destroy your enemies, go buy Awesomenauts now!

This game is more like a simple version of Star Trek. You command a small crew, which can grow by recruiting through different means other characters of different races. Your ship has different components needed to travel and fight against other ships.  The weapons vary and can be useful if you know how to deploy them. For example, the Ion weapon does not cause damage but can disrupt an enemy system. Target their shields and let a Combat Droid damage their ship.  This game does play hard ball, even in Easy mode. Your choices feel like it can be 50/50. Should you help the ailing ship? Do so and you can be ambushed. While I do not mind this, what I do mind are the other choices that may cost me a member of my crew in this game. Replacing them is often difficult. The biggest draw for me with this game is you constantly want to see what the next star system has. Will it be a pirate vessel waiting to attack you? Will it be an ailing Federation ship that has a person inside who will join your crew? You constantly want to keep playing to see.

Super cheap for a wonderful game.  If you aren't okay with dying and trying again and again, this game isn't for you, You will die. You will try and try again, and you will fail at the last moment, when you finally start to build up confidence. But it's wonderful. The game has tons of strategy to it, and many ways you can go about accomplishing your goals. That said, there is luck involved. If you hate relying on luck, this game isn't for you.   I love this game because I love the challenge, I love to stress about what to buy for several minutes before making a terrible decision. This game has sparked fond memories with friends, and is always a great time. It's the kind of game that will have you saying 'just one more run' for hours, but is forgiving enough that you'll want to come back and play every time.

I'll start with the ugly part of this review: Game is unplayable on a 32 bit windows OS. They have known about issues with windows 32 bit since 0.9 and I still can't play it on my desktop. I'm forced to divert to my less than adequate laptop to play this game. I personally think it's incredibly irresponsible of them to release a full 'complete' version of the game when the game causes catastrophic failures. You know the problems, so fix them. I wouldn't be so annoyed if this was just a problem with the latest patch, or if the game was still in beta, but there have been several patches already and this issue still isn't resolved. I held off writing this review because the game was in beta and a 1.0 was soon to arrive. Figured it would fix my issues. I was wrong.  The good:  In all fairness though, this issue I’m having is slightly different than before. So there is noticeable work being done to fix the issues. It used to crash during the loading screen, and then would generate a bug report with a message asking you to send in the bug report. Now, the loading screen finishes, and when the main menu should show up it just crashes.  this game is also a TUN of fun to play! If you can get past the bugs and have a stable playing session it will blow you away. There is a lot of creativity to the game. Half of the fun is designing imperfect space ships the look awesome and then watching as they explode. I would HIGHLY recomend checking the specs before you buy the game. if you don't match them it most likely wont work.

I played in the beta when Firefall literally first entered closed about 2 years ago. This game has been in beta for a long time and FF has gone through alot of UI changes including the crafting system which was never intuitive. Simply put, is FF something you want to spend your time on?  The simple asnwer is yes! The game is completely free to play. Those that bought a founders pack years ago were gifted a few more digital items as well as keeping our levels from beta which doesn't mean anything really as we still need to do the low level story quets to continue to end game. So what is FF?  Firefall is a third person/first person Themepark MMO shooter. So it has alot of the same leveling mechanics as most themepark mmo games. That being: going to level specific areas and grabbing missions from a job board. When you've leveled past that area, the game directs you to another area with story specific quests leading you the whole time based on your level. The only thing that isn't explained AT ALL is the crafting system. You will have to root around it for a few minutes to understand the basics but simply put, you spend resources to do research on gear, then use more reseources to craft that gear. You dont have to do this, but you get better gear this way than from quests. Your abilities can also be crafted. Keep in mind though you get the same abilities from quests that you already have they just go up in level. So there is progression that way.   Each battleframe acts as a class and you can spend real money to unlock them or grind each class to unlock an advanced one. I wouldnt say this game is pay to win because you can buy resources with real money but it really doesn't matter. You cant be ganked by other players unless you enter a pvp specific instance. So pay 2 win doesn't really matter in the pve world unless you care that one guy does more dps than you because he spent cash to accelerate his crafting recipes.   All in all I'd say it's worth wasting your time in. The suits you wear look pretty legit and if you want to customize you gear you can spend a few dollars here and there to do so. Now that FF is FINALLY out of open beta, it has a TON of players on at all times. Just check the community panel, it's over 10k on at any one time. So this means getting groups for stuff is fairly easy.  Edit: So most of my review was for the original game from beta. After dabbling with the release version I'd still say this is worth downlaoding for free and wasting time in but....it is a completely differant game than what I played a year ago.   I mentioned above that this is a themepark mmo and rightfully so. The original game was a sandbox experience with no levels keeping you from certain content. Now, you have to literally go to each zone that matches your current level or you get penalized. It's pretty ridiculous but again, its free. Its just sad that Red 5 changed the game so much from what it originally was. Its so simple now. You really dont have to equip much gear. Only 3 items that you have to worry about.   After playing the game even more in the release version I have to change my reccomendation to No. The game is just too tedious. You go to the SAME location multiple times for differant missions. So it turns into this grind of running back and forth to the same area then back to the quest hub. THere is nothing tying me emotionally to the game. Like I don't care really about my character or what's going on in the universe simply because there is no depth to the characters. They are all forgettable in seconds.

The keyword to describe this game is 'Love'. I couldn't tell you where exactly the love is going towards, be it the old Shadow Warrior, the old shooter genre in general, martial art action/drama films, Eastern pop fiction or just good game development practice. But one thing is certain: this has got to be one of the most surprisingly high quality games I've ever encountered. While initially excited about Shadow Warrior making a comeback, I had my doubts about this game, I had thought that Devolver was going to try and ride on the success it was getting with Croteam's Serious Sam 3 and milk games of similar genre for all its worth in the style of Activision's usual workings, and after playing Hard Reset I had once thought Flying Wild Hog a group similar to The Farm 51 in that they were a studio no longer in the glory days of People Can Fly, and while trying their hardest weren't quite yet at that standard of quality people like me would be paying attention to.  I was wrong. I was wrong on every front. Not only has Devolver proved me wrong and established its a publisher willing to put time and money into a product as absurd and obscure as Shadow Warrior, but Flying Wild Hog has also proven to me that they were the studio that cleary are the best of the people that fled People Can Fly, and have managed to take an old magic to soaring new heights.  This game is something of a small miracle; its the video game equivilant of The Lego Movie. It is baffling just how polished and sophisticated this game's mechanics are. The sword combat is sharp and absolutely sublime, so much that it feels alien, like it should belong in another genre, in a game that would play like Devil May Cry or something from the works of Platinum Games. The moves and powers you can pull off with the sword are visercal and satisfying, and enemies will dismember like paper mache origami made from blood and internals. The guns meanwhile feel like the best parts of Painkiller and Bulletstorm with the visercal satisfaction of the former and the stragetic rewarding of the latter. There's no gun that's a giant stakegun or a weapon that'll let you steer sniper rounds up the taints of enemies, but there's a gun that'll shoot high-velocity, explosive crossbow bolts, and baby, I couldn't ask for anything more delightfully enjoyable.  And its all because of that keyword: love. There was no reason this game should have been this good, or even good at all, but because of the love from a small American publisher and an even smaller Polish developer, this game has hit completely out of the park in every possible way.  I cannot stress this enough: you need to try this game. Its a perfect 10/10, and its forever put Devolver and Flying Wild Hog on my radar.

But when you finally get the hang of the controls, and you reload a clip seamlessly and quickly for the first time, you feel like such a badass. 10/10.

Preface: I liked mirrors edge and I have played a lot of shooters/melee games. I did not like dead island but did like it's option to throw melee weapons and modify them.  Now that that I have declared the things which may lead me to have some bias we can get on with it. The game has a reasonably good storyline albeit somewhat predictable at times. The reason for you being in the situation makes sense, the interavtion with other characters makes sense. If I were to criticise any character ni the ggame it would be the main character for not developing much of a personality until about 60-70% of the way into the story. You may have heard that it relis upon a damsel in destress storyline, this is NOT the case with the 'damsel' saving your life at least twice during the course of the game.  The combat is good and relatively fluid. you can equip 4 melee weapons at a time and 4 utilities ranging from a UV torch to electricuted riot shields. The stamina systems keep the game challenging and the different 'special infected' range from irritating to down right deadly and maintain that position until relatively late into the game.  The weapon crafting is reasonable extensible with the ability to add effects such as fire or impact to weapons as well as modifications which add to damage, handling or durability to varying degrees.  The exp rewards are reasonable and the skills you can unlock are helpful with only one of them, the grappling hook, really detracting to the difficulty of the game bar the ultimate upgrade from each tree, which essentially give you infinite stamina for their associated ability e.g. agility gemoves running and climbing stamina costs.  The different skill trees do add a lot of different playstyles to your arsenal and I will say it again none of the upgrades feel boken.  There is a reasonable amount of humour in the game including one of my favourite insults I have heard in years from 'the twins' who are a pair of intelligent yet condescending brothers with an inability to relate to other people.  The co-op is a good laugh and saves any progress you make to your campaign evein if you are not the host.  Overall I would recommend this game a lot. It may not be original but it has taken the best parts of a lot of games with only a few issues that I have noticed.

Night time is very good fun *Decent storyline and side-missions. Probably more than 20 hours of play  CONS: *Getting co-op to work may be a bit hard, had to use Hamachi even though both people had legit copies *Looting late game can be tiresome with hundreds of zombies *A lot of the perks obtained upon levelling up aren't really interesting. Grappling hook is obtained a bit too late I reckon *Occasionaly gets frozen...  Would recommend for sure!
