Very good game!. Although not a very long campaign it was still very epic. The nemesis system is pretty cool. I would give this game 9/10. I only wish it had a multiplayer aspect to it which would be if people online can rank up like the way the orcs/uluks do. And challange people to duels ect in the same fashion you set your orcs/uluks to fight /invade/ riot. With players able to form there own armys to fight others and work with other player captain warchiefs like the pc do.   Summary if you don't like reading- get this game. Its good --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Graphics - superb not the best ever but its not that far.  Gameplay-  Gameplay is amazing, you probably heard it a billion times to people comparing it to assasins creed. Its pretty much like that. I havn't played batman yet but its the same as assasins creed but with different buttons for countering depending on enemy types. Also ocassional quicktime events when you fight to kill captains/ chiefs ect. Enemies have various weakness and strengths and is especially more important to look them up fighting higher leveled captains / warchiefs. As sometimes they may have something such as a very good strength such as one giving you no second chances so if they get you once, you cannot quick time to save yourself. Also some were practicacly unstoppable. I remember one where he was immuned to combat finishers, stuns, stealth kills and was also a dual weilder. Duel weilders cannot be attacked directly with regular strikes and normally require stun, so finding their fears sometimes are essential. Although I feel like the vault stun was a bit over empowering. This is just for single campaign, I just started the DLC. Once I got the ability to brand captains (convert enemies to be allies) and orcs it was a cake walk though since I could just make them attack each other.Combat branding was a bit over empowering. Would have been better if you could not combat brand but request branded soldiers to follow you around. But still a one of a hell fun game to play.   Also just to note, Some of these captains / uluks have rediculous names lol. I remember one called I think it was 'wimp the dung collector'. He was a low level uluk soldier that got a kill off me when I was fighting hordes of those guys trying to escape. He became an elite captain but I killed him xD.  Also its best to not try to get killed in this game as because if they kill you, they get a pomotion, increase in power and therefore get new strengths to them as well.  Audio / Music:  Music is epic in this game with a obvious but cool war like theme. I especially like the scenes when captains/warchiefs arrive and the short cut scenes come up with a brief of hynm of their name. The trash talking of the uluks in this game is pretty good. Initially in the game I was suprised of the personality in this game. Voice acting is very good. At first I was surprised as nearly every time I encountered them they had something different to say, They even say things pertaining to your death, thinking you are dead or how you scarred them, ect? But later in the game some seened to reoeat some of the same things from previous in the game but it maybe because of the generic NPC that later become capatins and warchiefs when I lost and dead ones positions were replaced.  Story:  Like I said it was short. I spent much more time having fun killing captains but it was good. Although in the later half of the game it was alittle more generic. But Overall was still epic.  Verdict: Get this game, even if you don't know anything about even like lord of the rings. I don't really know lord of the rings but this game was amazing.

app_name: Dysfunctional Systems: Learning to Manage Chaos
review_text: This is probably the most gorgeous visual novel I've ever played, and the production values are top-notch. Moreover, it's smartly written (the interactions between characters is so fun and compelling, and I really enjoy its worldbuilding). This game has a fantastic premise and I find myself hungering for more. I really, REALLY like this game and feel it comes at a price where it is well-worth the investment. And I like the weight of the decisions I made and the possibility it could have on future installments.  My previous edition of this review warned that this series would remain incomplete, but since the successful kickstarter you can expect more installments in the future. Dysfunctional Systems has all the potential in the world, so keep an eye on it!  Look into Juniper's Knot if you haven't already as well, it's free, it's the right length, and it's very captivating.

Dominions is simple yet complicated. This board game-esque turn-based strategy game features a massive amount of different units, varying factions that all specialize in various areas, randomly generated maps, and even the ability to create your own factions and gods. The premise is relatively simple, you are a God who is battling other gods to ascend as the supreme God Ruler of all existence and the AI will not go easy on you. Trust me, I've played both the 3rd and the 4th games and the AI has beaten me senseless almost every time I've played. I highly suggest the game as the gameplay is relatively fast paced for a TBS and as such is incredibly addicting. You'll be hard-pressed to close the game when it'll only take 4 more turns to conquer the Undead guys. Definitely recommended.

Unfortunately I can't recommend this game. Nice ideea and could be a nice.  But it has so many problems that will keep you from enjoying the game (a patch could solve some of them but... well nobody cares): - there are anoying bugs (tells me the building isn't conected to the road network but it is).  - the selling process if very very bad. esentially you select what item you want to sell and for what price. the sell will be automatically IF you have available vehicles. If you don't you have to manually select thev ehicle (when it's available). It's not a problem at first, but when you advance into the game it will become a problem and it will keep you from enjoying the game. - you can;t buy materials so you only can produce them and transport them from one building to another . This will have to be done by a truck that is assigned only for that task or manually. And again, after some time in the game this management becomes hard.  If some of this issues will be addressed i am pretty sure that it will be a pretty nice game. But so far the game resumes to: build, wait, sell, build, wait, sell...

Ah, Trove. The single best game I have played in a few years now. That isn't saying much, because I don't play a lot of games, but... eh.  To talk of this game, the easiest comparrison I can make is to Cube World. Some say it is a blatent rip-off. I say it's everything Cube World was, but with a TEAM of devs who LISTEN to fans.  Let's get started, shall we?  PROS: -Trove has simple, fun gameplay that isn't overcomplicated and is fun for children and adults alike. -Every single battle has a chance to reward you, and all dungeons offer rewards. -The game's 'Loot Collector,' which is a machine that converts dungeon loot you don't need into basic crafting material, or Flux, is ingenious. -Despite this game being free-to-play, the COMPLETELY OPTIONAL purchases are either cosmetic or will help you skip work that is plenty easy to do on your own. This isn't some game that has insanely long obligatory wait times. There's a purchase-only kind of currency, called Credits, but it is used to either make purchases easier or to puchase cosmetic-only items. Not only that, but for certain Mastery levels you are awarded these Credits. And speaking of Mastery... -Styles and Mastery. For a completionist, this game has it ALL. This game could take you WEEKS to get ALL stuff, and that isn't counting event-exclusive stuff. Every new kind of style, every new pet, every new mount/wings/boat/tome/MORE gives you Mastery points, and a certain amount of Mastery levels you up in the Mastery department. Each level-up gives you a new reward. Yeah, you need to pay money to get all the costumes and such to fll it up... OR DO YOU? Not here! There is a trade chat channel where you can buy costumes for various amounts of Flux, often around 40k at the time of this review. And speaking of trade... -A community-hosted market. In the trade channel, you can buy pretty much anything aside from weapon, mask, and hat styles. You can also sell your product, too. And it doesn't benefit the makers, so we won't have the Trove Dev Team gettng lazy like Valve and pulling 200000000000000000000000 dollars a year off of the community market. -ACTIVE, CARING DEVS. These devs are smart and give the fans what they ASK for, such as a more complicated endgame, and they have promised to work on a PVP system later on. -The endgame. Thanks to the Rise of the Shadow Tower, we have a serious endgame with cool bosses such as teh Weeping Prophet and Pinata God (cooler than it sounds.) -The Co-op. EVERY MMO EVER NEEDS THIS GAME'S LOOT SYSTEM. As opposed to dropping loot that ANYONE can grab, loot is INDIVIDUALIZED. So if an enemy drops a rare item for you, someone else can't steal it, because it ONLY shows up for you and 7 other people nearby. ANd, let's say you're mining some Shapestone. Well, for every block you break, each person gets 1 block of shapestone. WHICH. IS. AWESOME. Runescape, World of Warcraft, EVERY MMO, LOOK AT THIS GAME. IT HAS THE SINGLE BEST LOOT SYSTEM I HAVE EVER SEEN. And almost every single task in-game is benefitted from having multiple people helping, so there really is no excuse to be alone...   These are just a FEW of the pros, but if I gush any longer, I'll forget to review the cons.  CONS.  -The INFINITE number of styles. Getting 100% in this is nearly impossible... That adds to the fun, because there is always more to explore, but to someone who wants to finish this and be done, you'll be here for a while... -Gameplay can be repetitive. There isn't any denying that the gameplay of each individual class varies, but... there is only one startegically beneficial way to play each class for the most part. And as others say, thsi makes the game very grindy to get what you want... I find this forgivable though, simply because if you get bored of grinding one thing you can grind another.  -THE TUTORIAL. Very short! Yet it also explains... next to nothing. It explains the main design and all, but this game suffers from what I call 'Terraria Syndrome.' Be ready to look a LOT of stuff up if you don't have a friend who can explain it! Why does every MMO ever SUCK SO MUCH...? -OVERLY active devs...? Yeah, the servers are kinda... down... a lot. And given the lack of single-player... it kinda... sucks. But they go down for maintenance, which makes them a LOT better. -FISHING. SO useful! You can turn 90 'Glim' (a kind of currency in-game) into 200-400. However... Fishing takes an ETERNITY...  Overall, I rate this game a 9.5 out of 10. The flaws don't detract from the gameplay enough for a significantly bad rating, despite their number... I would reccomend this game to any and every person who saw Cube World, liked it, but either couldn't afford it or were let down by its developer. Just be ready to looks some crap up...

Senselessly missing features present in old games, wildly inconsistent and wholly disappointing story mode, and - biggest of all - necessary online but no working server since launch day.  I downloaded this game on release excited as hell for the newest installment of the series. I was met with a message that it couldn't connect to the server, so my progress couldn't be saved. That was July 29th. It is now August 3rd, and I still have only been able to play once, and only for a few laughably easy story matches. app_name: Making History: The Calm and the Storm Gold Edition
review_text: This game is a gem that I have just had the pleasure of buying at a great price and having a good time with. I have only played a short time, but this game is so awesome I just had to come over to this screen and tell the world how awesome it is.   It's easy to pick up, fun and addictive to play, looks pretty cool, and honestly: it's really complex. There is A LOT that goes with this game. Not only does trade, diplomacy, and economy matter, but the game revolves around these aspects quite a bit.   Obviously being set during WW2, your job is simple: build an empire or outproduce everywhere (or both); every decision has a consequence, and I gotta admit, it gives the average person an insight into the functioning of nations today.   Wanna be an industrial power? You're gonna make less food; wanna field a large and powerful army? Hope you have financial reserves because guns aren't cheap.   It's a cool little game, and I have a feeling I'm gonna fall in love with it. I kinda already have.   Get it.

Overall, quite good for a point and click adventure, although a little overpriced. Most puzzles were easy yet enjoyable. Graphics are good. Would definitely pick it up for under 10, but 25 is a stretch.  Negatives:  Finding what you can/should interact with was a little more difficult than it needed to be. Lots of descriptive text for objects, but very little is actually usable. This makes it a little frustrating at times as you have to filter out quite a bit.  It was a little hard to fully empathize with the plight of the main character. More background on the main characters would have helped. There is a ton of background on other characters littered about in PDA and terminal entries, which accentuates how little there is on the main characters.  Voice-over and main character dialog seemed a bit cheesy. Is it bad acting, poor writing, and/or lack of background? I can't pinpoint what made it feel forced to me.

Undertale is a great game and I absolutely recommend it. The game deals with morality in games in a new way. Without spoiling too much, this game is very different from traditional RPGs in that for every encounter in the game, you can resolve the fight peacefully or violently. Which resolution you pick changes your experience greatly.  In terms of gameplay, Undertale uses a simplified of traditional RPG combat. Instead of learning new moves to attack with, you find new armor and weapons to boost your ATK or DEF. When an enemy attacks you, you have the chance to avoid damage not by RNG but by a bullet hell-esque situation where you literally must dodge the attacks.  This game is much better when played without prior knowledge so instead of continuing to write a review, I'll just recommend that you play it. It really is a great game and is definitely worth playing.

Let me tick off the first few things I disliked, followed by what finally had me just shut it down and uninstall it: First, unskippable intro videos for the various production companies. I hated it in 2009 with Borderlands, I hate it now. Second, kicking into 800x600 resolution. What year do you think it is? Nobody with a PC that can run any modern game has that resoution. Nobody. Autodetect or get out. Third, secondary sign in. I'm already on Steam, now I'm wiating for some other random sign in BS. I don't play games that require Uplay, I'm not going to put up with this. And the kicker that had me uninstall: when trying to correct the horrid resolution, I found my mouse clicks were all hitting down and left from actual mouse position. So, crappy, buggy, annoying. I grabbed it with a Humble Bundle just to get another game, and I still feel kind of ripped off.   Remember with these guys made Borderlands and it blew our minds? Then Borderlands 2 came along and did it again, even bigger? How long are we going to let them coast on that?

It is also very easy to figure out, and it seems to have good replay value.    9.5/10 would recommend.

In all honesty, I really want to like this game. It seems to have kind of a Walking Dead storyline going for it. Different survivors with different skills and whatnot. Also, the graphics aren't terrible, but they aren't great either. There are just too many issues with gameplay (horrible collision problems, terrible viewing setup and control, etc.) that keep me from being able to give it a thumbs up. Most of these problems could easily be fixed with a bit of effort on the part of the developers but don't keep your fingers crossed... it seems this title has long been abandoned. It seems like such a waste to have went through the effort to put all this together and just walk away from it without giving it a final polish so it could be enjoyable. To be truthful, I'll probably find myself trying to trudge through all the irritating issues and see where the story goes until I succeed or delete out of frustration. If your looking for a headache and new things to make you curse grab it,... otherwise I have to say steer clear of this one unfortunately.   Final Verdict- 3/10

I originally had this game on the PS2(I still have it) and spent hundreds, if not thousands of hours on it. I have played many Star Wars games on different consoles and the PC and trust me when I say that this game is fun. It is best played with friends, because you will have a lot of fun fighting with them for control of whatever planet you are on. The campaign is OK, not outstanding, but not bad. The missions are a bit short, but they can be fun even if you have played them a bunch of times. You are rewarded for your work on single player with various abilities and weapons if you can reach 'Legendary' status, which means you have got the medal 64 times. You can get way overpowered weapons once you get 'Legendary' on some challenges, but remember these only apply to single player games, not online. The AI on normal is a bit dumb at some points, but the Elite setting is more challenging, but not impossible. I would say overall it is 8.5 out of 10, but I would buy this game over and over.

I love this game.   Whether you are a creative builder type a la mine craft, a dungeoneer cavern explorer, or classic platforming god, terraria will have something for you.   Thousands of doodads, tons or armor sets, modifiers, weapons, spells, pets, and even mounts.  Build a castle, a house, or just a wooden square. Search out and gear up until you can take on all of the hardest challenges, or just sit and fish constantly. make a lava lake in the sky. Why not?  The game is best experienced with others, but the single player aspects are still meaty and full of worth. the gear progression is not grindy until late, and even then isnt anything aburd. The progression also seems natural, though certain design descisions seems to push you into certain playstyles (melee at first, then ranged, and finally full on magic) There is a lot of content for a pretty cheap price  If you arent a minecrafter, you may still find value in terraria, as its far more rpg than it is creation kit (the creation aspects are robust, mind you)  But it, love it, play with friends

The setting looks very cartoonish, but overall I think it makes for a cool atmosphere. Each class has it's own skill set, while it isn't very hard to get accustomed to each one. It felt very easy to jump right in and get accustomed to using each skill. A cool feature is that you can get rid of skill points for the last few levels that you chose. This is a great tool for people that just want to see how a skill works. The different weapons types are nice and I found the pet system feel awesome. I liked that you could chose any pet and not worry about which one is best.  Can't say anything bad about this game. 10/10

The appeal of Endless Space is simple but beautiful. Overall, it feels like some sort of Civilization Light (in space). It is not as good as Civ, because both diplomacy and battles are by far too simple. However, I consider these two points to be the only week spots of ES. Overall, it's more than just a solid 4x game. Faction creation, tech tree, colonozing, heroes - all these things are expertly balanced and carefully considering your next steps is just as much fun as it is in Total War or Civilization. Anyway, if you like 4x space games, you probably already bought this, right?

Great game, love the free content and getting the dlc now. Theres some base civilizations, each to a different playsyle, but you can make your own civiization, and make it to how you like to play, whether it be scientific, military, or economic. It's very easy to pick up and learn, with some more advanced options that you don'tusually see in games like this, such as tax rates. It's wonderful to be able to design your ships and test them out in battle, and this also makes civilizations to adapt to eachother during the war, and whoever can adapt and then pump out the new ships before the other can do so, wins. That mechanic also makes technology much more important, if your opponent has the tech to make it so ships carry more weight and you don't, that 20% difference in weight could win the war. This game also has a ton of options for how you want that specific game to be, galaxy type, density, amount of constellations, amount of wormholes, it's crazy. It is even better online with your friends or even ramdom people. The only reason I haven't played hundreds of hours is because of Civ 5 BNW. Although, the combat system is sadly lacking, apparently they fixed it in Dishormony, but I don't know, he problem is that the battles are all cinematic, and you can skip that, don't worry, but you can't control any aspect except for cards to boost and/or counter your/the opponents fleet. You can't even have them direct their fire onto one ship, and I've lost many battles when everyone shot at ramdom ships, not the obvious leader that carried half the fleet on his back. All in all Fantastic title, if you like stratagy games, totally worth your time, especially with these sales.

I loved this game on console and was happy to see it included in the summer sale on Steam, however, upon loading the game eager to jump in and rob some banks I discovered a game crippling bug, the in-game mouse will load but will not sync with the system mouse. What does this mean? It means many things, the main menu screen is only partially navigable through keyboard commands, the in-game menu suffers the same problem, and even worse, when actually trying to play the game, there is no way to force my character to change the direction they are facing, only move forward/back and left/right; and mouse click buttons are non responsive as well, essentially making the game unplayable.  I spent the majority of my short time on this game simply sitting on the home screen trying multiple different wired and wireless mouse combinations, steam controller and xbox controllers, but to no avail. Nothing works to engage the in-game mouse controls.   All that being said, I understand that this is probably a unique issue and not universal to every player, and I still highly recommend playing this game for those are interested in an antagonist main character experience :]

The AI is pretty decent, but cheap at times, some weapons have questionable use and controls are imprecise. Even with said complaints Hotline Miami is a great pick, if not just for the music.

This game took me by the heart.  Normally, I would dismiss such a game as a time-killing iPad app in PC clothing. But something about the charm of it lead me to anticipate its release.  Reus takes everything wrong with humanity and wraps it up in an atmosphere of childish innocense--- a beautiful take on our own world and on who we are.  The gameplay, smooth and accomodating, can be called puzzle-solving on analysis but does not feel that way at all; it feels like everything you do is for a reason and yields a natural consequence as in any good simulator. The challenge and reward system also makes for a heap of replayability and excitement for more to work with as you progress.  My only wish is that there had been more content and greater limits to strive toward--- but that is my desire of any great game. I'm looking forward to Abbey Games' additional contributions to my library.

Morrowind is a game that requires patience, critical thinking and an open mind. Morrowinds graphics may look aboslutely horrible, but it doesn't take away from the enormous world, interesting story, strategic (and frequently disliked) combat and the emmersive world. If you have a lot of time on your hands and are looking for an awesome open world rpg, give Morrowind a fair chance. It's different, but thats not always a bad thing.

The game itself is quite frankly...average. Or even below average.  The system itself is good and fun, lot of items to loot, monsters to kill, researches to do, empires to dominate. When you add mods it is even better. The sad part, and what makes this game almost '♥♥♥♥♥♥' is that it is so bugged, that it makes it sometimes unplayable (for a lot of people, just check official forum support page). The games crashes a lot, there is some kind of problem with memory leaks. The grid on tactical map is almost invisible so it is hard to tell where your troops can move. Sometimes the turn doesn't want to end, and only way is to reset the whole game. What is shamefull, is that mods from players are much better than the 'Vanilla' game itself. It pisses me off, to see 'another dlc' every month, when they can't even fix basic problem with game (bugs, crashes and incredible bad game optimisation).  In my opinion, the game itself is not worth the money. It can give a lot of fun, but at the same time the frustration is unbearable.

Unfortunately, this also affects developers who don't take advantage of the DLC money grabbing.  This game was from a -75% sale (0,74€), as well, which is quite a good price for this. Might have even paid 1,50€. But do note: while the idea is great, the game is indeed short. Then again, as a satire game, it doesn't need to be any longer. It has achieved in relaying its message.  Also, the gamepad controls are tight and accurate. Very enjoyable to play.

The controls, I would perfer to use my digtal pad to control my character and not the analog. This is a 2D dungeon crawler and a digtal control makes for better control. But like I said, a minor gripe. How you tackle them game, well the game lets you chose. This game can be as easy or hard as you like it to be. If it's too tuff just grind up your level on weaker creatures until you can overpower bosses or you can figure out the bosses weakness and defeat them the harder way. This is a game that will definitely suck up my time when I get so time to lose later but given the creativity in this surprising small gem, don't be surprised if you too get hooked. Definitely a game done right and smart. Go get Epic with your Unepic self...

app_name: Deus Ex: Human Revolution - Director's Cut
review_text: I bought this game when it was sale, and I do not regret my purchase. Human Revolution Director's Cut was my first Deus Ex game in the series that I played, and I will be now looking forward to buying Mankind Divided, Deus Ex GOTY, Invisible War, and even The Fall. I spent 30 hours on my first playthrough, and I was really satisfied with the gameplay, music, and presentation of the game. The game does fall short graphics-wise, since I found that faces of characters sometimes represent the uncanny and that the faces of the characters don't match the expressions they are supposed to. I nominated this game for the 2016 'Just 5 More Minutes' Award because the game is addicting. I could not stop playing it after I pressed 'New Game'. The ambition to find out what happens to Megan, as well as the beautiful cities the game transports you to, made me want to keep playing as much as I could until the storyline was resolved. I highly recommend this game to anyone who is new or a veteran to the Deus Ex series.

Did you like Diablo 2? Then you will like Path of Exile. Did you DISLIKE Diablo 3? Then you will LOVE Path of Exile.   To call it a clone of Diablo 2 would probably be a fair accusation, but that is far from a bad thing - infact it's exactly what I wanted. I still love Diablo 2, but I can't exactly go back to it considering there are practically no players, and is locked into 800x600 resolution, while I have a 1920x1080 monitor. The biggest differences is the way passive skills work, your abilities are controlled by gems socketed in your armor and weapons, and the end game map system. However, all of that is really awesome and makes it stand out - not the most new-player friendly, but I love it, and the versitility you have with your character. There is also the events/races system, and those are a ton of fun if you can catch them.   The best part for many people is probably the fact that it is FREE TO PLAY. The microtransactions are mostly just aesthetics. Literally, the only microtransactions that aren't just visual, are character slots (which you start out with a whopping 24 anyway), and bank tabs (which you also start out with a lot). I've already sunk 100+ hours into it. I whole heartedly recommend it.

I really enjoyed Thief II back in the day, and Thief 3 wasn't terrible, but this latest reboot has almost nothing going for it except the name.  I wasn't digging Garrett's new style, but I wouldn't have let that sway my opinion of the game itself. I gave it a fair chance, played completely through hoping it would get better, and that the story would finally hit its stride and start making sense.  Pros: Sneaking mechanics work well, and the 'swoop' ability can really make you feel like you own the shadows. Wide variety of arrow types. Visuals are generally very good. Voice acting is solid. AI seems solid, in the usual lovable sneaker-amnesiac sort of way.  Cons:    Sneaking works well,  when the level designers actually let you use it , which isn't often. There may be multiple paths around the city, but they are few and feel like linear A=>B tunnels. You will constantly be frustrated by the sheer amound of things Garrett   can't or won't climb  , or won't walk through, or mantle over, etc.   The story makes almost no sense. Most of the time when a cutscene started, I would find myself asking 'How the crap did  that  just happen? And when it ended, I'd be wondering what the crap just happened. It feels very disjointed, to say the least. Characters pop in and out, changing roles with the spastic plot twists.   Speaking of characters popping in... it's downright embarrasing just how many times our hero gets   jumped by a balding old creep with a walking stick and a limp  . Seriously Garrett, they call you the Master Thief; you sneak up on people for a living. You'd think some of that experience would bleed over into the cutscenes, but no. I threw up my hands more than once because I could see what was coming a mile away and Garrett was still completely oblivious.   And even the final chapter left me with little resolution or warm fuzzies, so I can't recommend this to anyone, really. Go play Dishonored instead.  As a final note, there's one mission that has some fairly explicit adult content that you *must* witness to completed the mission, so take that as you will.

Frustratingly brilliant game! I am now about 8 hours into my first functioning village - after countless attempts where everyone just died during the first year - and what can I say: I am absolutely hooked. This game features a set of rather simple mechanics that all play together to create a very challenging and complex game as a whole: manage trade, logistics, and so much more and pray that the Banished make it through the winter.

Pros. Price, graphically stunning, addictive, challenging, Simple Cons: repetitive. Lack of winning.  I love this game, Banished is medieval city building game that puts you in control of small group of settlers that have been banished from their homeland, trying to start fresh in the wilderness. While most games have you gathering resources and technology to advance , in banished the people are the most important resource. You want to protect them because once they die the only way for new people to come about, is for them to be born and it takes years for them to mature and be able to contribute to the community.   Graphics: The game feels like a living and breathing creature, from the movement of the citizens, to the smooth flow of the water, you can almost feel the wind blowing in the trees as they sway back and forth. The bounding wildlife looks alive as you watch them search for food, in this utopia.   Is it Addictive and challenging? This game will challenge you in a multiple aspects, from the way you deal with everyday issues such as feeding, housing and clothing your population. By far one of the hardest challenges you will face is the survival of winter. Other challenges include making sure your population is kept warm as a supply of firewood can burn up quickly. As the population ages they also die, soon you run into unique problems such as natural disasters like twisters, lighting starting fires and lack of population growth. If that happens jobs go unfilled and soon you find yourself in a nose dive filled with starving people, lack of firewood and gravestones.  Repetitive: Over all banished has a lot of great qualities, the amazing price. beautiful wilderness, and the challenge. however the game gets very repetitive rather quickly. Once you survive your first couple of winters and have built up a few farms and your population has grown enough to staff all the jobs, you can almost let the game go on autopilot, the day to day concerns become year to year concerns with you doing very little to improve the quality of life of your citizens. That the only thing you care about during summer is o please god winter is coming, i need more time, i need more food, i need more firewood.   Lack of winning: If you're looking for a game that will offer fulfillment this isn't it, there is no winning only losing. It just keeps going until you get bored or run out of space on the map for farms and houses. One of the few things that can make the game end is if you get unlucky enough to have a smallpox virus outbreak that kills all your people or running out of firewood in the beginning of winter. Which then causes everyone to freeze to death. However an even worse fate is still in store for the poor ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥s that you control and that is running out of food in a harsh winter and slowly watching all your hard work starve to death, as one lonely tear trickles down your face. The only thing you know is failure because no matter how hard you try, all this game wants is to see you cry.    Price: Dont let me, persuade you from purchasing this great game, its well worth every penny of the 20$ price tag. The god like complex you develop is worth well more than 20$ you spend. there has been many an afternoon i have heard my wife telling her subjects to work harder or she will not plant enough crops for them all to survive the winter. Over all banished offers a very unique set of circumstances and lets you go wild in the pursuit of the utopia you wish to create.

Excellent platforming done wrong. Your character will cling to every single wall it so much as touches, making some of the time-based challenges almost impossible, especially when not only does the character stick to walls, sometimes the walls stick to the character, leaving you unable to do anything for several seconds at a time.  The combat feels fluid to make up for that, but have fun finding a good key setup, seeing as the game has no mouse support at all and simply re-uses the same keys in different combinations. Also, to get that key set-up you desire will require you to systematically rebind every key in order. Got one wrong? Thought of something better during the process? Made a mistake? Too bad, you've got to redo the entire key-binding process.  Altogether, I enjoy the game for it's combat and flow, it feels good, the idea of being torn between good and evil is also compelling, but it feels like I'm trying to have sex with through a blender every time I have to go through any dense platforming section. Would not reccomend in current state.

Could be a decent RPG, yet is marred by an unoriginal and flawed RP system. A mass effect or dragon age 2 (not the brilliant Origins) dialogue clone, with 'joking', 'friendly' and 'forceful' given as dialogue options. A simplistic interactive 'click-through-images' story really, not real role-play, as the character can never become the player's own choice. Dialogue options are rather left obscurely behind the 'archetype' veil. Of which there are three. With no grey tones or shades inbetween.  The game's everything that's wrong with the 'modern' 'dumbed-down' xbox-direction Bioware is taking, only with a nonexistant budget and no flash. The anime aesthetic can be of interest though, and a perspective of dating sim aspects is solid yet niche as ever. It's quite a wide niche though and appealing for the love-affairs-building sentiments of many.  In gameplay, Loren is most akin to visual novels with barebones battle mechanics. Interfaces are quite ugly, thoughts going into choices (hence, choices) are minimal and there's no real joy in playing.  Music is bland, art is western wannabe-anime type, and backgrounds get rehashed a lot.  Yet there is some merit to how the story and characters unfold. Some fun moments here and there.   In a bundle it was a nice catch. All in all wouldn't recommend the game at all though. Simplified to boot, in gameplay, story and presentation alike...

As many before me have already said, Tabletop Simulator is less a game than it is a tool for playing games. The base game includes your standard tabletop classics, including playing cards, dominoes, and chess and checkers sets, but you can download other games from the workshop, such as Monopoly, Uno, and Cards Against Humanity. While the singleplayer part of the game is fine, with things such as jigsaw puzzles and solitaire, the options are limited. Playing with at least one other person opens up many more options, such as Chess, Checkers, Go, and multi-player card games, and it's even more fun playing a big game with a group of friends or even with other random people online. At $20, the price is rather steep and it's probably a good idea to wait for a sale, but if you think about it, it only costs as much as an actual board game or two. That said, Tabletop Simulator is very good, and I recommend it. Play on!

Screencheat is a game that hones spatial awareness and reflexes. As far as objective data goes, the mechanics are good, each of the guns have their own strategy and tricks. Every weapon, even the two melee options, have some kind of smoke or flame when reloading, and there are enough distinctive landmarks that you can find someone relatively easily.  As a human being, WHOOP HOBBY HORSE WHOOP! The weapons are fun to use and most are rewarding when you kill someone (*cough grenade launcher cough*). It's probably one of the most applicable first-person shooters ever, up there with Quake and TF2. you've got to know the maps, know the physics, know the trajectory and most likely path of the player in order to kill them. OR... you can just 1 v 1 in a few incredibly intense seconds with the blunderbuss.  At the end of the day, it's good to jump into Quake 3 and shotgun some bots, but only Screencheat will let me do it without player models.....  WITH A HOBBY HORSE.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
review_text: First review i've ever done so bear with me here:  Call of Duty is always called 'that one copy and paste franchise'. For Blops3, yeah it's pretty much the same.  I mean you get jetpacks and 9 TF2 classes to choose from in multiplayer, so that kind of mixes things up a little, but other than that it's the same as most other Call of Duty games. Multiplayer gameplay runs smoothly, the games are mostly fun if you know what you're doing and everyone on the other team isn't spamming Drakon.  As per normal Call of Duty experience, there are tons of racist scrubs who have something to say once they lose or you kill them in a certain way. My advice is to just laugh at them as they're just looking for an excuse to justify their deaths.   Onto Campaign/Co-Op, honestly this campaign has been the most fun out of every other Call of Duty game i've ever played. The Cores you get add a whole lot of variety of what you can do and can really change how you and your teammates accomplish missions. You like staying back and supporting your team with a sniper rifle? The Martial core's got you covered with smokescreens, active camo, pulling some Metal Gear Solid 4 and locking the enemies out of their weapons, etc. Like rushing in and beating the everloving crap out of your enemies? Chaos core lets you do that with flesh-eating nanodrones, mass histeria takeovers that make your enemies' communication systems malfunction and produce an earsplitting sound in their helmets that causes them to literally vomit to death, and setting enemies' explosives off without having to touch them. What about being that guy who has control over the whole battlefield? Your team is cornered by an enemy squadron with nowhere to run... but then you realize something. They have a drone. You quicky activate your ability and take it over, that drone turns on its masters and kills them all, giving your team a chance to escape. Or what about the enemy team having a walker and it's annoying the crap out of you? Not to worry, friend! You can just take control of it yourself and own them with their own incredibly overpowered tank.   Lastly is Zombies which is probably a huge thing for a lot of World at War fans. Honestly I don't play it much because I think Shadow of Evil sucks and is really confusing and easy to get lost on, but I'm probably just bad at it.    Pros:  Good Multiplayer Campaign has tons of replay value You can pilot a fighter jet and pretend you're Star Fox Hacking the enemy's robots in campaign never gets old  Cons: Everyone spams Haymaker and Drakon (I included) Campaign is trippy as hell They ruin the Control Core in the later parts of Campaign  If I had to rate the game, i'd give it an 8 out of 10

Axiom Verge is a classic-style Metroidvania game (heavy on the 'Metroid' part - as in, they could be long-lost twins). So, as someone whose list of top favorite games of all time contains Metroid and Super Metroid right up at the very top, you can probably guess how I felt about this… I loved it. The way I see it, if you're going to pay homage to the best, you may as well go all out, and do it right. And on that front, Axiom Verge does not disappoint. So if you’re a fan of those games (or the 2D Castlevanias, etc) then this is a no-brainer.   The game is extremely well-designed, looks great, has a good soundtrack, and plays well (for the most part). You can't ask for much else. I enjoyed the exploration, item collecting, boss battles, story, and the overall atmosphere of the game. My only small gripes have to do with the way a couple of the game mechanics work (or rather, don’t work) - specifically, the grapple tool and the dash/teleport ability were way too finicky and/or inconsistent to pull off, for my tastes. I'll concede that part of the issue is probably that I used the control stick on the controller rather than the D-pad for primary movement, which makes those abilities harder to reliably pull off, but in my opinion, that should have been accounted for as part of the design. In particular, the dash/teleport ability just didn’t work consistently enough for me, and made a few parts of the game extremely frustrating. But other than that, I really can't think of any other complaints.   All in all, this is about as good a game as one could possibly hope for in the classic Metroid style. It's a game I could/would have probably made myself, if I weren't too old and lazy now (or, like, had no job or family). So major kudos to the developer (just 1 guy) who not only had that technical know-how, but also the time, ambition, and dedication I lack, who actually followed through and made this. It's a very, very, impressive piece of work, and well worth checking out for anyone who is a fan of this genre, or classic-style 2D action-platformers in general.

'Mass Effect' and 'Splinter Cell' had a child named 'Alpha Protocol'. It turned out to be a flawed genius.   There are a lot of faults with this game. Broken gameplay mechanics, PS2-era graphics, inconsistent frame-rates and an uninspired beginning. But if one can look past its many shortcomings, this game turns out to be an absolute gem. A global espionage-RPG in the true sense.   To start things off, you first pick your character's class: Soldier (firearms), Field Agent (stealth), Tech Specialists (hacking) or Freelancer (a combination of skills of the player's choice). Each class determines how well you perform a specific task in the field.   With regards to gameplay, I'm sure the devs were targeting a realistic approach to combat. For instance, you have to aim at a target for few seconds to let the reticule converge. Thus, ensuring that every gunfight turned out to be a challenge. The melee system comprised of martial arts which allowed you to knockout enemies either stealthily or engage them head-on. All character abilities were upgradeable with sufficient experience points (XP).   Hacking was one of the highlights of the game. There was a unique system in place each for hacking computers, opening locks or bypassing keypads and alarms. Many people found the hacking system frustrating but I think it was fairly straight-forward. Challenging, yes, but very rewarding in the end.   The graphics were rubbish, plain and simple. Character animations were clunky, anti-aliasing horrible, lighting awful, movements jerky and texture loading very slow. These were some of the biggest down-sides of an otherwise fantastic game. Level designs were very good though with plenty of alternative routes to complete objectives.  The plot is where this game really shines. The story is gripping and opens up once you're done with the Saudi missions. And it hurtles you head-first into a global conspiracy mess. Allies (and enemies) are made along the way and your treatment of them has future implications in the form of gaining intel, weapons, money (if allies) or facing problems and hurdles (if enemies). Reading their dossier in advance helps you choose favourable options during dialogue sequences. Characters are interesting and unlike many other RPG games, dialogue never feels bland.   Alpha Protocol needs a sequel to set its problems right. It requires perseverance and patience to overlook its many problems and bear with its frustrating nature but underneath all that, this game is a true gem. One not to be missed.

First off, I'd like to say that for some reason, Steam is logging my time incorrectly. I've easily poured several entire afternoons into this game trying to get all the possible endings/pictures. Way more than the few hours it shows. There will be some light spoilers in this review, but nothing too groundbreaking.  From a writing standpoint, plot wise, this game is amazing. To get a full grasp of the storyline, you have to go through all the different routes. You only learn certain details from each route, so getting all of them really allows you to piece the entire story together. It truly embodies the phrase 'there are two sides to every story.' When following any of the incubi routes, Diana seems like the enemy. But when you play Diana's route, the opposite seems to be the case. By following every option, you get a much more well-rounded perspective of the over-arcing situation. As you can imagine, this makes the replayability extremely high. There are a good handful of romantic endings (as is to be expected) but also a couple great non-romantic endings that are really empowering for the protagonist, and even a few endings where the protagonist doesn't survive. It's quite unique.   The characters are a lot of fun. You can choose to romance male characters or female characters, which is a huge plus. (I'm always up for lesbian options.) You also have the option of romancing the human characters. The human routes are shorter, however, which makes sense considering the demons are the main draw to the game. It's a shame though, since I find the human characters to be more interesting.  The protagonist is great. Several times I was presented with violent options, which was a ton of fun. Punching Sam, slapping Erik, bringing an entire beatdown upon Lisette and Malix; the protagonist is not some passive 'damsel in distress' type like many dating sim protags seem to be. She definitely know how to hold her own in a fight.  The biggest critique that you'll see from most players is that the sex scenes are all basically the same. That doesn't really bother me at all, though, because I'm asxual and don't need fancy sex scenes. In fact, the scenes are optional, you don't need them to get the perfect ending with that particular character. (If you want all the pictures, then you have to do it, but you can just click right through the scenes since they're all extremely similar.) You only get the sex option with the five main demons, which is my biggest complaint. As I stated earlier, I prefer the human characters' routes, and it seems like they get the short end of the stick.  Overall, fantastic game. It's free, so you have no excuse not to at least give it a chance.

Don't let my play time fool you.... I've been trapped in Dark Souls for a number of years now... AND I'M STILL ON THE FIRST FECKING LEVEL!   Dark Souls III Review -  Pros -  + The graphics are amazing, with stunning visual and awesome textures as well as effects, this game is the nicest in the series.  + Gameplay is fun (If not tricky) and you will learn to get used to them pretty quickly.  + Story is a pretty good one this time around.   + Characters are interesting.  + Boss battles can be annoying but fun and rewarding afterwards.  Cons -  - Some of the AI are able to still attack you through walls  - Some of the Mini - Bosses in the game tend to be a little too strong for beginners to the series (Like myself)   - Graphics are great, but I did notice some dips here and there in the frame rate.  - Some of the features are locked behind internet connectivity.  Overall:   Play it!   It's not actually hard when you get used to controls and though the bosses can be challenging at first, you'll soon be able to find their weak spots and just go to town on them.

Serious Sam is a game that only works with a group of friends and a case of fluorescent cola. It has an excuse for a plot, a handful of idiotic fetch quests, and the only sensible way to play is with unlimited respawns to keep the game's howling unfairness at bay.   It starts out like any other mediocre third-person shooter - you, a vending-machine hero with a penchance for snappy one-liners and the personality of a sanitary napkin - are dropped into a ruined city. The co-op mode immediately offsets this with silliness, as you are accompanied by as many as 15 compatriots, each wearing a skin of their choice We played with a team of Sam, an enormous black man wearing a revealing pink frock, and Croteam's CFO.   The first few levels don't do the game justice - but it's not long until you are elbow deep in wide open areas, surrounded by waves of diverse and enormous foes, smashing into them with a sledgehammer and sticks of throwing dynamite just for the mad glee of it.

A simplified Hearts of Iron, with sole focus (as the title tells you) on the Eastern front.  Simple game, but complex enough to keep you engaged. Rome: Total War is a great game. You know this game came out in 2004 and twelve years later this game managed to heavily impress me. From the small part of diplomatic relations to the absolute massive battle scenes, the game just immediately sucked me in. Now the Total War games had never really been a big thing to me up until this moment so I was just a bit dubious about how much this game would appeal to me, but I am a history buff and I was trying to find a way to spend the summer so I picked it up during the sale. What I found was an actually really good game that managed to combine the turn base parts with the real time strategy.   The game as said is split into two types of strategy. The first part is map screen. From here you manage your settlements and your armies. You can upgrade your towns and recruit new units. From here you can also send out your diplomats to other factions in order to institute trade agreements and forge alliances for wars. The diplomats have actually quite a bit that they can discuss with the other factions. You can make demands such has making them hand over a territory or you can create a trade agreement that can help your income or just allow them military access to other parts of the world. Another thing that impressed me is that they can reject your offer and make counter offers. This really does feel like an actual negotiation rather than just a basic “I declare peace or war thing.” One thing I’m going to say though is that there is a severe problem with the lack of anything to really during peace time. In the end though the map screen is fantastic turn based strategy.   Now as for the actual battle screens it is one the greatest parts of the game. The sieges and the battles are just so massive that it is impossible to be swept away by it. The fights aren’t too complicated. It doesn’t take long to figure the strengths and weaknesses and of each unit. Just know when to charge and how to flank the enemy and that’s rather easy to pick up. What makes it really work though is just the basic ability to pause the game and figure out your positions and where each unit should go. This takes a lot the panicking and stress out of other strategies that usually takes me out of most strategy games. The units are widely varied and it is rather cool to see the progress of your units to much more powerful troops.  In the end what really sells the game is just the absolute scale it has. The game just feels massive and every advancement you do makes you feel more powerful. Some might say that the effects don’t look good and to those people I say they need to shut it. The game looked amazing back in the day and still looks line today. I don’t care if we can’t see every pimple on the soldiers they look fine. As for the multiplayer I don’t know what it’s like. I am not interested in it, but if you are then check it out. Rome: Total War is a game that everyone should check out if you are interested in history or if you think you might be interested in the game.

Introduction  I just added FEAR in my steam library, altough i own the game since it's release. I completed the storyline (for the 10th, at least, time) and it's time for me to write a review. To be honest, the only reason i started playing again, is that i am a nostalgic guy, and after so many years i wanted to play this game again. But now i think about it again, and i figure that this is the most irrelevant reason to play it ;)   Story(No Spoilers)  The protagonist is the 'Point Man' (no real name) of F.E.A.R. (First Encounter Assault Recon),a secret unit of the US Government, specializing in dealing with paranormal threats. The mission is to take down Paxton Fettel, and his clone army, known as Replica.   Game Time  It takes about 8-10 hours to complete the campaign (depending on the difficulty)   In detail  If you get this game, it wont be about the kickass graphics or its realistic gameplay, If you expect something like that, i bet you will be disappointed. However the atmospheric gameplay, created by the well-designed maps and the awesome light-shade effects, the enemy AI, which is far more advanced than even some today's FPS and the great storyline totally compensate the game's drawbacks. Additionally, the multiplayer was awesome and made me spend many hours playing. When i found out that MP doesnt' work anymore i got sad.    Pros  + Great graphics at the time of release + Atmospheric gameplay created by the lighting and shade effects, with the addition of even greater sound effets + Advanced enemy A.I. which can make the game challenging  + Interesting storyline and plot similar to an action-horror movie. The well created cutscenes add to that. + Variety of weapons and grenades   Cons  - No multiplayer ( T.T ) - Some glitches may occur due to specific graphic combos. - Usually repititive  Concluding, worth buying it since it's considered a classic!  10.0 / 10.0  1. Dont touch it even if it's on the house [0-3 / 10]. 2. If you are bored of every other game [3-5 / 10]. 3. Grab it whenever it's on sale (or pay the full price if you are wealthy enough) [6-8 / 10]   4. Must Have [9-10 / 10]

app_name: Fallout 3 - Game of the Year Edition
review_text: I've had two playthroughs of this under my belt, one vanilla and one modded. Don't let my play time fool you; since I use a mod for Alt Tab in this game, it doesn't count my hours I've actually played. First time I did around 50 hours; second time with mods is at 100 hours and still going. First time around was incredibly enjoyable but some features were a bit annoying. The story immediately feels immersive and unique, with a more relatable set of characters than Oblivion (which has a similar engine - it even has the same animations for dog attacks and sheathing/unsheathing melee swords). It pays to research how to build a character before investing in your SPECIAL stats as they aren't easily increased - basically, charisma is the least important, at least in my eyes. I'll point out pros and cons of vanilla and modded versions below, just like I did for Fallout: New Vegas.  Vanilla Pros: -Fascinating, large open world packed with loads of content -(Arguably) fun hacking system that requires some brain power -Exp system feels streamlined and motivates you to explore more -Being able to snipe - with headshots -Good dialogue and voice acting, with SPECIAL stats and skills contributing to available options -Dogmeat returns! -Cool armour and weapons, especially the Power armour variants and energy weapons -DLCs are great, especially Operation Anchorage and Mothership Zeta. The Pitt in particular breaks the monotony of being too powerful in late game by stripping you of your equipment for a limited time. Operation Anchorage is a good DLC to do early as it gives you powerful equipment.  Cons: -Too slow movement speed, thought it doesnt' seem to change much between light and heavy armour -Main quest feels too small (as usual for Bethesda), though the Broken Steel expands it to a better length -Level cap too low! You'll stop being motivated to explore (most likely) when you hit it, and therefore rush to finish the main quest without exploring half the wasteland. -Combat not as dynamic and intense as a true FPS - also I tend to miss sniper shots because of some object blocking me despite the target in clear view. Melee combat also relatively boring as most enemies use guns.  Modded (main mods I use are Fallout Wanderer's edition, Darnified UI and Weapon Mod Kits): Pros: -Improved combat dramatically. It's much easier to aim properly at bobbing heads of enemies when time is slowed down in bullet time. This also makes sniping easier and less frustrating. A quick grenade hotkey also makes them more useful. -Adjusted options to slow experience gain to an appropriate level. I chose 50% and was on level 25 when I started the final DLC with most of the main quest still to go - a good pacing percentage. -Ability to sprint is a godsend -Makes weapons and armour more diverse and even cooler-looking  Cons: -Requires you to know how to install mods (I'd recommend Gopher on Youtube for help) -Has caused a couple of strange bugs in quests, though solutions from the community and the pc console let me work around them -May lower your performance, though Fallout Stutter Remover alleviates this, depending on the types of mods you use  All in all, as good or even better a game experience than Fallout New Vegas, and different style from the previous Fallouts makes back play in sequence a likely route for me to take in the future. And I have to say I've always been a fan of the weapon and armour designs. I still love the look of Power Armour, and I loved my modded Gauss Rifle as a sniper weapon in the end. Mothership Zeta was the most visually impressive, with a lot of time and effort put into the area design. I particularly love open world RPGs, and therefore loved this game. Others who aren't a fan may not enjoy it as much as I did, but modded properly, these games are some of the greatest gaming experiences you'll have. 9/10 vanilla, 9.8/10 modded.

review_text: Sadly, would NOT reccomend. As much as I love the Ace Combat series, this game sadly falls short. The story and graphics aren't too shabby but the actual gameplay brings the whole thing down. I've always loved Ace Combat's not too sim-y and not too arcade-y feel but this game is way too arcade-y.  They've added a Dog Fighting Mode (DFM), that you are pretty much forced to use to progress, that is basically auto-aim for planes. The little side / mini-games. where you get to use a minigun or helicopter. don't help much either when it changes the pace significantly and brings you up close and personal with the low res textures most aircraft games use for the ground you rarely see unless you are crashing. They've even added a little tweak to the turning mechanic where instead of the usual turn faster while braking they've changed it to turn extremely fast when tapping the brakes so you end up doing a feathering motion of accelerating and braking constantly to turn around quickly to jump into DRM with an opponent.  All in all, I think they've gone way overboard turning this game into a pure arcade jet fighter. It seems like they changed the usual Ace Combat feel to a dumbed down version for all the pvp pwnkiddies for the great multiplayer experience that this game apparently has.

It's a good game. It could have been better but as a first attempt, it's good.  To be successful you do have to actually think about things. Your fans will expect similar kinds of games, but your reviews will fail if they're TOO similar. And you have to have properly trained staff, to keep up with research, have a good game engine, target the right systems and so on. It's more in depth than you'd initially think.  There are however a few flaws - the game time passes far too quickly. Some consoles can come and go before you can even release more than one game for them. The same goes for game engines. Very often, by the time you've made a game, new technology is out making it obsolete. (This may be realistic to an extent, but it's more frustrating than fun).  All in all though, it's a good game, and I hope they make a sequel. I'll definitely be buying it.

I'll try and keep this short since I'm certainly not a professional reviewer.   Before I start I want to say that Kill to Collect is easily one of the best games I've played in years. It has some flaws, but the pros far outweigh the cons. I'll shortly list those before I do an in depth review.   As an aside, I'm not sure if I have to clarify that I was given a review code for this game because I was given one of four from a friend who needed to test out the co-op aspect. So, I did that.  Pros: Game in absurdly fun.  Music is great.  The enemies are varied and creative.  Very nostalgic.  Cons: Difficulty is dodgy.  Level design can be confusing.  Occasional bugs and wonky hitbox detection.    I'll do the Pros first since this game deserves that much. First I'll give a quick rundown of the basic idea.    Kill to Collect is in essence a dungeon crawler. You splatter your enemies across the wall and floors until you find an elevator to the next floor and do the same process. Don't let that trick you into thinking the game has no depth. It has an passable cyberpunk storyline but the gameplay is honestly why we're all here.  At the end of each floor you can spend two types of currency you gained from that floor. Credits are awarded for performing well in the rooms and are used to purchase consumables like personal shields or more secondary weapon ammo. Food stamps are an oh so delicious reward for completing the various challenge rooms through the floors and are used to buy semi-permanent upgrades for your character. Anything you don't spend is saved for the next floor, and after clearing every floor in an area you're thrown into either a boss fight or a few waves of enemies. Once you clear that, it's on to the next area in the story.   The game is absurdly fun: It's absurdly fun! I have 14 ish hours by the time of this review and about 12 of those are in  co-op. The characters are all interesting with different movesets for varying playstyles. Each one has a Basic Attack, a Special Attack, an Ultimate, and a Mobility move. On top of that, they each have a unique trait incorporated in their moveset to further cement their 'role' in the party.   Music is great: The music very well done and atmospheric. Atmospheric may not be the correct word but it helps me personally get in the cyberpunk mood. Although it isn't something you'll (likely) be whistling at work like Zelda or Final Fantasy, the music is appropriate and on point with some bumping techno music for one level, and crunchy guitar music on the next.   The enemies are varied: Every area you enter has it's own flavor of baddies, with some levels catering specifically to a certain type like a robotics uprising having only robotic enemies in that area. Outside of those special occasions you'll encounter a pretty range of enemies from punks with axes and laser pistols, to robotic drones and large mantis like robots with claws. You may notice a few palette swaps to indicate enemies with AI but more never hurt anyone.   Very nostalgic: This is the point I most wanted to emphasize because I haven't seen any other reviewers mention this specifically. The game is almost over the top nostalgic for oldschool dungeon crawlers like Gauntlet or most prominently for me, the Loaded and Reloaded series. I see the word Roguelite being tossed around but I feel that's doing a disservice to this game based on the amount of trash 'roguelike' games being pushed out lately.   And now the cons. I'll reiterate that I played this game when it was technically in beta, but I've continued to play it after the full release and these are the problems I see still persist.   Dodgy Difficulty: There are currently three difficulty modes while I write this review. Easy, Normal, and Hard. The difference between easy and normal was literally imperceptible to me outside of beginning with slightly more HP. The difference between Normal and Hard however is like night and day and may deter anyone from ever trying it out. On hard mode you have 1 health point to begin with, and close to everything will kill you in one hit. Learning to use your weapons effectively is paramount and is what I consider to be the way the devs intended KtC to be played.   Confusing Level Design: This one is tricky because it could very well be just the group I played with. The literal level design is very simple and straight forwad with every floor being comprised of a series of rooms, roughly square shaped with different floor hazards like pitfalls or robotic turrets and vats of acid.  The real problem stems from the questionable color choice on some the floors and enemies. Very frequently my friends and I would find ourselves in a situation where the enemies and floor would be so similar in color that you couldn't tell anyone was even there until they killed you. In particular, it's the red flooring and red enemies that caused issues for us.   Bugs and hitboxes: This is easily the most frustrating part of this game for me. The hitbox detection felt so on point for many of the bad guys I encountered, but a couple in particular seemed to be wildly innacurate. In particular the little robotic probes and the sword and shield guys. There are times when you can easily be outside their range and still be hit by their attacks. It's especially frustrating on hard mode since that death can feel very cheap.  The occasional bugs weren't terrible but should be mentioned. On the loading screens, we would occasionally lock up and have to Alt+F4 out and restart the game.   There is another bug where your characters will seem to magnetize to one another and go launching off at breakneck speeds wildly. It caused a few deaths and can lead to you trying to avoid working together and just clear a little corner of the room each so you don't get too close to one another.   I know I said I'd try and keep this short, but honestly Kill to Collect is firmly one of my favorite games to play now and I had a hard time not rambling about how great it is. If you liked Loaded or Reloaded, give it a shot.   Oh, I don't know how to format on here, this is my first review. Sorry if this posts in a weird way.

The Binding of Isaac  was a dungeon crawler, top down shooter, rouge like indie game released in 2011. The games story is about a crying naked child, named Isaac, who have ventured down to the depths of his basement full of monsters escaping from his corrupted mom who had been told by god to end his life.   Charming, am i right?   The Binding of Isaac, with its addictive dungeon crawler, top down shooter, rouge like gameplay, dark tone and charming story went on to catch many gamers eyes, and love. Including mine. The game sold over 1 million copies, and is highly regarded as one of the best gems of the Indie industry.  Now, that ive told you how much loved the original binding of issac was, is it really worth paying three times the price of the original game for a remake? The answer is: Yes. Its worth every single penny. The game has benn rebuilt from scrath, whit an all new engine. Which brings new beautiful lightning effects and gorgeous 16-bit graphics, that makes the game runnable at 60fps on almost any computer. And not to mention all the new content that has been added to the game, but thats up to you to discover.  The gameplay remains just as addicting as the first Binding of Isaac's. The game is a joy to play, from the second you boot up the game, to the second you die. Thats when you hate the game. Remember what i said about The Binding of Isaac being a rouge like game? Well, if your new to the genre, a rouge like game is a game where you only have one chance to beat the game, and if you die you will have to start all over again.  For many, this is the reason why they run from The Binding of Isaac.  But for others, this is the main reason you buy the game. And all the different item drops, and combinations makes for almost endless replayabilty. This is one of the strongest points of The Binding of Isaac Rebirth, and it will take you weeks, and months to discover everything this game has to offer you. The Binding of Isaac Rebirth will keep you up late at night.  But This is also where the main issue of the game comes in. You see, everything in the game, from the level structure to the item drops are all decided in RNG. Aka, Randomly Generated Numbers. The game is all built around luck. The game can feel straight off frustrating time to time, and many times you will feel like the reason you died was all based on the items you got, not on your skill.   The Verdict:   But in the end, it dosent matter how good your items are, if your dodge is completely off and you cant hit your tears, you will die. Over. And over. Again.  And oh, remember what i said about Rebirth keeping you up at night? Well, i didnt really tell the whole truth. This game is made by sick persons, for sick persons. The game is deeply disturbing and contains so much gore and blood, that even i had to look away times to times. This game is not for everyone.  And the RNG that may feel so frustrating at times, is the heart and the soul of The Binding of Isaac Rebirth. And so much of the games charm, replayability and joy would be taken away without it. Not knowing whats around the next corner, or in the next item room is exiting, and essential for the game.  Because in the end, what The Binding of Isaac Rebirth does so right is its amazing, addicting gameplay, charming art-style and endless replayability.  And if neither the disturbing visuals, the RNG or the rouge like gameplay scares you away from this title, there isnt much on Steam that can offer this amount of content, and pure joy for 15 euros.  9/10

Would I recommend Lifeless Planet? Yeah, kind of. Would I play it again? Nope.  The game was, to a degree, fun. It had an interesting storyline, and some nice visuals. There was an interesting concept to boot. However, I feel like the game still isn't... finished.  The graphics aren't the best, but they're definitely acceptable, and they get the job done.  The gameplay is simple and makes it feel more like a visual novel. To put it simply, the game is a corridor; a simple hallway you walk through. Sure, there are some neat tiles here and there, and occassionally there's something you might need to step over. As much of a corridor that it is, it's a big one; both wide and long.  If you enjoy the occassional narrative, give it a try. Don't expect it to blow you away.  Over all, despite the game having been officially released recently after having been in open beta, the game still feels very much like it's in that beta stage.  While I would recommend this game, I'm going to have to go with the Ice Cream formula: With the money I spent on this game, would I have had more fun spending that money on ice cream and/or hookers? At some points, the answer is yes. At other points, no.  To put it simply, the game was a mixed experience, and I would recommend getting it again... at least, if it ever goes on sale at a fair discount.

This game is diablo-esque with more retro graphics. Try to imagine a mashup between diablo and the legen of zelda for nes and that's what you have.  I was pretty into it the first few hours but it quickly reveals how grindy the game is. Unfortunately your hero levels very slowly and can't kill enemies past the first couple levels without using special powers. Thus what you do a lot of the game is run around waiting for a power to come off cooldown while avoiding enemies. It's not really that fun. I think if you leveled up quicker it would be better, but maybe the devs want it to be grindy. The thing is it's not hard, just grindy.  There are plenty of classes to play all with really fun sounding abilities but again, I don't want to grind several hours on each to unlock the cool stuff. There's already several harder difficulites after normal so I don't see why normal needs to be so slow to progress.  I'd say stay away unless you're really into this style of games.

EDIT: Like some other reviewers, I'm changing my recommendation to NOT recommended. At higher levels, when the number of pieces increases, the annoyance of occasionaly grabbing the wrong piece grows exponentially. It is no longer an random glitch but a game-crippling bug. 9 times out of 10 you don't get the piece you grab, but some other close by or below it. When doing the edge pieces of a 300+ piece jigsaw, I ended piling all the wrong pieces to the board, then stored the edge pieces to another area of board and dumped the centre pieces back to the stream. There must be something wrong with the selection algorithm, didn't bother to test if the screen resolution effects it.  My old review below still holds, it would be a nice game without the effing bug! At it's current state it is only playable with low piece counts.  ORIGINAL REVIEW: Pretty good small puzzle game. I was a bit wary about the 'floating pieces in koi pond'-thing, but as the pieces are not rotatable and puzzle sizes are small, it is works quite well - it is easy enough to spot the pieces and there is no need for extra temp storage trays. A couple of negatives: you can't build separate sections of the puzzle (the pieces do not bind unless they are in their correct final position), and picking the pieces from koi pond is not always wysiwyg - you sometimes get the wrong piece even though your mouse cursor is pointing for the correct one. There seems to be something wrong with the grabbing logic.  If you are in search of a relaxing simple puzzle game, this fills the need.

The Good  + Very interesting concept of sandbox puzzle solving   The bad  - Cat lovers, never get this game. It's about rats teaming up with dogs to kill the cat. Basically, Tom and Jerry with lots of gore. - How did the cat even end up in that small space? What has it done to deserve such gruesome punishments? The thing about cats hating rats is just a myth anyway. I bet the developers have a fetish for cat abuse. - Though it is a sandbox game, the physics are really random. So the chances that you'll get your trajectory right is about 80%. - The surfaces are frictionless even for a sandbox game - There are 50 levels, which means you're going to spend a whole lot of time figuring out how to kill the cat.  My final rating: 2/10

When I heard the news that Guilty Gear Xrd -SIGN- was coming to PC, I was a bit skeptical. I am a huge fan of fighting games and didn't want this to be a horrific port. ArcSys dashed out ALL doubts I had. While I can't yet speak for the online portion of the game, the offline / single-player experience is as smooth as can be.   For $24 (as of this review), you get the full game (obviously), but you also recieve ALL DLC for no additional charge (at least that's how it was for me :p). This includes 3 additional characters, all costume colors, and all system voices to change the announcer voice to that of your favorite character! The game also has MAGNIFICENT art style (in my honest opinion of course). The game's music is also fantastic (and as a side note, the creator of the game is also the composer for all the music. How cool is that?!).   The only 'con' I have is that when you play the tutorial or challenge mode, the onscreen inputs change from the keyboard's keys to the Xbox buttons every couple of seconds (if you're using a controller / fightstick). But this is a simple fix as the game has an option to switch the input display from the Xbox / keyboard buttons to the abbrieviations of the attacks as they are called in the game.  So even though this review isn't really an in-depth one, I can honestly say I recommend this game to anyone looking for an anime game, (or in my case) a new fighting game to play. 10/10  ****EDIT**** After looking up information about the PC release, I have now found out why I have access to all of the DLC. For the first week of this game being on the steam store, you will get ALL the DLC for no extra charge.
