This is a good game, and charming. It makes a point of keeping you away from any sort of hacking device until you've wandered around for a bit and gotten a feel for the town and the characters, which helps with the human side of things.  The problem is that depending on who you are and how much you're into it, there can be a huge dissonance between the character and the player. The character isn't that great at programming and all he really wants to do is follow his love interest around like a creepy, stalking six-foot-tall puppy; the player, on the other hand, might not have the same experience.   If you're any good at programming at all, you'll probably break reality itself and remake your PC into an omnipotent deity after an hour or so of tinkering with the hacking tool; but in character he's always a huge dweebus. It's really weird. I just like to pretend he's screwing with everyone else.

I have mixed feelings..  Cons: This game has a lot of rises in emotions as you commit yourself to the old crew that you remember from Borderlands(1st). However, with the new additions and the shortness of the main quests it is very troubling to complete this game and actually improve towards beating it. In fact you can play at your current level and skill and sometimes with the most legendary items of rarity you cannot complete missions. You will beat main story missions and each time you do the enemies levels will skyrocket and outrun you in the long run, forcing you to have to spend more time on side quests and makeup work so that you wont die later on. This is harsh since there is a tax of 6% of all your cash to pay upon death. They also continue with making enemies even harder when you just wanna play with friends, adding challenge and better yet higher leveled enemies(Ofcourse there is slightly better loot? as said by some. I dont believe this AT ALL). In some cases you will make it through the mission and loot the area, but UH OH! WHATS THIS? YOU CANT EVEN USE THE LOOT, because your level causes you to be disabled of all things in higher up missions and quests. You can progress all you want, but your causing a lot of extra work if your not beating the side quests along the way. It is also annoying that your reward for beating the game is round 2 with even more messed up and unbearable standards. Foes are harder, loot is a bit better, but still not enough to hold your own. Another triggering effect is being told your going to an armory or a place of weapon stockpiles and you will only walk out finding like 10 guns or so, as you would in any OTHER mission. You will eventually reach rock bottom without working extra.. Now, to the pros.  Pros: There is tons of characters with many mixed features and they also take things back in time allowing you to see people, places, creatures, and things that the old game showed off. Such as Mothrakk and Brick (and the gang), even Fyrestone is in this game! A lot of extra new upgrades to weaponry such as adding risks you take on choosing a good gun such as using more bullets to have more damage or simply to shoot more bullets at once. Optimal performances with classes from easier to harder classes, gladly they kept a Soldier (AKA Commando) who can send out sentries as bodyguard. Lots of funny comedy to be thrown around to keep you laughing (Through the sore and pain the battles this game leave you for). The game also keeps a steady storyline and even was good enough to lead to a prequel for its expansive trilogy. The only thing that was bad between it is for you to find out as it leads on from spoilers.  If I had to say that this was worth its price I would say No, there are tons of other better choices $20 can get by at(It's just a risky buy). If I had to say if its worth it for someone who has the money and just simply owns all the other games they need and/or were previous Borderlands players, I would definatly suggest this for you and it will be worth it. No, this doesn't exactly mean that you should be a veteran to enjoy this game, but if your buying the sequel, I prefer you take the first game before this one or you will deal with the cons and have no understanding towards how this game actually works, in fact you may find this game as bullsh**.

Graphically the game is ok, the sounds that are the highlight with good effects and a great soundtrack, and a flawless voice acting.  The plot is another strong point, well worked and unique, which involves aspects of Christianity and has great characters. War, one of the 4 horsemen of the apocalypse and the playable character is without a doubt remarkable and captivating. Super MNC provides a fun, free, and interesting game to fans of shooters or ARTS games. Being a sequel to Monday Night Combat, this free to play third person ARTS game adds more influence from Dota along with evolution in style and feel. Each character, or pro, plays similarly to some others but have unique weapons and abilities to them. The art style is eclectic, ranging from the outdoors or urban environments of each arena to the differing styles of each pro. Like MNC, gameplay is a mix between TF2 and Dota, with more Dota style to the game. However, a new mode brings back the faster pace from the original MNC. In vein to games like LoL and Dota 2, weekly updates change pro rotation and add new features while a store offers cosmetics or pros for a fairly high price. SMNC is a unique kind of free to play multiplayer open to all the masses with strong developer support.

In 1994, a stratgey RPG titled: X-COM UFO Defense was released on the PC. It was a hit, and the series continued, it eventiually got very far away from its turn-based RPG roots (See XCOM: The Buereu). Now, Firaxis has brought everything back to the start with a reboot of the series. It is definatley a triumphant return. The game has been very changed, and even those who played X-COM to death will have a new experience in XCOM. Without getting into every detail, the one thing this game does is simplify the experience. Each unit has infinite ammo packs, there is only ever one XCOM base, and so on. This serves not only to say that this is a modernized version of the classic game, but also as a completely new one. Now, the best part of the game is the customization options. If you get both of the DLCs, the game has a number of different voices, races, hair styles (even Guile hair!) to choose from, allowing you to make a team of whoever you want, and not just having generic soldiers. Personally, my team was Hewiki 'Stoneshot' Ignika, Raida 'Radio' Justice, James T 'NotaRedShirt' Kirk, Barry 'Magnum' Burton, Jill 'Sandwich' Valentine, and Shaquille 'Slam-Dunk' O'neil. When you give your soldiers names, themed armor, and everything else, the game manages to make things personal. When a soldier gets critically wounded, the game will make you freak out. Somehow, the game makes you care for these soldiers, and that is the strongest point of the game. However, the game also has some serious problems. It's pretty hard to care for your soldiers when one of them gets hit, and then falls through the floor, you kind of get taken out of the experience. In my experience though, it hasn't ever crashed, just had some problems with registering flanking, aim calculations, and clipping. Major problems with clipping.  All together, with the amazing experience and the replayability the ghame has, with the buggyness in mind,  9.5/10 Pretty Amazing

The great story combined with addictive gameplay and wonderful graphics and the euphoria physics engine and bullet time. It's was like the best game features were combined together to create this wonderful game. The attention to detail is astounding too, the voice acting is top notch, Mr. James McCaffrey is a legend. The soundrack and everything else was perfect too. I played through the game nine or ten times, I can't even remember now and with each playthrough I discovered new things. It's a linear story based shooter, but it's so engaging and thrilling once you finished it, you want to play through it again and again. Rockstar has made a true polished gem out of Max Payne 3, although the second Max Payne and especially the first one made by the extremely talentend guys at Remedy will always stay close to my heart. Max Payne 3 is right up there with the first two games as one of the best 3rd person action games of all time  10/10

First off i gotta say i went into Kingdom wars aka Dawn of Fantasy with a couple of excpectations.  I was excpecting the game to be slow as all hell early game , cause its a MMO rts . And usually these kinda games can be a chore early on. Certian features seemd to look promising . Stuff such as naval combat and building your own settlement , defences and castle's . And finally i was excpectin some form of conquest .  Lets start off with the first bit shall we ? This game is by far one of the few MMO type startegy games ive seen where the ammount of resoruces you accumilate is drastically fast . Most games of this sort will have you mine for like at least a month before you get your city to a presentable/Combat ready level . Not in this case ,ive managed to get my city into a decent fighting shape within a week .And for those fearing they might not be ready by the time their battle protection runs out , no fear theres several systems in place that should assure that newbie players dont get ganked up on or attacked by a higher player .  Expectation number 2. Now sadly in the game everything is decided for you in terms of where buildings will go with specific poitns o nthe map . The whole point of the game is besiegeing enemy castles right ? So its kind of dissapointing that you have no say in where startegic defences should go , instead its all pre determined for you . Even the placeing of houses for human players is determined ahead of time .The game should at the least allow you a small ammount of freedom when designing your castle . Like being able to place decorations such as parks or statues etc as well as houses , will make besiegeing each city at least feel somewhat uniqe . From what ive gatherd Naval is currently not in the game as it was unbalanced , the trailer is misleading and they shouldve taken that out before the steam release .  Expectation 3  You can conquer NPC cities and make them your vassals . So theres that .  Now ...these things seem mostly kinda negative ? Okey now onto what the game does well and features that are good.  Quests while generally simple give the player a good sense of what he can expect to be up against , Quests also scale as the players armies grow in size (at least grind quests ). CoOP ing sieges or quests can also be alot of fun as well as you and a random strange coordinate your attack on a stronger opponent .  The gameplay during fights can be explained rather simple with one word . Chaotic. Now what i mean with this is , if you dont keep a eye on your units ESPECIALLY melle units it is EXTREMELY difficult to tell weather your units are winning or loseing .Cause all units simply mesh into a large blob , not to mention individual untis from a squad tend to just go wherever and seperate themselfs fro mthe group leading them to end up who knows where. As it is the units move / fight way too quickly to keep up with what they are doing and makes fights more determined by the guy with the army that has the higher level units/ numbers as opposed to strategy.The most input you have during a melle fight is watching out your guys health doesnt go to zero and pull him out , for a game like this micro manageign every unit like this can be a real pain. Bugs unfortonetly still plauge the game but the blow is somewhat softend by the constant moderators that in a sense refund you for your losses sustaind cause of a bug , so kudos to good communtiy managment .  So this review sounds awefully negative right ? In a way it is , but i DO recommend this game , its free to play and it feels like it , all prices and all things they charge for are extremly fair and it looks like it wont be yet another pay 2 win game .  The game has potential , especially seeing as how its developed by 4-5 developers . The games graphics are somewhat dated , but they get the job done , which means great news you dont need a strong computer to run it .  Overall i highly recommend Kingdom Wars for actually being F2P , being properly supported and fixed , good community and potential for a REALLY great MMO RTS with time as the game and developer grows who knows what we might see in the game

Wow. Easily my favorite Assassin's Creed I've played thus far, and I've played pretty much every single one up to this point. This is the first one I've ever attempted to get 100% sync in, and for good reason; it's just way too much fun overall. Spoilers may be ahead, read at your own risk.  Visuals: 8/10 My 770GTX and i7 run this decently on high settings. A lot of the environment looks good. I did experience some FPS drops, but nothing that made the game unplayable when it occationally occurs. There's still some graphical glitches to be found, like your character not appearing after a cut scene, but a quit exit to the main menu and back into the game usually solves it first try.  Sound: 8/10 First of all, ship gun sounds: awesome. The only real gripes I have are sometimes the dialog is too quiet and occationally I experienced a lack of sword clashing sounds randomly, but not often enough to ruin the experience overall.  Gameplay: 9/10 The controls are pretty decent. It's AC, so it's slightly repetative, but finally it doesn't seem to drag on. There's lots of different stuff to do so you can try something else when you get tired of one type of activity or another. Spearing sea life is exciting, fighting enemy ships is great fun, and even doing the hacking in the modern out-of-the-animus story line is somewhat entertaining.  Story: 7/10 Not the best story this time overall. Edwards personal journey is interesting to follow, but I felt there was a lack of connection to previous games with regards to the templar order and the grand scheme of things. Really only giving it a 7 vs a 5 because Edwards depressing journey made me want to cry at a few points, so that was worth it to me. The ending was sad yet sweet at the same time, especially the credits sequence, so be sure you sit through that once you get to it.  Replayability: I'm sure after enough time I'll come back and give this another go, just like I have most of the previous AC games, but like any story driven game, once you've seen the end, it's hard to play through it again as seriously as the first time. Nothing against the game really, it's just the reality of the genre.  Multiplayer: I can't comment, I haven't tried any AC multiplayer and don't really intend to.  Overall: 8.5/10 If you're an AC fan and can deal with the small glitches with patience, you'll enjoy this game quite a lot, probably more so than the previous installment.

A great all around package, boasting some of the best road cars around, flashy yet crisp graphics, and a very good looking UI compared to the competitors. It's not the top simulation on the market, but it is a great stepping stone for those looking to move beyond simcade games into something more realistic. While playable with a contrroller, you definitely want a full force feedback wheel to get the most out of this one. Below I will go over the pros and cons in a bit more detail:  Pros -the graphics look fantastic, and it isn't too demanding on your PC to get a beautiful looking race environment  - the physics, while not mind blowing, are more than sufficient to provide decent racing, and fun driving, particularly the road cars. Definitely leaps and bounds above what gran Turismo, Forza and PCars are doing in this department  -the car selection is great. While other games have done race cars to death, Assetto Corsa has the best selection of road cars around, from Ferraris to Mustangs and everything in between. It also has race cars, but the road cars really stand out, and with the above average physics and great graphics, it really makes for a truly immersive experience  -the laser scanned tracks are extremely detailed and stunning to look at, really bringing the experience together -BEST.DRIFTING.ANYWHERE......yes, you can drift here. Yes, it is awesome  -Force feedback isn't the best on the market by any means, but it gives you a good idea of what your car is doing and will help you master the art of controlling it. Still, above average and better than most everything other than GSCE and RFactor 2  -Updates. Kunos is always updating and improving Assetto Corsa, along with releasing more content. We are on Tire Model 7, and have had 3 dlc packs, free content, and have more being relesed all the time. Japan pack today!!!  -The mods. This title has a great modding community behind it, many modders carried over from RFactor, who put out tons of quality content. The mods work great here, and much of the time are indistinguishable from stock content   Cons -The sounds are far from perfect, and a greater range is needed (a bit bland, need to work on the highs and lows for the growls and whines of real cars). UPDATE: The Japan pack is a step in the right direction, and with a high quality set of headphones, they actually have quite decent range  -The AI started off as some of the worst anywhere, slow and stupid. They have since greatly improved, but they still need a good bit of work. Sometimes they are randomly aggressive, but can end up in a train, 'racing' single file if your unlucky. It's hard to find a good match for your skill level, 98% I beat them on most tracks, 100% and I rarely see them beyond lap 3  -The online competition. I can't really fault Kunos for this, but while better than the Gran Turismo/Forza crowd, you still do get a lot of noobs and wreckers. I think it's mostly because, as I mentioned before, Assetto Corsa is used as a stepping stone from racing games to true simulations. Maybe I am just spoiled from iRacing and RFactor 2 Leagues, but I am yet to have a race where half the field is still racing by lap 5. It could really do with a skill matching/ranked racing system.  As you see, the pros far outweigh the cons, and Assetto Corsa is a worthy purchase for any sim racer or car afficianado. With a sense of realism foreign to most gamers, it will have you kicking the backend around corners and fighting to gain each second. It still is far from perfect, and I still prefer RFactor 2 physics, and iRacing's competitive racing, but to just hop in a dream car like a Ferrari 458 or Corvette Stingray and tear up the track, Assetto Corsa reigns supreme. And with the constant inprovements, updates and new content being released all the times, who knows what the future of this title holds. A solid 8/10 from me.

Among the Sleep is a first-person horror atmospheric adventure game developed and published by the Norwegian Krillbite Studio.  The game focuses on exploration, puzzles and horror, seen from the perspective of a two year old toddler.  The game starts when the toddler is having his second birthday party at home with his mother when the party is interrupted by someone at the front door.  When the mother walks towards the front door, a man starts talking and a muffled conversation starts. Suddenly the mother raises her voice at the person at the front door.  The toddler is frightened by the angry tone from his mother and the blurred vision will come up, indicating the fear of the toddler. Then the mother returns quickly to the toddler with a present, a teddy bear but she doesn't tell from who the present is.  During the night you are awoken by a strange force that turns over your crib and Teddy is lost. At this point your are completely alone, you must look for help and your adventure starts.  During the game you are most of the time accompanied your teddy bear, Teddy and together you two start searching for comfort. Most of the time you are solving puzzles, navigating through the labyrinth of levels, dodging monsters and collecting memories of you mother to find her back again. When traveling through the dark areas you can hug Teddy who will temporary emit light that helps you navigate. You enter various dark and strange areas such as the interior of your house, expansive closets, the Gingerbread house, tunnels, an abandoned playground, forests, swamps etc.  When you are chased by monsters, you can hear them nearing by the increasing grating sounds and the more becoming blurry vision.  What I really liked about this game is the idea that you play as a two year old toddler but having a more developed brain/mind to solve these puzzles trough these surreal environments. If you like adventure horror games or just want to wander around as a two year old toddler (again), I really recommend this game to you, certainly when it is on sale!

http://youtu.be/Q8faH9AARkE   The battles in Paper Sorcerer are fun and the dungeon crawling can get addictive if you enjoy these types of turn based RPGs. The skills for each character feel unique and the enemies are diverse. The difficulty ranges from super easy to nearly impossible “80s” style. The main downside is that all of the dungeons look very similar and can get repetitive. The optional catacombs can be explored to break things up but I would have enjoyed more diversity of level design. The music is definitely not winning any awards but I felt like it fit in with the style the developers were going for.   I really had fun with it and if you enjoy turn based RPGs then at $5, it’s worth a buy, but if you are on the fence and you feel like the graphics/style might not be for you, then skip it. You’re probably right.

A very good game with some room for improvement though: This game has two main mechanics. The first in a race builds the track in 5 directions, depending on where he drives. You can either tightly turn left or right, slightly turn left or right or drive straight. The second mechanic is flying. you can lift off the track or cut curners by extending the wings on your vehicle. These two mechanics balance the game very well, since someone whos behind can often cut corners and overtake the one who is currently on the lead and has to build the track.  What I liked about the game: - the game mechanic - the look - the music  What could be better IMO: - its rare to find a lot of people online (so get it and play pls) :P - the online game modes are not very well explained and I frequently see people in a races, that play bad because the dont know some basic tricks. (e.g. cutting the track in 'ping pong', build towards the cube in 'collect') - in single player, the difference between bronze and gold is often just a margin, which is quiet frustrating since you need at least bronze to carry on with the next level.  Well spent 2 bucks.

15 hours in and I've done everything, built everything and suffered through whiney survivors, chain smokers and caffine addicts who can't seem to realise it's not a time to want those luxuries and just get on with your damn job. If I was stuck with Bruno or Katia in such a situation I would have left them days ago.  I managed to finish the game in around about 10 hours, not very lenghly for 15 pounds imo and the replay value that I was told was there, simply isn't.   Nothing new comes into play the 2nd time around, that burning building you loot for the basic supplies on your first night is in the same place and isn't any different to how it was the 1st time and even if you get a different location or two from the first time you played towards the end you'll realise you've done pretty much the same thing all over again.  Those 'dynamic' events i read about in reviews are about as dynamic as a college timetable, they happen in the exact same places with the exact same layouts. Sure you get people knocking on your door when your at home but again these aren't dynamic, I played in the same house in 2 different playthroughs and the events from the 1st time happened in the exact same order in the 2nd.  Sure the supermarket and church have a couple of different scenarios, friendly looters or a woman about to get attacked in the supermarket or a frtiendly priest willing to trade or bandits that are a cowerdly bunch to say the least and only one has a gun but he's clearly there to give you some stealth kill practice as he's so easy to sneak up on it was like attacking a dummy, same with the soldier at the supermarket.  Then you get places like the Warehouse which has no kind of change in their scenario, I always have bandits there that shoot on sight and never want to trade so I'd avoid if your not very good at the whole 'stealth' thing.  Which leads me to the stealth, 8/10 it works but occasionally it will glitch and instead of silently walking to a hiding spot you'll alert the whole damn building by charging full pelt at it so stealth is slightly wonky but bearable. But when your caught and surrounded by people who can see you and have guns then your dead, no other way around it and Dead is Dead with an autosave feature that stops you from reverting to an older save. I also found it annoying that this bandit i was chasing, who had no weapon in his hand, managed to beat down my healthy character who was carrying a frigging hatchet! Also even if you have a gun and plenty of ammo, good luck actually hitting anyone before they charge you and stab you in the gut.  So yeah the combat is very poor and uneven, the only sure way to get a kill is through a stealth kill from cover, anything else takes a few hits.  Sure it was fun the 1st time but for £15 I feel like kinda let down at the poor replay value. Maybe I just got unlucky with my scenarios and the luck of the draw was not in my favour or maybe it really is just repetitive garbage?  My raiting 4/10: Definetely original and interesting in concept but it just didn't work for me...Personally it would have worked better as a mobile game.

Its an fps platformer with some very simple puzzles. When you do well in this game and get the pacing and rhythm just right, oh man does it feel good. But the game quickly ramps up the difficulty and then pacing and fun breaks down; either because I am worse than average at FPS (I have always thought of myself as better than average but it is a possibility), this game is desinged only for hardcore fps players, or the game is simply too particular about players getting timing and movements exactly the way it wants.   As a result, I cannot think of a single person that I actually know that I could see enjoying this game as more than a quick playtest.

I will divide this review into categories (history, pros/cons, recommendations for playing) for those that wish to skip, or read the details. This review is in terms of a PvE perspective as I never bothered with PvP with this game.  **History**  I played this game before back in 2008 back when it was called Requiem Bloodymare. The game was relatively fun and had an active (in-game) community. Back then the game had two starting areas (1 being Kurt village [the desert area we drop into now] and the other being a snowy relatively nice looking area to enjoy). People ended up picking Kurt village as their starting point because the locations and enemies were closer and faster to get to. There were also a lot more quests to pick up from the NPC's in these starting locations than just the pickup scrolls. The user would bounce between the two starting areas just to get to level 25 before they proceeded onto map 2.  From there, groups would form to grind for the lamenting (cry/sound) gear from the 1 dungeon to the east in Map 2. then proceed on quest tracks in the shape of the infinity symbol (∞) [starting in the center then following the path SE to NE to SW then back to NW. By this time, the user was already grinding in the dungeon slightly to the NW to get their next set armor. Proceeding North to map 3 it was just a barren waste land where you had to follow the scrolls to know where to go. As to the never ending bugs with quests, people would pickup the quest then ask for the X/Y coordinates to where/who to kill to fulfill the quest. During that time I made 3 characters: Soul Hunter -> Dominator (for minion master purposes) Defender -> Commander (for taunts and buffs and medium tanking) Templar -> Radiant (for healing) After the summer of 2008, I took a break from the game due an increased work schedule and a decreased desire to grind in the game. I came back in summer 2009 when the game had (for lack of better terms) devolved into Memento. Skills that you had acquired before at earlier levels had been moved out of your reach. Having been primarily a minion master and finally unlocking the 4th summon (other than illusion) and then finding I needed to re-unlock the skills at much later levels was beyond annoying and so I moved onto my healer I had not progressed. Areas that you used to grind for gear had been stripped bare and the rate of drops had been massively decreased. If you had obtained the set armors for these in the past, their stats were not promoted and were incompatible with the newer versions of the set items that could only be equipped at later levels. The number of people still playing the game had also significantly dropped. Starter areas had been reduced to the 1 map kurt village and had maybe 3 people at a time in them. I lasted 1.5 months (only because of gear already acquired in the previous version of the game and wanting to experience the other classes) Come 2015 ... they added some non-sensible useless starter video. They added a weird tutorial dungeon that when you decide to walk through to the end of the dungeon it warps you to a much higher level area. I immediately used the town transport tool that was provided to me to go to Kurt village as I knew already that this was the starting location. I believe these transport maps list the towns in the order by level if memory serves me correctly. I proceeded to play the game for about 4 hours and by that time had made it to level 27, had over 1.6 Million in-game currency (courtesy of the 50k pog pickups they are providing). With the main quest they give you they seem to be trying to rush your character to the 2nd map and ignore the beginner area. Auction house is pretty devoid of armor/weapons. People in game are pretty much non-existent. Old quest bugs still exist. User-interface unimproved. **Pros/Cons** Pros: 1) Had a relatively enjoyable combat system 2) Decent mail/auction house 3) Decent Bank 4) Gives you a good amount of bag storage (if you do the main quest) Cons: 1) Graphics outdated. 2) Bugs don't seem to ever have been fixed from 2008/2009. 3) Quests are still hard to navigate with/without bugs and require veteran player knowledge or wiki to progress through. 4) The user keeps spamming the 2 attack skills they get until level 30 or so. 5) I don't know if they changed this but before if you died in Possession Beast mode, your beast was destroyed and you had to get a new one by grinding or buying from the auction house. 6) To re-open the miniquest window you have to toggle one of your quests in the main quest window to re-open. There is no user-key interface for the miniquest window. 7) Monsters will generally follow you for miles outside their territory until they have been allowed to hit you once. 8) Lack of users means no dungeons, means no progression, means game is worthless. **Recommendations for those that want to try it** 1) Get a group of friends together and plan whose going to be what type of class so that you can have a full party for dungeons ... and survive. This simply must be done first and foremost if you want to get anything out of this game post level 50 solo as there is an extremely small user base with this game already let alone within the areas of your level. 2) The game gives the new characters a 7 day exp boost / costume / mount. Whatever character your using devote yourself to this character or delete it and make it again another day if you're not intending to make full use of the exp boost/mount. 3) The starter area has pretty much turned into a 1-2 quest chain area where they go from Kurt village in the north to the South east town in the Shape of a S. Do these quests to get your bags and gear. 4) If you get the pog drop pickup ... just sell it it's worth 50k a piece. 5) Don't bother with scroll quests until Map 2 (~lev 25-30). Note that this is for exp rather than the petty reward they offer. 6) Once in Map 2 follow the the scrolls/quests in the infinity shape starting from the center going SE as explained previously in the history section. From this point on the wiki is your friend.

This game is beautiful. It has brilliant art and sound, and really sets the atmosphere. The cultural insights are done very nicely, as you actually learn something but they're not too long or frequent. They're actually quite interesting.  The puzzles are nice as well, quite challenging in some places and easy in others. However, sometimes a seemingly easy puzzle can be hard to complete due to the jumping being a little glitchy. I like the two different characters and the way they have different abilities, and how easy the controls are. The spirits are nice and make the game harder, but also glitch out sometimes, making it impossible to jump to them or move them to the right place.  It's got a nice storyline, but it's not great to replay as there isn't really anything else to do. It's a very interesting game first time you play though.  So, it's a nice game with brilliant graphics and an interesting storyline, but also with a few bugs which makes it hard to play at some points.  9/10

Are you seeing this because you are intrested to buy it on a sale? Don't even read further and get it right away for these few $ it is worth. Still curious? Let me explain why my hours for this game are so high.  This game has a very active modding scene who created a variety of different gamemodes. Unlike the name deathmatch tells you there is plenty of other things to do making it fun for everybody. The community is great and in many places very helpfull. They will sort out bugs you have or simply teach you how to play the game. You should definately buy this game! Expand the review to learn about its gamemodes.   (Team) Deathmatch  Servers : Common Sub Types : 1v1 , Matches , Bots , Variety of gravity's , KillBox  As the name of the game indicates this is the original gamemode of the game. There are many different kinds of maps. Killbox is mainly a box where you can hit about everyone with some stuff for you to mess with. Other maps can involve a variety of sceneries (Mappers like to use there own themes instead of just City 17). There is still an active competitive scene so if your up for 1v1 matches dig around and you can surely find them. Some servers often killbox like to turn the gravity down for some low gravity fun.  The gunplay is what i call 'Unbalanced'. This means that some guns are clearly superior over other guns and you will have to focus on getting better weapons from the moment you spawn (Quake style but not as extreme). If this is not your thing just continue reading because there are plenty of modes for you to find.  The most populair frag video from a well known clan :  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OtJgwl0EfgU     Cooperative   Servers : Common Sub Types : Exclusive maps to some servers, Multiple gravities, Exclusive Weapons to some servers, Dual Coop  This is my personal favorite mode. Infact i like this mode so much i built an entire  server  [www.bouncyball.eu]  around it.  In cooperative maps you work together with your team to destroy the aliens of the singleplayer game (Combines are broken). Additionaly some of the maps give you challenges similair to the physics puzzles in the original game or allow 2 teams to mix cooperative with the deathmatching (Both examples are rare as most maps are normal cooperative).  Here is some uncut gameplay from my server to give an incidation of what the gamemode is like :  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OxjxStzUAv4      Puzzle   Servers : Common  The name says it all. You are put in a map where you have to complete a set of puzzles in order to beat the map. The puzzles you get a very varied and can involve anything from finding a path in a minefield , driving airboats along a track , building your way to the exit or hitting targets with a specified gun. Puzzle map creators are very creative in the challenges they think up so each map will have its own unique challenges to beat.  Puzzle map gameplay :  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HUVN46I_TYw      Roleplay   Servers : Common  Roleplay is another fan favorite for many people of the game. It is cops and robbers style gameplay where you buy and sell your items and on alot of servers you can even buy and decorate your own house. Personally i did not play it much so my review would probabbly fall short of the possibility's in these servers.  Probabbly not the best vid :  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=o54-ZKvVP8A     Surf   Servers : Rare Sub Types : With or without weapons (With is more common)  Surf is something adopted from the counterstrike community. It all involves trying to surf down a track of slidey walls and not falling of the sides. On most maps depending on where you failed you get placed in a deathmatch box with a better weapon then those who failed before you.  Surf Gameplay :  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GuexNobUAS0     Trick Jumping   Servers : Medium Rare  These maps are similair to Surf in the way that you are trying to get from A to B. They mostly do not involve gunplay of any kind. Instead of sliding along walls you have to beat jumps of increasing difficulty. While fun on its own this is also often used to train for better movement in the deathmatches.  Hard Trickjump Gameplay :  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dEeuAHpI9l8     Zombiemod   Servers : Rare Sub Types : Hybrd with zombie survival , Straight up killing  While there are zombiemod servers they are not always around as these servers run different modes and the type of zombiemod also depends strongly on the server you play it on. Other servers usually have a bigger deathmatch focus and no npc's but since i only play it on mine i will tell you how it goes on our server.  The round starts with everyone as a survivor where you fight unlimited npc zombies in a set time. Once someone fails and greats beaten he will join the other team and only have a crowbar. Zombies are slower then survivors by default but are the only team that can use the spint ability. Survivors win by surviving long enough while zombies win by making sure all humans are dead.  In case you want to play it and no other servers are running zombiemod you can join our server  bouncyball  [bouncyball.eu]  and vote for one of the zombiemod maps using !jb.    Deathrun   Servers : Rare  In deathrun one team tries to run from A to B and the other sets off traps to stop them. There are a vareity of maps all with there own set of traps.  Gameplay from the most populair deathrun server :  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UYEpKmfEAz8     Scripting / Cheating / Building   Servers : Rare Sub Types : Cheating , Building using server commands only Wait what? Cheating? Hacking? o.O Why would anyone want to do this?  The answer is simple. Using cheats and 'Hacks' very cool stuff can be done in the engine. Do not expect these servers to be overrun with aimbotters (although some people will kill your godmode protection off). This is all about creating great things with the cheats in the game or your own custom addons.  Sadly all good videos of this have been removed but a quick summery of some stuff i saw : Giant Mechs (Working) , Space invaders remade , Dokuman attack , gm_flatgrass turned into a forest , Gigantic volcano jump challenge that will explode in a cool manner when beaten.  The possiblity's are HUGE!    Conclusion   I probabbly missed alot of gamemodes as these are server experiments. Think about FreezeTag or extremely modified servers.  The server in this video has been remade entirely and most of the things shown are no longer there but it is a good indication of how crazy the game can get :  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kCmgRmg92Yo .  It is also worth mentioning that as you can see in the above vid servers have a lot of control over the gameplay as they can program new features themselfs using the addon support for the game. Think about things such as statistics , jetpacks ropes and hooks , teleports , midget mode and many more.  I hope my insiders view gave you a good insight in why this game is so well loved as the steam store page simply does not cut its job. The game has its drawbacks that in the past it has been ruined by valve quite a bit (And then fixed by valve and the community for the most part). Because valve does not look at this game very often the description and screenshots on the storepage are simply acient and you can experience some bugs and crashes. The more populair servers will do anything they can to provide a sollid gameplay experience so no worries.

At the time of this review, I've played this game for about a month. Here is where my review currently stands.  Pros: The game is great, offering a fair amount of customization for your created characters which will actually affect your style of play in the game. The combat system, though simple, is wonderful and easy to pick up. Finding players that play in a style akin to your own is great as it allows for some pretty insane combos and damage. The story, although predictable (rince and repeat of the same story albiet with some changes) is a fun one as you can see your own character grow in power along with the cast of Dragon Ball.   Cons: The game has a sort of peer-to-peer connection system when it comes to playing online. This wouldn't be a problem except for the fact that much of the player base consist of children who have no control of their internet providers, thus resulting in some shoddy connectivity on some instances. On the subject of the young player base, 90% of this player base consist of the Saiyan race. The issue with this is that Saiyans can transform into Super Saiyans, which is a form that allows them to cast skills back-to-back at no cost at all, resulting in spam. This is infuriating in two ways. The first being that it ruins combos when playing alongside these spammers. The second issue is that PvP is stagnant, as these skills are easy to dodge and counter, so playing against another player that actually uses skills to fight is a 1/10 chance. Another issue would be the DLC quests. Although they are fun, the quests themselves are high level and have no restrictions on who can play them (granted that you've bought the DLC). Normally, you can't play certain quests unless you've gotten further enough in the story or beaten prerequisited quests. A.K.A: It boils down to you being a high enough level to even play them. With the DLC quests, all you need to do is buy them, so this results in people who just began the game fighting off enemies who are level 80+, so a lot of 1-shots to beginners. The sad part is that unless you view someone's profile yourself, you have no idea what level anyone is when in quest lobby, so you wont be able to tell if that stranger that joined is new or experienced enough for these quests.  All-in-all, a good game, but it's left in such a way that you'll dislike much of the community when playing, so keep your eyes out for the few hidden gems that play well and add them.

When the worst part of a game is that you recieve too many texts from a yellow science lizard, it can't be too bad right?  Maybe I should actually elaborate on this...  Undertale is a pretty unique RPG. Depending on how you play it, it can be funny, sad, scary, or just about anything in between. The breadth of this game's tone is actually quite astounding, and only one of the many great things about it. I found myself way more engaged in this game's setting, story, and characters than any other game I've played in quite a while, not only due to the quality of the characters themeselves, but also its brilliantly deconstructive sense of humor. This game uses the player's knowledge of common RPG tropes to its advantage to produce some really clever moments. On top of this, when it's not making you laugh, it's being incredibly heartwarming or sad. Fans of Earthbound and other such games will feel right at home here.  The music is freakin astounding and that's all I really have to say about that.  Gameplay wise, this game includes some rather unique battle mechanics for an RPG as well, forcing you to actively dodge enemy attacks in a fashion reminiscent of a bullet hell game, spicing up the combat and giving it a more involved feel. And, well, as far as actually attacking goes, you don't really have to. Every enemy encounter can be resolved peacefully with the correct actions, almost like a small puzzle. It's something most games like this just don't do.  As you may know, though, not every game is made for everyone. This is less a crtique of the game itself and more of a heads up for those entertaining the idea of diving right in: if you (somehow) don't like the characters, or the story of the game doesn't grab you, you're really going to be missing out on a big part of why the game is considered so great. Sure, the gameplay is pretty cool, interesting, and well-developed as far as the battles go, but it clearly is not the game's major focal point. This game is all about the narrative, and it absolutely requires you to connect with the characters in order to be successful. Fortunately, everything concerning that in particular happens to be incredibly good, so, uh, yeah. Have I mentioned how amazing the music is yet?  Really, all you need to know is that Undertale is an fantastic experience. Honestly, I've been really holding back from gushing incessantly for most of this review. The truth is, this game is something special. It makes you think. It makes you smile, sometimes without even realizing it. The characters are amazing, the humor is almost always on point and well thought out, the attention to detail is genuinely difficut to fathom, the battle system is actually unique, and it has a totally kickass soundtrack to boot. This game oozes so much heart and hard work that I'm still trying to scrub the stains out of my couch, and it's very likely going to be remembered for a long time. If you decide to get it, do yourself a favor and don't spoil ANYTHING. You'll be rewarded for your restraint.  ...  So what was I talking about again?  Oh yeah.  Only real negative: dinosaur won't stop texting you.  Just play it.
the game takes you head on where you don't have to fight to win.it's funny,clever,heart stopping,it's the full package of awesomeness

What do I have to say about this game..  Endless Sky is a completely free, top-down, 2D space game. But there's more to it than that; think Elite Dangerous meets 2D with text-based story telling charm.   Gameplay:   Gameplay typically goes along the lines of this: Game: 'Buy your first ever spacecraft! Pick from a transport, cargo ship, or a fighter!' Player: 'I want the fighter!' Banks: 'Let me just set your mortgage appropriately...' *Proceeds to suffer paycheck to paycheck*  Yeah.. Never start with the fighter. Bad idea.  Once you get the basics down, a lot of the gameplay boils down to your character trying to earn a living through either pirating, transporting tourists around, and/or shipping cargo around the galaxy, and avoiding pirate raids. Be warned, this game does take a little trial and error before you're able to get the hang of it.  What catches my attention is the surprising amount of story a free game like this has. The random events can range from a funny little fetch quest (Go save my pookie!), to helping scientsts with something, to throwing you the chance to go off on a sudden story quest.  The writing has a charm to it. The dialog is surprisingly well written, every planet, starport, and space station has it's own bio and photo (There are a LOT of planets), and the science is well researched.   OVERALL: 8/10  Upsides:   This game is clearly someones passion project, and the passion shows. From how far I've played, the characters have personality, the writing is vivid, the planets are well thought out, and the story that I've experienced thus far is interesting. I can honestly say this is the first time a completely free game has thoroughly impressed me. This game has well over 15-20+ hours of potential minimum playtime.  Well thoughtout universe and story!  Huge number of ships, upgrades, and planets!  No Microtransactions!  Good autosave system!  Forgot to save and now your flagship is destroyed, along with that cargo that was going to net you 124,000 creds? Don't worry about it, you can pick up back at whatever planet you last took off from!    Downsides:   The initial tutorial quests, which do have some variation (+Brownie Point Get), don't cover as much as they could. They explain the game concepts well, but the only way to learn the controls, how to hyperjump, target, fire, et cetera, is to look in your options menu. This isn't a huge issue, as there aren't many controls for the game and they're fairly simple at that.  The UI could use a little improvement. I'd like to have my flagship's shield and hull stats next to the rest of my fleet, and the enemy's stats nearby, rather than have all three seperated. It makes it difficult to properly manage a ship (let alone a fleet) during combat.  There isn't any way that I've found that will make all of the ships in your fleet jump at once, they go at their own pace and become scattered between planets if you move too fast.    Still not convinced that this game may be a hidden gem? Try it out, it's free, you have nothing to loose!

app_name: Attack on Titan / A.O.T. Wings of Freedom
review_text: The best anime game I've ever played, but wait for a sale if you're on the fence.  PROS:  It is very satisfying to use the 3d maneuver gear. It's not as skill-based as the fan-made game, but it looks and feels great. Cables actually have to attach to trees/buildings/titans in order for you to take off. Kind of like in spider-man 2 for the PS2.  Combat feels satisfying. Once again not very skill-based compared to the fan-made game, but you feel badass slicing titans up.  The story is just the anime, with something extra thrown in too. It took me 10 hrs to complete. Cutscenes are done very well considering it's an anime game. Voice acting is great too.  Multiplayer is kinda cool too. Adds some replayability.  Titans are very different from each other. Lots of variation. You might see the same titan once or twice, but not very often.  Graphics are very good too for an anime game.  CONS:  Game was a too easy, even while playing on hard. I would S-rank almost every mission without even trying, and died only once in the whole story.  Sometimes game bugs and titans just stare at you and/or clip into buildings/the ground.  Annie isnt aggressive enough.  Price is too much. Wait until it hits $30 or is on sale, if you are not a die hard Attack on Titan fan.   Oh, also use a controller with this game.

app_name: The Body VR: Journey Inside a Cell
review_text: This is a great free experience, and gives a glimpse of what the future of education - let alone the medical field - might be like. It's about 12 minutes of presentation deeper and deeper into a cell, plus a side option to view various systems of the human body, pull apart, and examine / scale each piece.  There are a few things I wish were different, such as being able to grab, scale, manipulate, and otherwise examine the individually named components throughout the presentation mode.   Overall it was a very fun way to experience biology like never before. Give it a try!

I'll be honest here - I bought this game expecting that I wouldn't enjoy it. And well, here we are. I understand that nobody likes a party pooper who buys overwhelmingly positive rated games just to leave a negative review on them, but I have a few important points to make.  Before anyone starts to think otherwise: In no way do I hate puzzle games. In fact, I would say puzzle games are my favorite genre. The feeling of satisfaction when one solves a difficult puzzle, the 'aha!' moment, is one of the best experiences one can have playing video games. But, for me at least, klocki fails to deliver on this experience.  The goal of each level in the game is to rearrange the pieces in it so that there are no 'loose ends' remaining. It's a very simple concept and the game communicates it to the player without using any words, which is one thing I'll give it credit for. Unfortunately, the depth that this system is supposed to allow for is sorely lacking.  When I say that, part of what I mean is that the puzzles are easy in this game. Quite easy, in fact. There weren't many puzzles that took me more than a couple of minutes to solve. Now, I don't demand that every puzzle game be Snakebird levels of hard (even if I prefer it that way), but there's no satisfaction to be had in solving an easy puzzle. Puzzles are supposed to challenge your mind in a way that makes you feel smart when you solve them. If there's no challenge, there's no smart feeling when you solve them. There are a few challenging puzzles in the game, but even those don't really give the game much depth.  Most of the game's difficulty comes through complexity - you make the levels harder by making them larger. This is poor puzzle design. A puzzle game's difficulty should come from exploring the strategies that the ruleset allows for. But in this game, there really isn't much strategy in solving the puzzles at all. When you discount the possibilities that obviously won't lead to a valid solution, each puzzle can be solved through pure trial and error. If a particular piece has some loose ends, you move it around to a different place, and if that messes up the connections of a different piece, you adjust that piece also, and soon enough with this process you'll end up with the solution. The game frequently adds new methods of moving pieces such as rotating handles and sliding tiles, and also new types of connections such as abstract shapes, but these fail to change the basic strategy of trial and error. In the end, they feel like a cover-up for the inherent shallowness of the core game mechanic.  So, if you're looking for a puzzle game that requires clever thinking and intimate knowledge of the game mechanics, you're probably convinced by now that this is not the puzzle game you're looking for. But I suspect that most people here don't really care about difficulty and depth of mechanics - they just want something that can pass the time while also being simple and relaxing. That's perfectly fine; life can be exhausting at times, and you might not want to play super-demanding games after you've had a long day. But even considering that, this game is poor value for your money.  Some people are going to look at me weird for calling a 99 cent game 'poor value'. But yes, this game is poor value for your money. It took me 45 minutes to complete this game, so that's 0.75 hours of gameplay per dollar. Which doesn't look too bad at first, but let's compare it against my favorite puzzle game, Stephen's Sausage Roll. (I'm sorry, I just can't help but be a zealot for that game. It's soooooo good.) Stephen's Sausage Roll is $30, and it took me 32.5 hours to beat, giving over an hour of gameplay per dollar. Just think about it: a game thirty times the price of this one  beat  it in value! And Stephen's Sausage Roll's puzzles are much more involved, so you could even say that each hour is more 'densely packed' than an hour of klocki. Even worse, you could play free online logic puzzle games such as nonograms or slitherlink, which I would consider to be around the same complexity as this game, giving a hours per dollar ratio of *gasp*  infinity! .  Now, I don't think hours per dollar is the best measure of a game's value, as the previous (ridiculous) example shows, but if you're playing games solely to pass the time, then most puzzle games are pretty much interchangeable for that purpose. And in any case, the 'good value' angle seems to be what developers try for whenever they release an absurdly low priced game like this, so I wanted to get that out of the way. If you want to spend money on a relaxing logic puzzle game on Steam, I might suggest Everyday Genius: SquareLogic, which provides thousands of procedurally generated Sudoku-like puzzles that actually have quite a bit of depth to them. Or maybe you could try the Hexcells series, which has over 100 handcrafted Minesweeper-like puzzles over three games, plus a random puzzle generator in the third game. Both cost significantly more than the 99 cents klocki costs ($5 and $9 for the entire series, respectively), but hopefully this will reinforce a point that too often goes ignored in this era of cheap indie games and Steam sales: you get what you pay for.  (I think I spent over three times as long writing this review than actually playing the game. What the heck is wrong with me?...)

This game is fun and includes some interesting new gameplay mechanics but lacks overal. + playable + is what the description represents + worth the sale price I got it for. 16.5 hrs of a unique game for $10 - enemy AI is lacking - story doesn't link with series and is too much of an epic - gameplay gets repetative  The basic play is that of an arcade shooter with the strategic bit tacked on. The controls of both your character and your squad is smooth (but not flawless) but the AI is completely lacking. Instead of having to adapt to the new environments gameplay is more of a constant grind because no matter where you are, cover is there, and the AI doesn't react enough to your movements.  The story (minor spoilers) isn't exactly what I expected, and for me that made the game less interesting. I expected a prequel to the new XCOM: Enemy Unknown and allusions to the expansion Enemy Within. I was hoping for what the trailer alludes to: an XCOM fighting during first contact fourty years before the main invasion seen in Enemy Unknown while erasing the evidence to create the history we know today. Instead the story takes some of the characters and ideas in the XCOM series and plows right over the story and known history in order for the story to shape up to the epic expectations of a blockbuster FPS with one man saving the world. While the story is as linear and unimaginative as an FPS storyline, The Bureau does throw in some tidbits of philosophy and hard questions.  Definately not worth the $20 list price, but depending on your interest $5-$15 might seem reasonable.

Decent enough game, that is swung into the 'don't bother' section by clunky controls and a truly awful team AI.  To begin with, it feels like a half-♥♥♥♥♥ console port (though it does play better with a controller). The cover system works well enough, but actually trying to glue your guy into cover fails about 10% of the time. If you're not glued to the wall, you're 100% out in the open - a recipe for an easy death, and yet another reload from the last save point.  The AI is laughably bad. If I tell a teammate to stand behind full cover, either he'll run forward into a different spot with half cover, or into the open to try and shoot at the enemy. Grenade lands nearby? They'll just stare at it before being obliterated.   Forget trying to flank or other fun tactics - while getting behind the bad guys' cover does give you big bonuses, the maps are so claustrophobic that it's almost impossible without soaking up huge amounts of damage, or getting stuck in a dead end and dying. Combine this with the clunky movement controls means that it's easier and safer to pick them off from a distance.  The storyline is decent enough and the hub is well set out, with mini missions through them to keep you interested.  The art and graphics are also neat, with some great atmospheric elements in the missions. It's just a pity that the gameplay lets it down.

I have played Civ since Civ 2. Loved them all. JUST ONE MORE TURN!!!  This one is different. Same game, but the advancements are all foreign. (You would expect that I guess because it is future, but they are not futuristic advancements.)    You have to read all the descriptions to figure things mean. The names are not intuitive. Does 'Node Bank' sound like city defense to anyone? It sounds like someplace to put your nodes, not that I know what those are...  You don't start with flight, or the ability to launch a satelite? How did we get to another planet without that? I could see not starting with the resources to build it.   Researching things like communications, after having interstellar space travel? I think we've have a real break down in... yeah....  I played one round on the free weekend trial. Well most of a game. I was on for about 4 hours straight and at about 1 am I was wishing it was over. Normally, I am wishing I had another few hours to finish it. I saved my game and tried to pick it up the next day. More about that later.  I didn't care for the UI either. The buttons are not obvious, you have to click on text that has no indication that it is a button. The 'Thank you' text to dismiss the advisor is an example of this. The upgrade unit screen was probably the worst for me. I had no idea what I was supposed to do there and had to start clicking randomly to find what I was looking for. The first time I got out, I wasn't sure how. But I knew it was possible for the second time I faced that challenge.  The colors and menu are all very flat an under done. From the world menu, you can't see what land you have explored, and can barely tell who owns what.   It is like they took the Civ V UI and drained all the color and texture out of it.   The leaders names are also too complex. Keep it simple stupid. I couldn't remember what leader went with which civ.  A bug, perhaps Linux only: I went though the load game screen the next day to pick up my game. Once I clicked the green let it rip button at the bottom of the screen, the music continued, but the terain was just black. I was able to hit escape and exit/restart/whatever. I restarted the game and tried again, same thing. I turned my video settings down and tried again. Still no luck.  I would love to recommend the game, but I can't as it is. I love civ, but will wait until this one is 75% off to buy it. 40% off wasn't a deep enough discount to make it worth it to me. Then I will probably just do it because I love Civ.

Steam needs to add a 'neutral' review option already. I really WANT to reccommend the hell out of this game, but the PC port is so bad that it becomes really difficult to do so. So I'll just pick 'yes' solely because the actual game itself is really awesome, but you're gonna have to potentially do a lot of manual installation of community-made packages that contain optional mods and numerous bugfixes if you want the best experience.   Not gonna spoil anything plot-wise, but this isn't some 'GTA clone' as the drones have been parroting for years. The game is a wonderful combination of bats**t wackiness and grounded realism. Everything the game has to offer is wonderful so I won't get into it much, but instead I'll focus on the negatives of the PC port.   *This port wasn't done by Volition (the original devs), but CD Projekt (not CD Projekt Red) who really flubbed the port job.  *Has really terrible audio quality overall, it sounds like 64kbps mp3 quality, not even joking. This goes for every sound in the game. There was a community fix for this which fixes the radio stations at least, but everything else is still jacked up.  *Has odd CPU timing issues where on some machines the game will run multi-times faster than the normal 1x speed it should be running at, leading to disorientation and annoyance.  *Prone to random framedrops and fluctuations regardless of how good your rig is *All multiplayer relied on Gamespy's servers to function -- now that Gamespy went under, the only way to enjoy multiplayer or co-op is via a VPN tunneling client of your choice, but even then it's not guaranteed to work. I tried and failed with a friend across two different VPN programs and we had zero success. Some people have this issue, there is no universal fix.  *Can randomly crash sometimes, of course.   Now the community patch, called 'Gentlemen of the Row' indeed fixes many of these issues among many other game logic bugs and whatnot, as well as coming with a huge number of optional gameplay mods and content mods that add a large swath of new vehicles, clothing, animations, customization options, and more. Definitely get that if you want to play this. The CPU timing bug requires another fix which you can find via some rudimentary googling.   Hope this primer helped someone.

Terraria is amazing. Just absolutely amazing, and if you look I've played this game for 920 hours, so I know my stuff. Terraria is a 2D Sandbox Adventure RPG game that provides you with hours upon hours of fun whether it be by yourself or with a friend. There are a total of 15 bosses (not counting the pillars), 4 events, tons of biomes, over 3500 items, NPC's, and even a Mini Minotaur! That's just a taste of what Terraria has to offer.  Granite's Score Card: 5: Highly Recommended 4: Recommended 3: Recommended With a Sale 2: Not Recommended Unless Heavily Discounted 1: Not Recommended No Matter What  First Category, Gameplay: Gameplay is the slippery slope of gaming. It can make or break a game. If the gameplay is god awful and the mechanics are broken it will automatically set a consumer off, why should I get frustrated over something that's not my fault? Even if the game is stunning and the story is beautiful, if the gameplay is off it won't sell too well. Terraria is one of those games that just aced the gameplay. It's extremely smooth for every single weapon, you dont feel a delay, you don't feel like the game screwed up and it put the arrow 30 miles from where you wanted it to go, if something went wrong it was your fault. Building is easy and not confusing, and mining the same. The gameplay is superb. 5/5  Second Category, Graphics: There isn't much to say here. It's pixel graphics, you can't do too much wrong with those. Like the gameplay the do it very well. The sprites are all interesting and unique and it never gets boring to look out. 5/5  Thrid Category, Achievement: Does Terraria acheive what it set out to do? Does it achieve at what it says it is? Yes it does. Its top tags are Sandbox, Adventure, 2D, Crafting, and Survival. Terraria is honestly one of the greatest adventure of all time. It is reasonably long and it will take you at least 70 hours to beat it if you're a first timer. By the end you feel like you've gone on your own adventure through caves, and jungles, and the Crimson, and Hell. It's a greeat adventure. It's a great sandbox as well. Like I said before over 3500 items and there is so much variety you never seem to look and not find what you want. It obviously achieves the 2D aspect. It does crafting extremely well, you don't have no idea how to craft anything when you first start out becasue the crafting menu shows you everything you can craft with the items you currently have and the Guide NPC can tell you every thing you can craft with that item. And it does achieve the survival aspect as well. It isn't primarily a survival game so it's not as good as other survival games such as Don't Starve but it still does well. By the end of the game it doesn't feel survival like because you are so powerful and have so many weapons but most survival games are like that. The beginning is what feels like a survival game. 5/5  Fourth Category, Replayibility: I've played this game at least 5 times over. It always makes you want to start over and choose a new class or make a different kind of house. You can fight every boss as many times as you want to get the rare loot from them, and it's a sandbox so you can alwasy just create new things. 5/5  Fifth Category, Fun: This is the most important category. Is the game fun? Do you have fun all the way through? My opinion would be 5/5, but that's a bit bias after 920 hours of gameplay. In the beginning there is a lot of dying and it can be hard to figure out what to do without looking at a wiki. Figuring out how to craft is easier now with 1.3 but with trying to figure out how to beat bosses it can be difficult and annoying without a wiki or experienced player. 4/5  Average score: 4.8/5 Final Thought: Highly Recommended

tl;dr: Recommended if it's on sale and you're a fan of the franchise. Avoid otherwise.  This game doesn't really have huge issues that can be pointed out easily, just a whole lot of aspects which are either less than optimal, seemingly not very well thought out or just a few years behind in terms of game design.  At its core it's an alright squad-based TPS, with each team member having a unique class with a bunch of different abilities, although none of them will have a significant impact on the way you play through the campaign. The campaign itself is, just like every other aspect of the game, not bad, but far from stellar. The story is merely there to give you a reason to continue shooting stuff, and apart from a few encounters with the classic cast it's not exactly noteworthy. It's also short: you can count on 3-4 hours for the main (umbrella) campaign and another 3-4 hours for the government (spec ops) campaign. This spec ops campaign is in fact one of my main gripes with the game, as I am under the impression this campaign was initially meant to be a part of the full release, yet was cut out for one reason or another (perhaps it wasn't finished yet?). The other DLC isn't worth having.  While the (coop) campaign was seemingly the main focus of the game, there are also a number of versus modes available. These include the standard team deathmatch and the usual RE variations on that same gamemode, most of which aren't really worth playing. In addition, you'll have to put up with GFWL, p2p matchmaking and a small degree of auto aim which they forgot to patch out. Still, you can have some fun in versus, and I personally always found people to play with between 7-9 pm CET.  Performance-wise it's not very well optimized, on my rig it actually runs worse than contemporary FPS's with all bells and whistles. The controls are a bit clunky in the beginning (could be better with a controller, haven't tried), but you get used to them. That said, I didn't encounter any bugs.   To conclude I want to point out the almost lamentable level of mediocrity throughout the entire game. It's not bad, the issues aren't huge and if you enjoy the franchise you can certainly overcome them, yet nothing stands out as being good. Therefore, and because of the bad optimization and DLC campaign, I wouldn't recommend buying this unless it's on sale.

The Alien Game we all deserved! There's a time and place for M41A Pulse Rifles and M56 Smartguns - this isn't it. The Alien is presented the way it was way back in 1979 - terrifying, lethal, unstoppable. That's right, while there are weapons within the game, don't for a second think that you can take out the sole Alien with any of them. If it sees you? You die. If you run? You die. If you make a noise? You die.   Every action in this game is risk vs reward - even saving the game makes you think 'am i safe enough? How is my inventory? Where did i last save if i get killed mid-save?'. Lockers & Cabinets become close friends. You'll find yourself in a puddle of your own urine, under a hospital gurney, not 3 hours into the epic that unfolds. And you'll love every minute of it.   The Art direction is stunning - environments lifted straight out of Ron Cobb's sketchbooks and they look jaw-droppingly gorgeous. You don't even need a particularly powerful PC to turn everything up high, and maintain 60fps either.   However, the thing that makes this quite possibly the best horror game ever, is the sound. Well, the Sound and the AI.   There's no soundtrack as such, in game, its mostly ambient, with a few cues lifted straight from Alien, and some expanded themes that give the in-game universe it's rich, immersive feeling. The game engine knows when you're in peril and ramps up the strings section accordingly, adding to the tension.   As for the AI - while there are a few scripted sequences, the Alien is entirely random - no playthrough will ever be the same, not even loading the game will yield the same result. It hunts for you. It will stop at nothing to find you. Use a noisemaker one too many times and it'll wise up and ignore it for a while. You have to keep changing tactics, you cannot just blaze through and think that a distraction will work. Toss a flare? Sure, first few times, it'll be interested. Then it becomes interested in who threw that flare. At which point, you'll crap yourself and find your head being pierced like a capri sun.   I cannot recommend this game enough, i really can't. If Creative Assembly don't win a slew of awards for this game, i will be very, very surprised!

Alright, let's come right out with this at the start and say it very bluntly:  If you want sneaky, stealthy, I'm a freaking ninja and you will only know I'm here because your entire vault seems to be empty, but I can handle myself if things go to hell 4 player co-op... then this is the game for you. Full stop.  If you want 'Run in, guns blazing, I'm a halo player LAWL!', you're probably going to hate Payday 2.  It's really going to be that cut and dry. Payday 2 is a game you love, or you hate, and it's going to be based almost purely upon personal preference, which has nothing to do with whether the game is actually 'good' or not.    So... let's get into a bit more detail then, shall we?  For starters, let's accept this for what it is: it's a game where you have a team... and you actually are expected to provide teamwork. It's not PvP, it's purely co-operative. Your goals are pretty varied, but they're pretty similar: get in without being seen, do some task, get out in one piece, fight like a crazed badger if you get caught and run like hell.  Most of the missions revolve around the significant preference for stealth over brute force; you're almost always better off getting out without anyone even knowing you were there, than you are going in guns blazing. There's a large amount of leeway, and generally you're not penalized too harshly for getting into a firefight, but there will be times where you're cursing yourself for getting caught by that camera.  One of the biggest key features of the game is the leveling system, wherein there are 4 skill trees, and you invest points gained from leveling into them as you go. You're not restricted to any one tree, and can even put points into all four if you want, though it's typically recommended to stick to just two trees to get to the end quality goodies.  Your four main choices are as follows:  Mastermind - crowd control and healing, specializes in pistols  Enforcer - Brute force, heavy armour, big guns, shotguns, etc. If you get caught, the enforcer saves you.  Technician - Master of the drill, shaped explosive charges and turrets. Highly versatile for any situation.  Ghost - Sneakysneaky, the ghost is based on getting in and out without ever being caught. Kinda sucks if the spotlight lands on them, though.    Types of missions:  The missions you can get are some seriously awesome stuff; the stuff in payday 1 is nothing in comparison to the payday 2 heists. One of my favourites, for example, is the Framing Frame heist. In this one, you sneak into an art exhibit, grab some paintings, and sneak out, sell them in a back alley deal to a corrupt politician after embedding cameras in them, then use the camera data to break into the guy's apartment and both frame him by shoving cocaine all over the place, and run off after cleaning out his private vault filled with gold.  Other missions involve the basics, such as breaking into a jewlery store and grabbing all the loot, to running your own personal meth lab, providing 'muscle' during an FBI raid on a truck filled with yourselves and a dozen bags of cocaine, or stealing a shipment of automatic weapons, breaking into the FBI, then burning a rival cartel's dirty blood money and filming it, then mailing them the tape to gloat about it... just to name a few.  In short, if you want to do some seriously screwed up criminal-based sneaky stuff, this is the game for you. Prowling around some guy's apartment at 4am in the dark and hoping you don't trip the alarm that locks down the vault can be nerve wracking, but it's where the fun really comes in, honestly.  As stated, however, those who prefer to run in guns blazing... well, you can, but you'll find there's much better games out there for you than Payday 2. For those of you who want to extend the Thief games to 4 player co-op in modern times? Yeah, you're not going to find anything that can match Payday 2.    Cons:  Well... there's some problems with Payday 2. Not many, but there are a few. They're still trying to fine-tune the balance, so guns are being homogenized a little bit; it's a minor issue, but it can bother a lot of people. The customization is kind of crap, which is a problem... the customization itself is nice, but all the parts are gotten from a roll of the dice, which the game is so kind as to let you pick one of three cards which you can't see... meaning it tries to claim you had any control at all over your blind luck roll (other than on special events) which mostly just makes you feel like crap most of the time. It's a pain to get anything you want, and it can take a very long time to get enough skill points to really be all that useful if you're not being carried through exceptionally difficult high level runs. Being sneaky just isn't really viable at lower levels, despite that that's kind of the whole draw of the game...  As such, I can't honestly give Payday 2 a truly glowing review. It has flaws. Big ones. Core flaws built into the very design kind of flaws. The kind that aren't likely to be patched out and fixed any time soon.  That being said, if you have patience, the flaws are easily ignored; if you have fun being sneaky and stealthy, you'll probably have the patience to grind through the levels to get the skills and gear upgrades you want, which will only make you enjoy the game even more.  In short, the target audience for the game won't care about the flaws so much, but the 'run and gun' style player that the game's not aimed toward, is going to find it drives them nuts.    Final Verdict:  It's overall a very good game when it comes to catering to the target audience they were aiming for. If you ever wanted to play Thief as a co-op game in modern times, this is the game for you (though lacking the dry, sarcastic humour, sadly), and you'll probably be more or less fine with the problems it has. If you're the stereotypical X-box FPS gamer, you're probably going to flat out HATE Payday 2. In short, if you like being sneaky, it's awesome, if you don't, it sucks. There's not many people who will be in the middle of the fence on this one.  Overall score: Probably about a 50% for those it's not aimed at, and 85% for the target audience. It does exactly what it aims to do very well, but you're probably going to hate it if you're not the target audience.
