First off: This game is short. Took me between 3 and 4 hours to complete it. So keep that in mind.  Now then, Papo & Yo is a heartwarming, uplifting tale of a child with a violently abusive alcoholic father. There was some sarcasm there, see if you can tell where.  It's a puzzle game, and you lead the Monster (the child's father) around to help you solve puzzles in an effort to eventually help him. Problem is, you can't do much to help an angry drunk who doesn't think he needs help.  The game mechanics are fairly simple to grasp, although rather neat in execution, as you physically manipulate the slums around you to advance to the next area. If you're looking for a challenging puzzler, this isn't it. It's fairly unique, but not difficult. Nothing stumped me for more than maybe 15 seconds, and there's hints in the game if you do get stuck.  And there's little dialogue. They don't hit you over the head with the abusive father thing. You know it's there, and you know it's pretty bad. It isn't preachy about its message, it's just showing you how this child deals with the crap he's going through.  All that being said, if you can get Papo & Yo for cheap, it's not a waste of your money. Don't buy it expecting a great puzzle game on par with Portal. That will lead to disappointement. Buy it expecting a bittersweet story told in a simple manner with a few entertaining puzzles along the way. That will lead to a pleasant way to spend an afternoon.

okay, negl the environment is gorgeous and immersive. despite the fact that many items are cloned and reused, there are still much more that are detailed and it really captures that era--mind u i don't know it too well. detailed letters and scraps lying around, brand designs of books/cereal/misc with labels on the front and back. so yes, they reused some things (toiler paper) but many others have a lot of writing that could be extraneous but adds to the reality. the rain & lightning creeped me out. the first time i booted up the game, i didn't want to play the game after like 10 minutes of having to turn on the lights by myself and listen to the storm. i did return eventually.  so it's not a traditional game. and it's better than you walk into this knowing that. you pick up stuff and observe them if you want. you can skip through everything and only pick up story-moving items. but i tried to get to as much as i could while leaving a mess behind. so is it a ~fun~ replayable game? i'd say no. but it is a lovely coming of age story with gal pal feels (c what i did thar) and even sister feels, with one coming home and the other explaining what led up to her leaving. so sister & home feels = slay me. gone home runs in the line of an interactive visual novel with more control than one might have in a traditional japanese VN. learning about the family background, their respective problems and their dynamic via found objects was a great way to utilize the medium (in this case GAME); so i'd actually count this as a success. it's not something for everyone; i can see how one might get bored. but i'm thinking more like how epistolary novels can have scraps of letters &c as an anthology. this does the same thing except you find the pieces by yourself, controlling via first person pov the older sister. choosing and maximizing the use of a medium is very important to me--from using pen or pencil, to whether to tell a story via commentbombs or logs--there isn't always a right answer but if you're going to go with one, do the best you can. and in gone home, i think they did a great job merging interactive play with a found object-story.  i didn't finish reading all the notes, especially the parent related ones, but i screencaped them and will double back later for sure. and as a disclaimer, i got this for cheap in a bundle. i don't know if i would buy it at full price.

Great design, fun time in poker... but filled with annoyances.  All the characters talk too much! It's one thing to have some level of small talk by your favorite characters... I mean, that's why I bought the game! Initial raise gets you a reaction comment. If you all-in, you get 5+ gasp reactions from the group, even if they've folded previously, then they'll fold 80% of the time, then you'll get a 'player win' or 'player loss' razzing from GlaDOS and whomever won or lost. Also, Sam might be playing, but Max constantly interjects... and I hate those two from the first game. The dealer randomly tosses insults you as you are dealt cards, reraise, win or lose hands... and it's almost always Claptrap at the showdown, so anyone who hates him will hate him on a 1-on-1 level.  The rewards system sucks! Original Poker Night would give you the 'reward' after you knocked out the one player w/ the item. This one has you complete 3 'challenges' to get a shot at a bounty, then you have to knock out all 4 players. One of the challenges is 'Spend 8 perfect tournament's worth of tokens to unlock the Portal set' so those challenges aren't fluff either. There's no reset for them aside from deleting your save. Some folks on the Steam Forums say they have been stuck for over a year on same challenge.   Even if you were magic, you'd have to do 10+ perfect games to get the unlocks... and they're time consuming! Until the blinds are 800/1600, these guys fold neatly on most raises like a pair of pleated pants. Oh, and despite having a SHiFT account and Borderlands 2 installed, none of the Borderlands stuff unlocked natively and I had to submit a SHiFT ticket with Gearbox. TF2 stuff unlocks W/O any hitches, as is the norm.  The game looks pretty. I hate to badmouth it... but I bought the game because I liked the characters and wanted the unlocks. Spending a ton of hours to get the unlocks, when I'd rather be in B2 or TF2, and starting to hate the characters I liked before I started playing... honestly made me very unhappy.

For version 1.0.3  Though the developers have released this from Early Access, it really should not have been. First off, the bugs:     missing image assets     broken dialog trees/subquests     spelling mistakes     crafting window which gets laggier and laggier each time it's opened.     constant shaking of the screen when left unattended for long enough (in my case, once to answer a phone call and another to deal with a someone at the door).   Beyond the bugs, the content of the game itself is surprisingly sparse. You just constantly descend without much incentive to explore other than to discover upgrade stations. You can also find NPCs and get their subquests, but even if those subquests aren't broken there's no real payoff for completing them.  At its current level, the amount of active development and polish needed before this would be worthwhile is probably measured in months, not days or weeks. I'd check back in six months to a year to see if things have gotten better or if the move out of Early Access is an indication that the dev team is abandoning the game and so might as well call it done.

app_name: Infinity Wars - Animated Trading Card Game
review_text: TL;DR Good concept and potential, but nagging problems prevent it from being good  Infinity Wars has a lot of innovative ideas: the command system, the different battlefield zones, players having both health and morale that they have to watch, but in the end the game is bogged down by a clunky progression system. It's very difficult to get new cards (two cards free per day, 3,000 IP (the free currency) for a booster pack, which takes a while to earn considering you only get a few hundred IP for a win, at most. Leveling is slow and eventually becomes a ceaseless grind at the higher levels, with no incentives or rewards other than getting a free pack every levelup (the game has 30 levels in all. I'm level 26) But the biggest problem isn't the price, it's the fact that the game offers no way to get rid of excess cards. You can include three copies of most cards at max in your deck, but the vagaries of luck and rarity have caused me to have over 10 of some common cards, and only one of others. As of this writing, I have 27 Wealthy Nobles. The game has no disenchant option, so the cards just sit there, effectively wasted IP. This also makes building your own deck difficult, as you can't get the cards you need.   I still think the game has potential and I would return if some of these things were fixed, but right now, I can't recommend it.

I'm new to the seires so I dont have as much background as others but I do have fresh eyes.  1) This game is insanely short (despite 5 hours of gameplay). Had the game been longer I would've reccomended it.  2) Gameplay is very good in there being multiple ways to do things, I pretty much always chose stealth approach and finfing backways. The robots however are insaenly overpowered, the only one I was able to kill was only because I spent about 15 minutes dodging bullets and firing bullets that did almost nothing. The shooting itself was very smooth but the guns didn't do a lot of damage, even with low level enemies it'd still take two shotgun blasts to the face usually.  3) Visuals were amazing (ignoring some odd polygons on locals that made their faces look so round they could be fruit while others were completely unaffected) and it definetly has the strong cyberpunk atmosphere it was going for.  4) Characters we really didn't see much of, I can barely tell you anything past the short backstory debriefings.  5) All the weapon upgrades are pointless, don't bother. It's difficult to get enough credits to buy one gun let alone the whole stock and the shop is useless because the game is so short there's no time to save up for let alone use the gun or weapon selection. I wasn't even able to buy half the aug upgrades the game was so short.  Overall, had the game been longer it would've been amazing. But because of it's short length a lot of the game was rendered utterly useless. I only reccomend buying if it's on a really good sale just to try it out, otherwise it really is just that much of a waste of time.

(I bought this 7 months ago on their site before the Steam release, don't let my logged play time fool you!)  During the day there is a sort of Tycoon-style farming game while you still maintain a restruant a la Cooking Mama and buy supplies to keep your restruant ahead of demand. To optimize recepies you can play a sort of Fruit Ninja-style minigame At night you go solo against zombies with nothing but veggie-powered defenses and your throwing arm to protect yourself.  After you get enough employees you can start exploring the woods for some helpful items and additional building materials. You might even argue in favor of this game being compared to Animal Crossing or The Sims in some aspects, though those games are not as goal-oriented.   When you've completed the campaign's tutorial, if you find the story boring or short you can switch to Endless Mode. Arguably the more enjoyable mode.  The depth of this game will sneak up on you.

Definitely worth the less than dollar I paid for it. Full price, probably not.  I'm not entirely sure what the point is. The story is quite weak. The gameplay is just wave after wave of enemies with a few bosses and dungeon instances thrown in. But every time you do an instance, the entire world regenerates when you come back out. If you wanted to dive into both of those dungeons that have appeared, you're out of luck. Maybe I just got lucky on my third guy, but I played for 3 hours straight and the game was laughably easy. It took less time to get the mini-bosses (which you encounter after every handful of waves and at the end of dungeons) down to 0 HP than it did for the game to do the death animation (they have a drawn out death animation where lightning strikes all over and an angelic choir sings and the boss goes 'AAAAAAAHHHHHHHHH!' for like 10 seconds).  The gameplay is fun, but it's nothing we haven't seen before. The 'rouge-like' aspect of it is a stretch. You just lose some of your stuff. 'With RPG Elements' means there's XP, Leveling, Skill Points, and an inventory. The inventory is confusing. The control scheme is weird and inconsistent (F is the action button? But sometimes you have to press Enter... Wat?). Um... the good stuff about it is that I sank a bunch of time into it without really feeling it very much (except for the 'too easy' bit).  It seems quite a bit of content is missing from the base game. Evidently, the DLC portions of the game are each worth at least 50% of the base game cost even though it doesn't seem like each adds 50% more game. I haven't purchased them so take that with a grain of salt. There's enough wrong with the base game that it didn't seem worth it to dish out $10 (full price) for DLC (that's more than the game!).  The multiplayer was down and I didn't want to make an account with these people, so I didn't experience that aspect of it. Maybe multiplayer is where the game really shines, but from what I played the game just wasn't compelling enough for me to want to keep giving it my time. For that reason, and because of it's faults, I can't recommend you buy it at full price.  5/10 because I want my roguelikes to kill me. And because it lacks polish where it counts (balance, core game systems, multiplayer servers...).

I regret that I did not ask for a refund earlier.

app_name: Tales of Monkey Island: Chapter 1 - Launch of the Screaming Narwhal
review_text: If you've never been either a fan, or even interested in Monkey Island, Tales still makes a pleasant introduction to the series. Guybrush Threepwood, mighty pirate, is played over five episodic adventures. The gameplay involves pointing, clicking, laughing, and doing many interesting puzzles as you go along trying to solve the mystery of the Pirate Pox. The Tales games remember what it is to be humorous and how to paint a world of colorful characters. Guybrush offers commentary throughout the entire game, and in a way serves as sort of a companion with the player rather than the player merely assuming his position. If you've ever wanted to evade a French scientist, challenge a pirate to a mean face competition, or create counterfeit action figures, this is the series for you.

Last few months I have gotten really bored of Hearthstone. This has been an acceptable second. [Old Review July 2015] So I installed last night, did the first campaign. After that I was sitting there with what felt like a thumb up my a**. It couldn't connect to servers. After one hour of uptime. Ok not really an issue. Oh wait, I can't do anything, and if I do manage to continue the quests, they don't get saved. [I do understand that keeping all the data up on their servers is anti-cheating/farming/security/whatever. And that is admiral and so forth.]  How on earth can a company not realize stress load in this day and age for a product they KNOW will be massively used. Hell they even have metrics and usage from all the previous messages. Seriously, pull your finger out. Get better engineers/technical program managers or such. This crap shouldn't have happened. I thought we had gone past the days of launch day taking down the product. This now looks like amateur hour.   [End Rant] The game itself was very enjoyable. The 'learning' bits were extremely basic, and to anyone who has played a single game of magic, would be pretty damn boring. But I was afraid to ignore them in case I lost out on gold :P First campaign was short, white weenie deck was fun. Storyline was nothing to write home about, but that has never mattered in Magic. Im looking forward to playing more.  The interface seemed pretty crisp. Menus were pretty much what they said on the tin, unlike the unmitigated car crash of Duels 2015. Load times were relatively ok. Could be faster, but could be an absolute disaster like Duels 2015. So when they figure out their network/processing issues, Ill be playing a good bit of this. That being said opening day was a disaster, even after they pushed back the release!  Recommendations: Game seems good. Technical incompetence screwing what is probably the best Duels to date.

Alone Grim Fandango is a testament to the genius behind a production that can craft such an award winning formula on a pocket change budget, yet to have their project stand the test of time near two decades later is equally as marvellous.

app_name: DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin
review_text: Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin is my first experience with Dark Souls II. I played Dark Souls: Prepare to Die extensively, with 200 hrs on it. The first thing that I noticed about DS II: SotFS is the difficulty.   To me, DS II:SotFS is very difficult in the early game. The AI is very good, and the tracking on enemies' attacks are very good.   I started as a Depraved Undead, which may have not been the most easy class to choose for the early game. Starting at Lvl 1, He is the lowest level of the classes on start up. His stats are average and the equipment he can effectively use at Lvl 1 is very limited. He does have available to him an item that allows him to ``respec`` or respecialize his statistics later, which can be helpful when deciding to ``reroll`` your character if you have spent some attribute points mistakenly.  Starting class aside, the reason why I found the game difficult than its predecessors was melee combat.   I would like to take a moment to discuss the various changes in combat.   Melee striking is pretty much the same. There are different animations of course for swinging your weapons around, including some new additions to weapons that you might have grown accustomed to using. I am still discovering new weapons as I have not quite finished my play through. In fact I believe that I am far from completing it actually, but I think my experiences are worth sharing.   Damage is a little bit different. Divine damage is no longer (?) as it seems to have been merged into LIT damage. I have not seen any indicator of DIV damage in the game. Magic / Fire / Lightning / Dark is still here. Pyromancy / Dark can now scale with INT / FTH. Poison seems to do much greater damage than in previous games, while Bleed seems to do less than it did before. However, Bleed also damages Max Endurance now and stamina regeneration, among a host of other things, other than straight damage. It has more of a combat debuff feel to it now.   Everything hurts really bad. This goes without saying to anyone who has any experience with the Dark Souls franchise. With Light Armor on, one should play extremely cautious, as multiple enemy groups are common and numerous. Expect to get stunned very easily. Shields help reduce the damage, but starting shields (the ones you get for free) do not reduce 100% physical, so it is best to purchase one that has at least 90% reduction, until you can find something better or unless you are practicing parrying with a smaller shield.   Poise can be gained through attribute points now, so that's a bonus. :D  Parrying is much harder, imo. While many of the attacks are parryable in Dark Souls:SotFS, it is unlikely to parry them without a shield / offhand that is meant to do so. The parry frames have been adjusted for each offhand item such that Kite Shields (Regular Shields) are FAR WORSE at parrying than they were in previous games. The parry frames have a delay on these shields and I have not the patience to even learn to parry things anymore in my short time with the game, nor has it hindered me.   I have gotten far with my 100% physical blocking shield and stamina management alone, and along with rolling every odd attack or two, I believe it to be a non issue outside of PvP.  Ranged aiming is better. Using items for combat is about the same (limited throwing distance). Throwing weapons have greater damage and bows have greater damage. Ammo can be expensive and hard to stay stocked up on. If you are relying on Bows a lot, it is best to gain access to Steady Hand McDuff and his bonfire. He has unlimited supply of iron arrows. These are my preferred choice of arrow atm and for 30 souls per, they are not too expensive.   Rolling is a little bit different than Dark Souls in the way that your weight adjusts something of a slider, instead of going from Fast / Normal / Slow as you would in previous installments. Now there is varying degrees of Fast / Medium / Slow Rolling speeds. The variance lies within the distance rolled and the speed. Test it out in game to find your preference.   Rolling has a lot fewer invincibility frames in this installment, imho, without upgraded Adaptability. I have yet to see a significant increase in invincibility frames through upgrading my ADP, and can only say that learning to use roll and using it at the right times (just before the swing) is very important here to avoid in taking damage.   *UPDATE* I had a brief look at the Dark Souls Wiki and discovered that I had 85 AGI, which is lowest.. I had 6 ADP. For a DEX build this is really bad, as I am reliant on staying mobile and avoiding damage. So I am currently boosting my ADP to about 30. That should be enough as it will increase my invincibility frames by about 250%. There is a chart explaining it here:  http://darksouls2.wiki.fextralife.com/Stats  . Videos on the AGI page as well.  */END*  I really cannot comment on anything relating to spells. I haven't cast any spells yet in game, and probably won't for quite some time. I have had spells cast on me by invading NPCs (of which there are many!) and they seem to function quite well. I will have to update later if / when I do have more experience with the spells.   One thing I can say is that I do miss KICKING THINGS. From Software has decided to replace it with a bashing motion. It interrupts shield block, but has none of the knock back effect of kicking. :'(  Oh yes, backstabbing and critical hits. They are still very effective when you can pull them off. However the enemy tracking does not allow you to easily backstab every mob in the game anymore. I can see why they wanted to change it and it's a healthy change overall. As for critical hits, I can't comment too much but it seems like a smaller hitbox. Its always been a small hitbox, but it seems like a minor adjustment in the enemies' position can make the hitbox miss very easily. I am not a fan considering how hard it is to parry things now.  But I am sure it will just take some time getting used to.   That's all I can say for combat. As for the rest of the game..   The voice acting is very good, level design is good, graphics are superb.. I am really satisfied with the game overall.   Movement has changed a bit (I was expecting this), however remains comfortable. Camera is a bit awkward at times, and I would warn everyone to STAY AWAY FROM LEDGES. I have fallen off of quite a few of them already.. The slopes are very slippery and I would advise to just stay away from them, when ever possible.  I like that Bonfires allow you to Fast Travel, although there is something to be said for walking a distance. I became very familiar with the area around the FireLink Shrine in Dark Souls, and found a strange comfort in traversing that area. I will say though, that the music around Majula is absolutely terrible and boring. Sorry Majula music lovers. :/  I feel as though adding more of the manual shortcuts like the elevators from Undead Parish and FireLink Shrine would have been a better idea to incorporate than teleporting, but teleporting saves time and is somewhat essential now that the Undead Protagonist cannot Level Up at any bonfire (He must talk to the FireKeeper Maiden in Majula for that).  (TL;DR)   Great Game, Worth your money, Just buy it. You can always make more money later. ;)

Exactly what it says on the tin. 20 minutes of JRPG from start to end credits. Some setup, some voiced dialogue (Charming amateur work.), some nice art, and one boss fight. It's all quite easy.  Just turn off active time battle. You don't know these characters so you'll have to check their skills.  You can change two party members, so you might want to replay to see what different plot there is.  Still, the whole thing is incredibly trivial. A lot of effort went into the art, voice work, etc, and everything is certainly workmanlike, but there's nothing all that interesting here. Fun enough for a freebie though.  In reflection, though, I think that is actually something profound. Look how rare it is for a game to have the monster ACTUALLY BE A MONSTER that must die. How rare it is for the heroes to actually save the day from a monster. There's no twist where you were the monster all along. No stupid navel gazing about two so gray so grimdark factions and having to pick blah blah.  When you get down to that, a game that promises heroes slaying a monster and saving the day and delivers exactly that... that is kinda rare these days, isn't it?

My my, friend; you're in an odd part of steam now aren't you? Welcome to Risen, a truly mystifying game by our friends Piranha Bytes.  See, I have a weird stiffy for ♥♥♥♥♥♥ adventure RPGs like Risen. I've played some of Gothic 3, I've watched lets plays of Two Worlds, I've experienced babby's first OHS(first Witcher), and I've a past time of exploiting physics glitches in the elder scrolls series.  So I'll be rating by five categories: Graphics, Leveling, Ambience, Gameplay, and Story   :First up, Greficks:  Visually, Risen is appealing and fully cranked has nice material effects on the items you wear. Everything has texturing and react well to ambient light. For example when you finally get an iron helmet it shines like it was really worked over with a hammer at a forge. Whoah, it's like we're playing a moden game! Some of the models look samey but there are enough human models and reskins of those models to make most characters you meet seem like they're different people. Monsters sometimes literally are green versions of other models with maybe a horn plastered on. meh  :Second, Leveling:  Leveling is done unexpectedly well in this game. When I say leveling I mean the developers putting monsters along paths that subtly coralle you to the way you're supposed to go. It's terrific because this negates the need for different difficulty levels. At the beginning gnomes gave me trouble and Sea Vultures fatigued me greatly and Ghouls hand delivered dread to my soul with their op shield breaking frost attacks. This isn't a game where you just run into weapons and armor like it's growing on trees, every weapon you replace your old one with is well earned, or bought for an exorbitant sum. I played a melee sword n board character for my first time through and when I beat the ♥♥♥♥ out of Brogar and took his spiked mace I felt like a badass to end all badasses then I got my butt opened fighting 10 grave moths in the same area.  But that's the thing, it's a game of raising the level of good that you are with whatever you're using to kill stuff. Later, monsters will use advanced tecniques like strafing and counterblocking. Not just 4 chain hits like the spiked rats do when you're barely level 2. The map opens up in piecemeal because of this and imo gives a very satisfying rate of progression because you can go anywhere(mostly) at any time but good luck killing that ogre with a stick you found on the ground m80. B)  Also, you learn new tecniques as well, but you actually have to go learn them. When you level up you get Learning Points that you can use -along with gold- to get a trainer to teach you new stuff. Like reloading c'bows faster or more chains for your sword combo. It's freakin cool and prevents all-rounders but allows for some cool multiclass builds. Like my character besides being buffman mcswordswing could brew potions. So I brewed 50 str pots and killed thick jaw sausage fingers in 3 hits it was a very big letdown honestly.  Save often.  :Third, Ambience:  This is a weird category but I feel like I need to say something about it. The reason I didn't lump with with graphics is because you can have ambience without good graphics I mean just look at morrowind. The lighting in this game is done gorgeously, the colors are perfect for their respective areas that lends a real feeling of being in a swamp, or a muggy little town, or on a fortress laden road build into a huge cliffside at dusk. It's very pretty. Roads are winding in places where you'd expect and wide like u're mum in others. Foliage is used well to hide things and there are some cozy little areas that with a keen eye and a levitation spell you can get to(I'm looking at you, Doyle!)  :Fourth, Gameplay:  Risen is an odd beast; it's not floopy and doofy like Gothic, it's strangely stoic and awkward. The animations are stilted and make Bethesda's look decent. I thought I was playing a game from 2009 not Runescape 2. Like Runescape though they have a certain charm about them. For example, you can break down certain walled off doorways later in the game with a pickaxe. The swings you make look like they couldn't tickle yer mum let alone break down a huge stone door, but it makes me smile when the pieces of door explode from their berth like you used a depthcharge and not a sternly worded pickaxe tap. Idle dialogue animations are hilarious and make it look like you're watching a school play animated by todd howard.  :Fifth, Story:  Here's the kicker for me, honestly. Though before I go into the story proper I'd like to mention the character you play. He's the biggest bumbler I've ever had as a main character. His dialogue, his stature, animations, dying noises, it all contributes to the image of a man that has no idea what he's doing. Even when I had my strength at 166 and could wield a two hander with a shield I never felt like I was some cooked up hero. I felt like I was learning along with this guy, that beating the impossible just took a few hours of learning the counter-counterblock.  The story though, left me deflated. Honestly most of this game seems like the devs stumbled into cool mechanics and ideas but they got no breaks with the main story points. The ending was abysmal and left me wanting when it dumped me back at the main screen with no aftergame to ♥♥♥♥ about in with my op quest armor. The villains that were hyped up were pushovers and honestly the grunts gave me the most trouble. Honestly I think I died most to wild animals out of everything that killed me.   The good thing is, that there's replayability. I barely touched anything relating to magic except near the endgame where you're forced to use levitation and telekinesis spells but you have scrolls for that and if you're a fighter you have a bunch of mana pots you're not using anyway.    Overall, I recommend Risen for the journey and goofiness along the way to the major plot points because the peaks in the story are very uninspired and full of mcguffins. The ending is ♥♥♥♥, just like the conclusion of this review. I'll probably play it again though.

My First Play on Launch Day & Review Video:  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hAcUxJu7oGc   Bah, this game defines EXACTLY the reasons that people seem to despise RPG maker games that continue to flood Steam. Even at $1.49, it makes you feel like the developer is laughing all the way to the bank. Too many things make absolutely no sense 45 minutes into the game, which was much further then I wanted to play this. Boring, uninspired, senseless plot makes this project feel like the developer rushed to push out a game for Steam to try and make a quick dollar, err or $1.49.   I give the game credit for some pretty good random scares, sound effects or monsters that dart wildly in front of or behind you can surprise you. However this just feels like a tacked on gimmick to try and convince you this game is worthy of a good RPG maker game.. it's NOT! To the Moon is a great RPG maker game, this is just more RPG Maker 'muck' that leaves you shaking your head and asking 'why?' over and over.  My score is a 24%, even at the price tag of $1.49. I want more then my $1.49 back, how can I request a refund of my TIME as well through Steam?!?  Stay away, even at 90% off and 14 cents, this game isn't worth it. Sorry dev.. try again pal, and next time don't try to push out a game in a week, eh?

At the time of writing this review I have almost logged 800 hours.  EVE Online where do I start with this game. EVE Online is one of those games that if you love it or hate it you will have a story to go with it. If you are thinking about getting into EVE because you saw a video on youtube or heard about a battle that caused more than a thousand plus U.S. dollars (or equal world value) and thought 'wow I want to do that too'. Well hate to burst your bubble but that does not happen alot, infact fights like that only happen once or twice a year if you are lucky. Your best bet on what your experience is going to be is that you are going to be doing your thing and then get killed by someone that has been playing the game for years. Now what you can do is join a player driven group called a corperation or corp. (FYI you will be asked LOTS of questions when your are joining a corp) When you manage to join a corp it will open up more opertunitys for you, but the main one will be the other corps that have formed an alliance with the corp that you have just joined. Then with that alliance (expecally in null space 0.0 to -1.0) will generally have a set space that they live in and fight in, but im getting to far in, lets see if I can atleast help you find a starting point. There are many things that you can do in EVE but I will try to narrow it out for you.  Some of the things that you can do in EVE is be a market traider, a miner, an industrialist, a pvp'er, a pve'er, a explorer, or a pirate.  A market traider is about as strate forward as it sounds, you would buy low sell high in stations in order to make a proft.  A miner in my oppinion is the backbone of EVE Online. As a miner what you would do is go to astroid belts and mine them for their minerals and sell them on the market. Why I call the miner the backbone of of EVE is because they provide the materials needed to make EVERYTHING in EVE. CCP does not add anything into the market in EVE except the skills needed to make an item and a BPO(Blue Print Original) of an item.  An industrialist in EVE takes the minerals that the miner has gathered and turn it into items that other players use on their space ships.  A PVP'er and PVE'er are basically the same thing execpt for what kind of enemy you fight. A PVP'er fights other Players in the game, while a PVE'er fights randomly generated NPC's (aka Rats). Both of these paths does earn you ISK (InterSteller Kredits as Scott Manly puts it). ISK has no exact meaning, its just the in-game currency.  An explorer is an interesting one. What they do is use special moduals to scan locations down and hack the location to get the items stored in it. An explorer is also the one who finds the NPC faction BPC's(Blue Print Copy). An faction item is generally better than the T2 version of an item/ship.  Now a Pirate is someone that everyone in EVE just about hates to deal with. Just as it sounds a Pirate is someone who kills players just because they can. Plus with Pirate corps they generally serve no alligence to others, one day they are fighting by your side, and the next day they are trying to kill you, but thats EVE for ya.  Now there are other things that you can do in EVE, but I tried to name the things that I think are important in EVE.  Now in order to play EVE you need a subscription, and it costs about 19 dollars (U.S.). Now you can buy PLEX (Pilots Lisence EXtenction) though it does cost about 800 mill ISK at the time of writing this, and you do need one PLEX a month to keep your subscription going.   Now saying if I recommend this game I would say atleast try the free trial first then decide if you want to spend money on it, and at least give it 2 or more months untill you can say if you like it or not. I mostly do PvP and it took me about 2 months untill I started to get good at it.   You can get EVE Online on steam or on the website its self. I play EVE on steam only because it was easyier for me to pay for my subscrition, but I will say this if you do decide to get EVE on steam you will have to deal with steam sometimes not connecting to your EVE account, but with the year I have been playing EVE this only happend twice too me.  As one of my corp buds once said 'the best way to learn PvP is to fit LOTS cheap frigates and keep throwing your-self in fleet fights untill you get it.'

Steam Workshop would have been great... -Bad netcode: do you know the feeling of waking up from bed, only to realize it was just a dream and find yourself back in bed? Yeah, same feeling. I would not recommend this game if you plan on playing online, friends included. -(Keyboard) controls: everytime I launch the game my mapped controls go back to default. Also I have yet to know which button is used to select options in the pause screen. Controls during gameplay are fine, though. -It's supposed to be a competitive game, but it is overall so shallow, monotonous... It makes you close the game AND wish it could have more content at the same time.

If there was ever a game to be placed on a mantle and said, 'This is a peice of art.', this would be my choice for that game. While it is a third-person isometric hack-and-slash, it is a good looking one, with a great soundtrack, and good voice acting, as well as a well tied together story... and, to my knowledge, one of the few games with a NewGame+ mode that has said mode being seen as a canon option; yes, starting over with all your unlocks can be considered an event in the story, that is how well wrote (or how well thinking the dialogue was wrote) the game is. I like the game, but you'll have a hell of a time getting me to play it. It's fun but tedious and kinda dickish, really. The absolute reliance on complete chance results in 'INFINITE REPLAYABILITY' on paper, but in practice it turns into '♥♥♥♥ING REALLY?'. It shouldn't be as bad as it sounds, but with more absolute ♥♥♥♥ items than worthwhile items, there's about a 75% chance of you getting kicked in the balls by something useless or of minimal value, which is then followed up by yet another item of absolutely no purpose. The other 25% of the time you'll be thanking *INSERT FAVORITE DIETY/MYTHICAL CREATURE/ELDER GOD HERE* that you finally got something good, which leaves you completely open for a double kick in the balls. I'd probably hate this less if there wasn't so much ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ content to go through, as well. There's an absurd amount of stuff to fight, obtain, etc, but the chances you'll ever get to it without an absolute devotion to the game are slim to nil, really. This game hates you and wants you to die and to never play it again, and the only way to extract any amount of joy from it is to either be a really lucky ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ or have an absurd amount of time to burn.

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: When I first started Dark Souls I could hardly change my weapon without pausing myself and looking down at the controller. And now I can figure my own strategies against bosses, learn new things on the go and ENJOY the super-hard-♥♥♥-game-that-will-question-your-sanity. I think I died more than a million times, failed more times I can even remember, made mistakes - but the important thing is that you LEARN from them. 'A mistake repeated is a choice.' As for beginners, the game might seem impossible. You might throw your controller away, shut the computer and not play for few months. But trust me, you want to go back. You want to pass, you want to succeed. You want to find out more. And it's all WORTH IT. (Pro tip: git gud) (Another pro tip: sleep on it. I defeated most bosses just by sleeping and when I woke up my brain was all set and it just happened.) Dark Souls: Prepare to die is truly living up to it's name. Definately the most mysterious game I've played (doesn't say much but...). It doesn't tell you anything. But, lucky for you, there are people who want to help. So praise the sun! 'Clearing' this game in around 80 h on my first try was cool. I felt so proud. Now I want to do it again. There are so many things you can do, so many characters you can meet, maybe even save.... opportunities are endless. Just be wary of the dark...

Are you a fan of the ol' days on Diablo 1 and 2? Then read this:  ---   Okay, well I'm not going to say it's a bad game, but it's far from good. I'm a HUGE ARPG fan, and of course like most of you, started in the Diablo days. After getting bored of the grind in Diablo 3, I went to PoE and that was great for 1 month, but then that started to wear down and thus lead me to Grim Dawn.  Here's what I like and dislike about Grim Dawn:   +++Positives:  +Great artwork & design  +Runs very well even on low-spec PC's   ---Negatives:  -No community (Yeah I know you can find players to play with, but no global chats, no trade chats, nothing)  -Skill system and stat system is not exciting (I normally get excited leveling up to use new skills. Grim Dawn just doesn't seem to do that for me. Skill tree feels quite bland)  -AI isn't on point (The mobs don't offer much of a challenge. Bosses seem a little too easy)  -PVP Disappointing (As a huge fan of the crazy havoc Diablo 2 offered in terms of PVP and the toxic community that came with it, I am disappointed to see no effort made for PVP on Grim Dawn as well as the other top ARPG's)  -Pathing and movement (I found the pathing of the characters was not as smooth as the other ARPG games. Especially when going up and down narrow sets of stairs)  -Story line is flat (I found myself mostly skipping all dialogue interaction with NPC's just to hurry up and progress forward. What happened to those amazing cinematic days like in Diablo 2 and the amazing voice overs?)  -Just feels like a single player game (I want to be emersed in an online world, especially when it's the type of game which requires grinding for gear pieces and so fourth. Playing with 1-3 other players is just not much of a multiplayer game).   ----   So I guess with more negative's than positives, I'd have to say I don't recommend this game for those who have the same types of expectations and needs in a type of game like this. They need to put in a lot more work before I can actually even casually play this.

Brink is one of the better squad-based FPSs to come out within the last year. The gunplay and character controls feel spot-on, and they're married with an elegant teamplay mechanic and fantastic graphic and audio design. Although the parkour is woefully underused by the players, mastery of this mechanic brings Brink up to one of my favorite shooters of all time. The parkour mechanic, like the Battlefield franchise's Destruction mechanic, will become genre-defining with time. Other shooters now feel incomplete without it.  Unfortunately, there are some under-the-hood issues with matchmaking and high-end graphic settings. Brink is also a complete mess on the consoles. Despite these flaws, I still have more fun with Brink than I do with all other shooters save BFBC2.

TL;DR: If you're the type that can admire a well-crafted and stylish world over flawless action, then I recommend you pick up Remember Me.    I just completed a blind playthrough of Remember Me. I had never heard of the game before and only picked it up because it was included in the most recent Humble Bundle. I'm very pleased because I was able to enjoy a game that I otherwise would have completely skipped over.   From the futuristic Neo-Paris penthouses to the dark and blood-streaked slums, Remember Me is quite aesthetically appealing. There is only one HUD element and it remains hidden unless in combat. The special effects and soundtrack match exceptionally well to the world. The game has unique style and an impressive amount of attention to detail. It is rare for an action game to be so focused on ambience and aesthetics. That focus is why Remember Me was so enjoyable.  The gameplay itself has some shortcomings, though. The camera can be frustrating at times and you're limited to about four combinations. There is some variability with how the combos can be set up, but largely you'll be mashing X/Y and dodging relentlessly. The game is also very linear. There is no exploration other than the occasional dead-end detour hiding a collectible. Finally, the unskippable cutscenes are a travesty of proper game design.   The combat is redeemed through a few clever elements. There is a feature where you can reconfigure your combos to heal, reduce cooldowns, or strike more powerfully. There are also activated abilities called S-Pressens which have varying effects such as turning invisible, stunning enemies, or causing a large explosion. The ranged weapon also adds some utility and combat value. Enemies throughout the game (and several bosses) might require a mixture of all the above.   I would rather emphasize the characteristics that make a game memorable than lament a combat system that is otherwise forgettable. I really enjoyed Remember Me. While I wouldn't pay full price for the game, it was an absolute steal at bundle/sale price.

I really wanted to like this game, but as it is now, I wouldnt recommend it to anyone but very hardcore rpg fans willing to put up with all its problems  pros :   -The character development is very vast and well done, it truly feels like a real rpg -From what i've seen, the story and the world seems pretty solid -Soundtrack is really good, graphics are ok  cons : -The control system is incredibly frustrating. combat is messy and a single missclick (which happens a lot) might mean your demise. The tactical camera doesnt help much. - Loading and saving takes forever. Coupled with the frequency of dieing due to random encounters makes for a very frustrating experience -There is no hint at all on what to do to progress a quest. While I can see how this appeal to hardcore rpg fans, truth is you will spend hours wandering around talking with the same people over and over trying to figure out what to do. - During combat, there is a huge amount of ground effects that can kill your characters in three steps. Coupled with the ankward controls this makes the experience for melee characters a nightmare - The in-party dialogues turn out to be a simple choice of what option to pick and are often single liners. In theory each option you choose gives different benefits but as the benefits are minimal and theres no clear way to distinguish between options, the system feels incomplete. Doesnt help the fact that there are only two recruitable companions who talk.   All in all seems to me a game that needs a few more months of polishing

La-Mulana is one of the greats as far as metroidvanias go. In this remake of the original freeware game from 2005 La-Mulana you play as Professor Lemeza Kousgi who sets off on an adventure to explore the fabled ruins of La-Mulana. Be warned though: this game is tricky. Even the best players might find themselves referencing guides on occasion. It's no small wonder why people call it the “Dark Souls” of 2D platformers.  In true Metroidvania fashion you collect collect mystical objects from throughout the ruins. These can be anything from new weapons to new abilities to something that just makes it a little easier to survive. With every treasure you collect you feel yourself grow a little stronger, and it's a great feeling to behold. Conquering challenges you never thought possible and scaling to new heights and exploring new areas feels great. Did I mention the music? A++ music... in fact even if you're not getting the game I suggest you go to a website called “Bandcamp” right now and look up “La-Mulana”. You won't be disappointed, trust me.  There are some complaints I had about the game though. Sometimes the puzzles and platforming sections just feel “Cheap”. Like the answer to a puzzle was so obscure you HAD to look up the answer to get past. Or you HAD to die on that trap at least once (or be able to see the future) to avoid it. The controls are stiff as well... but reasonable, and once you learn what you can and can not do Lemeza becomes a lot easier to control.   Overall I would say this game is a must have for anyone looking for a challenge or any fans of metroidvanias. The puzzles are hard, the platforming is brutal, the bosses are relentless, and the adventure is fun from beginning to end... and it was worth every second and every penny paid.

I really enjoyed amost every aspect of SoM. The combat runs smoothly and the parkour running very rarely has an issue (managed to jump outside the map once... That was interesting) The story lines are great and engaging though they feel a little slow and at times I wondered why I had to cross the map to go meet the same person I was just with when we could have just travelled together - Looking at you here Torvin.   The boss fights are oddly easier than the normal captains due to it just being mostly FX but the captains make up for this by occassionally being the toughest mother F**.... ever.  The internal politics of the Orcs is amusing to watch, seeing the orc that's set himself up to become war chief being overlooked for a newbie captain is fun to see. The complexity of the nemesis system binds into this - remember to chop the captain's heads off. You don't need to fight the same captain 3 times in 5 minutes like I did. -.-  I did like the mystery as to who the wraith was, but having a wealth of LOTR knowledge coupled with an overwhelming need to pick up every artifact and ithildin in the game meant, I worked out who he was well before it was revealed.   The game doesn't seem to have any major glitch issues that I've seen other than the afore mentioned out the map glitch which I was able to fix with a shadow strike kill so it wasn't a major issue.  Overall a 10/10 game whilst short it's intensely enjoyable with what seems like a fair chunk of replayability. Along with the trials you can get competitive with your friends - till they complain you have better runes than them and all the abilites unlocked.

One of the most satisfying gaming experiences I've had in a awhile. Xbox 360 gamers may have played the indie game Miner Dig Deep, and this game is very much like that, only with somewhat more focused gameplay and a faster pace. This isn't a minecraft style game or anything like that, but rather a simple, straightforward sort of action game where you dig for ore, sell it, get better equipment, and just keep going deeper. Eventually the game gets harder and slightly more complex in that getting around and getting all of the ore and Orbs (a special currency) begins to take a bit of smarter planning to get. While there are straightforward goals (keep digging until you reach a room that has your next major upgrade), the levels also have randomly placed 'puzzle rooms' where you have to use your abilities to survive or figure out a challenge to gain access to some Orbs and valuable ore. Extremely well crafted and paced just right so that it feels like every time you think you've mastered a particular part of a level, as you go deeper it throws more difficulty curves at you. I can't recommend it enough. I should point out my whole game time was just over 7 hours to find all the ore, orbs, completing every room and buying every upgrade.

Note: time played information missing because we've been playing this via my roommate's Steam library.  A barebones but fun couch game. There isn't a lot of content, but it's fast and hectic without feeling unmanageable, and the content stretches much farther than the few minutes estimated by a some of the reviewers. We've played a few several-hour sessions, and it's still a blast. The VVVVVV-esque gravity switching gives the game some unique flavor, and it controls very tightly.  The game probably doesn't hold up as well as some bigger-budget party games, but its gravity-swap mechanics give it its own flavor and it easily merits the tiny asking price. At time of writing, it's on sale for $1.24, and I'd recommend it to anyone with a couch gaming setup for that price.

If you're a fan of point and click adventure games Dropsy is a must have! I would highly recommend this colorful hugventure to anyone who is a fan of the genre.  At a frist glance this game may look quite strange with an even stranger protagonist, but over time Dropsy the clown will grow on you, and you'll find that despite his looks he's just a lovable person that wants nothing more but to help. The more I played, the more I found a place in my heart for the misunderstood clown. It's the kind of game that really teaches that looks can be deceiving, and really follows an overall theme of acceptence of others no matter what walk of life they come from, as Dropsy wanders about trying to hug as many people as possible.  As far as gameplay goes I found the puzzles to be challenging, but not so much that I couldn't figure out what I was doing after wandering around for a little while. I got stuck a few times, but I still managed to figure things out myself with out having to refer to a guide. Other puzzles can be fairly straight forward, and a good amount are side quests that end with a hug. Even though the side quests aren't nessicary I still felt compelled to hug as many people as I possibly could. There's something so warm and fuzzy about it that I just adore!  The graphics are wonderful to look at, pixilated, bright, and colorful. I really dig the art style, and although it can be very cherry, it can also be just as creepy and brooding when it needs to be. There is some creepy/disturbing imagery in this game so if you're a fan of spooky scary things you'll love this, and if you're not don't worry the game mainly focuses on the bright and cheery as opposed to the morbid. I for one adore it, but I do love me some horror.  I don't want to talk to much about the story, but the basic premise is that a during a circus preformence a fire was started burning the big top down and claiming a few lives. The media suspects Dropsy might have been the cause and most of the town hates him as a result. You do what you can to help others, while unveiling mysteries along the way. From happy, to tragic. There's also no text for this game, all of the dialog is done through symbols and pictures that appear in word bubbles. I was able to easily understand all of it, and only had a few that made me scratch my head. It's conveyed very nicely. Anyway that's all I want to say on that I don't want to spoil a lot, you really should experience it for yourself.  Last and certainly not least, in my opinion, the music of this game is supurb! I'm literally about to buy the soundtrack right now as soon as I'm finished writing this review. It adds to the overall atmosphere to the game, and I found myself getting lost in it while playing. It can be calm and relaxing, unnerving and uncomfortable, uplifting and at the same time meloncholy. I'm not music expert so take this with a grain of salt, but I believe the genre is sort of Jazzy? There's also piano, guiter, and forms of electronic music. I suggest playing with headphones.  That's about all I have to say. I'll just continue to hammer it in, if you're a fan of point and click adventures, seriously, PICK THIS UP! You won't regret it. If you haven't played point and click adventure games in the past, and you're looking to try something new, I would still reccommend this game. It might be a little more difficult for a first timer, but just remember to explore as much as you can, and investigate signs and posters. The symbol speech does convey what you need to do well, so you shouldn't have a hard time with it.   Thanks for reading!

I've heard smany are having issues launching this game; if that is the case then this is another story entirely, but this game is everything it promised to be. The multiplayer was reported to be nonexistent however there has been a lot of server stress and a few bugs so it is inconclusive to say the developers are liars. I have been following the developers for years now and feel confident that there is a legitimate excuse for every issue mentioned. This game's hype was generated by the community not the developers. It is the community's fault for not seeing that it is a small indie game directed towards a specific type of gamers: gamers that like exploration. Sean Murray has said many times that the game isn't for everyone yet everyone was interested in the game. As far as the actual gameplay goes I played the game for 9 of the first 10 hours after release and found only one minor bug. It plays well, and if you slow down and enjoy your surroundings you will find it is a very attractive looking game with very theme based and unique art. If you are like me, then you can get quite a few hours for your 60 bucks. If you are like many others, than you can follow the main 'questline' and get a roughly 15 hours (?) out of the game. But for many I would not recommend spending the money unless you are really interested; you just have to ask yourself what type of player you are. Are you the person who does only the main quest in fallout games? Or do you explore every nook and cranny? This game's core is exploration, it was intended for players to go planet to planet exploring the unique flora and fauna; if you don't think you'll enjoy that then don't buy it.

app_name: DYNASTY WARRIORS 8: Xtreme Legends Complete Edition
review_text: I love killing millions of Chinese people as I dominate the country. Simple, button mashing combat, and lame levels but I can't get enough of this series.

First, the pros of this game: the graphics are honestly beautiful (there were moments when I paused simply to enjoy the scenery), the story is sweet and enjoyable (you get pieces of the story as you advance, and you are just as interested as the in-game audience), and the general atmosphere of the game is pleasant (no violence, and any strange-looking characters don't stay around long).  The cons of this game: the mechanics can be wonky; if you overshoot your jump or mistime a grapple, you may be repeating a section over and over and over; the death/fall scenes are pretty lengthy and cannot be skipped; the checkpoints are not always close together, so you may have to start rather far back (multiple times).  If you are not good at these types of parkour and/or physics-based games that involve timing your jumps/grapples very well, or if you get frustrated having to repeat decently long sections multiple times if you fail, then you will not like this game. All the positive reviews led me to believe it would be a simple, enjoyable game, but it was so frustrating at times that I did not actually enjoy it.

A gorgeous, uniquely creative fantasy 4x game from the Endless Space studio.  Endless Legend takes all the good bits of Civ, cuts away the extraneous, archaic crap and infuses it with bold new ideas and ingenuity to make a fresh, frankly superior, game. Your military units are content to occupy the same patch of land because, well, we aren't trogloytes here on Auriga. Every city you settle claims a whole region as your territoty, so no scumbag rivals insert themselves between your cities in an effort to screw with you. Nevertheless, you may want to think about making contact with the natives before sitting yourself in their back yard.  And the factions are unique. Utterly unique. They don't just have a unique unit or two and some different stat bonuses, they all have completely different playstlyes. As an example, the Roving Clans are a mercantile faction who can't declare war on anyone and get to move their cities around on giant beetles, whereas the Necrophages are evil bug dudes who can't engage in peace treaties and actually EAT their enemies to get food for their cities! Unit customisation, minor faction mechanics and the handling of quest objectives are all other brilliant ideas that work harmoniously to create a fun gameplay experience.  There are a few bugs with multiplayer still. My friend and I are often unable to reload saved multiplayer games thanks to some unusual desync issues. Despite this we've been trying to get games of Endless Legend in as often as we can.  If you can get past a little lack of polish (they've done remarkably well with so small a team), Endless Legend is a fantastic strategy game that breathes fresh air into an increasingly stale genre. Keep an eye on Amplitude Studios.

I bought this game as a backer on Kickstarter, and let me tell you money well spent. The first chance I got to play it I was up all night playing this. This is crazy addictive if you are into the mechanics of a car. The auction is probably a favorite for me. Buy the rust bucket, strip it down completely, refurbish or buy new parts, spray it any color you want, test drive it, and just keeps going. You get experience as you are taking each piece off, granted it\\'s only 1 XP per part, but you use that experience to gain upgrades to how fast you unbolt/bolt parts together, disassemble, a tablet so you don\\'t have to keep running back to the computer, and a few other things. You also use this experience to unlock new areas of the shop. You gain money for completing a job order, use that and the experience to unlock parts of the shop. The money is also good to gain for cars at the auction, when you\\'re experienced enough you can get yourself a parking garage to store your cars. The DLC\\'s add such a great taste to it for all types of gearheads out there, whether you are into sporty cars or muscles, maybe even trucks. They thought of everyone when making the DLC\\'s for this game. Granted there are no name brand vehicles for legal reasons I assume. Still, the cars are nice. I highly recommend this game, it\\'s real addicting.

I have bought this game 4 times throughout its life and beaten the story multiple times. I don't need to play through the game again to prove that I love this game. But I can at least vouch for the port.  Phantom Brave isn't a perfect game or a masterpiece. It's not too hard to make the game easy, and the battle system, while different, isn't the deepest out there. That all said, the game is great and I highly recommend getting it if you're interested in a more serious SRPG compared to Disgaea.  The story centers around Marona and her guardian phantom: Ash. Much of the themes of the game revolve around prejudice and hope. Marona constantly gets burned by the people she helps, as their hate for what they see her as makes them treat her like dirt. Despite constant absue, Marona sticks to her parents' ideals they taught her in the hopes that she will one day be rewarded for her efforts. While it may be a bit irksome for some to have her be so optimistic, I do find it at least grounded by the fact that she's not inherently that pure. It is because of the support of Ash that she is able to hold on to these ideals; not because she's some pure person who can take all of this on her own. The secondary story, titled Another Marona, isn't quite as good as the main one. It does shed a bit of light on all the characters that one couldn't see in the main storyline, so it does offer something to those that enjoyed the characters and wanted to see more of them.  The meat and bones of the game is the gameplay. The battles play out in a turn based format on a girdless field. Characters have a set limit to their movement represented in DMs, but can move as many times as they want until the number of DMs is fully depleted. You can summon phantoms by confining them to various objects on the field, which grant certain bonuses. Phantoms have a set number of turns they can stay in battle, and when that depletes they leave the battle. Marona is the only character that stays on the field permanently, though there are ways to make some phantoms stick around as long. Attacks themselves have various ranges and AOE 'shapes' in which they can effect the field. If you find the going to get rough, then you can pick up and throw allies to better position them, or even toss enemies out of bounds at the cost of slightly empowering the other enemies(though the last enemy standing is immune, if I recall). Any items that a phantom has been confined into has a chance of being 'stolen', as well.   There also a number of sub systems within the game. Titles can be bestowed upon any phantom or weapons you have, and each title has various stat bonuses. Some titles even contain special attack skills. You also enter random dungeons, fuse items and titles, and migrate any non-unique skills. There's a lot of customization available to players in this game. You can also 'reincarnate' characters, which resets them to level one, but allows you to increase their base stat points in order to increase their overall strength.  The visual design is one of the few things that doesn't quite hold up. Sprites are somewhat simple, but not particularly ugly(unless you don't disable the filter they use in the options). That character design is pleasant, and there's a lot of variety in overall character design. The background art scales up well, and looks quite lovely. The game performs well, too, with easy 60 fps in most situations.  The audio design in the game is quite good. The English voice actors for the main story perform quite well, and the voices fit their characters. There's also a Japanese voice option for those who do not like dubs. The Music in the game is pleasant, varied, and is arguably some of the best that composer Tenpei Sato has put out in quite some time.  In terms of replayability, Phantom Brave has a lot to offer. There are a lot of items to collect through confining, characters to unlock, titles to find, and even a New Game Plus option. Delving through dungeons to power up can take a lot, or a little(if you know what to do), time.   In summation: The game is a great value at $20. The story, gameplay, and audio are great, with only some bits of the visuals not holding up quite as well. The port is functional, runs well, and contains all the content from every iteration of Phantom Brave. This is the definite experience, and is well worth your consideration.

**EDITED REVIEW**  I initially rated this game as not recommended, due to its port status. Why? Well, while NIS did some great work cleaning up the backgrounds and menu graphics, they left the original sprites alone. So, you have these crisp and clear backgrounds, this great text, etc, until the characters walk on the screen. Pixellated what? It's rather unfortunate. For all I know, Marona could be Ash! Seriously, it's not that bad, but it's not exactly great either.  Further, rather than port the PS2 version or even the PSP version, the team at NIS chose to port the WiiU version (again, as they did this for Disgaea, but at least Phantom Brave didn't have the launch issues Disgaea did). Here, rather than being glitchy and almost unplayable for a month or two, the menus leave out some important information that was included in the PS2 and PSP versions, which wasn't included in the WiiU.  One such glaring omission occurs when trying to fuse two items together using the Fusioner's mechanics. Fusion is already an obtuse and obfuscated mechanic already, so to hide your weapon's base equip percents is ridiculous (look to the discussion boards to see that a majority of players are flummoxed and befuddled by this omission).  Fortunately, the base stats are shown, and the altered stats due to the equip percents, so if you're good with math, you'll be able to make the calculations necessary to determine your raw equip percents.   But again, why choose to port the worst version? Likely because it's the most recent, I suppose, and the one the devs are most familiar with, so I understand the choice. But really, don't just port and run, D-bags; there are always issues with old games getting ported, which might need more care than a simple 'port-and-done' schema.   I hope the devs don't pull this same BS with their other NIS titles they plan to 'bring to PC.' But I'm sure they will. And I, the fool, will probably still buy it and recommend it. Why? Well, when you've got the only game in the house I'm interested in, this player's gotta play... So, I'll cautiously recommend this game to previous owners of the title, and those who enjoy NIS or SRPGs.

So, I started Riders of Icarus late at night after having some 'minor' sleep deprivation.  The key word here is minor.   I entered, and turned off the audio since I thought it was 'anime weeb' and therefor put on Sabaton. Of course, I decided to enter thine magic realm of Magical Anime Lands, and upon seeing the character creation I was determined. I was determined to make Fashion Hitler. But lo, FashionHitler was not avaliable, as in Hitler was prohibited. However, the almighty gods gave mercy to their humble servant for thy name, Gestapo was allowed. But sadly, I could not have my senpai's infamous mustache, so I simply settled for a goatee.  As I began my grand adventure, I began to narrate the characters like a drunk, citing many sexual gestures, such as 'succ', and 'I am the only normal guy in here' since my guy was not HUEG or midget sized. I was just a normal guy, and the only edgy part (besides my name) was slightly grey hair.  Then, I began my descent into madness, as I began I ran around, then I recieved the almighty power of taming animals. This power would be exploited by the Insomniac's Reich, as I was determined to tame them all, and name them after Nazi Generals, for some reason. My many adventures in 'Oh my god, I can tame that!' led me to my first familiar... Fuhrer, my loyal steed. For which I had purposely backtracked to get a wolf mount, because 'unicorns are anime and bad'. Then, I realized something... That I was extremely tired. And that, is how Icarus gave me sleep, after staying awake for three days watching nazi documentries.  Thank you for releasing me from my hell.  10/10, would make the S.S again.

This game is pretty much indescribable. It's one of those things where 'You have to play it to believe it'. You can get through a full game in 1-2 hours so it's simple to pick up and play, but to see the full game you'll have to beat it multiple times. There is also a lot of randomization, both in dungeon layouts and special items available at any given time, so no two playthroughs will be the same. On one playthrough you might be a disembodied head that spits chocolate milk guarded by the ghosts of your dead siblings, and then on the next playthrough you're cyborg jesus with a pet Meatboy to do your cannibalistic biddings. It's a horrifyingly awesome game.

Having played 2 hrs straight, this game is turning out to be epic. It's got a nice gritty feel along the lines of Max Payne, but with visuals of Killzone thrown in. The story so far is engaging and well paced and the narration is exactly with the kind of bleak cynical but 'die hard' attitude that I like. And the acting is pretty close to Tarantino's Inglorious ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥s. Running it on i5 3350 with GTX660 and 8gig RAM. Running it in Medium but even that looks pretty awesome. I think it uses id5 engine and it is brilliant. UPDATE: Ok. Now its quickly becoming irksome. The story has sort of fallen apart at the seams and the plotlines are getting murkier. The overall goal is still there, but it's got some pretty huge gaps as to why few Nazis seem to question the hero. Plus plenty of wierdness around. Also, the enemies are getting annoying. The AI isn't super intelligent, but the armored guys are near impossible to kill with conventional weapons. And there aren't too many nonConventional weapon ammo around.  Still, worth playing through.

To the Moon is one of a few games that I've played to the end in one go. Not because it's so short (took me 4h - not much, but I've seen many shorter ones), but because the story is so captivating that I just couldn't stop. Once I got past the awkward controls and the initial confusion, I could experience a profoundly sad and beautiful history that was worth every moment it took. An absolute must-play.  Pros: - beautiful, captivating story - great graphics - true pixel art, unlike many of the recent 'pixelated' games - nice music, on par with the mood  Cons: - awkward controls, as if the game was built upon engine that was created for another (type of) game - the achievement does not unlock on Mac and Linux version of the game - cloud saves don't seem to work - very linear and only partially interactive, but in a way it's inherent to the story and setting - out of place arcade elements  Overall none of the cons is relevant enough to spoil the experience, I can recommend this game with no doubt.

GameMaker: Studio is a nice tool to learn how to program, do some quick prototyping for larger projects, or just to play around with. In order to make some decent comercial-quality games you'll need some experience, but isn't that the case with everything? Sure, there still are some bugs you may have to work around, but with every patch they fix some of those. Game Maker's proven itself with games like the original Spelunky, Hotline Miami, Gunpoint, ... I don't think I have to go on.  The fact that you can export to a lot of different devices/operating systems from a single codebase (different configurations for different devices are stored in-project) is quite an amazing feature, and easens the burden of developing for multiple target platforms.  I definately believe GameMaker: Studio is worth checking out, whether you know what you're doing or not.  PS: I do recommend you don't get the steam version, and get a license from  yoyogames.com  instead, as you'll get a steam key AND a non-steam version of the product.

Now that I've had time to play it away from the anger of not having silver hair I have come to a real conclusion on this game. It's an 'eh'. Hidden behind beautiful environments and a fine premise is a combat system which in any game would be 'fine'. Here the approach to fighting is entirely dictated by the game and not the player. Enemy has a shield? You pretty much have to use your grabber to take it off them. Enemy is a specific colour? Well I hope you like the maximum two weapons that will hurt them then, because otherwise you're ♥♥♥♥ed laddy. Also the dodge mechanic. Now in DMC4 there was a similar skill which allowed for perfect last minute dodging and that worked fine because it could cut through your combos and allow you to escape. Here if you've fired up one of your slow attacks (all your red weapons) and you see something about to hit you, there's nothing you can do about it. You're stuck swinging this big ♥♥♥♥ off axe. This may not be such a problem if the game wasn't limited to 30fps where every frame counts and you've got to hit the right one to get away. In previous DMC games we had choice. We could string together any combination of moves, weapons and style to get what we wanted. They weren't always successful against everything but then we learnt to adapt. Here you are given no such freedom. You do exactly what the game wants and if you don't you get ♥♥♥♥ on. Bosses have huge glowing weak spots to wail on, overly telegraphed attacks and the platforming sections weren't fun when DMC3 did it. There is no quick thinking to be done, because if you're not thinking at the game's speed then you'll just end up frustrated. I want to enjoy it, I really do. But I can't stand more than a (incredibly short) mission at a time.

I'm still astonished that a game franchise has been built around a cheap German Super Mario Bros. knockoff from the '80s. Further adding to my surprise is that the latest entry, Twisted Dreams, is such an enjoyable, innovative puzzle-platformer.  The designers manage to wring quite a bit of creativity, demanding of a lot of quick thinking, out of the simple mechanics of switching between two sisters and their two worlds: bright/sunny and goth/macabre. Much of this value is borne from the game's odd rule that you can use either of two jumping moves, but each corresponds to one of the sisters and using the wrong one will succeed, but switch you to that sister. This might not be much of an obstacle, but platforms appear and disappear, gates open and close, and enemies change form based on which sister is out in the fray. To compensate, you often have to think sequences of jumps out in advance and be ready to switch sisters at the exact moment needed. Few puzzle platformers seed their puzzle elements with this much action.  Additional value to the experience comes from the game's stellar - at times, adorable - visuals and music. While the amount of recorded music isn't high, the game's predilection for switching styles to correspond to the sister being controlled escalates the music's longevity quite a bit. Everything also looks quite pretty in both styles; the backgrounds are very deep and detailed for an indie game.  Admittedly, not everything in Twisted Dreams is sunshine and rainbows (or, if you prefer, gnarled nettles and goblins). The girls' voices can get quite grating, particularly in the opening cutscene that insists on flashing its ugly parts whenever you load the game up: the sisters simply moan back and forth for ten seconds as they ponder a crystal they've found that transports them to the game's world. Also, one of the two jumping moves can be incredibly frustrating to control, often sending you off in a direction that, while not technically random, isn't easy to predict.  Nevertheless, the game is still well worth its admission price, not least because of its replay value due to the large number of collectibles and alternate paths in each level. Highly recommended to puzzle-platformer and indie game fans of all levels of dedication.

Possibly the best, if not one of the best Arcade ports ever put on Steam.  It's a no-brainer if you like Arcade games or even are just interested in the D&D aspect of it. Hell, even if you just like games you can play with a friend.  It's a beautifully rendered 2D side-scroller which includes two full retro games in one: 1993 - Dungeons & Dragons: Tower of Doom 1996 - Dungeons & Dragons: Shadow over Mystara  Each have different dialogue to choose and different paths to take, adding alot of replayability. Another feature added is in-game challenges and achievements.   Four-player drop-in/drop-out co-op with local co-op included.   Each game has multiple characters to choose and upgrade - that's right, upgrade. Nineties style. ToD includes a Cleric, a Dwarf, an Elf and a Fighter SOM includes those same four characters with an additional two: a Thief and a Magic-User.  Multiple graphical options are available, like the original pixels...or smoother pixels...or crispy pixels. As well as different views, like Fullscreen, Widescreen, Full Dual Arcade Cabinets, or choose between the left or the right side of the Cabinet.    HIGHLY RECOMMENDED FOR A HEALTHY DOSE OF 90'S NOSTALGIA.
