I already played the original Oregon Trail before this so I already knew this was gonna be great. It's essentially the exact same thing, except with ZOMBIES! And the APOCALYPSE! Okay, that sounds way more stupid when I say that out loud.  Just pick 4 of your friends to get ki- I mean, travel with you to the western coast because..... I don't know. Every city is essentially the same, except the supplies are different and people actually give you jobs. No, not those kinds of jobs. Just go across the street and pick up a box for 2 dollars while a swarm of the undead follows in pursuit, those kinds of jobs. Here's a tip for newcomers: They are a load of bullcrap. You also have to sleep alot if your friends want to heal themselves, so that's 5% of the game right there. Scavenging is also fun since you get more supplies than just meat. I'm not sure what kind of idiot just leaves his wallet right in the middle of the woods.  The worst part of the game is anytime somebody gets kidnapped. If that happens, you might as well blow up your save file since I have never figured out how to get your friend back without them being shot. Every time, either I shoot him/her, or the bandit does. The random stuff that happens to your friends also makes them look like the dumbest apocalypse survivors in the world, so don't be surprised if their all dead by the time you reach Colorado.  There really isn't much left to say but this game is awesome, and one of the best retro style games I've played in recent time. If you played the Oregon Trail, this is the exact same thing so it's nothing new or different, except the apocalypse and everything. Now if you'll excuse, I have to kill one of my friends who went to pee by themselves and get bit.

I have almost nothing but praise for this game. Damn is it good. The parkour is amazing, fast, fluid and sometimes dangerous. The day night cycle has been perfectly balanced, and you won't be getting ♥♥♥♥♥♥ off at how often night comes around. The combat is satisfying as all hell and brutal. Some people believe it to be exactly like Dead Island combat, but I was not fond of that combat at all.  The story was a surprise. I thought this was gonna be a great ♥♥♥♥-around game, yet I started to care about what happens to the characters, and was affected by the plot throughout the entire thing. This game is a bit strange when it comes to performance though. The View Distance bar is the only thing that really effects performance, so if you do get this game, set that to about a quarter of the way or half way, that should do fine with BARELY any difference at all. These issues are said to be getting fixed by Techland in the near future. (They did, but in a way that didn't make much sense)  There are plenty of weapons, side quests, and a massive open world to play in. The one HUGE problem I have with this game is something that happens ONCE, and that is a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t and annoying QTE boss fight. The entire game doesn't have them except when zombies bite you for about 3 seconds, so why the developers put a rather long one randomly in a boss makes little sense. No one likes QTEs.  The night time is... intense. I have experienced it quite a bit now and I still panic. My heart rate rises whenever I go out at night. Sometimes terrifying things will happen, and escaping with your life in those situations is a different kind of rush I haven't felt in a game before. Other than that, I can't think of any bad things about this game. I don't want to give scores anymore since I feel its a bit unfair to the developers to just assign their game a number, so I will sum it up. The world is gorgeous, the combat is great, parkour is fantastic and I hope one day there is a sequel to expand upon the parkour and maybe a different setting with a new city. I haven't had this much fun with a game in a long time, and have played it nonstop for 2 days whenever I got the chance.   (Side Note: There is a Be The Zombie mode in this game, which is a pvp 1v4, 1v3, 1v2 or 1v1 gamemode that pitches a powered zombie up against the survivors. I have only played this once, playing against the lead game designer of this game, and all I can say is that both sides have OP abilities, all of which will be annoying for the opposing side, but I have not played it enough, or even want to, to write about it here. Its fun, but the singleplayer is better.)

F or a series that hasn’t seen a release in 10 years Thief delivers a solid game play experience, modernizing many mechanics without incurring the usual penalty of simplifying them too greatly for mass adoption. Thief doesn’t rely heavily on its pedigree in order to deliver a good experience, being able to create its own distinct identity through it’s well executed game mechanics. Unfortunately the story is the giant black mark on an otherwise highly polished experience, leaving this and many other reviewers wanting. Still it’s hard for me to recommend against playing Thief as it really is a solid game, just don’t play it for the story. Roguelikes pretty much require one to be something of a masochist. Go into a dungeon or what have you, kill everything and steal anything not nailed down with only one live to spare and if a monster gets a good shot on you, well game over, try again.  And it is (imo) the gaming equivalent of crack. Monster kill you moments before you beat the game? Like hell you're gonna take that lying down. Repeat until win or you throw your computer out the window.  Rogue's Tale (hereafter shortened to RT)... makes me wanna do the latter after an hour. Like most Roguelikes, the RNG will be your godsend or it'll make you its ♥♥♥♥♥. Except in the case of RT, I lost count of how many times I ended up becoming the cosmic plaything of the RNG. EVERYTHING is randomized, including the dungeon, the items within and even the monsters.  And that's where the trouble starts. Almost everything is unidentified and that includes your own equipment. Which doesn't seem that bad; get it identified.  At 20 gold a pop, that option is not viable. And because you need food and water and those cost as well...  Let me shorten this. Monsters can easily one-shot you even with diligent planning, helpful items are very difficult to come by, and the whole game seems like a lesson in how NOT to do an randomly generated roguelike. I said in the beginning that roguelikes practically require you to be a masochist; even a masochist would have trouble enjoying this game.

This game is a gem, but it is not without it's flaws.  The game is gorgeous and well thought out. The landscapes and cut scenes are worthy of the homeworld franchise. The game play is fast paced and brutal. Tactics being your friend and hesitation your death.  I beat this game it about a week of on and off play. I played the first few missions on the hardest setting and the rest at 'classic'. many other people think this game needs the ability to pause the game and perform orders. The developers have put forth that they did not include this feature because it breaks immersion and takes away from the desperation the missions in Deserts of Kharak have you play through.  Personally I think it's ridiculous the developers try to defend the idea of not having any sort of 'real' pause feature auto-included in an RTS game (as anyone who has played previous titles in this franchise will know that such a feature is not only necessary; but really cool as you actually get time to enjoy the visuals which are a hall mark of Homeworld). Currently the only way to 'pause' the game is the main menu screen. This does not breeak the game but is disappointing; and honestly will prevent me from playing thorugh the campaign again as half of what goes on is understandably simply 'missed' by the player in the chaos.  Still I would readily recommend this title to anyone who enjoys RTS game play, and cross your fingers that the devs atleast implement an honest pause feature (even if they do not allow you to give orders cretae CTRL gorups etc).

As with many other reviews in games that have existed long before steam release Don't Be fooled by my hours played. I have over the 6 years played Mortal Periodically for around 2 years in total. I will to the best of my ability express why you should or shouldnt Play this game.    First I'm going to adress the Cons  - Buggy Glitchy and still Unpolished ( Not nearly as bad as it used to be.) - Community is a mixed bag, sadly with more rotten candy than I'd like. -It can be frustrating, especially when losing your stuff due to cons 1 or 2. -Slow Developement - Despite the plethora of available options, there are only a few tried and true methods that fit the 'flavor of the month' meta/late game. - Difficult to play solo, group orriented - Sizable Learning Curve - Can suck away your life. - PvP focused ( There is lot's of PvE content, however in the end it all fuels the endless PvP war machine.)  - One server means latency issues. ( To a degree)   This is a sizable and honest List, many people will get hung up on these issues and I don't blame you, though before you cast your hammer down in judgement, just hear me out.  The Pros: -Sandbox - Freedom is Nice -Crafting Is incredibly complex ( Some Professions more than others) -The PvP is intense and a blast. ( Full loot adds that adrenaline, you really feel that risk of losing your hard work during the fight) - No Levels, just skills that are trained through use or passively by reading. -Interesting politcal scene. (Large groups of people, limited resources, open PvP, all makes Server wars a common thing.) -One server ( Diverse fun playerbase despite the bad fruit, I havent met a more interesting smathering of players. Does -playing with Aussies, Germans, Swedes, Brits, Spaniards, Brazilians, Irishmen, and Canadians all in the same Team speak sound fun. Does to me) -A dedicated developer who keeps chugging away ( The little Starvault that could) - Being a trader and crafter is completely viable (Though you'd be missing a sizable portion of the game) - No other game quite like it. (It's why I keep returning) - In the end the Games world is shaped by the players.  - Game lore is pretty decent.   Despite the games shortcomings, I've had some of my fondest gaming memories on here and have met some of the coolest people too. Sure being a new player is harsh and I reccomend you find a good honest group of people to help you learn. There are many people in the game who will make your life hell, you will cuss and scream but in the end if you work hard enough, play smart and make quality friends, you can accomplish some pretty neat things.  TL;DR Games is mediocre, The expereinces you'll have playing it, Priceless.

Game is not what I expected. But perhaps my expectations were wrong, because I was unfamiliar with the roguelike genre. I had hoped that this game would be more like a sandbox in which you can play out your own stories. However, there is no way to create interesting stories in it at all.  You can marry to have children (in fact: you always only have one child and after that you die, and play on with the child). Besides the marriage itself, there is never any interaction between your character and the one you marry. There is never an interesting interaction between parent and child and friendship is non-existent.  In fact it's just some simple economics: building, harvesting, killing, gaining loot. Marrying is also entirely an economical decision. I think if that is what you play it for, it's fine.  In general I tend to dislike it, when games that pretend to be about adventure, are in fact only about economics and statistics. Wastes the theme for me.

Monstrum is a great indie horror game. It is a sandbox type survival/puzzle horror game. Your mission is to escape from an cargo ship where an predatory monster hunts you. There are 3 different ways of escape, but you first have to find certain items to make these escapes available. The rooms and item placements in the ship are randomly generated, so every playtime is different.   A fair warning though, if you read the wiki or watch guides online, it will take the horror element away, and this game becomes only an puzzle game with little survival on the side. Reading guides also lowers the replayability of this game, and in my opinion it is best to do all the 3 escapes without reading any guides.   For an indie game, it is suprisingly good. I would rate it 7.5/10. grab it from an sale

Street Fighter V is as fantastic a fighting game as one could ask for, the fights feel fast paced and the characters are varied and feel unique. The newly added V-Skill and V-Trigger system makes each fight feel exciting and changes the pace from other fighting games.    As great as the fighting itself feels the game around the fighting is severely lacking. As it currently stands ( as of 2/24/2016) the game modes are training, a story mode which is far too short and easy and consists of 2 or 3 very easy one round fights with static images and and voice over. The slim roster of 16 seems a bit sparse compared to Street Fighter IV which had 25 at launch.  The game also has Ranked and Casual matchmaking and Battle Lounges which currently only hold 2 players and have been not super reliable for even that. The matchmaking however has been super stable with very little laggy matches only one or two of my nearly one hundred matches suffered from lag that made it hard to play.   The unfortunate thing is the game itself feels great and I have been loving every moment playing it but without the arcade mode, trials and other modes you usually get in a full release of a fighting game it is hard to recommend for someone who is not a hardcore fan of the series.  The march update seems as if it will add some of these missing features which will hopefully round out what the game offers to people not as familiar with the series.  Although I would find it much easier to recommend this game if there was more content in it the stellar improvements over Street Fighter IV and the quality of the actual fighting outshine the rather bare bones package to make it worth your time and money if you are a hardcore Street Fighter fan.

X-Wing is not a perfect game. It is the first in a series of four games and therefore had basically all of the growing pains; the worst game engine, a story much worse than its follow up (TIE Fighter), a less than intuitive menu UI (I personally don't think this was fixed until the third game; X-Wing vs TIE Fighter), but... it's still a very fun game. The story goes from just after the first death star was nocked out on until Hoth and it's an enjoyable ride.   My recommendation is to use the Collector's CD-ROM version which uses the game engine from TIE Fighter instead of the less than great original one; same as with TIE Fighter there is a MIDI based soundtrack that adjusts to recent events. Enemy reinforcements arrived? Scary music. Dogfight? Exciting music. Just took out a TIE Interceptor? Uplifting music that fades back to the exciting music because you're still in a damned dogfight. It's great, though it is graphically inferior to the Remastered edition that uses a version of the X-Wing vs Tie Fighter Engine and the accompanying increased graphical improvements. The Collector's CD-ROM version also has great voice overs though it suffers from the low bit rate it was obviously recorded at.   Long story short: Collector's CD-ROM is the best. Remastered is prettier but misses out on some neat features. Classic is bleh (it's basically a prototype to build up to making the TIE Fighter engine and game).  Technical Side notes:   There is an XWA wrapper required to get the Remastered version working 100% correctly and a launch option of -softwarecursor must be set as well.  https://github.com/rdoeffinger/xwa_ddraw_d3d11/releases/latest  just paste it in your remastered folder for X-Wing.  You may have to fiddle with some DOSbox settings for some of the versions to get the correct sized window or what have you but it isn't anything particularly complicated. A good 4:3 window size I like to use is 960x720.  If you have issues with the initial screen doing the joystick calibration on launch; alt+c during a mission or training will launch the in engine calibration and you can do it then.

First Impressions after playing for an hour. This only reflects the first level of the game:  The game's combat is kinda....crap. Against weak enemies you can just mash the attack button and stun lock them into oblivion. For tougher enemies with more health, you can either spam your magic attach (which is rightly limited to avoid abuse) or trap them on a ledge to easily beat them without risking health (enemies can't jump and will only attack if you're right next to them).   I'd normally feel bad about that, but the combat just feels so slow. Trying to mash against a boss just starts a slap fight you're doomed to lose, so this is one where you must master the art of the jump slash - which is much faster and allows you to keep moving.   There is a backdash function which appears to exist to make combat more deliberate and complex, but enemies' attacks always seem to begin before your attack animation ends, so you're better off with the jump slash. (Maybe allow the backdash to cancel current attacks to make dodging more fluid?)  The music is ....fine. Honestly, metal can be cool, but, stretched over long periods with no vocals and incessant repetition, I, personally, couldn't stand it. After truning off the music, the game took a different tone. Instead of listening to thrashing power chords, I heard the dripping of blood and...not much else. The sound design relies pretty heavily on the idea that everything will just be drowned out by the music so not much actually has a sound effect.   The biggest complaint I have with the game, though, is the platforming. I did not think going in that this game would have such a strong platofmer component to it. Certainly, I figured I would run and jump to various challenges, but the first level basically plays out like:  Mash to kill an enemy, jump over moving platorms moving up and down jump over insta-kill trap  jump over moving platorms moving up and down mash to kill an enemy  jump over moving platforms moving up and down jump over insta-kill trap  jump over moving platforms moving up and down   To put it bluntly, the level design for the first level is somewhat tedious with a lot traps and pits that will kill you instantly - forcing you to restart from your last checkpoint, and, with such slow, limited movement, it doesn't feel engaging, or satisfying, to keep jump slashing my way through ghosts just to get across the same set of 4 moving platforms I know I've seen at least 3 times before.    I like this game - I like action adventure games with fast combat and striking visuals - this game....tries at that.  I'll keep my copy and continue playing to see how it goes (toggling the music as I go to give it a listen), but, so far, its in dire need of polish.

Pink Heaven is the second demo/preview/ad for Studio Pixel's new game, 'Kero Blaster.' It continues the story of a little pink secretary, which started in the first game, 'Pink Hour.'   Pink Heaven expands on the originals simple premise. Instead of one weapon, there are two weapons. Instead of one stage, there are two stages. And instead of one boss fight, there are two. (if you want to tackle Hard Mode). It is very short, and relatively easy. There are multiple endings, just like Pink Hour, which is an incentive to replay the game. The story is very simple, but it's cute. I especially like how it utilizes characters who are NPCs in the main game, Kero Blaster.  Where Pink Heaven really shines is in its Hard Mode, which unlocks after getting one of the multiple endings. Hard Mode doesn't give you less health and enemies more damage like most other games with varying difficulties. It completely revamps the familiar two levels from the main game, and even has an extra boss fight. Hard Mode requires extremely precise platforming, and a cool head during the final boss. Beating it took me multiple, multiple attempts, but the feeling of overcoming Hard Mode was immensely satisfying, and I'd recommend at least trying it out.  Pink Heaven (along with Pink Hour) is the perfect way to advertise a game. It has me invested, even if it's just a little, in these characters, and it showcases the gameplay style and overall feeling of Kero Blaster while remaining its own little side-game. With the price of 100% nothing, both games are worth playing through at least once. They definitely have me excited for Kero Blaster's Steam release.

I never really looked at this title until it went on sale during the Steam summer sale - and a good friend bringing it to my attention. I'm very glad he did. His opinion (and what he heard) plus the trailers really got me excited for this title.  My thoughts about Wolfenstein had been colored a lot by its previous title, which was ok, but nothing more... so I wasn't really interested.  But after playing a couple hours into it... it's reminding me a lot of the somewhat recent Tomb Raider title (without all the crafting nonsense, mind you). They've made Wolfenstein into a triple A title - worthy of that branding. It feels like a movie that you play along in. There are some lengthy cutscenes, but they're pushing a story that you want to see.  [Edit: The storyline and characters! Wow! They did a fantastic job in putting effort into them. Specifically the characters. You grow to like them and want to hear more about them. It's very enguaging and was the primary reason for me to wanting to continue to play to the very end.]  The maps are very well done - complicated enough so that you can explore, but not enough to get lost. There are parts of the maps where you're given very satisfying power to exploit if you wish. Then there are others areas where you feel the proper amount of presure pushed upon you, as you would expect in the overwhelming presence of the Nazi army. Which leads me to my next point...  The stealth system. They've attempted to put one in this game... but it's rather hallow and not fleshed out. You CAN be stealthy one some missions, but on others you're simply forced into fighting. Regardless of your best efforts to go unnoticed, you'll somehow be found and they open fire upon you as any typical AI would - no confusion, no reports going between your foes on your current location. They simply know where you are, and move towards you like dogs during supper.  There are collectables to search for. Some unlock more total hit points for you, some for play modes, and some just for simple achievements. Some of these are well hidden. During my first play through, I missed a bit more than half of them, and I was actively looking for them as I progressed.  But enough details... in summary... this game has good graphics, sounds, gameplay, and maps. The only adjustment I would wish for would be smarter AI.  I would give this title 4/5, or 8/10. Overall the single player was impressive, but lacking in AI (sorry, but Fear kind of upped the bar on this). I would definately recommend this title due to the other fine things it has to offer.  I purchased this title for $30 (during a sale, if you recall) and I feel it was almost worth that amount. If you can pick it up for cheaper, you would be wise to do so. My ideal pricing for this title would be between $15 to $20.  Also, please be aware that if you're looking for multiplayer, this game doesn't have any (which I'm grateful for after the previous Wolfenstein).

I got this game in the recent sale. I'm not familiar with the Tomb Raider series, but this game was quite enjoyable. It's really similar to the Uncharted series, with not overly complex puzzles, cover-based action sequences and amazing setpieces. It's not very long, but doesn't get repetitive at all. The only thing I didn't enjoy at all were the many brutal death scenes awaiting our protagonist. Well worth it's current price, it becomes a must-have for fans of the genre when paired with a decent sale. The DLC is largely forgettable, although the extra tomb was nice.

So, Tomb Raider is a game that I played as a kid quite religiously. I really enjoyed the game and it's difficulty at the time and after buying and playing the reboot, I watched some old videos on YouTube and enjoyed myself again.  The game took me about 15 hours to beat with a 76% completion rating. I didn't really go completely out of my way to search for things, and I never backtracked. There are times in the game where you unlock abilities and stronger weapons that will make places that you've already been accessible with your new abilities, etc. Lara controls quite well and realistically, bringing back the added danger that you cannot start/stop on a dime and forward momentum can really screw things up. The story is definitely cliche and the conclusion is something you see a mile away, but the game actually is pretty damn fun when it comes down to it.  The gameplay is quite nice and fluid, Lara handles easily and realistically enough. The trademark jumping and mini-puzzle sequences are there in the optional tombs and help bring you back a bit. (The puzzles are not hard at all.) The story is chock-full of the same old Hollywood cliches, full of liberalism, inter-racial relationships, etc. The worst part about this game, hands down, is the pretty awful voice acting. The voice acting often does not fit the emotion of the scene or what is happening in the game. The Russian language parts really are awful, and I even question if they hired native Russian speakers.   There is a lot to do, lots of things to collect, discover, and plenty of map to roam around. The upgrade system is nice and thoughtful and over all, I liked seeing what they created to help explain how Lara Croft started off as the #1 Tomb Raiding person in the world.

Fun game, entertaining enough during the time it lasts. You spend much more time shooting stuff than exploring or tomb raiding, but the weapon selection and shooting mechanics make that more bearable. it's a mixed bag, but worth a try at a discounted price.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: Personally as somebody who has put over 700 hours into this game I feel I have experienced all that there is to in this game.  This game to me is almost perfect, many things factor into this such as its simplicity and how its so easy to pick up and play for hours and hours of endless fun.  Another thing I love is this games Factions you get from the start (Yes I know I could be like 99% of all the other review and complain about DLC but that to me doesn't matter) The line up we are given in this game is extremely different to each other, this with the inclusion of mods that make every single other non-DLC faction playable makes it amazingly replayable. Every time I pick up and play this game it excites me for the kind of empire i'll build next, maybe i'll build an African empire with Rome or maybe i'll take on the barbarian tribes with Macedon ~  There is very few problems that I have with this game, one being the AI is too passive (although this can be fixed with a simple mod) I felt they weren't expansive enough and that they'd just wait in line for me to invade them next. Another problem is Pikemen, as fun as they are to use and they look really cool, I feel like they're overpowered and over used, any faction that can get them spams them so damn much it's insane. This pike spam makes playing as factions who dont have them I.E. Iceni, Suebi and Parthia really hard as you need to focus all of your power on them and it just takes a lot of fun away from battles and it just makes you want to auto-resolve the battles with pikemen.  Apart from those 2 issues I have the game itself Is one of the best Total war games out there! I'd seriously reccomend this to anybody wanting to get into the Total war franchise.  I give Total War™: ROME II a 9/10

Imagine yourself playing mini golf on one of those spinning circle things at the playground. Add moving turtles and tortoises onto the green, making it harder for you to get to the goal. Now, you have yourself a mini super hexagon. That's exactly what this game is about.  Basically, the Super Hexagon is a shape-changing-color-morphing geometric spinning shape trying to squeeze you, a triangle, into the black pulsating hexagon in the middle. You, being a triangle (the most structurally sound geometric shape), of course try your best to survive. There are various levels for you to try. A slow hexagon, a moderately fast hexagoner, and a ridiculously fast hexagonest.  After that, there's the very entertaining 'hyper' modes. However, I won't spoil it for you. You'll have to beat the Hexagon monster for 1 minute. Good luck, fellow wireframe triangle.

Morrowind might be an amazing game, with a great story telling the tale of the Nevarine, but if I cant even level up my skills then the game is all but unplayable, and I will probably not get to see the ending to this story. Remember Me is a uniqe attempt of a beginner studio at making a clever story about time travel. Since it's 2016 now, we have Life Is Strange - so go play that first, and then play Remeber me as a 'back to basics' vesrion.  Many concepts and even gameplay elements have a similar feel to them, but this one also offers combo-based combat (which is very well-made for busy people who cannot remeber a combo since they play on a weekly basis). The story is comparable to a book, but the experience is a game-exclusive one: you could never just watch it on a screen or just read it from paper. You have to DO it yourself.  Basically, 10/10 for effort and ideas, 8/10 for execution. Shouldn't be played as anything other than a storytelling experience, since that's what it is (although battle mechanics are quite nice).

app_name: Leisure Suit Larry in the Land of the Lounge Lizards: Reloaded
review_text: I didn't know about Leisure Suit Larry when it first came out.  So when I go to a friend's place to play some saturday night Magic the Garthing. We walked in on a friend that's playing a sperm swimming mini game which the title Leisure Suit Larry.  That burns a clear and unforgetable image of what this game serie is like at the back of my mind. So when I found out about steam is getting this one I jumped on the chance of getting this game.   To tell the truth is much less perverted then i expected. There are a few sex scene but they are all censored. And being the first of the serie. It doesn't have any minigame except slot machine and black jack. Which leave it a basic find item and solve situation kind of game. You can walk, unzip, use, smell/taste, talk and use item on lots of back ground objects in the game. And reacting you do is fully voiced with really funny remarks. The jokes alone is worth it for this game.  The puzzle and event you have to do to proceed the story is quite hard to find and some of the must needed information/event are very hard to find and they will come to you very randomly and without hint. (who the hell would be crazy enough to think about using hot sauce on the whale's blow hole to get an nessary item??)  The graphics and music are wonderful. The scripting of all the diagloge is masterfully done and will make you laugh all the time. The game play removes some fun because of the pure randomness of actions required but overall it's still a very interesting and unique game to play... Achievement of this game can be gotten offline but they will pop at same time when it's auto synced.  So if u looking for perverted game.. you will like this on the dialogue but disappointed by the actual event.  8.5/10 would be my final rating.. give this one a try when you are bored. But if you stuck.. make sure you have a basic guide ready.

Gone Home is a perfect example of why games are an artform. This gamecaptivates story, exploration, and discovery perfectly. Don't be fooled by some of the shoddy reviews done of this game out there (I'm looking at you IGN -.-). This is Call of Duty meets Medieval Times in a good way. A squeal of sorts to War of the Roses and similar to Chivalry and the multilayer portion of Mount and Blade.  The game is third person and boasts a progression system of weapons and armour. Most of the upgrades are cosmetic but different types of weapons and perks can also be unlocked as you earn gold, which acts like spendable xp, at the end of each match. The game boasts different classes the default being three, but quickly unlocked is the ability to choose your own load-out of weapons and perks. Teams are always Vikings vs Saxons, but the only difference between the two sides gameplay wise is their different appearance and vocalizations. Matches support up to 32 players but most servers limit player count to 24 which is a more comfortable fit for the map sizes.  In comparison to War of the Roses the game still has that extra mile of historical authenticity for all the history buffs out there (one of the end game unlockables for the Vikings is an Uthbert Swortd!) The game looks considerably prettier ditching the washed out look for a brighter and more colourful appearance, and the detail on the armour and weapons is impressive. The biggest casualty in features from War of the Roses is mounts which is a bit of a shame but does fit the time period especially given the lack of lances. The familiar mouse driven swinging and blocking is back but overall has been streamlined to the games benefit. For example, the stab attack and block action has been removed but this only makes the game simpler and lowers the learning curve a bit for new players. Furthermore the bow is mercifully much easier to aim compared to War of the Roses with arrows dipping much less below the cross-hair over distance. Special attacks with the 'F' key on the other hand are new and very based on what weapon is equipped. While powerful if missed they can leave you dangerously open for a brief time after for a counter so you must be careful not to over employ them and wait for the right moment. The sounds and music of the game is top notch. On the other hand despite an extensive beta at this time there are still few bugs lingering about, such as spawning too near enemies, but they are rare enough to not pose too much of an issue. Meanwhile there is a fairly basic game-mode variety but a healthy number of maps featuring a number of gorgeous locations with for example Saxon towns to crumbled stone ruins in an autumn forest acting as the backdrop. The combat is satisfyingly brutal with occasional decapitations and blood effects and feels visceral as opposed to floaty such as combat in the Elder Scrolls games for instance. The swing of your two handed axe looks and feels satisfyingly heavy upon striking the chainmail of your foe.  Keep in mind the game is only a multilayer offering, but if you like first person shooters like Titanfall or Call of Duty and the idea of s third person medieval setting this game might appeal to you. Similarly if you don't like those afore mentioned fps' this games different take of hand to hand combat might tickle your fancy. Bear in mind the game has a difficulty similar to those fps' however.  If you have an itch to play out some realistic medieval skirmishes, and don't a bit of learning curve if you haven’t played this style of game before, this game is worth the $25 asking price.   Grab a horn of mead, say a prayer to Odin, and charge into the fray. 8.0/10 (Great)

You are probably asking yourself why should I buy this game if Battlefield 1 is coming out? Well, let me just say, that compared to Verdun, Battlefield is unrealistic. This is by no means a bad things, but its a matter of taste. So, what I will say is this, I have played 0.3 hours online, but 2 hours offline. I will keep playing this even after Battlfield 1. This game is incredibly realistic. This takes the honor of the second game to ever make me feel bad for killing people. When you shoot someone in this game, they make it well known. They scream, and gurgle while throbbing on the floor. It is a horrific sound. This game has singleplayer and multiplayer. Yes, I know it says its multiplayer only, but that is because singleplayer was just added in the horrors of war expansion. You basically sit in a trench by yourself, and fend off waves of enemies. By yourself. This sounds hard, but I think they beef up your health a lot as well as damage, so, its actually challenging, but not inpossible. After one round, you get artillery, and then, a couple of rounds in, you are forced to put on your gas mask. Every death pushes you back, until you lose. This game is so good, I wrote out an entire paragraph on this. This feels like the product of a huge company, not a small indie team. It is the best WW1 game, the best unity game, and one of the best games I have ever played. Buy this for a realistic WW1 shooter or to sustain your hype until Battlefield 1. $25 is too low of a price for this.  Overall Score: 10/10 TL;DR: Buy for realism and/or hype until Battlefield 1. Dont buy if gory scenes are not your thing or if you need a campaign. There is singleplayer, but no campaign.

Eight Hours played, first day.  This is not a story game, or a combat game. This is an exploration survival game, where a whole day can be spent on a single planet learning how to survive in the varied environments, dealing with new creatures, and discovering secrets. Not designed for short attention spans, or those who require lots of hand holding. Much of the enjoyment comes from learning how to play. The game is intuitive enough not to need the internet, but not so simple as to lack the pleasure of discovery. This is a sandbox, not a theme park; a novel, not a television episode. There is plenty to do, and the game can be riveting and immersive. Hours of fun exploration are punctuated by moments of terror and heart pounding excietment. And then you fly into space.  The game plays flawlessly on Boot camp iMac with maxed settings, (Intel i7) I initally had trouble with the game crashing at startup, Then I upgrade my Nivida graphiccard driver, and it ran perfectly.  A plethora of goals are provided by the game which help motivate you, but I also found goals of my own. After eight hours of near non-stop play, I don't feel that I have scratched the surface of this game. So much to do, so much to learn, and of course, so many places to go.   Drawbacks: Not everything is unique. Some outposts are identical. And I can't expect all the planets to be completely different in gameplay challenge. I began on a radioactive world, then went to a burning hot world. Both were challenging in different ways, and forced me to learn the planet's secrets and adapt to survive, but I doubt it will continue that way. At some point redundancy will kick in. Also there is no building so far that I have encountered in the game, but it is still early, and I am no where near developing all the upgrades.  For those who enjoy slower, less violent sandbox games that focus on exploration, discovery, and development, this might be the best game you'll find. The closest I would say is Subnautica, which is another wonderful experience, but with that you only get one world.

Summary: Great concept, some redeeming qualities, fairly good campaign system. You spend very little time being able to actually interpret problems, think, problem solve, and make decisions, and spend the vast majority of your time doing tedious things, watching lame and tedious animations, or just waiting for the A.I. to complete its turn. This game compares extremely poorly to games like XCOM, which are vastly superior in virtually every way.   I was extremely excited about Mordheim. I have long been wanting someone to make a games workshop game like this. I have played almost every turn based games workshop game ever made over the years, and many others besides. This is the only GW branded game I've ever played that I didn't like. I have played tabletop Mordheim, and it is a fantastic game (though not without some ballance issues).  Pros:  Excellent concept, inspired by an excellent tabletop game, with the rich back-story and setting of the Warhammer World. Some of the graphics and artwork are very good.  Experience and campaign system is fairly deep and fairly well done from what I have seen so far. Gradual development (as well as deaths and injuries) to your warband are fun, and makes for addictive gameplay, very similar to games like Xcom. The scripted main story missions are a good idea, although they certainly could be better.  Cons:  Most things in this game take way too long, way longer than is necessary. There is a lot of waiting in between taking actions, but also a lot of unnecessary and tedious clicking, in part due to a very inefficient and counter-intuitive user-interface.   Aesthetically, there are a lot of problems with the game. Aesthetics should serve gameplay, but most of the eye catching details on a given map have absolutely zero effect on gameplay, and there are way too many of them, making them very distracting. The lighting in the game also often makes it more difficult to actually see things. I also find most of the art to be VERY ugly, taking away from the generally very nice and interesting looking architecture, and often excellent character models. The grotesque or horrible looking things would have more impact if they weren’t saturating every map, forcing you to always look at them. Sometimes, less is more.  There are frequent unnecessary animations for every uninteresting little action you take with your units. For example, when going into overwatch, or dodge stance, or ambush stance, an animation of your character plays which looks nothing like what they’d actually be doing if they were going into a stance to be ready to do any of those things. It wastes time, and looks stupid, but what’s worse is that these stupid animations are being constantly repeated over and over again, making it extremely annoying .  Navigation is very difficult. There is no minimap, and the map design as well as camera views and lack of informative visual navigational cues makes it time consuming to switch back and forth between the map view and the third person view, to try to figure out what plan to come up with and where to move your units.  It is very difficult to actually look at the terrain, either with the map view or third person view, to see where the various possible paths are, or even to figure out what the shortest distance is from one point to another. You can only climb up or down obstacles in pre-determined, arbitrary points, which are marked by ugly graphical indicators. For the most part, you can’t really climb up the sides of buildings, or on top of them (unlike with the tabletop game).  The terrain itself is often counter-intuitive, with many spaces which your units could obviously fit through, which are in fact impassable for no good reason. A large gap in a wall, or between buildings, larger than a doorway, is sometimes impassable. Large, open windows, are always impassable. Very small obstacles that could easily be jumped over, are often impassable.   The way deployment works is very clunky, and for some scenarios really doesn't make much sense to me. Deployment should be an important and interesting tactical challenge, and more thought should have been put into how this worked, at least making it a lot less clunky.   If you’re fighting over the resources on the battlefield, why is it that you don’t get to actually keep all of those resources unless you manually pick those pieces up before the battle is over? This is extremely counter-intuitive. You should get to keep everything on the battlefield if you actually win. What’s worse is how unnecessarily time consuming it is to actually pick these items up.   The A.I. is very poor.   A lot of the skills and spells aren't worth taking. You don't want a significant part of gameplay to be spending lots of time figuring out which ones aren't worth taking. Furthermore, the benefits which they give at first are so minor, that it makes choosing between them a tedious, uninteresting, and not very rewarding experience. I would rather see fewer skils, but have them be more powerful, but also have a wider variety of different useful skills, allowing for meaningful specialization and more meaningful, interesting tactical choices regarding how to best use them (or counter them) on the battlefield.   Making learning skills and spells cost money (in addition to points earned through experience) doesn’t fit with the setting and makes no sense. First of all, it makes no sense that your warriors would be taking short training vacations in order to develop their skills, who the hell are you even paying anyway? The amount of gold that it costs to pay for these is by far the largest expense of your warband, whereas in reality, the largest expense would simply be the costs of paying your warriors, and also buying equipment and weapons. It is very dissatisfying to have to spend most of your hard earned gold on training skills, and to be un-able to actually afford to use most of your skill points. Experience should always be convertable into improving your warriors, you shouldn’t also need money for it, it's a needlessly frustrating mechanic.   I understand that it's very difficult to make a game like this. I think the devleopers bit off a bit more than they could chew, and probably spent too much time on making artistic content, and not enough time trying to develop and polish the actual gameplay, but of course it's a lot easier to criticize than to actually do it right yourself. It's not easy to be critical of your own creation. I greatly appreciate the attempt which has been made here.   I would love it if someone would make another attempt to re-make this game, with a much more intuitive interface and faster gameplay (without sacrificing tactical depth).  Conclusion:   Another reviewer put it very well, and far more succinctly than myself, when he said 'Successfully combines all of the worst parts of XCom with none of the good parts.' Although that's not quite 100% true, it's pretty funny and unfortunately rather accurate.

Ehh...  I will just barely recommend this. I like the gameplay (simple, but effective), the ship designs, and the overall graphical feel hits the mark. However, the lack of story and dumb (though often hilarious) AI may turn people off, and I wouldn't blame them.  Controls seem tight enough, once I got used to them, and the lack of a z axis to move in is fine with me. It allows for some tactics, such as hiding behind asteroids to regroup and attack the enemy, that I otherwise feel I couldn't easily pull off if the enemy could easily move above or below me (I'm talking the main ships, small AI fighters don't have this restriction).  I don't have a huge amount of time to play games, so the simplified but tight combat, and decent upgrade system for the ships - who doesn't love upgrades? Nobody important, that's who - make this game enjoyable and relaxing. I've only really gotten blown up once, and that's because I was stupid.  So, tl;dr:  Pros:  + Combat, simple but nice, more like 'boats in space', but not necessarily a bad thing. + Upgrades. Everybody loves upgrades. Wait, you don't? The heck with you, you don't count.  Cons:  - Story. Wish there was more to it. - AI. I both love it and hate it. Hell, it was an enemy ship bashing into his bro and finishing him off that was one of my highlights of my gaming week. At the same time, it keeps the game from feeling as immersive as it could.  In conclusion, the gameplay is nice, but the story will likely make the game boring after just a little while. However, it's a fun enough ride so far that I'll give it a thumbs up.  Take care, everyone, and Pew Pew!

BAD: The main problem of this game is that killing enemys is a chore. With bigger groups it has a decent challenge but when the group is cut down to 3-5 individuals it takes to long to kill. You place the bombs on them directly, on the side or just spam them with bombs or carefully place a bomb on them and they wont die. Takes 3 to 5 trys to kill one in a small group. It kills the paceing of the game for an action game. And I swear they have some sort of short invincibility time after they get hit because of this. I dont like the fact that it advertises itself as a shoot em up as the only way to attack is to place bombs everywhere. It's interesting and the only problem I have with it is what I said as above and that placeing bombs dosnt count as a shootemup but you do get shot at.  I wish this game came with a tutorial because as I said above, you can place bombs on enemys and it wont do a thing when theres a few left. Because of this I didnt know if I was playing it right or not. Also with small groups not getting hurt the same thing applies to 2 bosses I have encountered but worse. They seem to be invincible at times not getting hurt at all only for you to do a small amount of damage. If theres an opening for when your supposed to hurt it I dont know what it is plus I wait 3 or 5 seconds before I spam it with bombs for a good 10 seconds Note: The bombs themselves dont kill them its the waves and ripples the bombs make. If you do get this game I find the minibombs to be the worse one to use. Still find it hard to kill them.  GOOD: One thing I do like about this game is the risk reward it has. The power orbs which play as money lay on the screen disappear when you get hit once and the power orbs can increase in value when they gather into one big orb.  Another thing I sorta like about this game is that you can make waves with the bombs to try and push them towards enemys,speed up or slow you down. The designs of the player character and the bosses are cool but the main enemys look like cereal.   In the end if you love these kinds of games and have more patience with trying to kill a couple of enemys that wont die untill the 3rd bombing attempt youll find some enjoyment with this game. I dont really like it but I might play an hour of it per month or just try to see all the levels then be done with it. Not a bad game but not a good one also as I see it. In the end I have to give it a no that I dont like this game. What really kills it for me is that it takes forever to kill a single guy and that takes up to much time for an action game. Fix that and ill just give this a pass. Mabye its just im more used to bullet hell games.

Its story is complex and spontaneous, which allows for theories and speculation to take place. Consequently, the game can be thought provoking as well as interesting. Going back to the gameplay, its traditional style makes it rather addictive. The combination between the story and addictive gameplay shapes a rather interesting game.  Economically, the game is well priced, according to the content, and the graphics match the atmosphere of the game.  A fine piece of platformer gaming.

A short, sweet and simple exploration platforming game, Grow Home takes a simple and somewhat unique concept and smothers it in charm and primitive beauty.  It feels like Ubisoft were given a simple concept on a piece of paper and handed it to the Reflections team with a tiny budget and timeframe, and got given another expectation-breaking gem in return.  In Grow Home I played as BUD who's just crash landed on a floating platform and jettisoned below onto the surface of a new planet, and my goal is to locate and grow a Star Plant to maturity using the environment with my robotic abilities while also traversing and exploring the alien environment. The gameplay is just as unique, interesting and alien as the world and its inhabitants, and it's a very refreshing, charming game that makes me feel at ease while having simple plain old fashioned fun. Great game, I love the cynical humor of the characters (don't skip the chat!) and the way you don't always know if you are helping the good guy or the bad guy until the story plays out. It is hard to work a decent story into a match-3 game, but they managed it pretty well. There is PvP in the game, but at this point, it is totally optional. You can and will get attacked by other players, but they are limited to taking a certain percentage of the gold you accrued while offline, not emptying your bank account. The PvP ranking system is a little sketchy, but can work in your favor as often as against it. You can be level 100 and get ranked against a level 295 but then 'try another match' and get a level 45. I have no idea what stat it matches you up by, but be patient and you can do well. There are guilds in the game. There is no guild chat as you may know it in other games, but instead there is a message board. The advantage to that is the messages STAY, so you can talk or give messages to players who aren't on at the same time you are.  Guild quests are simple. Give a little extra gold when you can. You pay to fill the quest bar. You don't need to be uber skilled to help your guild. The skills and abilities of your troops was greatly expanded about the time I quit playing, so I can not add a lot to that, but it creates a lot of depth and customization for fighting style and powers, that you really would not expect in a game that seems so simple on the surface. Pay to Win. Nope. Simple as that. Pay to speed things up? Yeah, of course. Dev's don't live and work for free. But there is no gameplay advantages to buying other than to get stuff done faster. You can patiently grind away to get those runestones and still get those talents on your troops you want without paying. It will just take a bit longer. I really like that. It kept the game from feeling like it was descending into a stale cash grab. The game is still vibrant and alive with active devs and a lot of players. I hope it continues to thrive.

I had to install this on my derptop a second time just cause I wanted to write a review, heh. I played this for a bit prior to its release on steam and sadly my machine is a smigin beneath what can comfortably run the game, I can play but frustraiting framerate drop kept me from playing for larger amounts of time. This is a real shame because the game itself is very nice far as first person shooters go.  What you essentially get is the experience and soul that brought you the S.T.A.L.K.E.R series in a first person pvp capacity. The world that vostok games definately will take you back to your days as a STALKER with gritty post apocalyptic settings combined with bazar anomalies. The game play is fairly enjoyable even if its nothing spectacular, grab a gun go shoot someone, get better with that gun find more guns shoot more people ect ect.  What Survarium does do well is sound, you will be hard pressed to find more realistic sounding guns in a game AAA or otherwise, wearing headphones and having someone sneak up on you and shove a mosberg into your ear is a startling experience often leaving you more shocked than bothered by your characters actual death. At the time of this review they had quite a few impressive maps (That graphically are closer to Metro than STALKER) a couple of modes (Essentially team deathmatch or Capture the Flag in box form) however the developers have repeatedly stressed their desire to implement a PVE mode and I look forwarde to that.....and having a PC that can run Survariums properly.   All in all I definately recommend Survarium, in its current condition it would easily justify a $10 pricetag but being free to play and starting you out with enjoyable guns make it hard to pass up.   Fair warning though, as you get better items pay attention to the level they have, as they will effect how matchmaker pits you against other players: If you go in with a bunch of lvl0 gear and a lvl10 gun your going to get your ♥♥♥ chewed up.

My very first of many reviews. I played this game for around 2 hrs. Let me start by saying, I've never played the original PA, I've only played Titans. That being said, for the low price of it being on sale I think it's a very good game.. Speaking only from a Single player perspective I will list some pros and cons:  Pros-  +Very quick to grasp IMO  +Fun Intergalactic concept +Nice feminine voice to guide you :-)  +Very Massive in scale depending on your settings +On Sale   Cons- -At first seems like a complete rip off of Supreme Commander -Gets tedious/repetitive after a couple hrs (but again it was on sale) -Lack of variety(different colors/names) (exact same units)  The similarity to Supreme Commander is undeniable, blow up the commander, mission accomplished.., I like that it is a faster and more simplified version of SC, the time it takes to build advanced structures/units compared to SC can be literally 30mins-1hr.. ridiculous SC....   If you have SC/ SC Forged Alliance, don't expect alot from this game.. Faster matches, storyline weaker, However Planetary battles.. In conclusion this game is a simplifed space/land battle with heavy emphasis on mass unit building, resource management is simple also, build a powerplant and a metal extractor (on designated spots). I'm going to stop comparing it to SC in case you don't own it. Your tech tree is broken into basic air, land/bot, naval structures and units. assuming you are playing skirmish (story mode you have to gather blueprints) once you build a basic X structure, you can build a basic x fabricator (mobile unit that constructs). That unit can build a advanced X structure that you can use to build advanced units, and a advanced fabricator, then there is a tech 3 unit and so on and so forth.. I really like it, I have SC also. I would recommend getting it on sale...  I have no clue about whatever shady dealings the company has done as far as releasing TITANS, and the original... I would recommond this as a stepping stone to SC... Get it...   7.5/10 Would launch a moon into a planet to destroy 5 structures again...!!!!

Jeez. Never have I played a game so nostalgic and heart-wrenching that I needed to curl up into a ball afterwards.  Emily is Away  is the story of you and Emily over the last year of high school and four years of college. The game lets you choose a screenname and a profile picture that sometimes have a small part in the story. But what really captures you is the realism of the interactions with Emily.  Most people (myself included) have gone through a similar experience in real life that the game simulates very nicely. But I should say that the ending   - regardless of the path you take -   will make a massive withdrawal from the bank of emotions: with interest. That being said, you will probably finish the game in about an hour. It's a great experience that will definitely make you think twice about how you value your friends and is definitely worth your time!

UPDATE #2 29th Nov: still no fix bar 1 patch that broke the controls for many folk and ignored the loading issue altogether. Since then the developer's community rep has gone into hiding and has not been seen again. If Bandai Namco can't support their own game why should you? Vote with your feet and support a developer that cares about its customers.   UPDATE: sadly I can't upgrade this review to one that is more positive as after 14 days of acknowledging these issues the developers have been completely silent. No patch or even a quick update for affected owners which is incredibly poor form by Namco Bandai.  As a result 2 weeks later still cannot play the game so have had no option but to stick in a refund request to Steam support.   Had the devs at least provided an update to keep owners in the loop (pun intended) I'd likely have foregone a refund and waited things out but without any indication of whether a patch is imminent, or indeed one will ever be released, it's simply not a risk I'm willing to take. A little communication goes a long way!!  I'm not saying you should or shouldn't buy the game, just sharing my experiences and how poorly supported this game has been to date. If you hit problems though just remember you have 14 days from purchase to request a refund (must have <2hrs gameplay).  ----  Like others when I try to launch the (mandatory) training levels the game hangs whilst loading and will have no option but to restart. Sometimes I am lucky and a reboot will allow me to play one of the levels but then the problem happens again.   My Xbox 360 wireless controller seems to work okay with the game (when I can play it) but the analogue stick on my Xbox One wireless controller is messed up and some directions work intermittenty, others not at all.  I I can only hope that a patch will be released shortly and I can update my review. But right now it is hard for me to recommend the game although I do appreciate that I may one of a small minority of owners who are affected.

Hitman Blood Money brings us the return of Agent 47 as the cool, calm, calculating contract clipper of casual clusterf*cks, seriously though if you are looking for solid experience that allows you to switch almost seemlessly betwen 'sneaky, silent sabotage' like a true wetwork professional and 'knock the front door down with my raging todger and anything with a pulse and/or looks at me funny gets a lead creme brulee' like a gun toting austistic adolescent who has revently been given several red bull IV's then look no more, my good chum.   The sprawling open levels and endless combinations of strategies allow you to try a multitude of different strategies, to name a few I myself have Poisoned donuts, Sniped a chicken man, knocked out a over zealous, horny housewife with animal tranquiliser, poured ether on a girls panties, come back from the dead and my personal favorite silently sniping a rich politician's son as he is sitting in a hot tub amongst bikini clad, bimbos with their pixilated cleavages out for the world to see.  In closing, if you find yourself on the steam sales this week and deciding whether you should buy FF8 or Sleeping dogs i would suggest you save your money and buy Hitman: Blood Money, because more than anything else it is fun, remember fun? before you cared about HD graphics or Realistic face textures? Give me Garotting a mall santa or Infiltrating the chicken suit party at Mardi Gras over 'oh my god his clothes actually look wet when he gets out of the water' anyday.  Gameplay 9 Graphics 6 Sound 6.5 Story 7 Replay Value 9.5  Final thought: The user makes this game, if you are an unimaginative asshat then perhaps you should stick to pwning nubs in Call Of Dooty. Otherwise I heartily recommend this game. 8/10

This game is highly underrated and has been given a questionable rating. This is possibly the top game in terms of a post-apocalyptic setting and gives players a pure believable survival experience. It's atmospheric setting in a darker world that we all know, and a plot to keep us enticed until the end shows us a gripping; captivating; depressing; miserable; and almost always utterly brilliant game.  The crafty and clever use of a dust-filled city may make up for the graphics that have been questioned but I am quite comfortable with how they are regardless. An interesting feature, which I would love to see more in games, is their use of the gun. We can stick people up. Literally, if you want something in this world you can point your gun in their face and watch as they cower. You can do the right thing and help people with supplies that are scarce, or rob them. Do not underestimate the scarcity of supplies because as I said before, it IS the most believable survival experience.  I actually felt like I robbed them for getting this on sale :/  Epic game and I am still looking for another game like it. 9/10

It was a fun game for the first few hours. Great graphics and sound, and they've created an immersive universe which feels just like the films.  However, once the alien appears the gameplay gets very tedious very quickly. It only seems interested in you, it completely ignores all the NPCs wandering around. Coupled with the fact that if the alien is scripted to be in 'a mission' it will just loiter around that area, suspiciously close to your character, seemingly teleporting around rather than moving along corridors/vents.  After the first few times it magically locates and kills you (the first time is indeed chilling), it just becomes a save/load fest devoid of any suspense or tension because you just wonder when the last time you saved it was and hope it wasn't too long ago. Compounding that issue the checkpoint save system will often have you redoing large portions of the game.  Another design problem worth mentioning is that when you encounter a hostile with a pistol and take them out, although their pistol drops and you can kick it about, you can't pick it up. You have to wait till the game allows you to obtain a pistol, which eventually is just sat on a desk.  I think it would have been a much, much better game if the alien didn't actually appear until much later in the game and you just heard it banging around in the vents, or maybe came across it's victims from time to time. Seeing it so often, and being killed by it so often, made the game ultimately dull and repetitive.

After playing Ace of Spades for over a day's worth of time, you know what it's like.   _The gameplay. That's what keeps this game going. All the gamemodes; Team deathmatch, Capture the Flag (Classic and Standard), VIP, Zombie, and more. You can build your own structures while in-game, and you can make your own maps. Plenty of Steam Workshop maps. _The graphics are... meh. Standard voxel graphics? I guess. Super simple, plain blocks. Not a strong point in this game. _AoS has some interesting zombie sounds 'n' stuff. Cool soundtrack. Solid audio. _The community? Eh, it's okay. Plenty of players if you wanna play CTF or Zombie, but other than those two gamemodes, not a whole lot of activity. You never know if you are gonna run into a friendly server or not. _Now, for my personal opinion: You can play this game as much, and for however long you want. I would buy this game for full price again for sure, but look out for sales, I seen it for $3.75 before. I have had, and will continue to have, a blast with this game. Ace of Spades won't go down in history as one of the best games ever (it has it's haters), but I certainly have enjoyed it. So many things wrong, and I haven't even gotten into the meat of the game yet at all. :( Very dissapointing, because the trailers and screenshots looked nice, and- on the whole- the gameplay was pretty good.  The real let-down though was how incredibly generic things were. I adore sci-fi of all kinds, but this literally brings nothing new at all to the table, either in terms of design or story. My first mission had me hunting bugs that looked so much like the ones from Starship Troopers that I'm a little surprised nobody has been sued yet, and straight after that it's off for a generic 'protect this item from swarm' mission set around a mining machine straight out of Dune. Even the basic story is one that has been done better many times over, and the introductory cinematic was so over-the-top super-serious melodramatic that it was actually a little bit cringe-worthy to sit through.  Another huge flaw, is no character customisation until you are actually IN the game. No option other than to pick a half-clad woman with a tiny head or a huge over-muscled brute (also with a tiny head, and probably a tiny something else, too...). Once in-game, nobody points you towards where you can change hair, face, or eyecolour, and even when you do find it, most options are not available unless you pay/grind for them. For any game in the MMO genre at the moment, this is a bit of a fail rigt off the bat. Even the paid-for options are pretty poor, to be honest, and would not inspire me to run out and either spend the time grinding to get them, or shell out real money.   Interestingly, the suit designs are more interesting.   I feel this game's biggest failure, however, is how seriously it takes itself. It's designed with a cute, anime-ish cel-shaded appearance, and drones on as if it's a greek tragedy in space. If it had exhibited some kind of humour, it might have been possible to rough out the introductory missions (all of which were just 'go here and shoot this' missions- nothing to write home about) and get into the game properly. As it is, it's really not even worth the download time and I would not recommend it at all.

That's right - you can still tweak the game to your liking with NPCs, Tweaks, and various other mods. Just google 'BGEE MODS'.  Frankly, the ability to mod the game was the real question - and since there are a number of great mods to choose from that work, you're good to go. Well, I'm gonna keep it short. Single-Player => good Co-Op => good Multi-Player => OH GOD NO, BURN IT WITH FIRE!  While SP and Co-Op can be very entertaining, with a decent story, although sometimes rather easy or boring, the MP is just horrible. Why, you might ask? well... Matchmaking, simple as that. Matchmaking continuously ruins my personal MP experience due to completely unfair matches and such. probably 95 out of a 100 matches end up with either a random clan or organized lobby on the enemy team, or simply players with full level. So unless you can bring along a full suite of 4 friends to play against random players online (or maybe even up to 8 people for a private match), you'll end up having one hell of a painful time trying to play, being either melee'd by an enemy (even though you've been mashing the melee key ~20 times 3 to 5 seconds beforehand), getting insta-melee'd upon spawning (yes, there is NO spawn protection, so if you're out of luck, you might end up with waiting ~20 seconds to spawn, then instantly getting melee'd and then having to wait another ~20 seconds) or simply being unable to find good spots on the map to hide/ambush/fight from due to half of the enemy team knowing most of the spots already (most likely, unless they havent bothered with it).  If you're looking for a good SP and/or Co-Op game, buy this now! If you're looking for an enjoyable MP experience, either hope you have better luck than me, or bring along enough friends. Otherwise, avoid this like it would give you cancer, AIDS and Black Death all at once.

So what i really think about Magicka 2 compared to Magicka   pros : -Better graphics / art style -Amazing soundtrack -Crazy looking new abilities and quick cast options -The maps can be massively explored -Weapons and staff's have benefits and detrements instead of just elemental shields or quick cast spells (the quick cast spells from the wands in Magicka have been moved to the quick cast buttons 1,2,3,4 instead of middle buttoning with a staff) -Player customizability is REALLY good.  cons: -AoE is really really small, it pretty much got nerfed... -Cant spam abilities as fast as Magicka, theres a few milli seconds worth of delay between ability casts (minor con) -The single self shield got nerfed, and the barrier spells got nerfed (stone walls with/or withought elements) -Less magicks, cant combine water + fire or water + ice to create 1 new element (so less space in queu)  All in all, i was really debating buying Magicka 2. But its a must buy if you want to continue with the story, cause other than the few cons, the game is really fun.  My final rating would be a 7/10   Forgot to mention you can blow up sharks and horses now. 10/10 would blow again.  EDITS: additions to cons, pros

TLDR:  Atmosphere: 10.5/10 - An always beautiful landscape with an overwhelming tone of dread that seeps through the pores with every step. With a real-world cycle of Day and Night, the threats are forever changing keeping the game's atmosphere fresh and terrifying. Side missions do an excellent job of making the world feel alive, even if mostly everyone around you is dead.  Sound: 10/10 - Being surrounded by the infected, sounds have never been more important. Techland did a beautiful job rendering the effects. You can hear every distant gargle, every faint scream, every angry grunt. The sounds for this game are prominent, crisp and clear.  Gameplay: 9/10 - Crafting items and weapons has always been a core element in open-world games, but the way Techland presents it makes it accessible to anyone, which isn't always easy to do. While the amount of Blueprints are abundant, the weapon variety is not very diverse. After putting about 10 hours in I had seen every weapon I was going to see for the rest of my playthrough, which was dissapointing. The free running is the biggest aspect of this game because it is such a new concept to include in a zombie genre title. It really brings the mechanics full-circle and makes the feel of surviving and traversing the landscape more realistic and in your control. That being said, it would have been nice to have implemented your parkour into your fighting a little more. Also, some of the story missions tend to miss the mark on occasion. You'll even find that some of the side missions are more enjoyable than some of the story missions. That is a little unfortunate. However, the ever awesome new game plus unlocks once you complete the main story and it lets you carry over everything except main/side mission progress, increasing the overall games difficulty to a more suitable zombie apocalypse. It is truly an awesome feature that I am highly glad they added.  OVERALL: 9.5/10 - It is a grand zombie-slaying experience and it is also the pinnacle of zombie gaming thus far. The amount of content you get for the price is no doubt worth it. I recommend it for any gamer and for just plain fans of zombies. This is just a must have title.  HIGHLY RECOMMENDED.  Good night. Good luck. ________________________________________________________________  BIGGEST STRENGTHS:  Free Running - The city of Harran is literally your playground. You have complete freedom of movement, which is everything in crucial situations...like a zombie apocalypse. You can scale brick walls, thirty story buildings, giant fences, city bridges; jump over vehicles, rooftops, even crowds of zombies in order to stay alive. You can upgrade to use a grappling hook and get to hard to reach places or get inventive with your parkour! The movement if very fluid, it just takes some getting used to. It has a real weighted feel to it which adds a very nice dynamic to the gameplay that I dig alot, personally.  Day / Night Cycle - This is my favorite thing about this game, hands down. During the day you have an advantage because the zombies are slowed down by bright light so they act and react lethargically. But at night...everything changes for the worse. Common infected become stronger and more aggressive, while Virals get even angrier and tend to hunt you down in packs. The stars of the show are clearly the Volatiles, though. With their mandible jaws and mutated nude appearances, they look like they were ripped straight from a Clive Barker short story. They only emerge from their hives at night to stalk the city streets in search of prey. They actively pursue you and if they catch you in their sights they will alarm the others. This is when you either stand your ground or you run like you're on fire.  Online Zombie Mode / Co-op - You or others can invade games as a Unique Infected called a Hunter. It is PVP, but you have the entire environment to stalk them. Like the humans, this Infected comes with a skill tree to upgrade it's zombie abilties to become the ultimate predator. Online can be played with up to four players, including the story line...which is pretty groovy.  **NEW GAME PLUS** - I could not stress how important this is in an open world game. It was such a nice surprise. New game plus unlocks upon 100% main story completion and it lets you carry over your stats and inventory while resetting your campaign / side story progress. This does quite a few interesting things to the AI. Not only do they all act more aggressive all around, there are way more of them. Each kind, too. I've been finding Bomber's and Goon's left and right just filling the streets. At night time, I thought the original campaign was enough Volatiles. Apparently not. On NG+ it's like a Volatile BREEDING GROUND up in this ♥♥♥♥♥, I swear to God. It's the most amazing thing ever. After plugging 40 hours into the original I was no longer threatened. I could kill anything I wanted and not worry about taking too much damage. With the enemy's newfound strength and population...I can actually feel my heart beating again....   OTHER STRENGTHS:  Crafting - Collect a large amount of blueprints by exploring the city and doing missions. Collect the necessary items on the blueprint to create badass weapons of mass destruction...or different medicines to give you advantages in battle. Not only does it give you another excuse to explore the maps, it gives more depth to the melee system that is the core of this game.  *Mission Structure - This is a special case for me. As far as story's 'writing' goes, they knocked this out of the park. I was almost always on the edge of my seat and ready for the next mission to see where they will take it. The story has a lot of depth and a lot of heart, the Dev's clearly wanted that to come first. But as far as the mission structure goes...it varies. While they were fun and definitely a joy to play, albiet may be for the initial excitement of the first playthrough, but overall it felt a little underwhelming at some parts....In my own opinion I sometimes felt a little restrained by the game itself. When there were times that screamed for me to take action the character was set on autopilot for narrative's sake. I do understand why, but it's still unfortunate because it took away some of the immersion. This game has quite a bit of innovation as far as gameplay mechanics and zombie killing, but there was also some missed potential that you could feel under the surface. It's definitely a gigantimous skyscraper of an improvement over it's predecessor and I highly reccomend it to anyone who games. I just sometimes find myself looking forward to what they are going to do next more than what they gave us. The side missions were worked on really hard and that also is very apparent. They are usually multi-layered and the voice acting is spot on. You can feel there is a lot of emotion and when the story is mixed altogether with the base game it feels a lot more comfortable and wholesome. Techland did an outstanding job at making the environment around us a living and breathing world. I dig it alot and I can get lost in it for hours on end.  Skill Tree - Like a lot of open world games, this one has skills to unlock the more you progress. Each skill makes you more of a bad ♥♥♥ and there are a lot of cool skills here to play with. In this you have three medium sized trees to choose between: Survivor, Agility and Power.
