A couple nights before release, I had the pleasure of playing a board game called Tokaido. I had just lost badly at a very strategic game where I had made some critical errors early on that hampered my score too badly to come back. Tokaido, though, brought me right back. It is a game about a journey, where you are rewarded for taking your time, seeing the sights, exploring and completing. No Man's sky is a similar game.  Many people were expecting more from No Man's Sky. Of course those people were disappointed. There was a lot of hype, a lot of speculation, maybe a couple expectations set too high. Make no mistake, a universe of worlds to explore, procedurally generated, is no small feat. Hello Games is to be commended for their ambition.  What we have here, right now, is a single player game that's different for everyone who plays it. I don't expect to ever see a system discovered by another player. With the way the algorithms work, and the amount of content, I can see myself hitting a wall in maybe another 20 or 25 hours. Maybe then I'll start all over, from the beginning, after a break. The game's like a journey, and a journey should have a beginning and an end and should be an escape and not a pastime or profession. So far I've seen some weird things, some amazing wonders.   The graphics, well I can see why they are an issue for some people. I am very lenient with grpahics myself and it does not bother me that some things are pixellated and sometimes floating objects don't draw into full size untill I'm almost past them. I already feel like the gameplay is a bit lacking in challenges, but I am not a skill gamer either. I've played many AAA games that tried too hard, set expectations improperly, and came out feeling empty. Spore comes to mind immediately as a comparison, and so far I'm certain this is no Spore.  No Man's Sky is a game, like Tokaido, about a journey. It's about seeing the sights, taking your time, and meandering towards a goal. The hype is over, the game is here. Those who can't accept an ambitious indie game that's not a platformer coming out with a few bugs will leave. More undiscovered stars in that endless ocean of space, will never be seen by human eyes for that reason. It's a vastness that thrills me to the bone, and I'm diving in deep.

In short: a complete disappointment.  Not at all what is show in the videos, the game is sold as being one experience that totally is not there. All I can hope now is for STEAM to refund me via Steam Wallet.

This is a really fun baseball game, something that the PC has been sorely lacking. Don't let the cartoony characters throw you off. There is a lot of depth and challenge here. I especially appreciate that the difficulty sliders, or 'ego' settings, can be varied in four categories; batting, pitching, fielding, and baserunning.I've always enjoyed the batting and pitching part of PC baseball, and less so the fielding, so I let the AI handle more fielding than the others.  Hitting has a very solid feel to it. When you jack one out of the park, you really do feel a sense of getting all of it. Solid base hits and extra base hits feel equally rewarding.  The 'mojo' facor and emotional reactions are a brilliant touch. Watching the pitcher and batter literally sweat it out in a duel at the plate in late innings in a close game really brings the game alive.   There also seems to be a very nice balance in letting the AI batters swing at pitches off the plate rather than having a perfect eye like so many other baseball games have had. Even the umpires seem to have some leeway in their strike zone.  Congrats to the developers. They were also very responsive to an issue I had posted in the dicussions page, which did indeed turn out to be a problem with my controller.

Besides the survival and crafting gameplay elements, the game offers a decent and solid RPG experience with different types of armor, weapons, enchantments and skills for you to develop your own unique character.  Definitely worth the 10 bucks.

OK. I heard a lot of the reviews saying that this is the weakest part of Half Life 2. People say that it's still good but it's not as good as the others. Well, I can't believe i'm going to say this but I enjoyed this way more then Half Life 2. Yeah, seriously! To me, Half Life 2 felt like a really long and sometimes boring but really good action film (the ravenholm chapter was defiently my favourite like many other reviewers have agreed) but this felt like a really, really good short action movie from start to finish. The levels in this episode are so cleverly laid out and they were so fun to complete. In Half Life 2, there were a few boring and frustrating sections like the part where you lay down turrets in a sort of tower defense mission for the tower being you or the boat ski sections, but in here, there was not one dull moment. For god's sake, the episode starts (NO SPOILERS BY THE WAY) with you being pulled out of a wreckage, going into a car and then the car falls down this combine hole as you go on a scripted rollarcoaster sequence. Tell me if that's not awesome.  So in the end, I liked this episode way more than the original game. I moaned that in Half Life 2 there wasn't a speaking protaginist and the loading times were too long but here I can accept it for the less amount of loading times and the fact that not many people are talking at you at once. So becasue of that: Half Life 2: Episode 1 get's a 10/10.

Great game. It has such a simple systematic behind it, still it's very complex. Every session is different, depending on which people are playing. So it puts you right on the edge, where it's a tough decision. Didn't have this much real fun in a game in a long time.  I understand the negative reviews. It's frustrating if you buy a claimed finished game, and it's broken all the time. A finished game that's full of bugs and stuff is a real pain in the a**. And it borders to a scam that its not 'early access'. And some bugs really destroy the experience, but I think it`s getting better. Wouldn't give a positive feedback if it wasn't.  But for the moment, it`s at least a fun game to play with friends. I cought myself smiling while playing it. It's really really fun...when it works.

One of the few games I can keep coming back to, which makes it one of the better MMO's out there.  There is so much freedom, character creation wise, you can either go with a pre-made skill tree, or you can put one together yourself, I've found myself to do this more frequently, not one character I make is the same, ever, this keeps it interesting, not to mention I can unlock more if I ever find the desire to spend the money to unlock it with, not yet though, there's still much to explore without spending a dime! I've spent about 10 Euro as I'm writing this, and compared to a lot of the games I've played, time/money ratio is excellent,¨(I am an extremely patient player, so this may vary from person to person) So even if this is a F2P game now, don't be afraid of spending a little cash, unlike some games, what you buy stays with you, so you don't get screwed over and feel like you're being punished with a 'demo' once you quit paying. I'm looking at you, DDO, LoTRO and you other little ninjas hanging out there.  One complaint though, it's way too easy, with the group I frequently play with, not even well geared, we can handle dungeons undermanned, we can solo rifts meant for a standard group, it's actually one of the reasons I never play this game longer than a month or two at a time, before having to take a break again, as there's no real challenge, it can't be right that I can tank an entire boss as a mage without getting hurt. I hear this was very different before it became F2P, why it would change for the worse, I don't really understand, mass appeal for the casual gamers out there perhaps?  There's a few features that I'd like to see implemented: - Being able to play music, I was once a very loyal LoTRO player, and there was nothing better than freestyling on a lute for me, in my house. - Lotteries, chance to win ingame cash, mounts, pets, cosmetics as long as you sign up from the marketplace perhaps. - Actual challenges, I hate fighting a boss and suddenly seeing 50+ people taking them down in a matter of minutes when it was supposed to be difficult - force their damage to event related bosses, to be lowered perhaps? Anything? Also, it seems to be headed in the direction of WoW, where you can win by simply rolling your head on the keyboard, please don't do that, it's the reason I quit playing that game (back when you could only be level 40 or so). - I'd love to see more races with more unique traits, it doesn't seem like that anything you pick will ever really be more than a simple convenience for you at times, where most of it only functions out of combat, or makes such a little difference that simple looks wins the battle over practicality, and doesn't even deserve a spot on your hotkey bar after you reach up high in the levels. - Fix the auction, I'm at that point in the game where I don't care if I buy one thing for 2 platimum one day, and 32 for the same thing another day, simply because I make so much that money has lost its value to me entirely. There is no sense of consequence about frivelous spending whatsoever. - Progress is too fast and 'linear', from the time I started I feel like I could max everything out in no time at all, on top of that, once you've played through the first time, you find yourself rushing things because there's only a few different paths you can take before you've played every single mission and storyline, add a little more factions, areas, rivalry between factions (meaning you won't be loved by everyone in the end, add consequence and critical thinking!!) - Add more variety to the adventures, they stay fun for 5 minutes, then you start seeing the same pattern repeating itself over and over, this makes me quit, repetition is a game killer.  All that being said, I enjoy this game more than most, it just needs that little kick to actually keep me in the seat more than what it does, and it's a sure winner in my book.

I recommend this game personally because I enjoyed it and I think it's worth giving it a try. Whether or not you'll actually enjoy it too depends.   Diaries of a Spaceport Janitor is a cute and adorable little game with a nice aesthetic. The music and atmosphere is very good too, especially on the day of the week when there's a festival on. On that day it's nice just to walk around and take in the world that's been set up for this game. It's a shame more isn't done with the world though. But maybe that's the point, there's a lot going on in the world, but it doesn't affect you, you're just a janitor.   In that way, the gameplay of this game is really quite minimal. All you really do is run around the spaceport day in and day out, picking up and incinerating trash for your measly pay at the end of the day, as well as finding rarer items and running around the port for someone who might like to buy it from you. You'll be turning tiny profits each day after you've fed yourself and maybe bought the drugs you're addicted to.   Your goal in this game is to scrape together enough money to try and get off the planet and leave your life as a spaceport janitor behind. This game starts with you being hit by a curse that you get by going into a dungeon thinking you can turn your life around by getting a great treasure. The curse doesn't really seem to do anything though, besides lowering your (very easily raised) luck stat, and every now and then go BWEH at you.   But the game needs a goal, so you spend your time in the game trying to get rid of the curse, which is done though a series of quests all of which just need you to get together the money to buy the items you need by burning trash and selling items.   But, I kind of just like running around the spaceport picking up trash.  If you don't enjoy that part of the game, there's not much else going for it. I liked it enough to keep going until the end, which took me just over 7 hours. You might got bored before that, but it's not an expensive game. I still think it's worth a try, despite it's bugs and flaws.

This game was really fun for about the first 3 hours, but I can't recomend this game in its current state. The visual style is fantastic, but it would have to be in order to hide the visual glitches that you see throughout the game.  Tecnhical issues are everywhere. Let's start with the biggest issue I've hit so far. When I saved and quit my game ALL OF MY ♥♥♥♥ DISSAPEARED FROM MY CHEST. This included NECESSARY ITEMS for the progression of the game. If I can't trust my box not to delete things like that this game to me is unplayable.   On top of that there is a weird glitch which cuts the screen in half and rotates it all over the place which I think is a visual glitch. The worst part of this one is that the game has great effects that it plays when you're sick or high on space drugs so sometimes I can't tell the glitches apart from the effects that are put there intentionally. This problem is made EVEN WORSE by the fact that it is easy to ignore the actual issues your character is having at the same time as you are ignoring the weird blur pop-in and things like that.  However, when your character is sick and the real effects start to become prominent it really gives me the feeling of powering through discomfort. When your character gets tired it just becomes difficult to control the game and all you want to do is make it back to bed. About 3 hours in I still can't afford anything in the shops but I've found cool ways to make a bit more money.  One of my favorite parts of the game is that the map is confusing and not just given to players. It's very easy to get lost when you don't know your way around and I had to ACTUALLY get out a piece of paper and a pencil in order to keep notes on where people are and what I've found around the spaceport.   The notifications in the top left hand corner of the screen only show one thing that you need at a time. So if you haven't gotten any gender for a while (or maybe you just don't want to spend the money on this useless second food and accept the consequences) you won't know when you're also hungry unless you really look for the effects on the screen; and as much as I like the idea of those distortions slowly creeping into your vision, the visual glitches are so prevalent that it can be hard to tell what you're looking at.   The Elephant in the Room: I'm willing to acknowledge that alien genders necessarily exist in a universe with aliens in it. I'm not even mad that you can change it by eating a gender chip or whatever the thing is... I just don't understand why you can get the things like candy out of a local vending machine when the withdrawl effects are blurred vision and vomiting. And 'Spiny gender' sounds more like a race to me. The description is 'you get spikes all over you and...' so is that going to help me sleep with aliens that procreate using spines? Does this stuff have anything at all to do with sex??? I just wish that the gender thing were more useful or interesting instead of just a flavor text resource that you have to keep getting.  Also, I got the one that destroys my gender and I became like a Catgender or something. Why am I not GENDERLESS if I DESTROYED my gender? Confusing design decisions like this make this game almost impossible to fully grasp because you can't tell what is important and what is just nonsense for the sake of it.  This game needs a lot of work. The first portion of the game needs to be expanded on. Quest markers don't show up correctly sometimes or they show up over people I've already spoken to. I want so badly to love this game because it's fun and interesting with some great ideas driving it. This to me is precisely the kind of thing that should have been released in early access.

Hoo boy, this is one tough ♥♥♥♥ cookie. Where to begin, where to begin... ok, how about the beginning of the game.  You start the game (after a stupidly easy 'boss' fight) by having your ♥♥♥♥ sucked by two clones of the Olsen twins with zero facial muscles and incredibly annoying voices. You then attempt to play pool with balls that defy all known laws of movement. A quick strut down an ego-hall leads you past a bunch of women (who still have no facial muscles) grabbing their boobs at the sight of you, down to a TV host (who still has no facial muscles) who tells you your interview has been cancelled due to aliens lining up to get a hamburger. No, seriously, the first thing this intergalactic menace does is go pick up some burgers.¹  After this, you get about 20 hours worth of incredibly linear shooting galleries with brain dead AI, poorly executed sex jokes, and one of the dumbest ideas in video game history, which I will describe after the line break for emphasis.  OK, that was the line break. Now, one of the major selling points of this game was the variety of weapons. All the trailers up to the ones just before release advertised the huge variety of weapons. Well, Mr. Pitchford, if you're so proud of the game's wide weapon variety, WHY THE FLYING ♥♥♥♥ WOULD YOU IMPOSE A TWO/FOUR WEAPON LIMIT?  OK, that's the single player out of the way. The multiplayer nearly - NEARLY, you understand - made the game recommendable. Good maps (mostly), fun mutators, a wide range of customization options, low lag (mostly), quite fun. Now I know what you're thinking: 'But you seemed to like it, why would you not recommend the multiplayer'? Well, it's quite simple. I liked the multiplayer because it reminded me a lot of Unreal Tournament, one of the greatest multiplayer games ever. It then struck me that it would be a much better idea to just go buy one of the Unreal Tournament games instead of buying this for its multiplayer component. Plus, unlike this, the Unreal Tournament games still have a strong playerbase.  I've heard that the 'Doctor Who Cloned Me' DLC was decent, better maps, actual funny jokes, facial muscles, but... I dunno.  TL; DR version: Meh at best single player, good multiplayer, but you'd be better off buying Unreal Tournament for multiplayer purposes.  (Oh, in case you're wondering, no, there is not a single facial muscle in the entire game.)  ¹ - OK, I admit, that bit was funny.

Bastion is one of the best indie games I have ever played. It is a piece of art. From the graphics, to the soundtrack, to the narrator and the game mechanics, it is flawless. The story is great, especially the way it is told by the narrator, who can be consider the second Morgan Freeman. One aspect I really like about this game it is that you can adjust the challenge through the game activating 'Gods'. The 'Gods' will make the enemies stronger but you will also gain more experience and 'money'. The game has also a very versatile combat system, because there are many different weapons, melee and range. You are able to carry 2 weapons, plus a secret skill, which you able to try different combinations of weapons and drastically change the gameplay. I absolutely recommend this game to anyone, because it can be really easy if you wish so or you can make it very difficult. Anyone will enjoy the story, the grapichs and the soundtrack. 10/10.

app_name: Company of Heroes: Tales of Valor
review_text: Company of Heroes is one of the best WW2 RTS games I have ever played. Of course, like all games, it is not without faults. However, the vast majority of gameplay concepts and mechanisms have been perfected to such a level that you will not regret your purchase.  I am a big fan of WW2 history and I have always wanted a real-time strategy game that would allow me to master combined arms tactics which were developed during WW2. The game has a wide variety of units including but not limited to infantry, tanks, mobile artillery, mortar and light armored vehicles. In order to defeat your enemy, you will have to use a combined arms approach that integrates all of these units. You won't be able to steam roll through the enemy lines with a single Tiger tank as it would get torn to shreds by Bazooka-wielding infantry units.  Overall, I must say Relic has created a true gem for all WW2 game fans. I wholeheartedly recommend buying this game!

The game was pretty fun when it started and I had originally given it a good rating, the game is plagued by massive balance changes that alter the gameplay at it's core. These occur every few months and players will find themselves having to re-learn everything outside of the core fps basics. Aircraft control physics feel like the change with every few updates, very unsettling for frequent pilot players, and while the theme of the UI remains the same. Every so often you'll login wondering what the hell you click on to go to point A and then wonder how you ended up at point Z when there was no visual queue indicating you could be going ther at all.  The game is by no means terrible and many people still enjoy it however it is nowhere near the quality it was when it first came out. massive battles that could spontenously occur anywhere have been reducted to predictable skirmishes at predetermined points to cater to a Generation Z player base used to instant gratification and reward for failure.  If you want to play a game were you are rewarded for failure and giving it half of your effort, PlanetSide 2 is the game for you.

One of my favourite games to play when I'm bored with CS:GO, MW3 (or any other game for that matter!) is Little Inferno. Little inferno is a great example of a pointless, addicting game! Little Inferno lets you burn things, which gives you money, so you can buy more things to burn! Pointless? Obviously, but the game has you hooked to it for about an hours worth of playing! It is most ideal to play this game when you are either bored of playing your usual games in your steam library or just for a bit of fun! The graphics for this game have a realistic, cartoon-ish tone to them, plus a nice, hot, glowing fire! The gameplay is pretty easy to learn to, as all you have to do is click your mouse for fire, and the chain-reaction lets you watch the fire burn down into crumbly black ashes. Little inferno is an average game in my opinion yet it kept me watching my computer screen and holding onto my mouse the whole time! Overall, Little Inferno is an average, basic puzzle with new and unique gameplay that gets me addicted everytime!  Little Inferno's Rating is as Followed: Gameplay: 6/10 Graphics: 4/10 Landscape: 4/10 Uniqueness: 8/10 Fun I Had Playing It!: 7.5/10  Overall: 5.9/10 C  Yours sincerly, Liam

Ah, Ace of Spades, where to start. This game used to be the ♥♥♥♥. I played it before it came to Steam, and it was a simple, yet addicting mix if Team Fortress and Minecraft, and had touches of Battlefield and Roblox. So when it came to Steam, I was understandibly excited. They had greatly improved the gameplay, and had added a plethera of maps, gamemodes, weapons, and 4 classes. The gameplay was tight, the graphics greatly improved, and each class was pretty balanced (Except for the Sniper).   And then came the updates. First they added a new DLC, which was 1930's Chicago themed, (No idea why they chose that theme), and for me and many other people, the game would crash instantly when you played it for the next few months. That was the begiining of the end. People could only play the DLC maps if you owned the DLC, and the gamemodes were exclusive to those 2 maps. (People can probably agree with me that City of Chicago would have been an awesome Zombie map.   Then they changed the skin of the commando to look more 'Rambo-y', which looked awful, then they nerfed the Rocketeer class, and turned it into the 'Engineer'. I actually stopped playing then, 'cause everything that was fun about the Rocketeer (verstile, hit-and-run tactics, great in zombies), and nerfed it. Instead, the Enginner had very low health, a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ jetpack that ran out of fuel almost instantly, weaker weapons, and weaker items. About half the community, said '♥♥♥♥ this' and walked away. This just left the new players, and the whiny 12 year olds who spammed sniper. This completly ruined the game. Then as if they hadn't done enough damage, they removed the ability to browse servers, and added a lazer module to the Sniper, so whenever you zoomed in, your position would be revealed to everyone on the map. Then they just went ahead and added the Chicago DLC to the game, so people had essentaily wasted 5 dollars on buying it before-hand. The final straw was when they added the 'Battle Builder' element. Sounds pretty fun right? No. No it wasn't. The game would lag when ever you tried to put down a larger model, and often the maps were so big that the game would crash. Also nobody played the user-made content. People can't even host their own games.  Right now I can unfortunetly not reccomend Ace of Spades to you. If Jagex (who has a long history of runing games) decides to patch the game, and remove all of these mistakes, then play it again. But right now, the game is broken, the achivements are unattainable, the community is awful, and filled with griefers and hackers, and the gameplay has been completely ruined. So, with great sadness, I say that this game is unplayable currently, and I would not advise buying it.

Very rough around the edges, some of the worst illustrations I've ever seen in a game, and poor UI graphic design can't stop the main event being massive fun. Stronghold is back!  Once you get past the horrible front-end, you'll find an addictive and highly engaging RTS in the style of AOE3, with a highly developed castle building element. The economy echos the Anno games too, but in a much more streamlined form.  In-game graphics are good, with nice character designs, and some good animations. Castles look well planted, and the environment is fairly interesting.  Voice acting is as excellent as ever, but it sounds like clips from the older games have been re-used, including much of the fantastic music (possible the best I've heard in any game).  The game has currently dropped a lot of the whimsical characterisation of the peasants, and certain popular features, such as the castle enhancements and punishments, have been left out, but crucially, Crusader 2 plays better than the original Stronghold. City management is easy and rewarding, castle designing is incredibly simple and flexible, and the siege element is the best in any RTS yet.  There's much more to come, according to the developers, and i believe them. Stronghold Crusader 2 is the game that should have been made years ago. It captures the Stronghold atmosphere perfectly and actually improves on the original's core game play.

This game showed me much of what has happened in the three decades of Video Game History. It was a mind blowing experience for me to go from Classic action-RPG of Zelda to a 3D RPG like Final Fantasy, Diablo, and some other popular titles under those titles. When I played through certain parts, There was an mini-game which remind me of Final Fantasy VIII and IX, which was a card game. The Combat even varys as you go on through the game, giving you different experiences all together.  I will have to say, This game was inspiring. Inspiring to those who loved the older RPG classics, giving them an memory lane like trip with notable easter eggs from Final Fantasy, Zelda, and Diablo. Still I wish that it could include some Platformer elements or other game elements, so it felt like it spread out more than just the RPG titles, but throughout a part of gaming history.   This game is still great, even without the additional stuff I mentioned above. It's a game worth looking into, allows you relive the RPG experience of the past 3 Decades or learn of those elements from those times. The game deserves an 8 out of 10 for the experience it gives out. I doubt anyone is gonna care for my review, but I wrote it anyway.

I honestly did not expect there to be another installment of Hexcells, but Hexcells Plus proved me wrong. The game not only tests you with standard deduction reasoning for marking and breaking hexes like in Minesweeper, but also makes you think even deeper about all the clues that are given. I literally spent about five minutes staring at my screen before going 'Aha!' after a couple more moments of thinking. It feels very satisfying to break or mark a cell, knowing that you were able to figure everything out. Trust me, this game's a bit on the harder side, but the challenge is definitely enjoyable. If I had to think of a downside, I'd say that I was a bit disappointed that there was no save feature. As I said, the levels are hard and might take a while, so be sure to have lots of free time going into Hexcells Plus. Anyways, go give this a try if Hexcells left you begging for more or if you're just a fan of logic puzzles like minesweeper, sudoku, etc, just maaaybe wait for a sale to occur.

This game has some truly frustrating moments, such as an almost pitch black maze which I needed a walkthrough to solve. YEt, at the same time, it also has some really interesting ideas. Items can be examined in detail, for example, and discussed with your partner. The horror is also drawn mainly from atmosphere, rather than jump scares. And the story is long, complicated, interesting and doesn't talk down to you. The interface too is surprisingly effective and fun.   Don't get me wrong; the AI is hokey, the FMVs are often bizarre and the level design is atrocious at times. You may well need a walkthrough to finish certain elements - or just to understand how some new items or weapons work - but there are some good ideas in here which make ROTH worth a look for those with enough patience.

LOL. 30 minute wait? Send me an e-mail when you add more servers and I'll come back and play.  Edit: Next day, 59 minutes. We're going the wrong way here. Don't be fooled by the time playing, it continues to run while the thing is trying to connect.  Eidt: About a week and a half in and now there are no queue times. Well done developers, you've changed mine to a thumbs up.  So far, I really enjoy the gameplay. I can see where it may get a little redundant but it has enough variety to hold it together for a while. I do wish worlds were persistant. Each time you port to the level 7-8 world, for example, it is a new world. It would be neat to have landmarks and such and maybe this is something that comes with club worllds or later levels. Crafting is pretty much built around home appearance and a few weapon advancements. I'm only level 11 now and maybe the game holds more in store for me? Now that the queues appear to be fixed, I'll be sticking around to check it out.  Edit: Spoke to soon. Back to 30 minute queues two days in a row.  So, that's not bad if you have the time to wait around and play solo but if you're trying to coordinate and play with friends, we don't all have the time for this.  It really does seem like the Devs are working on this so hopefully it gets worked out soon.

It took me a few tries but I finally got into this game and now I'm hooked. It really does take a while to get started and the pace really is slow so I understand that some people might be turned off by it. But I stuck with it and allowed myself to get immeresed in all the little stories you start weaving together once you start exploring more and now I can't stop playing.  I'm also not a fan of Rogue-like games in general but since this is more Rogue-lite I find it more forgiving that other games like FTL. I feel like the rogue-like aspect of the game is just enough to keep the tension up during your long travels but not so hard that it feels frustrating. Plus, as long as you live long enough to have a will, it also ensures that you'll be better equipped when you start a new game.   All in all it's a nice little game that I play if I want something atmospheric and not too intense too play.

So let me get this straight. I buy the game, and then I'm expected to buy content that's literally three-times the cost of the game I just bought in order for me to feel like I get the full experience and options. That's literally disgusting. I feel violated as a consumer. The outrageous EARLY-RELEASE-DLC prices, plus the way I know the Publisher/Developer is treating the community of modders, can only lead me to speculate that these idiots don't care about anything except for loading their wallets, while carrying complete disregard for customer-satisfaction. Here's a fun fact: Tecmo actually came out and said that they used fan-inspired artwork for some of their costume-packs. This further discourages me from telling anyone to buy this game. Why? I'll answer with a question of my own; how is it, that it is ok to take fan-inspired work, and sell it for ridiculous sums of money, but it's not ok for fans to share their own ideas for the game with each other, FOR FREE? In Tecmo's head, I feel like the only answer is that they cannot monopolize on such activity. Disgusting behavior. Absolutely disgusting. I know there are a lot of other reasons this port isn't good either, but the way Tecmo has been treating the PC community just felt so disrespectful-- I just had to put my two cents in. Don't buy this game. Just don't.

Mega Coin Squad is an original and entertaining local multiplayer game with loads of replayability.  Each game is composed of several rounds of randomly chosen (or create your own playlist) game modes. In between each round you get character powerups based on your rank in the last game.  The game modes consist of a simple deathmatch, a mode where you have to collect and bank the most coins, hold the rainbow for a certain amount of time, or collect a set number of megacoins (that spawn one at a time) and bank them to win. These modes are all quite different and fun.  Mega Coin Squad is a very nice addition to any local multiplayer lineup. For right now I'm going to say No for recommending. I've encountered cheaters/hackers and it takes more work to report them than for them to make a new account to continue cheating. Here's proof of a cheater  https://i.imgur.com/4DFV3qv.jpg  . Vote to kick wasn't working and to report a cheater you need to go to the Nexon site and go through a number of steps. Steam does not show people you have recently played so it can be hard to find their exact account. Balance seems off on somethings, but that'll just take time. The worst part is the store. Only 2 characters are free and there is a rotation of 3 characters free a week, I think it's per week. To unlock all characters at this time it'll take 470k ingame currency or $6-10 a character. I was averaging 4.4k points an hour while being in the top 3 pretty much every match. It would take me 100+ hours to unlock all characters without spending a dollar. On top of that, there are loadout cards. You get free equipment cases but the odds really are against you for getting something even decent. You can trade up on cards but it takes cards of the type you want plus ingame currency. The game content is locked in a way for you to buy characters and loadouts to experience the whole game kind of like the League of Legends approach. I would prefer a Dota2/TF2 approach where all characters are unlocked, all weapons are sidegrades, and cosmetics are what you buy if you'd like them.  The game was fun, but the problems chased me away from it. I feel like this is what Brink could have been. Just be aware that if you play, you may incounter cheaters/hackers and will not be able to experience the whole game without spending either 100+ hours or more than $50. Hopefully they will fix these issues before launching out of beta. I'd love for them to rethink their store too, but I doubt that will happen. It was fun for 10 hours, but all of the problems finally pushed me out of the game.

UPDATE 20 JULY 2015: Another game update. Still no tooltips on tech tree, no mouse on screen edge scrolling. I don't think they care about making the interface more PC-like at all. Either that or they are using some crossplatform framework which doesn't allow PC features so they -can't- make it palatable to pc users.   UPDATE 19 JUNE 2015: I checked the game again to update my review. Nothing has changed except there is now a full screen option.  There may be a good game inside here somewhere but I'm having trouble persevering with it due to the atrocious interface, which is exactly the same as the mobile one and makes no effort to take advantage of the PC.  The game runs in a window at a set size and you can't change it. (update there is now a fullscreen option) There are no tooltips, so you have to press buttons to find out what they do then click back. This is especially annoying in the technology tree where you have to click on everything one by one to find out what it is. It's seriously tedious. There is no mouse on edge of screen scrolling. No use is made of right click.  Just like a mobile game it has a 'please review the game' button and another button to 'more games'.  There's quite a long time between turns after you press next turn. Some kind of progress indicator would be reassuring.  I mentioned when the game first came to PC in early/mid September 2014 that they needed as a matter of urgency to move away from the mobile interface and make a new interface for PC and I seem to remember a dev saying they would be doing just that.  It's been nearly 6 months. It's clear they aren't going to do anything. The game has been written to make money on mobile and then as an afterthought they have dumped it on the pc to make some more money, but they aren't willing to actually put any work in beyond making it actually run on the pc.  The worst effect from the mobile interface is the tech tree, which I mentioned earlier. There are a ton of technologies on the screen and you have to click each one to find out what it is, (like Warp Drive 1 or Spanner Throwing 3 or whatever). There's no way to know just from the icon what the tech is and clicking it takes you to a whole new screen which pretty much just says the name of the tech, then you need to click back to go back.  It would be so, so easy for them to add tooltips to the tech tree and it would be remove a massive frustration from the game. They've had 6 months to do it and they haven't done it.  I have no respect for the devs at all.  I seriously suggest not buying this game.

This is a game that makes itself difficult to recommend.   If you've gone through other reviews, you probably know the graphics are great, the gameplay is solid. On an ATI card I had no crashing or game-stopping problems as of 9/12. A lot of people are upset about the microtransactions, and while on principle we can make hay out of this all day, it has zero impact on the meat of the game - I wouldn't know it existed if I hadn't read about it.   Having said that, what ultimately soured me on the game was the multitude of minor and thoughtless, but ultimately endless, fumbles on execution.  Acceptable gameplay hours, but the story doesn't possess the scope or heights of previous entries. They're obviously aiming for an arc, but it leaves what is presumably the first part of it fairly mundane and disappointing. In their favor, the side missions are far more fleshed out than previous ones, but their success only serves to underscore the mediocrity of the primary plot.  Having only a single hub leaves you running into later areas early and looting them dry. I couldn't count the number of times I had to go back to a building with enemies who seem not at all surprised about every door and computer being unlocked, every security system being disabled, and a pile of discarded items in the middle of the floor.  Gameplay achievements can be broken by storyline choices. If you're going for a no-alert run, google GARM and foxiest of the hounds or you'll wind up with 25 hours of wasted effort. Further story and endgame achievements are locked behind a mandatory credit roll that otherwise has to be task killed to stop - to make it worse, it's nothing but arrogant pictures of the devs being drunk and/or stupid.  Tons of lazy bugs. Dumpsters that go flying when you brush against them. Knocked out enemies that continue to groan and contort minutes after being knocked out. AI that's confused on when it should and shouldn't be upset.  Overall, it's a sophmore effort. Worth $30 at most, I have hopes that the devs will learn some humility from this and try harder on the next installment.

I love the Total War franchise, but its always a gamble for me when I try out new ones. Some, like Rome: Total War, are great, with a continous development and a possibility to actually conquer most of the 'world'. Others, like Empire: Total war, are too difficult and slow, making expansion take forever and always leaving you exposed no matter what you do.  Total War Shogun 2 has the perfect combination of continous development and difficulty. At the easy setting, you can more or less steamboat through the entire campaign, and at the hardest setting you have to plan your every single move. Personally I mostly play at the normal difficulty, where you get some opposition and have to be a bit tactical, but still see some development and progression in every other turn.  As with any Total War game, there's a high replay value, as each faction offers you a new challenge, with only the end-game becoming seemingly similar. Once you've played through this game a couple of times, I recommend getting the expansions: Fall of the Samurai & Rise of the Samurai, as these will further change the gaming experience and tactics that you have to use, without changing the difficulty (as did the expansions to Rome: Total War, which made that game unplayable).  Great for casual gaming, as I usually watch some tv-series or standup, with the game volume turned off.

D4 : Dark Dreams Don't Die is an episodic detective game in the tradition of tv shows like Twin Peaks or Lost.    Gameplay 7.5 of 10   The gameplay is typically point and click stuff. Find objects and interact with them. The question that remains is if it is always meaningful, e.g. you can click on a flower, squirrels, newspapers, the cat, and furniture, almost anything and are also able to interact with it. Which is kind of fun. But....you lose stamina. For every action you take, you lose some, which has to be refreshed by eating something. You also possess a detective 6th sense, which when activated, trigger a vision of meaningful items in the vicinity. But this also uses up the 'vision' bar. If you need more vision you need to drink a coffee, tequila or something. Very neat. I recommend you play the game with a gamepad, rather than with the keyboard. Because there are times where there are some seriously difficult and time controlled quick time events, where playing with a keyboard will fail you.   Graphics & Sound 7.5 of 10   The graphics are comic-style and have a very unique and distinct style. You can interact with various objects and the surrounding is very detailed. I would rate it a solid 7.5. The sound is very sparsely and only sometimes there are complete musical score played. It’s okayish. Was not that impressive, yet did not disappoint at all.   Story 8.5 of 10   The story is where this game really shines. Starting out just as another 'traditional' detective game, where you, David Young, are looking for the killer of you wife 'Little' Peggy. But the more you play you realize there is something wrong with the story. Because some facts are not adding up. Why can David travel through time and space? What's the meaning of the house cat and why can only David see a mysterious gigantic doctor. I won't spoil anything, but it's one of a kind story and something really unique in pc games.    Final verdict:    I can truly recommend this game for all gamers of detective games and if you liked Twin Peaks as well you will like this one for sure.

Note: This game was reviewed with a code provided by the developer this doesn't change my stance on the game.  Super Mutant Alien Assault is the unofficial Super Crate Box sequel that all of us have been waiting for.  Super Mutant Alien Assault as stated before is a very similar game to Super Crate Box which is a one screen platformer where the main goal is to survive long enough to see the other level, but this game has a ton of different objectives. A few of the things that levels have you accomplishing are surviving, bringing fuel, boosting thrusters and cool down pillars which will blow up and of course at the end of every galaxy there's a boss fight waiting for you.  Super Mutant Alien Assault takes place in the future where Earth has blown up and Alien ships are chasing human ships to destroy them and you play as a little droid who has to save humanity.  While the story wasn't needed it was certainly a very nice addition to an already pretty good game.   The graphics are great. The pixel art style is beyond awesome with the environments and the enemy designs being really cool.   The soundtrack is also really sweet, with techno and dubstep music accompanying the fast and tense gameplay. It might not be everyone's cup of tea, but I personally loved it and I think you will too.  The game has three galaxies to go through and each one of them is 4 levels long. All levels are randomly generated and when you die the levels will reset into something new which is a very welcome addition. To unlock the higher difficulties you will have to play through the game without dying once (which I wasn't able to do) so people who want replay value will have a lot to go through.   But people who don't like that big of a challenge don't worry, because after you unlock a galaxy you can start the game from whatever galaxy you want.  The game took me 1 hour to complete the three galaxies, but there's a lot of stuff to do. As mentioned before you can go and play through it without dying and there's also endless mode where you can put your skills to the test and try to survive as long as possible.  I didn't encounter any problems with the game whatsoever. It ran smoothly from beginning to end, so I doubt any of you will encounter any problems.  Super Mutant Alien Assault is incredibly fun and is definitely a game I will come back to in the future. It's incredibly fun and without a doubt the best Super Crate Box type of game I've played to date.  Pros: + Insanely Fun + Great Soundtrack + Awesome pixel art style  Cons: - Only Three galaxies   8.5/10

I decided to buy this game when I saw it on Youtube. Looked like lots of fun. I was wrong.  Well, first of all, it is totally random. Every strategy you have thought about could be destroyed after 1 unsuccesful roll of dice. BAM!, you lost half of your gold and you can not use a card which could lead you to the victory. Go on, take a walk around all board, get close to the required square and again... BAM!, you are arrested. Second big problem are hidden teamers in almost every multiplayer game. When you hope for a fair FFA4, but you meet 2-3 ppl playing only against you, the rage could explode your head. Despite the fact its kinda punishable, but there is no in-game mechanics to complain. You must have screenshots or video, post about it in forum and Devs look into every case manually.  Dont play multiplayer, try a game vs AI, you say? Well, if you choose to play vs stupid AI, its totally stupid and there is no way it can win. If you choose vs normal AI, its still stupid, but it cheats: the results of dice rolls doesnt correspond to the probability theory at all. Three-four times x1 in a row is a usual thing and its frustrating like hell. Despite everything mentioned above, this game can be funny and enjoyable, but only when you play it like a real board game: in a small group of friends. The concept of the game is great, but it has no effective security from malicious players. If you are a hot tempered person - dont buy this game, it will make your chair burn.

I do not reccomend this game. I will start with the most glareing reasons. I am sad to say this though because I love the concept and really tried to like the game.   So to start off with we will do Cons since I don't reccomend the game:  First Glaringly unplayable due to lots of issues with glitches. I have about 10 friends who play this game of those 10 atleast 3 experince glitches that range from weird and annoying to game breaking. I have videos on my stream of the gamebreaking glitches happening lots of times while I am live. I can go into details but most glitches include character models tripping out or worse broken game client that doesn't work/crashes entirely kicking you from the match.  There is a little box that comes up saying when did this happen? I wrote in a few times but quickly gave up and waited a few weeks for a patch. Most might say (oh it's on your end something isn't installed properly but I have done all the appropriate things to make sure the game should have no issues. I have a brand new I5-6600K processor 16 gigs of ram a gtx 980ti etc. etc. You get the point it was proffessionally built and less than 2 months old. No issues with any other game I play and verified intergritty of the files in steam. These glitches happen 1 out of 3 games and leave me relaunching with no points and a loss for the match.   Outside of that matchmaking is REALLY REALLY BAD. By this I mean the times are huge considering everyone is playing this game, the ranks are so spread out bottom ranks playing agasint top ranked players etc. one or the other would be meh fine. But I have an average of 3-5 minutes at least for queues and then I will have a rank or level that has atleast 10 times as much time in the game as I do. This coupled with the crashing make the game really hard to play in the first place.  Those are out of game issues or playability. So how is the game when it is running properyly? It is fun definitely fun. I'd probably change this to a reccomend but not a 10/10 OMG get this reccomendation. as it stands. The game has a fun concept solo tough guy vs 4 weak guys trying to escape. Think mario party but a bit more complicated.  With that being said it isn't super duper complicated. The concept is simple and easy enough to figure out after a match or two making the learning curve not too steep. However with perks and items there is enough to keep you invested for quite a while perfecting your master plans. I give it a 100 or 200 play hours before I would find myself bored or craving something new. However I can't do it with all the issues. With that being said 100 hours on a game that's only 20 bucks? totally worth it!!! I think this is a great game that should have had more polish and a cleaner launch. Worst launch ever?? No blizzard holds that record in my book. but blizzard also cleaned up a lot faster this is 2 or 3 weeks out and I still see people having issues no one can solve.   All in all do you loovvvveee the concept and you're willing to take the risk? If so buy it. but if you're going to be frustrated by mechanics not being polished, random crashes, and horrible matchmaking? stay away!!

app_name: Over The Hills And Far Away
review_text: I bought this game for 40% off. I think it was just over $5. I am not through with it as of the time of this writing and will probably not update my review, but I can tell that I am close to the end. This is a good story, decent writing, some humour, some heartbreak, really puts you in the moment of what is happening to the characters. Can't speak of the ending yet as again, I am not there, but the majority of the game has been good. While there is little interactivity, they really do add things to the story visually and audiotorilly (not sure what the right word is here) that enhance the experience more than if you were reading it out of a book. Character portraits, moving character portraits, maps, sounds of random stuff like hiccuping or door creaking, shaking the camera when someone falls or snatches something out of someones hand, shadows from the sun shining through the trees, little flames shooting up from a fire, etc. Some of the art is absolutely beautiful and some seems a little less well done, but none of it is bad imo. It just seems rather inconsistent in quality but this does not detract from the novel much. The real draw here is the setting. I don't think I've ever seen a setting in a video game about this time period (war of 1812) that was story based. Just a very unique visual novel and if you can get it for around 5 bucks it is totally worth it. $9 seems a tad expensive for around 3-4 hours of content but the story is quite good so who knows maybe you will value it more.   EDIT: Sooo I wasn't going to update this at all but the ending really blew me away. It was brilliantly done. Saying anything more than that is borderline spoilers but IMO the ending sequence, art, and ending song on the credits are really beautiful. Really made me think philisophically (sp?) about things, much more than a game normally would. With that being said, I would now say this game is definitely worth $9 but still try picking it up on sale if you can.

Having played this game for over two years I've had my ups and downs, the game revolves around Glory Points - the more land you own the more Glory you collect, and those who obtain the most get several rewards such as Card Packs, Premium Tokens and Wheel Spins for being part of the winning House. Teamwork is key for success and leadership can make or break a House!  There are no set objectives in the game, meaning all wars and peace negotiations must be created by the players. Villages and research are also varied aswell, there is no one clear way to success. In the end it will take a combination of different factors to find yourself in the Hall of Heroes.   To help with research, building your village and ranking up you can purchase Crowns in-game. These can be used to spend on Tokens or Cards to que research and buildings, or to give production boosts.  Overall a very good game as you can see amount of hours I have. I have played several Worlds where each one offers a new approach to Alliances and Wars, it is often a good idea to play alongside players from previous worlds as it will help alot.  Best Regards, PsychoticUK

Well...  Guests: I like the way they design the guests, there are individual, group, family with children. They feel 'alive', and cheerful, not some bleak human shaped polygon with some outdated animation.   Graphics: It's quite exquisite, i'm amazed with it.  Performance: well i got i5 haswell, gtx 1060 6gb and 8 gb ddr 3 ram, when they reach 2k+ guest with bunch of rides, coaster and object, i got 15-35 fps, but mostly got 25 fps, so given this game's genre is not a fast paced shooting, no problem at all. It runs better actually than previous installment (RCT 3). I see on the forum so many people are disappointed with performance, but let me say this, the game is not graphic intensive, it's cpu intensive. I think the game require high computation on physics, generating complex behaviour of guest (waypoint, mood, ect), financial calculation and so on. So yeah many simulation game has this kind of problem.  Path: Actually i prefer old style path builder like on rct 3 (i know it's the matter of preferences), but preset path builder is not so bad too, i'm just not get used to it yet. But there is room for improvement. 1. We can't build underground path instantly (again like rct3), the way i know is build the path first, then costumize the terrain (raise it) so it looks like underground path. 2. The problem with path size, if we want larger path we can't just replace it, we have to remove it first, so it quite pain in the bottom. You can't widen the path by adding path by its side (like rct3). I don't know, is it because i don't explore the game enough or it just the way it is.  Other than that path system is ok.  Management: It's doesn't have complex deep management system. It's very simple, so it's matter of preference whether you like it because it's simple, or you dislike it because it's too simple. But i guess they need to add little bit more complexity on management. But if they don't, it's ok because the game is closer to sandbox game rather than management game.  Content: So, with its weakness on management, we should more focus on content. so my opinion... We Need More Content, it doesn't have enough content, hopefully they add more content, rides, coasters, shops, and sceneries.  Scenery building: Well it's great, you can build beautiful scenery with your imagination, great posibility of costumization.    Sooo, it's good park theme game, proper successor of RCT 3, you can build beautiful park with huge posibilty of costumization, there's some cons but in general it's worth it.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops II
review_text: Single Player: Compared to the other CODs this one's unique. You have the ability to have different endings depending on choices and also how you play. It's nice but I feel they should have added a few more choices in the game. I also like how it gives you the ability to change your loadouts before going in. The story itself is alright, not bad but not very great.  Multi Player: I really like the create- a - class in this game, you get to choose 10 items (i.e. Guns, attachments, perks, etc.), allowing more variation. The maps are okay, some are horrible, others are good. It would depend on your preferences. The big issue I have with multi are the overpowered guns in the game *cough* Remington 870 MCS *cough*. Overpowered guns lead to many players using them. Unless you're extremely skilled with other guns, you're probably gonna end up using the overpowered guns in order to survive at least.  Zombies: Personally this is my favorite mode out of all of the Treyarch CODs. In every zombie game, you must survive as long as you can against wave after wave of increasingly stronger zombies. You gain points by either shooting and killing them, or boarding up windows or doors where zombies spawn. You use those points to open doors, guns from walls or the mystery box, perk drinks that help you survive better, or even upgrade your guns using a pack - a - punch machine( if you can access it). You can either play by yourself or with up to 3 other people. When you buy BO2 you get the Green Run map, It includes Tranzit(Available at Bus Station), where you play as one of 4 survivors and you travel around Green Run on a bus surviving against zombies, Grief(Available at Town and Farm), where you and 3 other people survive with or against another team of 4 at one location of the Green Run map, and Survival(Available at Bus Stop, Town, and Farm), where you and 3 other people survive against waves of zombies at one location of the Green Run map.  Overall Black Ops II is a good game. The whole package includes a fun and interesting single player with an okay story, Okay yet frustrating multiplayer, and great zombies.  I'd recommend this game, but unless you're a die hard COD fan, I'd wait until this is on sale for 50% off, maybe 25% off.

I disagree with those statements, sure it does indeed have some performance issues, however the game is completely deserving of being the final chapter, it's set in a chaotic Gotham where everyone has been forced to leave and the Police have lost all power. A sort of Dark Knight Rises setting, through out the story Batman is put in emotional situations and many situations where he thought he would die in the process of saving the city, it's a rollercoster of emotion that never dies down on action.  The combat system is the same as the other games, with some other animations and features such as environmental take downs, even the normal enemies have been given more attacks making them harder to fight. The Witness is an incredible puzzle game. each puzzle is fair - it's not like myst or monkey island, where you might have to do something unorthodox, like combine a monkey and a wrench to get a 'monkey wrench'. this game eshews the concept of an inventory in favour of having each puzzle represented as a glowing maze panel. in doing so, The Witness removes all ambiguity between what *is* and what *isn't* a puzzle, which was a really smart design choice.  this game was designed and iterated upon for nearly 10 years after the release of Braid, the developer's previous game, and the amount of polish and attention to detail in the island is, quite frankly, astonishing. the island is dense, packed with puzzles, shortcuts between areas, and gorgeous views; it's one of the most engaging worlds i've explored in a game, right up there with Dark Souls. one aspect of the world design that i was not fond of, however, were the audio logs scattered throughout the island. they don't explain the plot of the game, but are rather a collection of miscellaneous, thematic quotes from famous philosophers and mathematicians. these audio logs feel a little pretentious, and very superfluous (it's a tight, polished puzzle game, but it has audio logs arbitrarily tacked on).  i have a few other minor gripes about the game, including the lack of a conclusive ending, but this isn't a game i played for its plot. over the course of my first playthrough, i had countless 'a-ha!' moments, which is a benchmark for any puzzle game. The Witness is so rewarding and well-designed that the game's few flaws can be easily overlooked.

I was wary of this. I played a ton of alpha, even after I had unlocked everything and finished all the levels. It was a lot of fun, and I appreciated the minimalism of the game. I paid for the advance copy something like three years ago, played the game, then stopped and sort of put it behind me for a while.  Then, just a couple weeks ago, I saw that it was released on steam. While I thought about looking up my copy of it, minutes later I received a message saying because I paid in advance I got a steam key!  So, I cracked it open. And for a brief minute, I was shocked. This isn't the blocky spreadsheet I remember! Where did all these graphics come from? Why are these characters saying things? A town? I miss my grid of things!  Of course I got over that quick. What I wasn't sure about is what changes there would be, and after playing about 10 hours of the game, I am excited to say that I think they've put an amount of polish on top that has made the game even more nuanced, more complex and overall, well, more.  The base aspects of the game are the same. You are in a dungeon with monsters levels 1-10. Killing them nets XP, but killing monsters of a higher level than you gets you exceptionally more experience. And you will need to kill higher level monsters, because otherwise you are unlikely to level high enough to kill the boss. Lucky for you, the monsters are pretty lacksadaisical and tend to just stand around in the same spot, and won't strike unless you strike first.  It also has a clever mechanic where revealing tiles in the dungeon is the prime method of regaining life and mana (aside from leveling, which refills all your life and mana). The monsters also refill their life, though, so you can't just take anything out by regaining your life over and over.  It sounds simple, but when you count class skills, items, gods and so many other factors, there is just so much to this game. And the random generation of levels makes every game, even of just random games, a thrill and a different experience. And I love the way they build level by level as you experience more and more - the learning curve here is a big improvement on the original which pretty much threw you right into the fray and you had to guess what would be hard or easy. Frankly, that never bothered me because a game like this that lets you take your time, you learn in your own way, but I appreciate what they've done here. It opens up pretty fast anyway, and I think your particular sense of play can lead you towards certain classes as the most fun or interesting.  So basically. I'd recommend this. A lot.

I just wanna start off saying, if you're the type of person to base a game's worth based off of hours of gameplay (like $1 per hour or something like that), then you should probably not get this game. Even if you're not, you may want to wait for a steam sale. But I would definitely recommend grabbing this on a sale at least. Just for the really kewl and unique experience of getting to play a really old skool game I never thought I'd get to play (Not to mention a really unique old skool game in terms of art style and gameplay.)  In terms of Gameplay: Obviously, this is a port from 1983, so it's to be expected it's not 100% seamless transitioning between a command and the video display. The reaction commands can be lazy at some points while being unforgivingly instantaneous at others, but the fact that you can continue as many times as you want, coupled with the introduction(?) of flashing hints and arrows to let you know where you need to go compensated for some instances where I would have otherwise said 'What the ♥♥♥♥ am I supposed to do here?' and given up. If you played RE4, that game stood out because of its great reaction commands. It was part of what made the game, in my opinion, more fun than some other games in that franchise. Other game series tried to imitate it with lesser success. (I remember Kingdom Hearts 2 came out a bit later with reaction commands and they felt really forced most of the time.) I was really pleasantly surprised to see an old skool game be based ENTIRELY on reaction commands and (keeping age in mind) I'd say it even rivals RE4's cutscenes in terms of fun (Which were probably the most fun part of that game. At least for me.). That being said, the gameplay is not very long. Even dying over and over my first playthrough took me a max of 30 minutes. But honestly, I enjoyed that. I got to beat the game multiple times and the achievements generally add to the replay value of the game. I don't have a lot of extra time to try and replay a minimum 4 hour game just to get one 'Don't fire your gun!' achievement, but in this case, if I mess up it's not a huge loss of invested free time.  On to Features: I never played the original given that this game came out six years before I was born. But for people who may have played before and were unsure which features the game may have it does have a scoreboard for you, includes the ad for the game (which I think is shown in the steam store too, because why not), allows you to watch the game as a movie without having to input any reaction commands (I enjoyed watching it afterwards because sometimes you become so focused on the arrows and crap, you can't really pay attention to the details on the screen itself). There's two difficulty modes. I've beaten both...I'm...not ENTIRELY sure what the difference was...You have the option to turn the hints on and off. I didn't really look at the settings though. Appaently you can save (although I have no idea why when the game's so short [high score, maybe?]) and there's also a tutorial which I..uh..ignored.  Aesthetically: This game is funny. The death's aren't always as unique and hilarious as a game like, say, McPixel where part of the gameplay is to figure out how many ways you can die, but the voice acting is hilarious, The WAY Dirk moves is hilarious at times and, really, it's just freaking funny how you're constantly on the edge of danger. How every room is just one reaction command after another reaction command after another. Left side of the room collapses, dodge right. Knives fly out of the right wall, leap forward. Spider drops down and hugs your face if you don't kill it. Rest of the room collapses and then the door explodes right after you go through it. It's like what isn't trying to kill you is just unmaking itself. Enemies charge you just to plummet into a newly opened abyss once their onslaught fails. That's hilarious. The cartoony movements and vocals of the protag is hilarious. I'm not a huge fantasy fan, (I just like other genres better) but I think this artist's style is still worthy of at least one spectated run through.  Overall, I bought this game for $6 and got more enjoyment out of the 75 minutes I've played so far than I got from two or more hours of other games I've played. I definitely factor in dollar to gameplay hour ratio in my considerations for purchases (that's why I didn't recommend 140 for its market price), but I feel like this game is worthy of exception. $15 may be steep, but a chance to play an excellent old skool game like this is definitely worth an attempt at haggling.  [Footnote: If you DO buy this game and like it Space Ace is very similar and also lots of fun :)]

This game is something between Left for dead and diablo 3, certain 'classes' (characters) each with semi different arsenal and different playstyle with emphazis on close combat, I have never been fan of L4D, but i must say I got into this game quite quickly, due to character progression and solid challenge with great pleasure of slicing/crushing rats  Pros: + balanced weapon, classes ( i have basically seen all characters on all difficulties with all weapons)  + good optimalization (ive seen some people on forum saying that this game is badly optimalized but with my 5 year old PC i play on medium settings and honestly this game is one of the smoothiest ive played in a while, but maybe im just lucky)  + graphics looks great, especially lightings  + good community (again difference experience for everyone, but in my 70+ hours of gameplay, ive had just one experience with 'toxic' behaviour, most of the time people help each other and sharing tactics, loot, simply the game difficulty requires it)  + nice level design  + every changeable piece of outfit is appearing on character (trinkets, hats, weapons)  Cons: - loot system is really rng (random) based and even though you can add to those chances (but it heavily increases difficulty), you can get easily screwed, eg. getting the worst quality item on nightmare difficulty which is the 4/5 of current difficulty modes), getting geared in this game is pretty hard (reminds me old world of warcraft 'vanilla' hunting down the 'bis' (best in slot) equipment  - every level of equipment is increasing your damage, that means forget going nightmare or cataclysm difficulty if you have white/green weapons, artificial barrier based on luck  - game with tight meele combat should have stable servers, well, this is not the case sometimes, especially when it relies on the one who hosts the game, in this game, one lag can kill you (double applies for higher difficulties)  - some maps are 'farmed for loot' more due to its simplier design and easier map progress (better defense points etc), making some maps redundant and playing these maps can be very sporadic  - random spawn enemies with random loot rewards with random statistic on weapons, did someone mentioned hearthstone?  - no quick emotes during battle, like 'come defend here' 'behind you' etc (is eliminated with voice chat but not everyone uses it)  - relatively small playerbase (but ive never had problem finding matches for all but cataclysm dificulty, but I can assure you, cataclysm is not something you would be running regularly everytime you turn on the game)  - repetitive  - bugged enemies, AI, hitting through walls (not regularly, but again very unpleasent when happens, lost me match on cataclysm one time)  - horrible AI of bots (even worse than usual, hunting down one vermin across half of the map is not uncommon, yuk)  This game is well done and hopefully devs wont be sitting on their a***, bit patch coming soon, probably fixing stuff with loot, with solid gameplay and learning curve, the price is also reasonable and it is worth it even if you are not planning on getting really hardcore (cataclysm, nightmare) because the game is challenging on all difficulties

Before I start this review I would just like to remind everyone that this is a game that might not be for everyone, and that's okay. This is a game for those that like exploration, survival, and space. There are many reviews for this game from people who were either misinformed or built it up so much in their head that it would never live up to their expectations. For some of us who were paying attention though, this was the game we were waiting for.  Sean Murray has always said that this is very much a single-player game and that multiplayer would be very limited. He reiterated many times that it was a single-player game and that if you were expecting to play with friends you would be disappointed. The beauty of No Man's Sky is the exploration, and sharing your discoveries with friends through videos and screenshots which makes it a more communal experience. But this game is about you and the story you choose to write for yourself.  People complain about the $60 price tag and I don't really get that. Not long ago every new game that came out carried the same price tag and only dropped in price after it had been out for awhile. We see reduced prices nowadays because of early access but those games come with their own share of problems and disappointments. The value of the game is relative to your personal experience. I have over 46 hours in the game as of writing this review and I've also played it some on the PS4 before the PC launch. Ultimately you have to decide if it is worth it to you. For as long as I've played, I still see myself playing it even longer and will eventually double the time I have now and beyond. I've gotten many hours of enjoyment and will have even more. So for me it was definitely worth the price. If you get the game and put in at least 30 hours it will have been worth it. $2 an hour for entertainment isn't bad by any standard. We spend $10+ on a movie for 2 hours of enjoyment so why would this be worse? But if you simply can't stomach the $60 price tag, just wait for a sale.  I see other reviewers complain about the combat. Sean Murray has stated that this isn't a combat focused game and that this game wouldn't cater to CoD fans in any way. Combat in this game is only to help aid in your exploration. If you were expecting a twitch shooter you obviously didn't pay attention to any of the pre-release trailers and information.  The exploration in this game is top notch. You will come across some very unique planets and even more unique creatures. Sure, you will see a lot of similar things, but that's to be expected. This is, after all, a procedural game and it has to work with a limited set of options that will sometimes see some overlap. However, I see people complain about not finding planets with a lot of animals and vegetation. It seems that if you stay on the path the game lays out for you, finding those is a lot rarer. Visit this link,  https://www.reddit.com/r/NoMansSkyTheGame/comments/4xle85/psa_there_are_four_types_of_star_systems/  and when you upgrade your warp drive you can visit the better stars and increase your chances of finding better stuff.  The survival in this game is also done pretty well. It's not a very prevalent thing unless you visit a planet with extreme conditions. But even then, a few simple upgrades to your Exosuit can make it a non-issue. Resources are fairly easy to come by and plutonium is abundant just about everywhere. You can use it to charge your life support, launch thrusters, and multi-tool. So make use of that!  I'm happy with the game as is. However, that's not to say there aren't any flaws. I wish I had full control of my ship when flying on planets. No matter how hard you try you won't be able to crash your ship. Landing is handled automatically with one button press which can be nice, but it's also really hard to tell where you are going to land. Even when you are trying to land on a landing pad or next to a landing beacon you can't see below you at all so it can be difficult to line those up sometimes. Unlike other people, I have only experienced one crash in all of my PC playtime. Other than that, my game has performed perfectly and looks absolutely stunning on my setup. You can also tell that this is a port from the PS4 to PC. The menu and inventory management was designed for that system and it took a little getting used to. I would love an option to change it or at least an option to remove the 'hold the mouse button down to confirm' nonsense.  All in all, I'm very happy with the game and if I was to give it a score I would say it's about an 8/10. A great game with some flaws that don't detract from the overall gameplay.  And the beautiful thing is that the game can only get better. It has been announced that there will be no paid DLC and we will get new content in the form of free patches. I'm excited to see how this game evolves.  TL;DR  A great game with some flaws that don't impact gameplay too much. If you enjoy space, exploration, survival, and crafting elements this might be the game for you. While I'm happy with the game in its current form, it can only get better with time. We are getting free content patches instead of paid DLC. The $60 price tag was well worth it for me as I already have 46+ hours in and will probably double or triple that in the near future.  Thank you for reading and I hope this review was helpful!

I'm far from the first one to state this, but for as much possibility as this game has, it falls flat on pretty much every level. The only consistently interesting thing about this game is the initial plethora of new and exotic looking species, and planets. But even that gets stale after the 6th or 7th planet you've visited. It might be worth a play just based on the often stunning visuals some of the more exotic planets give you, but the graphics just don't hold up. Amazing Game. When I first bought the game and installed it on my laptop opperating on windows 8.1 it crashed a lot. I later found out all I had to do was open up my task manager, select details, right click on Blitzkrieg exe. and set affinity to 0 (click on 1 so only the 0 is left x in). Doing this removed the crashing completely. However, you will have to repeat this process every time you load up the game. I have played many rts games (Supreme Commander, Company of Heroes, World in Conflict, Sins of the Solar Empire, ect) and I really enjoyed this one due to how resourceful and careful you have to be. Every unit given to you has a purpose and once lost (with the exception of infantry unless you loose your supply truck or your transport plane is shot down) cannot be replaced. All in all in my opinion its a great game and well worth $5.00.
