A Druid's Duel is a very simple Strategy game on the surface: but that's only on the surface. What's underneath is a chess match that is pretty excellent: with the ebb and flow of the mana pool being the resource you need to take control of the match, and the different use of tactics with each of the 4 units strengths helping to really bring out your A game in terms of luring your enemy, and tactically taking regions. It's not a game where you're going to make an epic comeback on, as at a certain point the game goes down hill for the team with the mana deficiency, but overall if you're looking for a cheap and interesting strategy game, this is it. Easy to learn: hard to master.  +: Simple 4 Units and skill design make for a lot of tactics despite the lack of overall variety. Emphasis on land control and mana collection and how mana is collected makes a great pull-push nature to gameplay.  Art design and sound design make for a relaxing experience but does the job. Building land and those mechanics help add to defensive play and does open the board as a whole.  Quantity versus Quality of units makes a huge difference in strategies and attack plans: reacting to opponents layout is key.   -: Lack of options to view range/enemy unit range, which could have helped in strategy elements. Learning mechanics could be better for medium/high level tactics. Archers seem a tiny bit overpowered in some senses.  For more information and Gameplay:  http://youtu.be/whCOe-IHamk

*** MINOR GAMEPLAY RELATED SPOILERS BELOW. IF YOU WANT A TRUE EXPEREINCE WITH THIS GAME, THEN KNOW THAT YOU NEED TO 'TAKE A BLIND RUN' AT IT AND PROBABLY SHOULDN'T BE READING DETAILED REVIEWS BEYOND 'THIS GAME IS AWESOME'. YOU HAVE BEEN WARNED. ***  Now then, since I know many will disregard the warning, and without spoiling too much of the game, I will simply say this: the replay value alone is higher than you'd expect from a game with 'simpler' graphics. The gameplay and story progression combined with an 'evolving around on your actions type story' based concept provide at least 2 solid playthroughs (of which are polar opposites) depending on the player's actions in the game.   Most players, on their first go, will kill a few things, gain a few LOVE 'levels', and proceed more or less through the story just thinking 'it's another game'. They are wrong, and it takes about a full playthrough to even grasp an idea of how deeply woven this game's story and background really is.  Killing some, but not everything, is considered the 'neutral path' is usually what players do on their first playthrough and has it's own ending accordingly. Well, then, you may ask, what about killing no one or killing everything?  Well, sparing everything is indeed another path, and uniquely challenging as each 'enemy' reacts differently to different things and it's up to you to figure out the way to avoid killing them. This proves challenging at times for the boss 'fights', especially the very first times you attempt it. But make no mistake, everything in the main story can be spared. This turns the very core concept of most games on their heads, as even old classic games from the 1980s had us 'beating up or killing the bad guys'. And yes, refusing to kill anyone will first yield the neutral final boss (you'll understand why when you complete it), then continuing your progess can 'evolve' your final boss and ending into the pacifist one once other hidden criteria are met. What criteria? Sorry, I played this blindly, and that is where most of the fun honestly is.  Anyway, that's being a 'good guy' or at least 'not being a total jack#@%'. Then there is killing everything, or 'genocide'. It's more or less as it sounds: slaughter everything and leave no one alive in your wake. You'd think this path, as it sees your LOVE increase (as well as your strength, defense, and HP) would be easier than sparing everything, or pacifist. Heh, you couldn't be more wrong. Again, not spoiling anything, but there are a.....few situations you'll find yourself in along the way that may make you rethink the whole 'I'mma slaughter everything' idea. But if you can 'stay determined' and push your way through even the toughest boss battles this game has to offer, then the events that unfold are truly worth seeing.  One true word of advice though....if you plan on 'seeing everything' as I did, then you want to SAVE THE GENOCIDE RUN FOR LAST. Trust me, as once completed, your game is forever changed (and again, you won't realize how until another playthrough is done).  This review is getting far longer than I intended, but it's games such as this that remind us that we as people are still capable of 'getting sucked into' a good story. But I've said enough I think. My final score is 5/5 with this last thought: a game doesn't need super graphics to be good, and this game proves it. Want something story driven that can capture your attention and imagination? Give this a try. Odds are, you won't be sorry you did.

app_name: The Metronomicon: Slay The Dance Floor
review_text: I really love this game. I could go on and on about it but among the best things to say:  Great music: A ton of great tracks, all of which I had never been exposed to before. Some of the tracks are specifically made for this game and I like nearly all of them. I didn't buy the accompanying soundtrack at first, but now I'm considering it.  Fantastic difficulty curve: This one is important, I was kind of worried when I first tried some hard songs that I would never get good enough to beat them. A couple weeks in, and I can beat every song on hard and am working my way up on some leaderboards. It really is addicting because I never felt 'stuck' in the game where I stopped accomplishing things.  Cool RPG elements: At the end of the game, you have enough equipment and unique abilities to really create some special task forces for accomplishing certain goals (like most damage on a song, most enemies killed on a song, most spells cast). Creating a party around specific goals is actually really fun too, it's not just all about the rhythm game!  Overall for a summary of the game, I would definitely say this is a rhythm game first and an RPG second. But it's a really well done fusion of the two. Pretty much all skill levels are welcome; I think most anybody should find easy doable, many can probably jump in on medium. I will say if you are some kind of pro at StepMania or FFR on keyboard controls, then even the hard difficulties on keyboard might be a bit easy (I've seen what you people can do!). That's just because these tracks were all handcrafted to be dance pad friendly.

EDIT: Developer added key rebinding and joystick support.  This game is crazy, the atmosphere, graphic style and especially the music are very strange. Got it for 2 bucks shortly after launch, but well worth 5-10 if you are into contraption building and roguelike shooting.   So this is like a post apocalyptic cybertron planet thing, and you are flying in a space truck cabin, that you can upgrade by picking up modules (green wrench), and pressing space to enter the builder menu (keys are not rebindable atm, which is a shame, probably only playable with KB&M). You start with a basic set of cabin, gun, side armor and engines, and go from there. You only lose when the cabin is destroyed, but once without engines, you move very slowly, so unless you got replacement engines handy you are as good as dead, especially at the core of the planet.  The level. Yes there is only one level, but it's never ending procedurally generated. You have the surface, with the run of the mill enemies, then when you enter the tunnels to go undeground, you start finding the tougher stuff, and when you get into the core, unless you are heavily upgraded, you are going to die within seconds.  + Very cool upgrade/building system + Smooth performance + Can reach very fast speeds with upgrades + Ridiculous weapons + Big and difficult world  - It's Unity with cheap assets - Needs an extra pan camera view on the builder - Very expensive for what it is, unless on sale.

'Syberia' is a point-n-click adventure about mechanical automatons (robot-like wind-up machines) – and teasingly, about mammoths. With rare but existing weird pronounciation in both, the English and the German version, the heart of this game lies in the impressive achievements of Hans Voralberg, an ingenious savant engineer, who left home in search for a legendary land called Syberia.  The story is quite charming (or the premise, at least), though, its protagonist not too believable: I think Kate Walker makes the (greatly appreciated) personality changes – from obedient to self-confident – for the wrong reasons, resulting in a superficiality based on unhealthy selfishness that I don't like. Every so often she will receive a call on her cell phone from her boss, friend, boyfriend or mother, which stops Kate in her tracks for a few moments, for a dialog sequence. In these dialog scenes she arguably distances herself from these people more and more as the story goes on.  I was especially angry about Kate (or rather the, I'm assuming, unintentionally misogynistic developers) for one particular puzzle: the dam. It is here where she can't cross a waist-high river – why can't she cross it? Waist-high is not that deep. Afraid of getting a bit wet? Oh well … 'Oooh, that stick is too dirty. I won't carry it.' … And ultimately the scene where she says: 'I can't push the lever, I need help.' – and then she goes and gets the boy Momo to do it *for* her instead of *with* her. Ugh … That whole puzzle was just awfully executed, imo.  All that crap aside however, the overall atmosphere is pretty mellow. You've got many 'rooms' with nothing to do but walk through in order to get to the actually (gameplay-wise) interesting places; 'rooms' where you can enjoy the chirping birds and nice vistas. Running around and repairing 'broken' automatons by simply winding them up again feels kinda mellow, too – not that the puzzles are dull or too easy! It just *feels* mellow because, once wound up, the machines work as intended and 'do the work for you'. They do *different* work than what you had to do to get them working … *sigh* nevermind.  I think that the areas explorable from the get-go (in each chapter) are rather vast and can be easily overwhelming, although it's all manageable if you don't rush it. Unfortunately however, the game has many, many clickable doors where all Kate will say is: 'I don't need to go there.' That's, in this amount, unnecessary and, quite frankly, a bit annoying.  The context-sensitive GUI can be a bit confusing at first due to icons too hard to distinguish. I wish they had made the speech bubble a filled circle instead of a ring, I feel like that would've helped a lot. Other than that it is a nice fitting simplistic style choice that I can get behind.  As for the dialog system, the game uses rigid topics to choose from – which, in some conversations, don't make much sense. But it works most of the times. And maybe I'm spoiled by today's games but … the similarly rigid 'sequential animation chains' that sometimes occur can be a pain to watch and endure. For instance, Kate walking up a flight of stairs: first center position, then walk cycle, then sometimes popping, finally 'release' back to interactivity. Just make sure not to use stairs too often.  It's a good game, but I don't know if I would recommend playing it without the sequel as an immediate follow-up.  [7.o/1o.o]

This is a tough one. The original Operation Flashpoint GOTY game was one of the greatest 'realistic', tactical, sans-health bar FPS games of all time, with two fantastic expansions which both added huge amounts of content to the game (in much the same way Morrowind was improved by Tribunal and Bloodmoon) and a wonderful gameplay style that gave the game tremendous replayability and gave the player tremendous scope to imagine the world around them.  Of course, after that original title the companies that were responsible for the game split up - Bohemia Interactive making the ARMA series (eternally disapointing in kind of the same way Gothic was: just not refined, polished, bug-free or fun enough to match the technological ambition) and Codemasters making this game.  Dragon Rising was many years in the making, and while you can say that in certain ways it was a better shooter than the ARMA titles, you can say that it was nevertheless dissapointing and an inferior title to the original OpFlash in terms of how it contrasted with the titles already available.  If we are to compare this game with ARMA then you might say that the game is a bit more stable, the missions a bit more cinematic and well paced, the guns a bit more realistic and satisfying to use. These advantages are real and enjoyable, but none of them represent industry leading iterations of the features - the original OpFlash lead the industry in map size, replayability, freedom, difficulty (if you so desired) and scope, but Dragon Rising doesn't really set the world alight in any one way.  This does have to do with the bugs that, while not as annoying as in ARMA 2, for example, are still present. I once played a mission where a tank somehow got stuck to my soldier by an invisible, 10 meter pole. no kidding, I walked around the map with a many tonne tank spinning wildly ten meters above my head, smacking and clattering into the ground incessantly whenever I tried to get in a vehicle but, somewhat disapointingly, refusing to crush the squad mates that I tired to hard to splatter. Another time I had to give up on an entire playthrough as a certain mission bugged and was simply impossible to complete.  The zones are still big, the soliders still responsive to orders, the enemies still reasonably deadly, but the island of Skira fails to burst into life in the same way the original OpFlash island of Everon did, largely because the game is let down by instability and unconvincing scenarios. It is a huge shame that this game failed, and while some admirable advertising almost convinced me to give Red River a go it was apparently even worse than DR, and as such sadly I fear we must lay this game to rest.  I am reminded of the classic xBox shooter BLACK. Quite forgotten now, it had a really polished feel and really immersive, solid missions. It is perhaps where the ingredients that would have made Dragon Rising a really good game snuck off to.  I still recommend this game, because it is better than ARMA 2 for as long as it lasts before it bugs out, and it just has a better title, let's face it.

Mass Effect is a role-playing shooter in a beautifully realized sci-fi world. It was the second-to-last game made by the – legendary – game studio Bioware, before the inevitable quality decline set in, that occurs when a studio is incorporated into the EA roster.  Even though Mass Effect was originally an Xbox 360 exclusive, Bioware did not let the limited hardware hinder the game's potential. The game was a visual masterpiece, and the tight movement and combat naturally inherited from the Unreal Engine played wonderfully into the game's core shooting mechanics. The intense firefights with lasers, explosions, and kinetic powers, kept you at the edge of your seat – but it was the expansive universe, and heavy story elements, that kept you from leaving it.  If I were to make a parallel between another much beloved piece of media, I'd say that Mass Effect followed the Matrix formula to a T. Much akin to the Matrix trilogy, the first part of the Mass Effect trilogy is an independent piece that stands on its own, it builds up a mysterious and interesting world, and let's you take it in however much you like – but just like the Matrix trilogy, the second and third parts thoroughly disappoint.  Why The Matrix trilogy went the way it did is a mystery only the Wachowskis can answer for, but as for Mass Effect, we can clearly point our fingers at EA and say: “They did it!”. Mass Effect, along with Dragon Age: Origins, were Bioware's final games that showed the brilliance of the studio. The games that followed are little more than fan-service on top of bad writing. Let's not even start with The Old Republic.  As for Mass Effect, it's one of the most fleshed out worlds ever created. The game hooks you from the start, and won't let go – even when the credits roll. You're gonna wanna go back, and it's recommended, as the game has twists and turns that will only reveal themselves on second or third playthroughs.   I recommend this game to anyone and everyone.

Unlike Sonic 4 Episode I, Sonic 4 Episode II doesn't just reuse a bunch of old things from the classic Sonic games, puts them all in one game and call it a sequel. Instead, it actually adds new things and tries to fix any problems with the first episode.  Most of the levels, besides Oil Desert Zone and Sky Fortress Zone which look like Oil Ocean Zone from Sonic 2 and Wing Fortress Zone from Sonic 2, look very original and are extremely creative and fun to look at.  The graphics in this game are a huge improvement in this game, the game itself is now is 2.5D and now everything looks a lot nicer and beautiful. Sonic's textures in the game also aren't extremely light blue like the last game and he along with everything else in the game, looks great.  Even though this game is nice, I wouldn't call it “Sonic 4” because it still doesn't feel like a direct sequel to Sonic 3, despite the game still being really good. I feel like the game should have had some more characters and stages to lengthen the game and also make the game feel alot larger than Sonic 3 was, but Sonic 4 is an episodic game so I guess both Sonic 4 episodes 1 and 2 would basically be one whole game and if you put combine all the zones in the game you get 10 zones which is close to the amount Sonic 3 & Knuckles has.  Because of all of this, I recommend you buy this game at some point and if you want to play the whole “Sonic 4 Saga” then wait for Episode I to be on sale and buy it at a lower price.

I'm not really sure what to say here. Brutal Legend is... weird. Let's discount how old the game is, even if it was just released on PC. I'm not really sure if I should judge this game as an indie title or not. While we all know Double Fine by their unique indies with a very clear sense of style - and that is definitely showing here - it seems to me that Brutal Legend strives to be something more. It seems - and plays - like your average action adventure game. And nothing more. That's the problem. It is just... average. Every single aspect of a 'game' here is 'Just good enough'. The combat is fun, but shallow, and lacks any weight. There's a couple of combos, but nothing to indicate which is the more damaging, which moves hit more enemies... the RTS portions are, again, fun, but could be better developed, though I'll praise the unit dynamics and the interaction between the player and the troops(buffing your team with solos was great. Double teaming with units rarely did me any good though). The story serves it purpose,with just enough personality in these characters to carry the script, but it has pacing problems towards the end(by the last third or so of the game it feels like the story is dragging on, and then it's all of sudden 'Hey, final boss now, we're ending it.'). I'm not really sure if I'm being unfair to the game. It's in a weird gray area in which it's more than indie, but not quite developed enough to be considered non-indie, especially considering Double Fine's pedigree. Only it's not bad.  Yeah, not bad. I had my fun. The game has a witty sense of humor that doesn't show up enough times. And, par for the course for Double Fine, it nails its amosphere. It's so metal it hurts. Hell, I'm not a metalhead myself, but this game - and its soundtrack for the matter - OOZES awesome. And a good atmosphere will do wonders to a game: it's hard to care about how deep the mechanics are when, unarguably, I'm having fun being a flying-lighting-axe-wielding-demon commanding flaming motorbikes and mobs of headbangers while summoning crashing zeppelins with a wicked guitar solo.

If you're looking for a review on this game you've come to the wrong place; if you're looking for an outstanding pie recipe you've come to the right place.  Ingredients: 1. 6 tablespoons unsalted butter 2. 1/2 cup white sugar 3. 1/2 cup brown sugar 4. 1/4 cup water 5. 1/4 teaspoon cinnamon 6. 1 pinch salt 7. 5 apples - peeled, cored and sliced 8. 1 pastry for double-crust pie   Directions: 1. Preheat oven to 425 °F (220 °C). 2. Combine butter, white sugar, brown sugar, water, cinnamon, and salt in a saucepan over medium heat. Bring to a boil, remove from heat and set aside. 3. Roll out half the pastry to fit a 9-inch pie plate. Place bottom crust in pie plate; pour in apple slices. 4. Roll out top crust into a 10-inch circle. Cut into 8 (1-inch) wide strips with a sharp paring knife or pastry wheel. Weave the pastry strips, one at a time, into a lattice pattern. Fold the ends of the lattice strips under the edge of the bottom crust and crimp to seal. 5. Pour butter-sugar mixture over top of pie, coating the lattice, and allowing any remaining sauce to drizzle through the crust. 6. Bake in the preheated oven for 15 minutes. 10/10 Ooooooooooooooh yeah! This is a re-release of a classic RTS of long standing. For 10 years this has been the high-water-mark of the genre, and most of us who had it on day 1 are veteran players of those 10 years. For those of you who don't know (and I guess that's why you're reading this after all) Imagine if Total War had a lovechild with Age of Empires. There's a turn-based global map where your civilization expands and fights on their way to world domination, and each turn there are battles for the territory you wish to gain or hold. Each nation has an innate advantage, and with the building of world wonders on the global map, can be enhanced further, while resources from captured territory are available in later battles too. All of this might make you think that the RTS part is less vital, but in fact the action is just as hot down there. A wide range of units and technologies in each historical period are on hand to take down enemy settlements, and a variety of defensive structures to hold them off. On a downside, there are a number of known bugs right now, but the devs are releasing regular patches to deal with those.

A solid, low-budget first-person survival horror closely linked to   Slenderman: The Eight Pages  , housing a fair amount of haunting images and thick atmosphere, evoking a handful of steady cold shivers and durable tension within its short run, mostly due to the eerie and restrained sound design.  Your daughter has gone missing from the asylum where she was being treated for attempting suicide. Once you enter the vacant facility, investigating her whereabouts, you begin looking for pages from your daughter's journal while being hunted by a dark presence wearing a hat.  The basic gameplay of finding a bunch of objects to unlock new areas for further exploration is quite easily comprehensible, yet it may be a fair disorienting challenge to obtain all 20+ pages since the randomly altering level design relocate the pages on each playthrough; though once you pinpoint the location of a page, whispery voices are in effect and narrowing it down become child's play.  The stiff level design is utterly mediocre at best and visually it is quite dull, but the ridiculously awkward voice-over is the shattering winner to listen to and hence should have been left out. Also, the poorly written dialogue comes out far more silly rather than intriguing, and finally - considering the source material that is based on real events - the outcome turns out quite immaterial.  At a discount however, coupled with appreciating solid scares, Game Mechanics' short piece of indie horror, regardless of being a Slender copy, earns a night's attention.   5,5 / 10

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: GROUND ZEROES
review_text: Gear Up!  Metal Gear Solid Ground Zeroes, now with a quick google and forum search, you'll see the torrents of abuse towards this 'Half the price as a full retail game tech demo' nonsense. On top of it all, ignore that.  This is Kojima's masterpiece of advertising, almost a teaser.  From the moment you press start, this amazing cinematic of ground zeroes instantly pulls you in. The sound, the atmosphere, you feel as if you're part of some summer blockbuster.  Then once you begin, you're awestruck by what even a modest PC can show you.  Using the binoculars I zoomed in on two marines having a chat about an event in the opening cinematic, the faces photorealistic, not a single balaclava or full faced helmet in the entire complex (Apart from prisoners I must add, but see any camp x-ray photo of prisoners if you think why would they do this?).  On one of the missions you are to retrieve intelligence, but knowing the events of the mission, I snuck around and nosed at what the soldiers of camp omega where upto. I was amazed to hear two soldiers chatting about knowing a certain secret of the mission and how its been altered in a subtle way. Now 99% of players wouldn't of heard this, but for it to be put it, on the off chance, astounded me.  Now to move onto the missions on ground zeroes, yes they're short, but you can tackle them a million plus ways! I did playthroughs of most missions where I pretty much crawled through any possible track of cover to get this done, I then did this whilst doing my very best Rambo / Arnie impressions.  Every single mission, every single play style, resulted in totally different results and reactions.  Kojima is a master advertiser, by releasing this he has ramped up so much anticipation, I don't believe any trailers, press releases or even a leak (barring an iso leak) could of drummed up the attention focused on the phantom pain.

review_text: Briefly:  I highly recommend this standalone for all the players who haven't played Darks Souls 2 yet or are planning to play it again after a break.  All DLCs are bundled nicely together and the access items hidden in Drangleic in a more suitable manner than in vanilla. Difficulty increased slightly but nothing unfair. Personally, I enjoyed greatly the improved AI/pathfinding and all the seemingly small improvements in enemy placement/respawn behaviour. It is just so much better than in vanilla!  It can be argued if the upgrade is worthy for the players already owning all the DLC but for the rest - it's worth every copper coin in your purse.

I know I'm getting old when I start thinking 'Is this game ever going to end? I have a life to take care of.' Gone are the young days when I wished games would never end.  This game is packed with content. I'm maybe half-way through it and I'm standing at 38 hours of play time. Then there's the replayability factor, since I like to have different kinds of party makeups. This game is definitely worth it, if you like old school open world RPG's.  I wasn't a big fan of the separation between realms for a part of the game, since it breaks that open world feel, but it's still a good game. It reverts to a full open world experience after a while.

As a straight female, I enjoyed this game more than I could have hoped. Yes, it's a fanservice/hentai mess that was probably designed for horny male weeaboos, but I loved it. The puzzle aspect is addictive, and the fact that you can cash in points to earn special items and power-ups makes it even better. Every time I thought that I'd finished the game I discovered a new unlockable character. I like that you can choose your gender at the beginning as well (this only changes the pronouns that the characters use when referring to you in-game, but it made the whole experience less awkward) and there was a good amount of variety between the girls that you interact with. The game is fully voice acted and the characters appear with a variety of outfits and hairstyles, which are unlockable.   There were a few things about this game that bothered me:  - a few racist remarks from the love fairy who guides you, as well as racial stereotypes with which gifts the girls like. - DD was the highest bra size that any girl claimed to have, despite the fact that their boobs were obviously at least E or larger (I'm nitpicking).  - The weights that the girls claimed to be were unrealistic for their body types.  - There is no real 'ending' to the game; no ending credits or final congratulations for completeing all of the girls (including the two secret ones). I wish that there was some sort of final sequence or the ability to choose one of the girls to be in a relationship with or something, you know?  Overall, I really liked this game and recommend it to anyone interested in playing it.

This is a review for the game played on an HTC Vive only. I purchased specifically to play in VR as soon as support was added by the developers. Something is very strange with the stereo rendering in VR. The near track and bug look 3D, but the track in the distance becomes 2D, and every boss battle looks like you are staring at a 2D cinema screen up close, with the boss showing as a projected movie. And when your bug explodes, debris close to you again looks 2D. This sadly detracts from the game in VR, and at one point I was trying to fight a boss with one eye closed to try to fool myself into 'believing' that everything was actually in 3D. The game could have been amazing in VR, but this strange 2D-ness totally ruins it. From the Discussion page, the devs are aware of this, but seem to suggest that there is no possible fix and that it is a failure of VR as a whole that far away objects look flat and only object at a virtual distance of 1 to 3 metres will ever look 3D. I am really not sure this is the case, given a large number of other games made for VR (as opposed to having VR bolted on a 2D game like this) work fine, and so as it stands I cannot recommend this game for VR. Sorry.

A very story driven post-apocalyptic FPS game. Think an excellent mixture of Fallout and Half Life. Very well put together.  One of the best stories a video game tells, in my opinion. You are able to skip cutscenes, but you won't want to because the story is just that good.  The ambience of the environment, whether its the dark tunnels, the barebones settlements, or the caustic surface, is among the best at portraying the situation you are in. Even the Fallout series wasn't as good at keeping you afraid of the dark or of the mutants because you become a walking god in them. In this game you are very much mortal, ony able to carry a maximum of five health kits and four guns at any given time with ammo very scarce. So scarce, in fact, that it is used as currency in shops.  You don't even have just the monsters in the dark to deal with. World War Two is being fought again, the Communists and Nazis believe you to be part of the other side and will shoot you on sight. Stealth is a very big part of this game, but how well hidden you are depends on how far into the shadows and how quiet you are. No more crouching in front of an enemy and having them go 'Where'd he go?'  I personally give this game a 9/10 for the story and setting alone. With everything else included, that is bumped up to 10/10. Very well put together game, a work of art.

For example you there is a selection of grips but no option for aftermarket rearsets. Some modifications have brands and models, and other things like brake calipers don't. Overall the game is well done. The only problem i'm having is figuring out how multiplayer works.

Dragon Age, the classic game brought to you by the makers of Mass Effect, KOTOR and Jade Empire. Certainly better than its half arsed sequal after EA ♥♥♥♥ed Bioware into a shell of its former self.   You are the grey warden and you'll be going on a quest to save the entire realm from darkness... yeah it's pretty much the typical good must triumph over good story, nothing really original but still interesting nonetheless. You'll battle creatures like the undead, dark monsters, and surprisingly a few dragons.  This is Biowares classical style of game, where the main character is a mute and there's a lot of detail to the world you inhabbit. I do prefer Dragon Age 2's voice acting and some of the characters, however in this game when you make a character from the start you have the freedom to make a human, elf or a dwarf and a choice of different backgrounds to start the story from. Whether you want to be a human noble or an Elf ranger, as well as the choice of class for your character, of which it is limited to 3: Warrior, Rogue and Mage. It would have been better if there were more of a variety of classes like the Elder Scrolls, or even their own game Mass Effect which has sci-fi rpg classes of about 6.  Dragon Age: Origins is a must have if you are an RPG nut and you like fantasy worlds inhabbited by Elves and Dwarves.

Valve gave themselves a big challenge when they decided to make a sequel to Portal. The original was one of the most original and successful puzzle games ever made...was it possible to keep the spirit and integrity of the original, while making new changes to keep the game fresh? That answer is a resounding “yes”. Portal 2 is not only a worthy sequel to Portal, but it also surpasses its predecessor in quality, design, and gameplay.  Much of what made Portal a success returns in Portal 2, with a few wonderful additions and changes. To those who have played the first Portal, Portal 2’s gameplay is a reunion with a dear old friend. The level and game design, however, are even better than the original, with more varied environments to explore, plenty of easter eggs to discover, and amazing scripted scenes. The special moments add a new level of excitement to the gameplay, and I always looked forward to what was coming next. There are also cool new design elements to many of the puzzles. Portal gave us cubes, turrets, and deadly goo, but Portal 2 gives us hard-light bridges, excursion funnels, propulsion gels (my favorite), and many more. The iconic humor also makes a welcome return, and is sillier and quirkier than ever.  What really makes Portal 2 a true step forward from Portal, however, is the new co-op mode. Portal puzzles are fun and mind-bending by themselves, but playing a puzzle made for two creates a whole new meaning to “fun with science”. The amount of communication and teamwork that these puzzles require is astounding. To give a few examples, players must perform actions at the same time, rely on one to help the other reach an object or location, and figure out a way to get both players to the same location. This is one truest forms of co-op that I have ever played. In most co-op games, if one player is inactive, others can still play without too much of a hiccup. That is not the case in Portal 2. Since there are only two players, and the puzzle solutions require both players to work together, if one player doesn’t participate, there is no way to complete the puzzle. Because of this, I highly recommend this co-op experience to anyone seeking to improve trust and team-building skills with a friend.     Though not available when the game first released, Portal 2 now has a level creator for both single player and co-op test chambers. Couple this with flourishing support by the Steam Workshop community and there is no shortage of quality player-built single player and co-op maps to download. I’ve tried my hand at creating chambers myself, but level design is not my forte. For those who fancy themselves as the next big game designer, though, do not hesitate to try your hand at creating some content!  I have yet to play a “perfect” video game; yes, even with this glowing review, I do not want the reader to feel that Portal 2 is perfect, although I will confess, its flaws seem few compared to some games I have played.   Puzzle games are not known for their stories, and Portal 2 is no exception. The plot is somewhat different than the first game, but ultimately ends up being quite similar. The beginning and ending of the game are fantastic, but the ending is directed at those who played the first game. I wonder exactly how much gamers who did not play the original Portal will understand.  Also, one big element to the Portal series is the fact that the player is never truly alone when they complete puzzles. Indeed, in basic form, there is always someone--or something--commenting about you or your environment, often to the player’s amusement. While Portal 2 has an even better, sillier, and more diverse cast of characters this time around, there is one--albeit small--section of the game where player is in fact left all alone in a rather bland environment, with no one and nothing commenting about their performance. This may be my personal low of the game, but it leads into my very favorite section of the game which more than makes up for this low. I do hope that if Valve is planning any more installments in the Portal series (which seems unlikely) that they keep the humorous commentary coming. Portal just isn’t Portal with dead silence.   Other than those minor quibbles of mine, Portal 2 is a fantastic experience. Like its predecessor, I heartily recommend it to puzzle lovers looking for a fantastic brainteaser, to anyone who tires of blowing things up in first person shooters, and now also to those looking to build a strong bond with a co-op partner, and those looking to create some fantastic level designs to add to their portfolios. If you seem to have “The Itch” for testing, Portal 2 is ready to help you scratch it. You monster.

First off, Civ 5 says I've played 24 hours, but in actuality it's closer to 224 hours, as I played around 200 on another account in 2013, so I have a fair amount of experience with this game. Almost all the time I spent was with the Gods and Kings expansion pack, and I don't know the Brave New World expansion very well, nor have I played any other Civ games.  This review will focus mostly on what differentiates Civilization 5 from other 4X strategy games I've played. I'm going to start off with my disappointments with Civ 5.  THE BAD  HAPPINESS SYSTEM: I find the happiness system to be the worst thing about this game. I have seen happiness done well in other games, but in Civ 5 it's very flawed. Every city you have automatically generates unhappiness. There are various ways to increase the happiness, but it mostly involves constructing buildings (and cities can only only have one of each type of building), or reaching certain cultural advancements. So why is it bad?  Let's compare with another happiness system, from Via Sol 2. In that game, happiness decreased when the baseline needs for your population were not met--food, housing, and electricity. If those needs get met, happiness stops falling. In Civ 5, your people ALWAYS generate unhappiness. What is responsible for your population's huge amount of discontent? Simple existence. They ALWAYS hate life, and all you can do is try to cancel it out with some positives. They don't have actual needs that must be met. All you can really do is get them lots of luxurious resources and buildings to try to turn things around. In many games, that's not possible, until you reach a higher era or just happen to find a luxury resource someone is willing to trade to you.  And what happens when happiness is low? At first cities will experience slower growth, but if happiness drops just a little lower, to -10, cities won't grow at all. Cities that don't grow can't utilize all of their potential resources, which means lots of wasted land. It seems that the primary design goal of the happiness system is to limit growth.  LARGE EMPIRES: Many design choices in Civ 5 are clearly just there to keep the individual Civs as small as possible. Capturing even a single city from another Civ can be quite a struggle, and once you do it's usually a Pyhrric victory. Lots of the buildings in the city are instantly destroyed when you capture it, meaning that even if you captured a city rapidly, you'll still have a punishingly long slog to rebuild the place. Plus, such cities bring in a ton of happiness. This means that capturing an enemy city really won't be worth it unless there are a bunch of luxury resources available to it. If you do capture an enemy city that possesses no luxury resources, you may as well just raze it, as you'll be taking a sizeable hit to your happiness otherwise.  And all this said--I DO like the idea of putting some limitations on expansion. I just think Civ 5 handled it all wrong.   CONSTRUCTION: Building anything in Civ 5 is a pain. Even a lowly freaking scout takes way too many turns to build. And if you forget about scouts--want to build even a handful of military units early on? Prepare to sacrifice tons of production to do it. Just one single unit will take turn after turn after lousy turn to build. And the buildings are just as bad. Want to build a basic watermill? Expect it to take 5, 10, 20 turns. Constrast with for instance, Stars!, which is my favorite strategy game of all time. In that game you can often build 2, 3, or 4 scouts from the very first turn, then send them off to rapidly explore your galaxy. Why are even the most basic of things in Civ 5 so incredibly expensive? Exploring nearby territory for interesting discoveries should be one of the most enjoyable parts of the game, but because Civ 5 gives you almost no units to do this, much of the possible fun is lost, and you will probably still have considerable unexplored territory quite close to your first city, even by turn 10.  AI's: There are few diplomatic options in Civ 5. Pretty much all you can do is some limited trading, make some basic threats, and some basic requests for assistance against other civ's. The designers clearly a lot of effort into creating Civ 5, crafting a number of different aspects of the game. In fact, although I complained about the happiness and construction in the game, the creators at least put some thought into it. Diplomacy, on the other hand, is very minimalistic. I've not played a ton of games, but if you want to see a much more robust diplomacy system (as well as a game with a whole lot of other superior features), consider picking up Space Empires III. Sure, it's about 20 years old now, and the graphics are dated. But it at least serves as one example of diplomacy done better. Why in the world haven't modern games caught up with advancements from way back in the 90's?    THE GOOD   GRAPHICS: The graphics in Civ 5 are fairly pretty, the landscape of the world tastefully artistic. You may want to spend some time just looking at the mountains and rivers, the hills, tundra, plains, forests and jungles. There are a number of different types of terrain, in fact. However, once I've explored a fair amount of territory, the game starts to slow down, and the more territory I explored the more it slowed down. In my huge game I spent ~200 hours on, I eventually had to switch the game to strategic view, meaning I didn't have the pretty graphics anymore, but at least the thing was playable.  I should also note that each civ leader has their own separate diplomacy screen--Montezuma, Queen Elizabeth I, Catherine the Great of Russia, George Washington, and plenty of others appear before you, beautifully animated in 3D. Unfortunately, once you become familiar with the diplomacy functions on these screens, you will likely find yourself disappointed: Firaxis spent so much time on the graphics here, so why not the actual diplomatic functions? It's a classic case of a shiny veneer without substance.  GOLDEN AGES: I love Golden Ages. In spite all my complaints about the happiness system, once you understand it's flawed logic, you will probably learn to avoid growing your civilization too much, in order to keep your happiness relatively high. Each turn, the net happiness or unhappiness you generate is added to a running total from many turns bfeore it--so if for the first 5 turns you generate 5 happiness, then 6 for the next five, then 7 for the next five, you will have 90 happiness saved up at the end of those 15 turns.  Once your happiness 'bank' is full, your civilization will experience a Golden Age. This is a unique feature, something I've never seen before, and a very enjoyable thing to have occur. Your civ will generate more production, culture, and money as well, all of which are resources you can use for different things in the game. The Golden Age itself lasts for several turns as well, dependent on how long the game is. I think I played on the second-longest game length setting, and Golden Ages lasted 10 turns for me. It's a nice way to reward players for managing to keep their happiness high.  CULTURE: Another feature I've never seen in a 4X. Certain buildings and civilizations give you the ability to produce culture, which is actually generated numerically and used as a resource. What do you use it for? Cultural improvements. They're just buffs for your civilization really, and some of them lock you out of certain other cultural paths. You'll want to strategize to get the most out of these improvements.  SCIENCE: Research different technologies to improve your civ further.  RELIGION: You can also use religion to improve your civ and expand your influence across the globe.   Steam is limiting me from writing more, so I'll conclude: Civ 5 is fun and enjoyable, but with glaring annoyances.  8/10.

Played from the first-person perspective, this action game focuses on environment navigating through parkour (a form of urban athleticism , melee fighting, and the option of firearms (using basic real world guns). While the story is a little lacking and is on the short side, it has very enjoyable gameplay (which can be a little frustrating at times due to the player perspective) and a beautiful cityscape to traverse. A unique take on first-person games.

app_name: The Dark Eye: Chains of Satinav
review_text: I play classic point & click adventure games a lot and I also played The Dark Eye RPG in the pen & paper version in my youth. I cannot remember all facts and stories of the world Aventuria in which Chains of Satinav plays. Anyway I do not care whether the world is 100% reflected by the game. Nonetheless, the game designers have done a remarkable job to feel like being in Aventuria.  And also to create a really dense atmosphere with characters that you learn to love during gameplay. Like many Daedalic adventures the story is not a simple good vs. evil plot. Below the obvious surface (evil seer, ravens, dark creatures ...) one may notice thoughts about being determined in life though people don't support you or in general whether ends justifies the means (lies). An important part of adventures are the puzzles and Chains of Satinav has a lot of good ones (especially in the faerie world). Unfortunately there are also times when the puzzles become easier again. Why? Although I appreciate that Chains of Satinav takes you to many places, the problem here is that some of them are very restricted. You cannot go further until you solve that handful of puzzles which makes it too easy sometimes and makes the game feel too 'vertical'. What was really refreshing however was the idea of having a destruction and (later) a repair spell in your repertoire which distinguishes the adventures from others a little bit. Those spells are also really well woven into the other puzzle sequences. The world is drawn by wonderful fantastic sceneries which fit very well to the various places the adventure takes you to. The animations could be smoother though. The voice acting is done very well at least in the German version I played - whereas the music is let's say unobstrusive and could be diversified more and emphasize some events better.  A very underrated adventure in my opinion. All in all a game that I really enjoyed a lot. I will miss Geron and Nuri.

Because of these options Dishonored has masses of replay value and has a wide selection of choices with different tactics that are rewarded with spacious environments riddled with pathways and secrets.  Unfortunately Dishonored isn't perfect with the stilted voice work and animation and the lack of stealth options that gets beaten out by action gameplay even though it encourages stealth to get the good ending.   Dishonored may have flaws, but they are outweighed by the beautiful atmosphere and openness of the level structure and won't cause massive damage to what is a fantastic game.

Excellent game, hardly any problems to speak of. Would recommend to basically anyone who can still appreciate older style games and who isn't turned off by cheesy japanese storytelling. This game is also very friendly to people with high end PCs, supporting resolutions beyond 1080p and refresh rates beyond 60Hz. The game actually renders very well at higher resolutions, though the character sprites and portraits become somewhat pixelated.  Ys Origin is essentially a 3D action platformer in the style of Alundra for PS1, and to some extent the 2D Zelda games. I would compare it to a sped-up Link to the Past. Emphasis is primarily on combat, but also on finding your way through the game. Each area has a few branching sub-areas which you must navigate to find a key to the boss room. This usually involves going through the area until you reack a fork in the road where reaching one end of the fork will activate some switch that will allow you to grab the boss key from the other end of the fork.   Combat is very fast and consists of attacking, using special abilities, jumping, and some other moves. You have 3 special abilities that you can use in combat that offer you some mix of damage, utility, and range. The abilities are different for each of the 3 playable characters but serve the same general purpose. Part of the game is figuring out which ability is most effective for the area you are exploring or the enemy you are fighting.  The best part about this game is without a doubt the bosses. Every fight throws something completely unexpected, yet refreshingly original at you. Very rarely will you ever go up to a boss and simply whack it until it dies. Almost every boss has a weakness that must be revealed in some way before you get the chance to actually deal damage. And each boss has attacks that you need to dodge in creative ways instead of just running in a circle.   I will give the example of what was probably my favorite boss in the game. The boss is basically a centipede where each pair of legs was its own section of body. The way to kill the boss is to destroy each body segment. The challenge lies in the fact that each time you destroy a body segment, it will start spewing poison bombs that explode if left on the ground too long. This severely inhibits your mobility and also throws a lot of extra damage into the mix. Keep in mind the boss is constantly moving, so you not only need to dodge everything, but also catch up to it so you can actually deal damage. By the end of the fight the field is entirely covered in poison bombs and you have to find a way to weave through them, jump on the boss, and kill the last segment before you die. This boss killed me probably 20 times, but it was still a great experience because each time you can make tiny optimizations or notice something new about an attack that will let you avoid it more easily.   Though this game actually involves very little actual platforming, the game utilizes 3D space very well. You are constantly moving up and down through the environment which is a nice change from the typical top-down games where everything is just a flat plane. 3D space is even utilized in combat, with airborne enemies that must be taken down with jumping attacks, to boss attacks that can only be dodged by jumping. From my boss example above, the same boss is able to climb on walls, going completely out of your reach. While doing this he will shoot lightning at you, which you must avoid to keep from falling behind. However, you have a special ability that when charged up has a huge vertical range. If you want, you can hit the boss with this move while he is on the wall. This introduces a risk into the fight by letting you hit the boss while he is high up, but you need to come to a stop to use the attack, opening up the possibility of getting hit by the lightning. Many combat moments in the game involes this same sort of risk/reward that weighs more damage against a higher probability of getting hit, which leads me into my next point...  This game has no consumable healing items and no way to heal yourself during combat. This is especially relevant during boss fights. You only have one health bar, and you have to kill the boss before he kills you. This makes for very tense fights, knowing that each mistake is permanent, each risk you take is a real risk and can't be immediately undone by healing yourself. These fights are reminiscent of many boss fights in JRPGs where you desperately hope that you can kill the boss before your healers run out of MP. You can't cheese out any boss by spamming healing items each time you take a lot of damage. This game forces you to learn the patterns and exploit the weaknesses of every single boss. The same is true during regular combat, but normal enemies drop health pickups and there is also an accessory in the game that allows you to heal which standing still. Even so, the game sometimes throws extra tough enemies your way, and you are forced to kill the lot of them in one go which can be just as challenging, especially if none of them drop a health pickup.    Now for some minor complaints:  The game starts throwing really annoying enemies at you late in the game. While I appreciate challening combat where you don't just mash a button until everything dies, these enemies also flat out waste your time either by being extremely resistant to damage or having small, spread out periods of vulnerability.  The plot of the game is pretty bottom of the barrel. It's the exact same good vs evil, friendship is power, find your true strength generic japanese crap that we've all heard a million times before. The game isn't quite as cheery as something like Dragon Quest, but you can pretty much predict the exact plot of the game if you've played any other game that follows the same story structure.    Here is some stuff that may be good or bad, depending on your tastes:  While there are no healing items in the game, you can always just grind levels and become near-invulnerable if you are having trouble with a boss. I would really recomend not doing this, as every boss in the game is beatable in a reasonable amount of tries without this, but it's still possible. Sometimes grinding a bit can really help out, as being underleveled during a boss fight is extremely punishing as you can sometimes be dealing damage in the single digits and taking huge chunks of damage for small mistakes.   The game takes place in a tower and only in a tower. This means no towns, you never encounter any new NPCs to talk to, the only 'shop' is at save points, and the only way to go is up. This does bring about a few perks as well though. If you like streamlined games with no fluff, this is perfect for you. The tower structure also lends itself to being a game with almost zero backtracking through previous levels. The only times you do this are for quick story progression segments.   Overall, this game is great and worth playing at least once, if not multiple times with each playable character. It is very reminiscent of older games that were made by a small team of dedicated devs, and you can tell the game was made with love. If you are already a Ys fan, expect something above and beyond. If not, maybe save this one for last becuase I truly think it is the cream of the crop as far as the PC titles go.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: Rome 2 has its problems but it has been bandaged quite significantly since launch. As a Total War fan, this game feels very different from previous installments, which adds to the challenge. About a 100 hours into this and I am still enjoying the game and still have not completed the grand campaign for a single faction.  Graphically, the game is impressive; the waters are hypnotic with excellent light and shadows. Make sure your rig is up to it though.  Issues it suffers are artificial intelligence making unintelligent decisions, pathfinding issues on the grand campaign, and a naval warfare that one can easily win by stacking artillery ships. Additionally, there are features that I wish CA retained from previous installments (family tree, battle speeches, etc) and features that I wish they fleshed out more (mainly the internal politics feature). Overall, I am satisfied with the price I paid for this game because of its entertainment value despite its issues.

app_name: Eador. Masters of the Broken World
review_text: I have played this game for so long that it became my nightly ritual. This game is relaxing yet mind-engaging (perfect combo). It's a perfect game to play after a long day at work. I typically mute the game sound/music, and play my own relaxing music in the background.    This game is a MUST for people that Enjoy:  1) Turn-based strategy games. Some battles are straight forward easy, others you will really need to plan/think.  2) Playing King Bounty (KB ) Games. It is very similar to KB, except soldier units can also level up and perma die.  3) Playing Fire Emblem. 4) Character building by leveling up and choosing new skills (RPG-like) 5) Progressive difficulty that can only be overcome with proper tactics. Example: magic beats physical armor, etc. 6) Many ways of playing a game (4 heros, and tons of soldiers to choose, they all have different skills and builds)  7) Taking your time to thoroughly complete a level rather than rush through it. No need to rush, enjoy the battles.  8) Loot, Loot, Loot. Decent number of items to find and collect with various tiers.   I would recommend it to anyone that enjoys turn based battle tactics.   Ed

A lot of games benefit from having random or procedural elements, but it never occurred to me to apply those principles to a stealth game. I always thought stealth games needed to be tightly tuned, with carefully-designed levels and sharp AIs on patrol routes and such. Sir is very much a stealth game that benefits from randomness, but not without some significant drawbacks.  Sir drops you into the middle of five randomly-generated islands, each populated by murderous metallic monsieurs out for your human hide. A mysterious device brought you here, and is now scattered into 16 pieces across the archipelago. Each piece must be returned to the standing stones at the center of the center isle if you are to return home. To aid you in your task, there's a whole host of items to scavenge up, some useful, some not. As you find more pieces, the robots step up their efforts, including robo-dogs, scarecrow sentries, hot air balloons, and more monstrous machines to hunt you down.  The robots are the heart of this game, and they are at times terrifying. Sure, they make conspicuous beep-boop noises as you draw near, but once they spot you with that heart-stopping bleep, you're in deep trouble. The 'bots are almost as fast as you, but they have guns and are pretty good shots with them. They're also intelligent about chasing you, following as far as they saw you go and then making educated guesses about where to search from there. Most robot patrol the islands in groups, paying close attention to structures that can be scavenged but following and suspicious leads they find. Other robots mill around the artifacts you need to recover, making sure you have to deal with some of them to get home. Getting caught can spell disaster if you can't get away fast enough, or if they plug you with a lucky shot that leaves you bleeding out.  You have two important numbers to keep track of, Health and Vitality. Either one bottoms out, you're done. Health regens slowly unless you've been shot, in which case you need to bandage yourself up using an item. Vitality drains over time with activity, and must be restored with food. And THAT means risking patrols to sneak into abandoned villages and churches to loot. You can't enter any structure in the game; the doors to buildings serve as loot containers. It's a bit of a blow to immersion and options for hiding, but other than that the loot system is solid. You have limited space to store weapons and traps, food and bandages, and any other interesting odds and ends you find. It's up to you to figure out what use some items have, which adds an element of mystery and experimentation to the game.  You can only save at the standing stones or the boats to other islands, so you really have to think twice about risky moves. Stealth in this game is tricky... it seems more based on proximity than action, and the robots are good about looking over their shoulders. Proper sneaking takes a long time given the distances, and waiting for patrols to move on is the safest but most time-consuming route. I'm still learning how to deal with them, but honestly I've been able to run by and grab a few artifacts right in front of them without being murdered. Getting caught can be frustrating though, given how hard the robots are to lose. You can fight back if you find weapons, but combat is not a focus and can be pretty fiddly. And in terms of navigation, you have a compass that marks the boats, but if you want to get any additional use out of it you have to find a map and mark it up yourself with points of interest.   Is it a good game? Yes, but one you have to put time into. Finding 16 fragments across 5 islands is going to take time, even moreso if you play the stealth game right, scavenge diligently, and use your map to the fullest. The randomization keeps the game fresh but can lead to situations where you're trapped between patrols for extended periods or find artifacts in nigh-impossible locations. It's not a particularly attractive game either, with some low-poly models and muddy textures. But if you can settle into it and give it some time, it'll keep you busy for a good long while.

app_name: I Have No Mouth, and I Must Scream
review_text: Interesting 90s point & click that passed me by until now.   Good:   -It's based on a novel and the story/concept is pretty cool. The novel's author also wrote most of the game's dialogue and voiced the antagonist. Story is fairly 'deep' for this kind of thing, heavy on metaphor - reminds me of Silent Hill 2 in that way. -Surreal nightmare environments are pretty cool -Controlling multiple characters in their own 'episodes' which are united at the end - different format from most p&cs -Multiple possible endings  Bad:  -Obscure fail states. If you fail an episode, you have to start it again (or reload save). Trouble is, it's not always obvious that you HAVE failed, let alone why you've failed. You can be running around for hours looking for a solution that doesn't exist because you've already broken it. Terrible design. -Usual point & click issue of certain puzzles making no sense & having ridiculous/obscure solutions that defy any logic. Problem is the old 'use everything on everything' trick can break the game (as mentioned above) so you have to resort to using guides :\\   Picked it up for a fiver, worth a punt at that price if you like point & click games.

app_name: Dysfunctional Systems: Learning to Manage Chaos
review_text: All right, so. I orignally bought this game as a gift for a friend for Christmas and stuck it on my wishlist because I can play visual novels on my PC and not much else. My friend Chris gave me a copy for my birthday a few days ago.  Immediately into the game I was sucked in. I related to the main character, Winter, in ways that I wasn't expecting. Meanwhile the world that is built around her and Cyrus is captivating. The concept of mediating is beautiful. I felt like I could really submerge myself into the gameplay and enjoy this for hours. I became emotionally attached.  And not too long after, my first playthrough was over. The episode felt short to me (I had taken the quickest end by accident). So I played it again. And again. And again. I unlocked all achievements. I want more.  I came to find out that Dysfunctional Systems was originally a flop on Steam, but now Dischan has the second and third episodes are being funded through Kickstarter. After checking the Kickstarter, I found out that for March 10-March 17th the game is only 99 cents.   I urge every single one of my friends to play this VN. It is an action-based, decision-making VN with great characters and fantastic dialogue and a BRILLIANT concept. If you enjoy it PLEASE help fund the Kickstarter - they need roughly 15k CAD (roughly 13.5k USD) in 11 days to fund the rest of the trilogy.  The Kickstarter can be found here:  https://www.kickstarter.com/projects/dischan/dysfunctional-systems
No one Plays this Online so if those were your intentions dont buy this game . today is 6/20/2015

FIRST IMPRESSIONS:  First of all, I've been waiting for this game for a long time, being a huge fan of the first one. It is actually the only game I've ever pre-purchased, and I think the only one I ever will.  That said, this game feels like an evolution of the first one. It takes what made the original one great and expands upon it. The randomness, the huge item catalog, GREAT replay value, etc. is back, everything you love, plus a bit more variety. Surely I'll be playing this one for a long time.  If you never played it, and want to give it a chance, make sure to do so with an open mind, as the theme of the game is quite polarizing. The game is very dark and gory, but also there's poop. A LOT of poop.  Now let me tell you about what you MIGHT find alienating if you played the first game in the series:  First of all, The GRAPHICS.- This game is supposed to go for a retro, 16-bit-ish look, however, I personally feel it is in that uncanny valley where it looks a bit too modern (think Gameboy Advance graphics) but not quite, and not old enough to look the part. I dont mind retro, pixel-art games, and while it might look pretty, I'm pretty sure someone will take a look at it and just plainly say it looks a bit 'low res'. Sure, that's the point of pixel-art, but like I said, it doesn't look old enough to feel retro. I do like how it looks, tho.  The MUSIC.- This is a bit different than in the first game, different composer, different feel. I find it very tasteful, qute capable of getting you into the mood, and a lot chiller than the first game. I thought I wasn't going to like it very much from the previews but I ended liking it up A LOT, just listen with an open mind.  Bottom line, I suggest giving this game a chance, there's replay value by the bunch, great music, good looking visuals, and at $15 (or 10 if you had the first one) it is a steal.

An old faithful Amiga player returning to look at Speedball 2. It's the same game, and at the same time it really isn't the same game!  For any Amiga purists out there, you'll notice that causing injuries to other players is a lot easier on this 'HD' version, and the ball isn't quite as quick or fluid in the way that it moves around the screen. To me at least it feels like passing became slow and easy - and the ball certainly doesn't travel as far. Gone are the days where you charged the opposing CF and threw it to your winger with plenty of style and grace.  The faces of old memorable characters are still there, but a real shame they haven't kept their old names - greats such as the old Jams, Norman, Montez, Cooper... all present, but with stupid names. I'd love to have seen them keep those, or at least make player names editable.  If you can overlook what some would say are trivial aesthetics, the game is enjoyable, with added features such as scouting your opponents stats before playing them and suchlike.  At the Steam sale price of under £2 its an excellent bargain.  Now, if only I can find Bloodwych, Supercars 2 and Sensible Soccer on here... ;)

Great game. I have almost 400 hours in this game for good reason. Rocket League is easy to learn and difficult to master, and has a great learning curve. The devs actively try to improve the game's features, functionality and fairness. It is very satisfying to finally hit the ball well, then to hit it into the goal, then to start doing aerials and aerial goals and many more tricks and skills. It looks great, feels great and is overall a fun game with great features such as cross-platform, splitscreen and great car customization.  - Side note: If you do buy this game on PC (which you would if you're on Steam), try to start with a controller and not a keyboard and mouse. A game pad has more functionality in Rocket League and other driving games and has a higher skill ceiling than a kb/m. Plus if you do switch from kb/m to controller at a higher level like I did, you'd have a hard time adjusting to the different control style.

app_name: Valiant Hearts: The Great War™ / Soldats Inconnus : Mémoires de la Grande Guerre™
review_text: This game is really engaging. Taking place during WW1, you play as multiple characters that are affected by the war in some way or another. You journey through tourn up cities, towns, trenches, and battlefields all based on events that occured at the time. It has very interesting puzzle mechanics that feel very fresh. It uses the characters' abilities such as digging, healing or cutting very effectively. The game is also very immersive. You know what the characters and all the other NPCs are thinking. Whether it be suffering or joy, you will always know. This game also gives you collectibles and trivia about the war that help teach a thing ot two. The game's only real shortcoming is that it is rather short. As you can see, it only took me about six hours to complete it and that was probably because I got stumped on certain puzzles. But overall, if you like puzzle games and you are interested in WW1, definitely buy it. And let us honor the soldiers that sacrificed themselves for their country.

I mainly play in stereoscopic 3D so this rating is largely based on that aspect: The environment and effects render perfectly and overall game runs/plays very smooth, this alone makes it a must try in my book. I know these factors can vary by PC build, so they have a demo available if you want to test your setup.  From a gameplay point of view, I would honestly consider the game more of an action genre than a suspense. For example; I would compare it to a creepier version of resident evil than to something like Silent Hill. I also like the fact that it has simple enough controls, accurate aiming is dam-near-impossible in 3D so the auto-lock feature makes the game actually playable.  As for the actual story, it’s not too bad this far in but honestly the 3D immersion adds to a lot of it, I tend to get sucked into the story easier and can play for hours at a time before the wife gets mad.  Overall 10-10 for a stereoscopic 3D experience!

My mouse has never been so abused in its life. In this first person shooter (diver) or 3rd person (shark) you play underwater to kill each other in a man vs shark epic fight. This is DEPTH. A quick tutorial that you learn that as a diver, hearing a heart beat means stop looking at S.T.E.V.E.( your underwater robot) and picking up gold/treasure ( witch you buy guns and upgrades like mines and explosives and medikits with) and keep your gun up, because a shark is aiming to EAT YOU. Then you learn that swimming in the blue deep as this ancient predator is amazing satisfying and bloody. Easy controls and great graphic visuals, you easly see a beutiful and deadly undersea paradise. The maps truely feels like ancient ruins or recks of ships. There are 12 so you have plenty of ocean to explore.Tight corners to hide in and windows/doors for sharks to bust through and EAT YOU. Not much story other than divers want treasure and gold and sharks are well, predators. After the tutorial you have 2 modes. Blood and GOld, in witch 4 divers vs 2 sharks go head to head. Divers need gold for their upgrades and to make it back to their ship with the loot and sharks need to just EAT YOU ( kills award upgrades points for evolutions). Megaladon is the hunt of divers vs one very massive overpowerd shark. FUN!. Well divers do have a diffrent veriaty of guns and explosions to kill the sharks with. Also mines and sonar to help you find them before they find you, Or at least alert you when they are just about to EAT YOU. Sharks just have a short and long lunge attack. Also they can eat seals to gain health. Unlock abilities and evolutions by leveling up your profile. Typical. Divers and Sharks do each get diffrent upgrades in witch they can purchase during game to help them in the epic battle. Divers can unlock or buy with gold, net guns or posion bullets and the dreaded harpoon guns. While the shark upgrade points get evolutions like serrated teeth for bleed damage. Also sharks each have a diffrent special abiltiy for each species. Tiger, Great White, Thresher,Hammerhead, Bull, and Mako. Each has a diffrent base stats and ability for a diffrent play style and a way to EAT YOU. Sharks when they do get ahold of you, have to thrash their mouse to cause damage. Yes, this is where my mouse has been ruined comes in. Be prepaired. After unlocking abilities and evolutions you can also unlock skins for weapons and suits and sharks. FUN! There is even a Steam store for this. Play in your own JAWS movie being the shark or the diver. The tension and heartpounding is real, this is great to play during shark week.

Andrzej Sapkowski created a masterpiece - CD PROJEKT RED gave life to it.  I think this is one of the very few games that I felt the urge to buy when playing it. It's THAT perfect.  This game made me fall in love with it. I fell in love with the characters. With Yennefer, even if he's annoying at times. With Triss, the beautiful red-head who is almost like a victim in Geralt's story. With Cirilla, the beautiful Lady of Time and Space. With Vesemir,   the martyr Witcher who held on to his beliefs until the very last breath was taken away from his body  . With Zoltan, Dandelion, Priscilla... everyone. Except Dijkstra. ♥♥♥♥ that guy.  I was heartbroken because I was finishing the game. It was impossible. I couldn't accept it and I didn't want to accept it. Now I'm reliving the adventure trying to get all achievements and having a 100% completion. Even if it will hurt again to let this beautiful story lay to rest after all will be done, it won't be something that will be forgotten.  This game just set new standards to what I'm going to love and play in the future: storywise and character-wise.  Geralt is by far one of the most badass characters in the game. I don't think this needs to be argued. It's just that this guy is freaking epic.  Jumping back to the game - why is the story so catchy? Every choice you make will, more or less, have an impact about what will happen. You can't choose Good or Evil, there is only Greater Evil and Lesser Evil. You will be moraly tested at some times when you will literally feel not worthy of calling yourself human because of the choice you did or you will think you had too much mercy and you wonder what would have happened otherwise.  This game is an emotion-blender. It literally makes you go through all the phases. You will fall in love, you will feel desperate, you will be heart-broken, you will be like a child waiting for christmas present, rushing to see what happens with the character you just fell in love with and if you like to adventure into the unknown, this game is full of it. A lot of sidequests and places to discover. It's beautiful. It's perfect!  Also, I recommend reading the books. The game is based off a book series written by the polish author Andrzej Sapkowski. I think there are 6 or 7 books (question mark?) but it's worth it. I started reading them because of this game. Now I can't let go of THIS game and of the book.   Final mark? PERFECT/10.  You want a great game? Have The Witcher 3. You want a fun game? Have The Witcher 3. You want a beautiful story? Have The Witcher 3. You want to fall in love with the characters and the plot? Have The Witcher 3. You want to have an adventure you will never forget and you'll fall in love with? Have The Witcher 3. You want to have the best game? Have The Witcher 3.

I started playing this game, and was immediately immersed in the story. It's all dialogue, and not action-based, reminded me a little bit of old-school RPG's, the pen and paper type.  Anyways, you play through several characters and are presented with response choices that really make you think. There are a lot different elements that you're presented with, but I'm not sure if it affects the outcome because...  It has the most abrupt and unexpected ending, that I've ever seen in a modern game. I took my time through each of the character story lines, and during the last part I'm talking to someone and it cuts to the end credits!  I have no idea what ultimately happened to the city, and it was just a really poor experience... after so much potential.  It looks like the creators/developers ran out of money.  But yeah, this is not worth $15.00. In reality it's nothing more than a demo that will entertain you for a few hours. I want a refund, and the devs of this game should be ashamed with charging so much for a product that clearly doesn't deliver enough content.  Grades:  Graphics - 5/10. This game is not about graphics. They're stylistic and simple. Sound/FX - 7.5/10. Easily the strong point of the game, although the tracks do loop. Controls - 6/10. They work without any bugs, but this is a simple point and click game only. Story - 6/10. Early on it's interesting, until the terribly abrupt and unexplained ending. Replayability - 0/10. There's no reason to play a game again when it only lasts (at most) a little more than 4 hours.  Overall - 3.5/10. Save your money unless wasting $15 for a 4-hour game is your thing.

While I didn't really enjoy Animal Gods, the game has some redeeming qualities which could make some people enjoy the game a lot more than I did.   The biggest problem I had with Animal Gods is that it is just a pretty boring game. The art style is pretty good and the story could be interesting but the game play drags down the game in my opinion. The controls work fine but the mechanics just aren't that interesting. The dash ability is fun for a while but I was kind of bored with it by the end of the 'dungeon'. The combat in the game is pretty dull since all you have to do is find a safe spot close enough to the enemies to be able to strike them without being hit since the enemies won't chase you.  If you like games that mostly focus on the art style and story I think you could enjoy Animal Gods quite a bit. If the game play is most important to you though, I would at least wait for a sale to purchase Animal Gods.  Note: I received a free review copy from the developer.
