A nice compelation of the three LiEat games. I've always loved Miwashibas games, and while LiEat is actually my least favorite of their works, it's still a nice series in it's own ways. Miwashibas art is great, and other then 1bitheart's sprites, it feels the most polished in the pure amount of CG's and Movies that are in the game. Miwashiba has also always had great character designs in general, and with how the main characters change in appearance (Efinas papa especially) it's fun seeing how they evolve.  The story can be enjoyable depending on your preference. For the most part it feels like episodical games ala shonen anime, but the first game is somewhat connected to it's prologue novel, and the last game is very character centric over the main characters past. I do feel like there should have been more fleshing out on the characters and story, and the battle system honestly isn't neccessary. I think Miwashiba realized that fault, since their later game doesn't include a battle system at all, instead opting for Dangan Ronpa/Ace Attorney style gameplay. All in all, LiEat has it's faults, but it is genuinly a fun and beautiful game to play, and I also reccomend playing miwashibas other games, Alice_Mare and 1bitheart if you find yourself enjoying miwashibas storytelling, characters, and art.

This game is remarkably addictive and fun. The cyberpunk theme, killer soundtrack and RPG-ish aspects really make it shine. The game uses a semi-Roguelike constant-progression system that penalizes you for death whilst still making your failed efforts worthwhile, even if only incrementally. The game can be a little tough at first but once you've bought some upgrades and got some progression under your belt, it becomes much more manageable. The game even manages to work some narrative into the campaign, which I did not expect.  I am not usually a fan of twin stick shooters, so I was skeptical at first, but the incredible soundtrack, RPG elements and cyberpunk style won me over. A great game.  PROS + Lots of fun and long-term replay value  + More depth than most games in the genre + Fantastic soundtrack and a great cyberpunk style  CONS - The procedurally generated levels can be a bit samey-samey

My YouTube video of part 1 -    https://youtu.be/jmvM6ba_TZQ    First of all, I would call this game more of an experience than a game. While it hints at a 'gameplay' aspect, it's really more like an interactive little art film with a couple mini games thrown in. While I would say that the memory game isn't as bad once you know how to exploit it, that aspect of the game just isn't fun. Memory is an entertaining concept for a child, and adding an unforgiving timer doesn't add to the fun or challenge, just the frustration. It would be like making you play hop scotch on hot coals. Adding annoyance to a kid's game doesn't make it better, just painful and embarrassing to screw up at as an adult.  Where The Orphan Dreams really shines though, is the pace. It gives you just enough time to absorb the situation, move on to the next scene, and then it throws you for a loop. I feel like I needed a counter for every time I said or thought 'Now what the f*ck is that?!' in genuine surprise. Little details immerse you and make you want to see just a little bit more, and it's well worth it.  I only got through part 1 so far, but it was a blast to do it, and I'm looking forward part 2. I hope Tayfun has some more twisted stories to tell in the future, because I'll certainly be watching for the next game.

Remarkably awesome spectacle fighter that ends up not taking itself too seriously with it's great sense of humor, in contrast to how it starts out. Admittedly, it starts out kinda weak, but about a quarter of the way through it begins to get real good, then at around the middle point, when you take control of Dante(whom you kick insurmountable amount of ♥♥♥ with in true DMC fashion) is when you realize how awesome this game is. Its by no means flawless(what game is?), the Gyroblades and the dice 'puzzles' were just annoying and in poor taste IMO, but too few and far between all the ridiculous ♥♥♥ kicking and platforming to come even close to ruining the whole experience.   It goes without saying that theres a lot of depth in the combat and the cutscenes are simply spectacular, but the cherry on top is the fact that DMC4 is just an exceptional PC port, probably the best port of all time because it rarely dips below 120fps while looking absolutely stunning, the only times it does drop is  sometimes  at the start of cutscenes(probably due loading) and also if you have vsync enabled it bugs out occasionally and drops to 60fps in some parts, but I tested it and with in-game vsync disabled its a constant 120fps otherwise. You'll probably wanna force vsync from your driver because it tears pretty bad. A minor issue which never happens during combat, but worth a mention I suppose.   Worth multiple playthroughs /10

Beautiful, minimalistic, atmospheric, and just a little pretentious, Mandagon is a very short game which is very much worth your time. There is little challenge beyond exploration and the most basic of puzzle-solving as you cannot die, get stuck, or otherwise reach a failure state, but the brief quest your little totem embarks on still manages to feel like the epic trial it is implied to be.  The story is perhaps a little TOO minimalistic, almost completely conveyed through the poetic ramblings of the totems scattered about the world, a couple of pictures painted on the walls, and a brief monologue from the game's sole NPC. Considering the subject matter (which I shall not spoil), it would have been better if there had been a little more shown and a little less told- maybe a few simple, symbolic cutscenes, to help you empathise more with a major character who never actually appears, and is only mentioned.  The atmosphere and aesthetics, though, are simply unparalleled. I'm honestly not sure whether there were 3D graphics used in the game or whether it was simply extremely elaborate pixel art used to simulate 3D motion (the fluttering of banners in the wind and the like) but either way the game is one of the greatest testaments to the effectiveness of 2D graphics in our modern 3D age. The game's design is rooted in Tibetan culture, and also seems to contain elements of Hinduism, but either way it certainly gives everything an extremely unique appearance. The music is stunning and the minimalist sound effects punctuate the mood with ringing intensity.  What little gameplay there is, is fine. Controls are responsive, and while the game world initially seems massive and easy to get completely lost in, frustration is minimal. Your tasks are initially completely opaque, but rapidly become clear and you will not be likely to become stuck and frustrated unless you're particularly slow or just plain unused to video games in general.  Mandagon is ultimately a beautiful, if brief, experience well worth the short amount of time it will take you to complete it. It's hardly life changing and could possibly have done with a  bit  more fleshing-out, but it's still an impressive piece of gaming art. Recommended.

There's no disappointment quite like seeing a game with great potential shoot itself in the foot. This game has a fantastic aesthetic, all lovingly designed and animated. There's plenty of character customisation available, the whole build-your-own weapon idea is brilliant, and gameplay is fast-paced and fun, in all the ways an old-school arena shooter should be. At least, I think it is, because I only got to play a single match.  The problem with Loadout is that the whole game is inaccessible, for two reasons: one,   matchmaking doesn't work.   Also, there's no dedicated servers, and, according to the devs, no intention of adding them either. Also, one time I accidentally clicked the 'form a party' button instead of 'find a match', and was directed to page with buttons to leave the party, find a match, add new members and chat, all of which were greyed out and unusable, which meant I had to restart the game in order to go back to matchmaking. So basically, you can't actually play the game. That's one comically oversized rocket to the foot.  You also can't do the other bit, i.e. customise your character or weapons, unless you're willing to drop some real cash. In game currency comes in two varieties: spacebux, used to unlock cosmetic items and extra loadout slots, which can only be acquired by purchasing them directly; and blutes, which are used for buying new weapon parts, which can be earned very slowly by grinding... unless you spend real money on a booster. Also, you need to level up to use/upgrade many parts, which you obviously do by playing. Not levelling up quick enough? Three guesses as to whether you can solve that problem by throwing money at it. Even better, the game has the audacity to state that because you can't  directly  buy weapon parts, it's therefore somehow not pay to win. Did you hear that sound? That was the sound of Loadout shooting itself in the foot again with the gun that it totally didn't pay for.  If it worked and it wasn't pay to win, I wouldn't have minded paying for Loadout. It really could have been a good game, but as it, this limping failure is to be avoided.

Postal 1 is more fun than it may seem, but the reasons for that are quite vague.  The game is an isometric shooter with buggy hand drawn environment, extremely janky controls and poor semi-scripted AI.   That said, the weapon variety is rich and the maps are interesting. Most importantly, it actually feels fun to 'outsmart' the dumb AI by shooting enemies that are stuck in the scenery or cannot see you and to trick them into blowing up and burning each other. It's a bit weird thing to say, but, as it turns out, the game's biggest appeal lies in its shortcomings.  Despite the fun factor, I would not recommend the game to myself, had I not played it a long time ago. The reason for this is that I'm not feeling comfortable with such level of violence anymore. I'm OK with some cartoony murder of faceless low-poly clones, but when the police ladies you have to kill start screaming in agony for half a minute and the game has a dedicated button for putting dying people out of their misery, that's a bit over the line for me.  On top of this, the game crashes quite frequently, which may be very frustrating when it happens after nearly completing a level. I don't recall this being a problem with the original game, so there must be something wrong with the revamped Steam version.  If the excessive violence, low resolution and general instability do not turn you off, however, you may have some good fun with the game, especially on the hardest difficulty (which is really not that hard, once you get used to the controls and the pacing).

10 out of 10 This game is a open world delight, 12 hours in and I've barely scratched the surface. It's got all the makings for a great game, an already established story line the writers contribute to and add on to what the movies have already done. Multiple great elements of gameplay, the major two being car combat and hand to hand fighting. Take the best part of the Arkham Asylum Batman games, the hand to hand combat. Then take the best part of GTA, the driving combat but in a more expansive environment. Don't worry about smashing into a building if you need to do a quick donut to evade an enemy... Mad Max was one of the original takes on the Post Apocalyptic future, and the game holds true to his (director Baz Luherman) lunatic fringe vision of warlords, gas guzzlers, and sand kings. THe game has a great soundtrack, on par with Skyrim for me, It rises and ebbs according the action in the game, and when its at its hottest, drums pound, choruses chant, and you really feel the adreline. There are plenty of different cars to choose, and mods to customize the hurt you bring you with metal angel. However you choose to ride out, this game is well worth the money. Soooo, this game, HOLY CRAP, I haven't fully fallen in love with a game in years. One of the first to absolutely consume my time was mirrors edge on the xbox 360. This game is mirrors edge blended with an open world zombie survival scenario. This game does an incredible job of making you feel like you're an actual survivor and the side missions to just help survivors in Harran made me feel like i was actually a part of their community. I wasn;t just going to get meds or bandages for quest points but because MY people needed them. If I could help i would.  The story line is full of intrigue and multiple twists and turns. Anyways all in all this game blends, survival in the form of combat, running, parkour, crafting, helping other survivors and trying to just not die. Also the volatiles and different types of zombies are badass. for the first 10-15 hours of gameplay i wouldn't leave safezones at night. First real game to fray my nerves in years while also making me feel like a badass running around the streets of Harran. Night running is now an amazingly satisfying experience! :D well that's my rambling on this AMAZING GAME! If you liked mirrors edge but wanna bash some zombie brains, get this game, you won't regret it.

I picked this game up on sale because it was 80% off. I'll be honest I wasn't expecting much due to the price tag and that I had never heard of it before but oh boy was I wrong. Visually this game is stunning, the levels all have a tonne of detail and are very well designed. The backgrounds of each level in particular are breath-taking. In the 4th level I found myself dying just to have a look around! Not to mention that the soundtrack compliments this game perfectly, and you find yourself soaring around without a care in the world because of the atmosphere created by this game.  Moving onto the gameplay - it's fun! I've never been a big fan of platfromers, especially 3D platformers, but this game got me hooked and I found myself playing it start to finish from the minute i picked it up. The jumping physics allow you to truly feel free in the air, and the grapelling hook gives you a fine degree of control as you soar from platform to platform. Certain parts can be frustrating as it is very easy to overshoot a platform or just fall short, but with a little bit of perserverence you should find a way through pretty quickly. I found the waypoint system reasurring and extremely helpful as it can be very easy to lose yourself in the surrounding enviroments.   The voice acting in this game isn't great, and neither are the character models, but then if you pick up this game, chances are you won't be playing it for that. The plot itself is rather simple, esentially just being a hunt. However, the way that the story is told is rather charming and I found myself rather enjoying the narrators commentary as the game progressed.   My only other gripe is that the game is rather short, but then when you consider how much you're spending, then it shouldn't suprise you. What matters is the quality of this game, not the quantity and in that respect I believe it shines.   Definately worth picking up and having a go!

I see some reviews here expressing disappointment that this game has a very low dollar-to-gameplay-time ratio. I would like to adress this, because I think that the games industry needs to stop it's obsession with gameplay quantity, and move more focus towards quality. I am an adult with many engagements and a growing awareness that my time here on this earth is limited. I don't want to squeeze as much game time as possible out of every dollar - instead I want to to squueze as much profound experience and fun out of every hour. I feel that the game industry could cater better to me, and Inside shows how to do it.  This game is 3-4 hours of *amazing* gameplay. Every minute has amazing artwork, level design, and narrative (without speaking a single word). What I like about this game is what I really dislike about 'open world' games - consistent production quality and though-through narrative and pacing of gaming elements. There is no dilution, no side quests, no unnecessary repetition, just great content all the way through. I wish that more games were like this, and I think that Playdead is showing the way of what a game can be with Inside.

This game is slow. It's pacing. It's methodical. It's maddening.  Maddening, like sailing into the pitch black sea with no land in sight and with nothing to go on more than someone told you there was land 'to the east.' But you've been out at sea so long you've forgotten where you started from and where you were supposed to go and all you know is you need to keep going east.  Then you see the problem. You're low on fuel. You're low on food. You're low on sanity. And you are far, far from home. You scramble to try to get home, trying to draw a balancing act, since if you run out of fuel you'll be stranded, but you can burn some of your food and use it as fuel, but what's the point of making it back to London if you're all starved to death and all that reaches home is a boat filled with corpses?  But in the end, you make it. You burned your last ration, you may have sacrificed one of your crew members to some nameless god in exchange for fuel, but (most of) you made it. A harrowing experience that drove you to the brink of madness in which you saw horrible things that would drive most people to claw their own eyes out.   And then, after you rest for a day or two, you head out to sea again.  Sunless Sea is a game about commanding a crew of sailors (or 'zailors', if you've taken up the local accent) through the nightmare of the Unterzee, a massive ocean entirely inside a massive cave system. Populated by madmen and nightmarish creatures, you strike out, seeking fame, fortune, an empire, or perhaps your father's corpse. Whatever your desire is, you will run into strange places and stranger people. An island of LARPers where everyone is expected to play a role and wear a mask. A place with a civil war going on between two bands of sentient rodents. The underwater descendants of Ghengis Khan. A hidden cave with a sentient force asking to play a game of chess against you. No place is quite like the one you just left.  There is no voice acting in this game. This is important for many people. There are thousands upon thousands of carefully handpicked words for you to read. The stories are beautifully and enigmatically written. Everyone has a story, and everyone has their secrets. What will yours be, Captain?

man, this game has some serious bugs/weirdnesses. the V-Sync issue is the most annoying one but there's a very easy couple of workarounds. you will see some in-car odd camera behaviour, and that's acceptable too. it just takes a bit of getting used to. the coolest thing to me is how dumb the NPCs are and the things they say. I hope this isn't by design or Hong Kong citizens wouldn't be too happy to be portrayed as stupid, say-all-kinds-of-random-things-just-to-sell-you-something kind of people. the side missions tend to get repetitive and the story... although is not that engaging it is fun (another British villain, really? I didn't see that one coming!). I know Hong Kong used to be a British colony and therefore it's safe to assume a lot, if not most people can speak English but there's this inconsistency I find weird: Wei Shen is from Hong Kong but has spent most of his life in the US. we got that! but he can speak Chinese, can't he? only problem is that he doesn't. he uses English 99% of the time. even with characters that never speak English. this is an annoying trick used in all kinds of media to help reduce the amount of subtitles the viewer has to read, but it feels odd. I speak four languages myself and I never had a conversation in which I would speak a language and the person I was talking to would speak another.  check my profile to see the amount of hours I've put on this game. it's amazing how a game with so many problems can be this much fun. I recommend it.

I was one of the testers before-hand, and have more hours than shown. I have three or so hours from offline play.   Being a big fan of difficult, rogue-likes and randomly generated content, I can fully recommend this game. The gameplay, like most games similar to this, takes adapting. You most likely will die a lot, at least at first, but you learn from each playthrough.   The art is simple, yet great. It really fits the vibe of a grim indie-RPG game with rogue-like features.  Each playthrough is entirely random generated. Enemies, weather, locations, layouts, all random. It's quite well-done, I would say.  Unlockable characters add to the replayability of the random generation. I've personally only used the first character, so I'm not able to say much on others.   All in all, I believe this game provides more than enough quality content, especially for such a low price. Thumbs up.

8/10 Great for fans of the first game, but the game style has an acquired taste.   This is a game for those who like tinkering with systems and enjoy building the best designs. Think of the old adage 'try to build the better mouse trap' The game itself is simple, design ships, face off against an array of enemy's and win. What this game does though to is credit or determent, depending on who you ask. Is that you must make all your plans and strategies before you even warp in.   No there is no way to directly control your fleet in battle. This game is pure macro management and completely cuts out micro. This is done for fairness in facing off agings other players fleets. Which is the main part of the game once you beat the main mission chain. The fact that since the opponent can only set guidelines for their fleet before they upload it is only fair that you must do the same as well.  The challenge and what makes this game have a high replay value is that you must build your fleet on a budget. Each match has a max number of points 'Honor' that you can spend and what you don't spend you earn in points at the end of the match provided you win. These points are then spent to unlock items like weapons, parts and new ship hulls including bits and bobs that you can use in the editor. You however can only earn that extra honer once and to earn more you must beat the challenge again with a smaller fleet.  The big new thing over this sequel is the ship editor. This allows you to truly make your own fleet. Form the base hull you can then add items to it to allow for a large range looks. Make your own race or try to remake your favorite SciFi franchise.   Also in this game modding is built in and fully supported. Adding new components is vary simple so long as you have the software to make the part and their is the full workshop integration that makes the steam version truly shine.   As of the writing of this review there are some stability issues that are being worked on by the team and updates are frequent. There are also a number of things about the interface and how things are done that can be improved. And personally the support from the developers is really great and fast.   For what the game offers the current price of 29.99USD may be a bit steep for most. Considering that there should be a good amount of DLC if the first game is any indication.  GSB1 was one of my favorite games ever and I'm sure I will get as much out of this one. If you enjoyed the first game you really will like the new one.

The cancellation of Silent Hills, and the subsequent removal of P.T. from PSN remains the single biggest blow to Triple-A horror fans in quite some time, if not ever. While Konami continues to burn every bridge that linked them to console games, disappointed horror fans can at least take solace that new and exciting horror games continue to manifest in various places courtesy of Indie devs. There is also the possibility that Hideo Kojima and Guillermo Del Toro will take their abandoned ideas for Silent Hills and kickstart their own horror project free from Konami’s incompetence, but until then we must stop dreaming about the future and partake in the nightmares of the present day horror scene. Uncanny Valley, the debut title from Cowardly Creations, is a game that very much borrows elements from Silent Hill, Twin Peaks, and other surreal stories of insanity. Players assume the role of Tom, who starts things off fleeing for his life from shadowy pursuers, then waking up from the apparent nightmare to begin his new job as a security guard at a mysterious facility. His only companions at his new job and residence are the overweight and overly cynical Buck and the soft-spoken yet friendly Eve, respectively. Other characters of various classes and corporeal existence come and go throughout the day, but the primary goal is to work each shift and shuffle on home to bed. The first unique feature of Uncanny Valley is the most noticeable: the visuals. While 2D pixel art is becoming more and more common, particularly with Indie games, it’s still a welcome sight to see the age-old medium used to tell a mature story, such as last year’s Gods Will Be Watching or the 2012 cult hit Lone Survivor. What Uncanny Valley lacks in facial detail, it makes up for with expertly crafted animations that convey what Tom is feeling at all times, whether it be pain, fatigue or fear. The second unique feature is what the game is missing, and that is the ability to fail in the traditional sense; there are no Game Overs or respawns, even when players fail to evade an inhuman pursuer. The days progress regardless of what action the player takes; whether Tom is completing his job as a security guard or lounging about in his apartment, once his internal clock ticks he’ll cease consciousness and drop to sleep regardless of where he is. Naturally, this results in multiple outcomes as well as multiple endings, which in turn results in multiple playthroughs where players can experiment as much as they want to see where their actions take them throughout the story. Or they could just look up an online guide and follow that instead. Regardless of how players approach it, Uncanny Valley is an interesting idea that is mostly marred by its technical oversights. Small examples include being forced to turn on the flashlight every time players switch screens, a pitifully short stamina meter for sprinting that takes ages to recharge, and dialogue text that tends to be obscured or cut short due to scripted events. There are also numerous puzzles and situations where the solution is not always clear, and since time progresses regardless of whether or not you succeed, it can prove annoying having to start the game from the beginning because of one botched event. In the end, Uncanny Valley brings an interesting premise and gameplay features that are plagued by arbitrary mechanics and technical limitations. With a bit more polish, Cowardly Creations’ next game may be the one to put the Indie studio on the map. Until then, give their first experiment a try once curiosity (or a Steam sale) puts you in the mood.

Undertale is a rarity, telling a story through the game mechanics in a way few others have managed to achieve. It has a sense of self-awareness that I've never seen in another game There's very little I can say without spoiling this beautiful gem, which is unfortunate.  The sound design and soundtrack are beautiful, complimenting the pixel art style with midi chiptunes and 16-bit sound effects. The two are so interconnected that if either were done with a higher quality without the other, it would not have worked as well as it does as-is. There's a certain level of charm that the 16-bit style has which helps solidify the emotional connection this game creates.  The characters are wonderfully written, the tone of the game is well paced in where it shifts between highs and lows, and there is a degree of challenge to be had as well. Not a lot else I can say for now, aside it's well worth the purchase price.

This is a pleasant match-three puzzle game with some good art and polish, and it's not too long.  In this game, you have to dig through a bunch of dirt so that some liquid can flow to the end of the level. You dig by eliminating tiles on top of the dirt. The camera scrolls on its own as it follows the liquid, and you can't scroll back to earlier parts of the level, so you have to deal with only what you see on the screen. Sometimes the randomness will deal a bad hand, but you can get out of trouble with planning and clever use of powerups.  I admit, I first thought it was a bit easy and simplistic for my tastes -- there are only four colors to match, and the powerups were a bit too powerful. About halfway through the game, I realized the main enemy was the timer, and it could put up a good fight. Later levels got fast and frantic as I had to dig through huge levels in only a few minutes, and the final few levels were quite a satisfying challenge.  Overall, it was pretty good! It seems like something straight out of the golden age of PopCap. If you have fond memories of Zuma, Bejeweled, Chuzzle, Bookworm, and the like, you might find this very enjoyable. For achievement hunters, this is a no-brainer. Get it on sale for an easy 5-7 hour perfect game.

Boring straight FPS with none of the charm of the others. This is BioShock in name only - the story and setting do not even relate to the rest of the franchise, other than a couple of minor similarities and a pointless (spoiler alert) cameo at the end. The DLC also attempts to shoehorn the stories together - poorly, but really I don't know why this even bothers to be called 'BioShock'. It is entirely dissimilar, and when you take away this being a sequel (since it isn't), what you're left with is really just a boring shooter with a couple of interesting but repetative mechanics, all of which were much-better executed in the first two games.  All that said, the non-gamey elements (for example, the opening sequence, everything up until you immediately need to kill or be killed), are completely excellent. It really set me up for a let down, as it had such a strong start. The story itself (for the main game, not the DLC) is interesting right up until the end (which.. to put it lightly, is awful). If this did not have the BioShock name, or the poorly-copied BioShock-like elements, I probably would have enjoyed it more.  Also skylines. Those things are completely stupid.

The game is set in the 1900's during the progressive era but you can think of Columbia as a conferdate dream city. There are some plot holes but overall the story will keep you engaged to the point, where you will wish there was less fighting and more story. You will not be dissapointed in this game. I think it's the best out of the three by far. Down side, without the DLC's. The game is only about 20 hours if you just stick the main plot line. Maybe you will get another 5 to 10 hours out of the game if you go off and explore everything there is, and another 2 hours of gameplay per story DLC.

When I learned that we are going to play as Ezio again, I was confused – I thought, we had enough of him during two games. But, it concluded the game just perfectly (along with Embers it concludes Ezio’s story – from birth to death – what game character can boast such attention to his life?) So, everything good that was improved compared to the previous game(s):  Bombs actually add more fun, the only bad thing is that because of them they had to change the puppeteering controls, which can be confusing a little bit. Several bombs are useful, but some of them were added just for the sake of having more possible variants. I do not get blood, skunk and money bomb. Used them only for completing challenges.   Remembering Ezio’s words from the corresponding book, hookblade is exactly what he needed. It really helps traversing the city faster. Of course, it is unrealistic, but please... we are talking about the game where you land in a haystack.   Mediterranean defence system was a major improvement from the previous part – adding the possibility of installing bureaus in occupied cities.   Multiplayer got my favourite feature – Deathmatch. Nearly 95% of my playtime is played on small maps. But, there is also a degradation point – now all the characters have the same weapons, no more unique ones. It is axe, sword, mace, etc. Not like previous game (razor, prosthetic claw, compass, etc.). At least, in high profile they have unique animations.   What I definitely didn’t like is that challenges didn’t change a bit since Brotherhood. I understand that not too many core gameplay elements were added, but some are practically identical.  By all means, recommending this game. You must know how Ezio’s history concludes.

I do think I could write rivers of ink about this game but, mostly, that would be missing the point of a good review, in particular a review that should be good for new player.  So let's start with basics and the game's feeling: you control a dinasty, you get to decide things about your realm, your subjects and your family members... and then your ruler dies horribly and then you get a new character and everything can change (usually for the worse, true GOT style).  There is little logic in Crusader Kings II mechanics, and most of time this happens because it actually reflects historical Feudalism. If you like logical strategy games you are mostly in the wrong place. That does not mean that you cannot find your logic trail here and there bust most of the time you just need to KNOW what triggers what. It is mostly about knowledge and less about strategy.  That said the best part is the narrative side. The mix of events, character personalities and objectives can create funny stories.  It is, and it has been for a while, my 'just 5 more minutes' game, replacing Civilization, and I am quite confident it will stay like that for a while.  If you like this mix but also more coherent and hardcore strategy experience move toward Europa Universalis IV. That is the agme for you

The battles are determined by DDR-style 'press the arrow key when it gets to the end' sequences, and whoever fails first loses a bit of health. Pawns do less damage to pieces in battles than other pieces, so if you're fighting, say, a knight vs a pawn, the knight only has to win 2 or 3 times vs the pawn's 4 times. If the captor wins, he captures the piece, otherwise they 'retreat' and both pieces sustain some damage.   Basically unless your skill levels in the duel battles are even, and you enjoy that kind of thing, the whole chess game doesn't work.   Online multiplayer does work, you just have to make sure both sides forward the two ports; it worked for us on the first try. Go to 'LAN/WAN' to play online multiplayer.   I paid for this at an 85% off sale, for which I'm very glad I didn't pay more. There are a lot of other game modes, but that seemed to be the most interesting, and it wasn't much fun. Single player might have been better, I guess?

Such a charming game. Everything about this game is so good. The objective is simple, get your ship to the end of the series of sectors without getting stomped and ransacked and destroy the Rebel mothership, which is a giant hunk of OP metal. The mechanics are all there and all have their place and strategies. The story is negligible but the scenarios are believable and tension is very much there as you progress and have to manage your resources and arsenal. Music is wonderful and really captures the atmosphere, or lack of, of space and has a beautiful serene feel to it but quickly shifts to tense battle music when confronted. Unfortunately, a lot of minor things are reliant on RNG, something I find a negative in almost all cases, such as hit%, fire, and whether you'll land on a star, pulsar, or have a crew die to some random event you run across. Hit% improves as the game progresses and there are ways to know where you're going but this still doesn't change that the RNG is very real.

The shooting in the game is extremely satisfying and there is a lot of enemy variety and gore. However the game seems to be inconsistent, with its setting where one moment you are in some giant graveyard, the next in a train station and the next in some giant platform fighting a giant flying demon and I am pretty sure I did not skip any of the cutscenes... Also, I couldn't be bothered to finish the game after I reached a boss which annoyed me and couldn't be bothered to finish. The levels themselves are great fun but the boss fights are not fun to fight in my opinion. Also the game is supposedly only 6 hours long which is something to consider.  However, if you find the game cheap, I would reccomend it.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: A very good strategy game, I love that one can explore and recreate the history and lore of this time period. These sort of historically time-framed games that the Total War franchise has created has been nothing short of a pleasure.  For those who are new to strategy games; this is definitely the one you will want; to ease you into that 'Emperor's' mindframe. The prologue campaign illustrates how to work the game to its best effect. However, the way to make whatever faction a person may choose to be the most effective is all dependent on the decision one makes. This is honestly what makes this game fun for me. The ability to choose and follow whatever path that will lead to victory.  The only negative mark that I have for this game is that there are still problems with the effectiveness or 'polish' of the AI. AI will still make some dumb decisions in a battle, and ultimately leads to making the game a bit easier on the player.

Amazing MMO with no subscription, story focus and active community. Make sure to join the server-wide community made channel '/chat join #Sanctuary' to always stay in contact with people.  Explore Kingsmouth (a city mixing Kingsport and Innsmouth from Lovecraft), Egypt and Transsylvania, uncover conspiracies and deal with ancient evils. Combine weapons and hundreds of skills and passives together to make a build tailored perfectly for you - or follow one of the pre-built decks. Assault Rifles combined with Blood or Chaos Magic? Entirely possibole in The Secret World. (as always, wait for a Steam sale)
